topics.title: Overview of CAD/CAM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT613 - COMPUTER INTEGRATED MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Overview of CAD/CAM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is CAD/CAM? — The Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):**
> **CAD (Computer-Aided Design)** is the use of computer systems to assist in the creation, modification, analysis, and optimization of a design. It encompasses both the geometric modeling of physical components and the engineering analysis (CAE) that validates the design before physical prototyping.
>
> **CAM (Computer-Aided Manufacturing)** is the use of computer software and hardware to plan, control, and automate manufacturing processes. It translates the design data (typically from a CAD system) into specific instructions for machine tools, robots, and other production equipment.
>
> **CIM (Computer-Integrated Manufacturing)** is the holistic, enterprise-wide integration of CAD, CAM, CAE, and all other business functions (inventory, finance, shipping) through a shared database to achieve a fully automated, paperless manufacturing environment.

**Key Industry Metrics & Standards:**
- **CIM 5-Level Model:** Level 0 (Component), Level 1 (Machine), Level 2 (Cell/System), Level 3 (Shop Floor), Level 4 (Enterprise).
- **NC Part Programming Languages:** **G-Code (ISO 6983 / RS-274)** and **M-Code**.
- **Standard Data Exchange Format:** **STEP (ISO 10303)** and **IGES (Initial Graphics Exchange Specification)**.
- **Geometric Modeling Schemes:** **2D Drafting**, **3D Wireframe**, **3D Surface**, and **3D Solid Modeling**.

---

## 1.2 The Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of CAD/CAM like a Master Chef's Fully Digital Kitchen.**
>
> Imagine a chef who never tastes the dish before serving it. In the old days, an engineer would draw a part on paper, build a real metal prototype, machine it, and *then* discover the stress concentrations. This was expensive and slow — like the chef cooking blind.
>
> **CAD** is the *digital recipe book and 3D virtual plate*. The chef can visualize the exact shape, simulate the bite, and even test the temperature (stress) before the food exists.
>
> **CAM** is the *robotic sous-chef and automated oven*. You take that perfect virtual recipe and feed it directly to a smart oven that cuts, bakes, and plates automatically.
>
> **CIM** is the *entire futuristic restaurant* — the recipe (CAD), the kitchen (CAM), the inventory fridge (MRP), the cashier (ERP), and the supplier deliveries, all wired to one central brain so nothing is ever lost in translation.

---

## 1.3 Why CAD/CAM Matters in Modern Engineering

> [!TIP]
> **The Paradigm Shift:** Traditional manufacturing was **"Make → Inspect → Fix → Repeat."** Modern CAD/CAM-enabled manufacturing is **"Simulate → Optimize → Make Right the First Time."**
>
> This shift is the foundational concept behind the KTU Module 1 syllabus. Every subsequent module in CIM builds upon this digital-first philosophy.

The primary drivers for adopting CAD/CAM in KTU-aligned industries are:

1. **Reduction of Product Development Time** — typically **30% to 70% faster** time-to-market.
2. **Lower Production Costs** — fewer physical prototypes, less material waste, optimized toolpaths.
3. **Improved Design Quality** — engineering analysis (FEA, CFD) catches failures before tooling is cut.
4. **Database Consistency** — a single source of truth across the design, tooling, and inspection teams.
5. **Customization & Agility** — rapid design changes enable mass customization without retooling.
6. **Manufacturing Automation** — direct transfer of geometry to NC machines eliminates manual programming errors.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of CAD/CAM Architecture

The theoretical foundation of CAD/CAM rests on three interconnected computer-based subsystems. KTU examiners frequently frame Part A questions around these pillars.

### Pillar 1: CAE — Computer-Aided Engineering
This is the *analytical brain* of the system. It uses the geometric model created in CAD to perform engineering simulations.

- **FEA (Finite Element Analysis):** Divides a complex part into thousands of small "elements" and solves stress, strain, and deflection. Governed by the matrix equation:
- **CFD (Computational Fluid Dynamics):** Simulates fluid flow and heat transfer.
- **Kinematic Simulation:** Verifies that mechanisms (e.g., gear assemblies, robotic arms) do not interfere with each other.

### Pillar 2: CAD — Computer-Aided Design
This is the *creative workspace* where geometry is born and refined.

- **2D Drafting:** Flat drawings (length × width). Used for simple prismatic parts.
- **3D Wireframe Modeling:** Only vertices and edges — no surface information. Like a birdcage of a part.
- **3D Surface Modeling:** Defines the outer "skin" but has no volume. Used for complex aesthetic shapes (car bodies).
- **3D Solid Modeling:** The industry standard. Defines full volume, mass, and material properties. Enables automatic mass property calculation (Volume, Mass, Center of Gravity, Moments of Inertia).

### Pillar 3: CAM — Computer-Aided Manufacturing
This is the *production execution arm*. It converts the digital design into physical reality.

- **NC (Numerical Control) Part Programming:** Writing G-code and M-code for CNC machines.
- **Toolpath Generation:** Software automatically calculates the cutter path for milling, turning, EDM, etc.
- **Post-Processing:** Converts generic cutter location (CL) data into machine-specific code (Fanuc, Siemens, Heidenhain).
- **DNC (Direct Numerical Control):** Networks multiple NC machines to a central computer for direct code transfer.

---

## 2.2 The CIM (Computer-Integrated Manufacturing) Wheel

KTU Module 1 heavily emphasizes CIM as the unifying concept. The **CIM Wheel** illustrates how a manufacturing enterprise functions as a single, integrated organism.

| Functional Node | Full Name | Engineering Function |
|---|---|---|
| **CAD** | Computer-Aided Design | Product geometry & drafting |
| **CAM** | Computer-Aided Manufacturing | Tooling & production planning |
| **CAE** | Computer-Aided Engineering | Simulation & validation |
| **CAPP** | Computer-Aided Process Planning | Routing & operation sequencing |
| **MRP** | Material Requirements Planning | Inventory & BOM management |
| **ERP** | Enterprise Resource Planning | Finance, HR, logistics, sales |
| **QA / SPC** | Quality Assurance / Statistical Process Control | Inspection & statistical control |
| **PBC** | Production Buffer & Control | Shop floor scheduling |
| **DNC** | Direct Numerical Control | Machine networking |
| **FMS** | Flexible Manufacturing System | Automated cell control |
| **AGV** | Automated Guided Vehicle | Material handling robots |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the core mathematical and quantitative relationships a KTU 2024 student must memorize for this topic. These formulas frequently appear in Part B derivations and Part A numerical questions.

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Boundary Conditions | Practical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Volume of a Solid Model** | $V = \iiint_{V} dV$ | $V$ in $\text{mm}^3$ or $\text{m}^3$ | Auto-calculated by solid modelers (e.g., SolidWorks, CATIA) |
| **Mass of a Solid Model** | $m = \rho \cdot V$ | $\rho$ = density in $\text{kg/m}^3$, $m$ in kg | Determines shipping weight, balance of rotating parts |
| **Center of Gravity (CoG)** | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\iiint x \, dV}{V}, \quad \bar{y} = \dfrac{\iiint y \, dV}{V}, \quad \bar{z} = \dfrac{\iiint z \, dV}{V}$ | Coordinates in mm | Crucial for tool design, fixturing, and rotor balancing |
| **Moment of Inertia (Solid)** | $I = \iiint \rho r^2 \, dV$ | $I$ in $\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^2$ | Required for vibration analysis, flywheel design |
| **NC Block Format (G-Code)** | $\text{N} \, G_{n} \, X_{x} \, Y_{y} \, Z_{z} \, F_{f} \, S_{s} \, T_{t} \, M_{m}$ | $F$ in mm/min, $S$ in RPM | Standard line of a CNC part program |
| **Cutter Path Time** | $T = \dfrac{L}{F}$ | $L$ = path length (mm), $F$ = feed rate (mm/min) | Used in CAM to estimate cycle time |
| **MRP Net Requirement** | $\text{NR} = \text{Gross Requirement} - \text{On-Hand Inventory} + \text{Scheduled Receipts}$ | Units in pieces | Inventory management in CIM |
| **CIM Level 4 (Enterprise) Cost** | $C_{total} = C_{design} + C_{tooling} + C_{machining} + C_{QA} + C_{logistics}$ | Currency unit | Total cost justification for CIM investment |

**Critical Note on LaTeX:** All table cell content uses `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the vertical pipe symbol to prevent markdown table syntax conflicts.

---

## 2.4 The "Why and How" Behind Each Step of CIM Integration

1. **Why share a database?** Because the design team, tooling team, and shop floor must all reference the *same* latest version. A paper-based system introduces version conflicts (a classic KTU pitfall question).

2. **How is the integration achieved?** Through a **Common Database** (the central nervous system), **Network Communication** (the nervous signals), and **Standard Data Protocols** like **STEP** (the language).

3. **Why is the CIM wheel circular, not linear?** Because feedback is continuous. A QA defect triggers a design revision, which triggers a CAM toolpath update, which triggers an MRP reorder — the loop never ends.

4. **How does a KTU examiner test this?** They will ask you to draw the CIM wheel, label all nodes, and explain the *flow of data* between two specific nodes (e.g., how does a design change in CAD propagate to a CNC machine?).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Estimating Total Production Cost in a CAD/CAM Environment

This is a **classic KTU Module 1 Part B (14-mark) question** because it tests the student's ability to integrate cost components from multiple CIM nodes into a single engineering decision.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Context:** A KTU engineering firm is bidding on a job lot of **N = 500 identical brackets**. The bracket has been designed in CAD with mass $m = 0.45$ kg, raw material cost $c_{mat} = ₹240/\text{kg}$, and requires a CNC cycle time $T_{cycle} = 3.2$ minutes per piece. The CAM system will be used for programming, and the firm must evaluate if CIM justifies the tooling investment.

### Step 1 — Define the Total Cost Function

The total cost $C_{total}$ of producing $N$ units is the sum of four major cost buckets:

$$C_{total} = C_{design} + C_{tooling} + C_{machining} + C_{QA}$$

This is the foundational equation. Every other term is a sub-expansion of one of these four.

### Step 2 — Expand the Design Cost

Design cost includes CAD software licensing, engineer hours, and CAE simulation runs. For a one-time design amortized over $N$ units:

$$C_{design} = \frac{C_{CAD} + C_{CAE} + C_{drafting}}{N}$$

Where:
- $C_{CAD}$ = software license + workstation cost (amortized) = **₹18,000**
- $C_{CAE}$ = FEA simulation compute time = **₹6,000**
- $C_{drafting}$ = engineer time to finalize drawings = **₹12,000**

Substituting values:

$$C_{design} = \frac{18000 + 6000 + 12000}{500} = \frac{36000}{500} = ₹72 \text{ per unit}$$

### Step 3 — Expand the Tooling Cost

Tooling cost includes the CAM-generated NC programs, cutting tool inserts, and fixture fabrication. Amortized over the lot:

$$C_{tooling} = \frac{C_{CAM} + C_{inserts} + C_{fixture}}{N}$$

Where:
- $C_{CAM}$ = CAM software hours for toolpath generation = **₹4,000**
- $C_{inserts}$ = total cutting inserts over the lot = **₹8,000**
- $C_{fixture}$ = custom fixture fabrication = **₹15,000**

Substituting values:

$$C_{tooling} = \frac{4000 + 8000 + 15000}{500} = \frac{27000}{500} = ₹54 \text{ per unit}$$

### Step 4 — Expand the Machining Cost (with Material and Cycle Time)

The machining cost per unit is derived from the CAD mass and CAM cycle time. The two sub-components are:

$$C_{machining} = C_{material} + C_{cycle}$$

**Material cost per unit** uses the CAD-derived mass:

$$C_{material} = m \cdot c_{mat} = 0.45 \cdot 240 = ₹108 \text{ per unit}$$

**Cycle cost per unit** uses the CAM-derived time and an hourly machine rate $R = ₹900/\text{hr}$:

$$C_{cycle} = \frac{T_{cycle}}{60} \cdot R = \frac{3.2}{60} \cdot 900 = 0.0533 \cdot 900 = ₹48 \text{ per unit}$$

Therefore, total machining cost per unit:

$$C_{machining} = 108 + 48 = ₹156 \text{ per unit}$$

### Step 5 — Expand the Quality Assurance Cost

QA cost includes CMM (Coordinate Measuring Machine) time, SPC data logging, and rework. For a process with first-pass yield $\eta = 0.92$:

$$C_{QA} = \frac{C_{CMM} + C_{SPC}}{\eta}$$

Where:
- $C_{CMM}$ = CMM inspection per part = **₹15**
- $C_{SPC}$ = statistical data logging per part = **₹4**

Substituting values:

$$C_{QA} = \frac{15 + 4}{0.92} = \frac{19}{0.92} = ₹20.65 \text{ per unit}$$

### Step 6 — Assemble the Final Cost Equation

$$C_{total} = C_{design} + C_{tooling} + C_{machining} + C_{QA}$$

$$C_{total} = 72 + 54 + 156 + 20.65$$

$$\boxed{C_{total} = ₹302.65 \text{ per unit}}$$

### Step 7 — Managerial Decision Analysis

For the lot of $N = 500$:

$$C_{lot} = N \cdot C_{total} = 500 \cdot 302.65 = ₹1,51,325$$

The KTU student must then comment on whether the CIM investment (CAD + CAM + CMM) is justified. The breakeven point where CIM pays for itself is:

$$\text{Breakeven Units} = \frac{\text{Fixed CIM Investment}}{C_{savings} \text{ per unit vs. manual method}}$$

If manual method cost is ₹380 per unit, savings per unit is $380 - 302.65 = ₹77.35$.

$$\text{Breakeven} = \frac{50000 \text{ (fixed CIM setup)}}{77.35} \approx 647 \text{ units}$$

Since the lot is 500 units, CIM is *marginally* justified, but future orders will be highly profitable. This kind of conclusion is exactly what earns full 14 marks on a KTU Part B question.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Apply Which CAD Modeling Scheme

| Design Requirement | Recommended Scheme | Justification | KTU Exam Tag |
|---|---|---|---|
| Simple flat plate with holes | **2D Drafting** | Lowest computational overhead, fastest output | High-frequency Part A topic |
| Complex car body styling | **3D Surface Modeling** | Captures freeform curves; no need for volume | Common in design engineering modules |
| Engine block with mass property check | **3D Solid Modeling** | Requires volume, CoG, moment of inertia | KTU 2023 Dec asked this directly |
| Quick dimensional check of a bracket | **3D Wireframe** | Fastest rendering; ambiguous surfaces are acceptable | Rarely the correct answer for production |
| FEA stress analysis of a bracket | **3D Solid Modeling** | FEA meshers require closed volumes | Mandatory for CAE |
| Toolpath generation for CNC milling | **3D Solid or Surface** | CAM systems require either volume or surface geometry | Direct CAM prerequisite |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — "3D Wireframe is NOT the same as 3D Solid."** A wireframe has *no surface* and *no volume*. You cannot compute mass, cannot perform FEA, and cannot generate a reliable CAM toolpath from a pure wireframe. Students who confuse these in Part A lose 2 of 3 marks immediately.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The CIM Wheel — Master Diagram

This is the single most important diagram for Module 1. KTU examiners award up to 4 marks in Part B for correctly drawing and labeling this wheel.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["CIM Central Database"]
    nodeB["CAD - Computer Aided Design"]
    nodeC["CAM - Computer Aided Manufacturing"]
    nodeD["CAE - Computer Aided Engineering"]
    nodeE["CAPP - Process Planning"]
    nodeF["MRP - Material Requirements"]
    nodeG["PBC - Production Buffer Control"]
    nodeH["FMS - Flexible Mfg System"]
    nodeI["DNC - Direct Numerical Control"]
    nodeJ["QA - Quality Assurance"]
    nodeK["ERP - Enterprise Resource Planning"]

    nodeA -- geometry data --> nodeB
    nodeB -- design output --> nodeA
    nodeA -- geometry --> nodeC
    nodeC -- tooling data --> nodeA
    nodeA -- model --> nodeD
    nodeD -- sim results --> nodeA
    nodeA -- design data --> nodeE
    nodeE -- process plan --> nodeA
    nodeA -- BOM data --> nodeF
    nodeF -- inventory status --> nodeA
    nodeA -- job orders --> nodeG
    nodeG -- schedule --> nodeA
    nodeG -- control --> nodeH
    nodeH -- commands --> nodeI
    nodeI -- NC code --> nodeH
    nodeH -- workpieces --> nodeJ
    nodeJ -- defect data --> nodeG
    nodeA -- financial data --> nodeK
    nodeK -- cost feedback --> nodeA
```

**Reading the Diagram (for KTU 7-mark sub-parts):**
- The **Central Database** is the hub.
- **CAD ↔ CAE** loop: design geometry is sent for simulation; simulation results trigger design revision.
- **CAD → CAM** flow: finalized geometry drives toolpath generation.
- **CAPP** is the bridge between design (CAD) and manufacturing (CAM) — it decides *how* to make the part.
- **QA → PBC** feedback: defects detected at inspection loop back to the production buffer for re-scheduling.

---

## 4.2 CAD/CAM System Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

This block diagram maps the hardware and software stack of a typical CAD/CAM workstation. KTU examiners use this style for "Explain the architecture of a CAD/CAM system" type questions.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeU["User / Engineer"]
    nodeS["System Software - OS"]
    nodeA["Application Software - CAD Module"]
    nodeB["Application Software - CAM Module"]
    nodeC["Application Software - CAE Module"]
    nodeG["Graphics Processor - GPU"]
    nodeCPU["CPU and RAM"]
    nodeDB["Central Database - STEP or IGES"]
    nodeOut["Output - CNC Code or Plots"]
    nodeIn["Input - Mouse Digitizer Scanner"]

    nodeU --> nodeS
    nodeS --> nodeA
    nodeS --> nodeB
    nodeS --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeG
    nodeB --> nodeG
    nodeC --> nodeG
    nodeA --> nodeCPU
    nodeB --> nodeCPU
    nodeC --> nodeCPU
    nodeA --> nodeDB
    nodeB --> nodeDB
    nodeC --> nodeDB
    nodeDB --> nodeOut
    nodeIn --> nodeA
    nodeG --> nodeU
```

**Block-Level Functional Walkthrough:**

1. **Input Layer (nodeIn):** Engineer feeds raw ideas via mouse, scanner, or digitizer.
2. **System Software Layer (nodeS):** The OS (Windows/Linux) manages resources and file I/O.
3. **Application Software Layer (nodeA, nodeB, nodeC):** Specialized modules — CAD for design, CAM for manufacturing, CAE for analysis.
4. **Compute Layer (nodeCPU, nodeG):** CPU handles logic; GPU handles 3D rendering of complex models.
5. **Database Layer (nodeDB):** The single source of truth, typically using **STEP** or **IGES** standard formats.
6. **Output Layer (nodeOut):** Produces CNC code (G-code), engineering drawings (plots), or simulation reports.

---

## 4.3 Data Flow from CAD Design to CNC Machine

This diagram traces *one specific data path* — a frequent KTU sub-part (a) question.

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1["3D CAD Model - Solid Geometry"]
    node2["CAM Software - Toolpath Generation"]
    node3["Cutter Location - CL Data File"]
    node4["Post-Processor - Machine Specific"]
    node5["NC Program - G Code M Code"]
    node6["DNC Network or USB Transfer"]
    node7["CNC Machine Controller"]
    node8["Physical Part on Machine"]

    node1 -- STL or STEP export --> node2
    node2 -- generic CL data --> node3
    node3 -- machine specific --> node4
    node4 -- formatted NC code --> node5
    node5 -- upload --> node6
    node6 -- stream to controller --> node7
    node7 -- servo commands --> node8
```

**Why this diagram matters for KTU marks:**
- It demonstrates the student's understanding of the **post-processor** step (a frequent 2-mark question).
- It clarifies the difference between **CL data** (machine-agnostic) and **NC code** (machine-specific).
- It shows why a CAD file *cannot* be sent directly to a CNC — it must pass through CAM and post-processing.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define CAD and CAM. State any two advantages of integrating them in a manufacturing environment.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **CAD (1 mark):** Computer-Aided Design is the use of computer systems to create, modify, and optimize a product design. It includes 2D drafting, 3D modeling, and engineering analysis (CAE).
- **CAM (1 mark):** Computer-Aided Manufacturing is the use of computer-controlled machinery and software to automate the production of parts, including NC programming, toolpath generation, and DNC.
- **Two Advantages (1 mark):** (i) Elimination of manual drafting and NC programming errors. (ii) Direct transfer of design data to manufacturing reduces lead time by 30–70%.

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**List the three types of 3D geometric modeling schemes. State one limitation of wireframe modeling.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The three schemes are: (i) **Wireframe modeling**, (ii) **Surface modeling**, (iii) **Solid modeling** (1 mark each).
- **Limitation of wireframe:** It contains only edges and vertices, with no surface or volume information. Therefore, it cannot be used to calculate mass, perform FEA, or generate reliable CAM toolpaths (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q]**
> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a CAD/CAM system. Label all major components. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the CIM wheel in detail. Discuss the role of CAPP and MRP nodes in the integration. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

1. **Block Diagram (3 marks):** Draw the layered architecture:
   - Input layer (mouse, scanner, digitizer)
   - System software layer (OS)
   - Application software layer (CAD, CAM, CAE)
   - Hardware layer (CPU, GPU, RAM)
   - Database layer (central DB using STEP/IGES)
   - Output layer (NC code, plots, simulation reports)

2. **Explanation of Each Layer (2 marks):** Each layer's function must be described in one or two sentences. Pay special attention to the database layer being the *integration backbone*.

3. **Example Walkthrough (2 marks):** Trace a simple part from sketch → 3D model → FEA → CAM toolpath → NC code to demonstrate how each layer interacts.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

1. **CIM Wheel Diagram (2 marks):** Draw the circular CIM wheel with all 11 nodes: CAD, CAM, CAE, CAPP, MRP, PBC, DNC, FMS, AGV, QA, ERP. The center is the **Common Database**.

2. **Role of CAPP (2.5 marks):** CAPP (Computer-Aided Process Planning) acts as the **bridge between design and manufacturing**. It receives the CAD geometry, references the standard machining operations in its database, and generates a process plan: list of operations, machine selection, tooling selection, and sequencing. **Variant CAPP** uses standard templates; **Generative CAPP** uses AI/logic to create new plans from scratch.

3. **Role of MRP (2.5 marks):** MRP (Material Requirements Planning) takes the **Bill of Materials (BOM)** generated by CAPP and calculates the *net material requirement* using:
   $$\text{NR} = \text{Gross Requirement} - \text{On-Hand Inventory} + \text{Scheduled Receipts}$$
   MRP ensures raw material is available *just-in-time* for the FMS cell, preventing both stockouts and excess inventory carrying costs.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks**

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Q]**
> **(a)** Differentiate between 3D wireframe, surface, and solid modeling. Give one engineering application of each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A company produces $N = 1000$ brackets. The design cost is ₹40,000, tooling cost is ₹30,000, material cost is ₹120 per unit, machining cycle cost is ₹55 per unit, and QA cost is ₹18 per unit at a first-pass yield of 0.90. Calculate the total cost per unit. Justify whether CIM investment of ₹80,000 is justified if the manual method costs ₹300 per unit. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

| Feature | Wireframe | Surface | Solid |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Stored** | Edges and vertices only | Outer skin (faces) | Full volume + mass properties |
| **Mass Calculation** | Not possible | Not possible | Automatic |
| **FEA Compatible** | No | No | Yes |
| **Visual Realism** | Low (see-through) | High (rendered) | High (rendered + sectioned) |
| **Application (1 mark)** | Quick layout of piping | Car body styling | Engine block design |

**Applications (1 mark each):**
- **Wireframe:** Quick 2D-like check of part dimensions and interference between simple components.
- **Surface:** Industrial design of consumer products (e.g., mobile phone housings, car panels).
- **Solid:** Mechanical engineering components like gears, shafts, engine blocks — anywhere FEA or CAM toolpath generation is required.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Per-unit amortized design and tooling cost (2 marks):**
$$C_{design} = \frac{40000}{1000} = ₹40$$
$$C_{tooling} = \frac{30000}{1000} = ₹30$$

**Step 2 — Machining + Material cost per unit (2 marks):**
$$C_{machining} = 120 + 55 = ₹175$$

**Step 3 — QA cost adjusted for yield (1 mark):**
$$C_{QA} = \frac{18}{0.90} = ₹20$$

**Step 4 — Total cost per unit (1 mark):**
$$C_{total} = 40 + 30 + 175 + 20 = \boxed{₹265 \text{ per unit}}$$

**Step 5 — CIM Justification (1 mark):**
Savings per unit vs. manual method: $300 - 265 = ₹35$.
Breakeven units: $\dfrac{80000}{35} \approx 2286$ units.
Since the current lot is 1000 units, CIM is **not yet fully justified** on this lot alone. However, with future orders, the breakeven is achievable within 2–3 more lots. **Recommendation:** Proceed with CIM for long-term competitiveness.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in CAD/CAM Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing CAD with CAM (–2 marks):** CAD is *design*; CAM is *manufacturing*. If a question asks "explain CAM" and you describe only 3D modeling, expect a steep deduction.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Central Database in the CIM Wheel (–2 marks):** The center of the wheel is NOT empty. It is the **Common Database / Central Computer** — the integration backbone.
>
> 3. **Calling 3D Wireframe "3D Solid" (–2 marks):** This is a KTU-favorite trap. A wireframe cannot compute mass; a solid can.
>
> 4. **Skipping the Post-Processor in the CAD-to-CNC flow (–1 mark):** Many students jump from CAM directly to "G-code" without mentioning the post-processor, which converts generic CL data to machine-specific code.
>
> 5. **Using only one type of modeling in the analytical matrix (–1 mark):** Always discuss *all three* (wireframe, surface, solid) when asked to compare. A partial answer is a partial grade.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Overview of CAD/CAM**

- **CAD** = design & drafting on computer. **CAM** = manufacturing automation. **CAE** = engineering analysis. **CIM** = total enterprise integration.
- The **CIM wheel** has 11 functional nodes rotating around a **Central Database**. The wheel is *circular* because feedback is continuous.
- **3D Modeling hierarchy:** Wireframe (no surface) → Surface (skin only) → Solid (full volume + mass). Only solids support FEA and mass calculation.
- **Standard Data Exchange Formats:** **STEP (ISO 10303)** is the modern standard; **IGES** is the legacy standard.
- **NC Code Path:** CAD model → CAM toolpath → CL data → **Post-Processor** → G-code/M-code → CNC controller.
- **CAPP** is the *bridge node* between CAD and CAM. **MRP** handles inventory. **FMS** is a flexible automated cell. **DNC** networks the machines.
- **Total Cost Equation:** $C_{total} = C_{design} + C_{tooling} + C_{machining} + C_{QA}$.
- **CIM Breakeven Formula:** $\text{Breakeven Units} = \dfrac{\text{Fixed CIM Investment}}{\text{Cost Savings per Unit vs. Manual Method}}$.
- **Key ISO Standards to Memorize:** ISO 10303 (STEP), ISO 6983 (G-code / NC programming).
- **The Five Levels of CIM:** Component → Machine → Cell → Shop Floor → Enterprise.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Importance in Modern Manufacturing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OERUT613 - COMPUTER INTEGRATED MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Importance in Modern Manufacturing # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Modern Manufacturing** is the application of **computer, communication, information, and automation technologies** integrated throughout the entire production lifecycle — from product design and process planning to production, quality control, and logistics — to produce economically competitive, high-quality goods with minimal human intervention and maximum flexibility. In the context of KTU's *OERUT613 – Computer Integrated Manufacturing*, the importance of modern manufacturing is framed around the shift from **isolated, island-based automation** to a **fully networked, data-driven enterprise**, where every functional department (Design → Planning → Production → Quality → Dispatch) operates on a unified digital backbone. > [!NOTE] > **CIM (Computer Integrated Manufacturing)** as defined by Dr. Joseph Harrington (1973): *"The manufacturing approach of using computers to control the entire production process of a product, with the goal of integrating all aspects of design, manufacturing, scheduling, and quality control."* --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Smart Tailor vs. The Modern Garment Factory" Imagine two ways of stitching a shirt: | Aspect | Traditional Tailor (Pre-Modern) | Modern Garment Factory (CIM) | |---|---|---| | **Design** | Sketch on paper, hand-cut cloth | CAD software generates digital pattern, optimizes fabric usage | | **Cutting** | Hand scissors, manual measurement | CNC laser cutter follows digital pattern with **0.1 mm accuracy** | | **Stitching** | One operator, one machine | Computer-controlled lines, multiple sizes in parallel | | **Quality** | Visual inspection by the tailor | Vision systems + SPC charts in real time | | **Delivery** | Days to weeks | Hours to days, tracked via ERP | | **Customer Feedback** | Rare and delayed | IoT sensors + cloud dashboard | > [!TIP] > The key insight: **Modern manufacturing is not just about robots** — it is the *seamless digital handshake* between design, production, and business systems. A factory full of robots that do not "talk" to each other is **not** CIM; it is just islands of automation. --- ## 1.3 Why "Importance" is a Mandatory Module 1 Topic The KTU 2024 Scheme places this topic at the very beginning to establish the **"Why"** before the "How." The course outcome (CO1) typically maps here: > [!NOTE] > **KTU Course Outcome Reference (CO1):** *"Understand the fundamental concepts, evolution, and importance of computer integrated manufacturing in modern industrial environments."* The term **"Importance"** here carries **three specific dimensions** mandated by the syllabus: 1. **Economic Importance** – Manufacturing as a percentage of **GDP**, **employment generator**, and **export engine**. 2. **Technological Importance** – Driver of **R&D**, **innovation ecosystems**, and **Industry 4.0** adoption. 3. **Strategic Importance** – Enabler of **mass customization**, **global competitiveness**, and **supply-chain resilience**. --- ## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics Used in This Module The following industry-standard Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) are repeatedly referenced in KTU question papers and must be memorized: - **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** — measured in **%**, with a world-class benchmark of **≥ 85%**. - **Takt Time** — measured in **seconds/unit**, derived from customer demand. - **First Pass Yield (FPY)** — measured in **%**, world-class **≥ 99%**. - **Inventory Turns** — measured in **times/year**, healthy range **6–12**. - **On-Time Delivery (OTD)** — measured in **%**, world-class **≥ 95%**. - **Manufacturing Value Added (MVA)** — measured as **% of GDP**, the global average is around **15–20%** for developing economies. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Alert:** A common mistake students make is treating *"Importance of Modern Manufacturing"* as a generic essay. Always **back every point with a numeric metric or a defined KPI**. Generic statements like *"it improves productivity"* carry **0 marks**; quantified statements like *"CIM can raise OEE from 55% to 85%, doubling effective capacity"* earn full marks. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Importance Modern manufacturing's importance is best understood through **five engineering-economic pillars** that KTU examiners consistently test: ### Pillar 1 — Economic Backbone of a Nation - Manufacturing typically contributes **15–20% of GDP** in industrialized nations (e.g., Germany's MVA is ~22%, India's ~14%, China's ~28%). - Generates **secondary and tertiary employment** through supply chains — every direct manufacturing job creates **2.5 indirect jobs** (multiplier effect recognized by the World Bank). - Drives **exports** — manufactured goods account for **~70% of global merchandise trade** (WTO data). ### Pillar 2 — Productivity Multiplier - CIM reduces direct labor cost per unit by **40–70%** compared to manual lines. - Machine utilization rises from typical **25–35%** (standalone) to **75–85%** (integrated). - Setup/changeover times drop by **50–90%** via SMED (Single-Minute Exchange of Die) integrated with scheduling software. ### Pillar 3 — Quality at the Source - Real-time SPC (Statistical Process Control) + automated inspection pushes defect rates below **50 ppm (parts per million)**. - Six-Sigma quality levels (**3.4 DPMO**) are achievable only with CIM-grade integration of metrology, MES (Manufacturing Execution Systems), and feedback loops. ### Pillar 4 — Flexibility and Mass Customization - Modern systems allow **batch size of one** (lot size = 1) economically. - Product variety has grown from a few hundred SKUs (1970s) to **millions of variants** (2020s) — impossible without CIM. - Lead times compressed from **weeks → days → hours** in sectors like electronics and automotive. ### Pillar 5 — Innovation and Technology Diffusion - Manufacturing R&D spend drives **cross-sector innovation** (materials, robotics, AI, IoT). - Modern factories are **living laboratories** for AI, digital twins, and additive manufacturing. - Generates **patent density** that fuels long-term GDP growth. --- ## 2.2 The Competitive Dimensions Model (Hayes-Wheelwright) The **Hayes-Wheelwright Product-Process Matrix** (1979) underpins KTU's understanding of why modern manufacturing matters. A company must align its **process capability** with its **product life cycle stage**. | Product Life Cycle Stage | Order Winners | Required Process Type | |---|---|---| | Introduction | **Flexibility, Innovation** | Job Shop, CIM-flexible cell | | Growth | **Quality, Reliability** | Discrete batch, FMS | | Maturity | **Cost, Consistency** | Dedicated line, CIM-continuous | | Decline | **Cost only** | Disassembly, remanufacturing | > [!TIP] > **Why this matters for KTU:** This model is the single most cited framework when examiners ask "Why does modern manufacturing need to be flexible?" — because **modern markets force products to live across all four stages simultaneously** (a phenomenon called *hyper-competition*). --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet The following table consolidates **every equation** the KTU paper-setter expects you to know for this topic. | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | Typical Value | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | Overall Equipment Effectiveness | dimensionless (0 to 1) | **≥ 85%** world-class | | 2 | $A = \dfrac{Run\ Time}{Planned\ Production\ Time}$ | Availability | dimensionless | ≥ 90% | | 3 | $P = \dfrac{Ideal\ Cycle\ Time \times Total\ Count}{Run\ Time}$ | Performance | dimensionless | ≥ 95% | | 4 | $Q = \dfrac{Good\ Count}{Total\ Count}$ | Quality rate | dimensionless | ≥ 99% | | 5 | $Takt = \dfrac{Available\ Production\ Time}{Customer\ Demand}$ | Takt Time | sec/unit | depends on demand | | 6 | $Productivity = \dfrac{Output\ Value}{Input\ Cost}$ | Manufacturing productivity | ratio or % | 1.5 – 4.0 healthy | | 7 | $Lead\ Time = Wait\ Time + Queue\ Time + Transport + Processing$ | Order lead time | hours / days | minimized | | 8 | $MVA = \dfrac{Manufacturing\ Output - Intermediate\ Inputs}{GDP}$ | Manufacturing Value Added | % of GDP | 15–20% typical | | 9 | $WIP\ Cost = WIP_{units} \times Unit\ Cost \times \dfrac{Lead\ Time}{2}$ | Work-in-process capital tied up | currency | minimized in CIM | | 10 | $ROI_{CIM} = \dfrac{Net\ Savings\ from\ CIM}{Total\ CIM\ Investment}$ | Return on CIM investment | % / year | payback in 2–5 yrs | > [!NOTE] > **Symbol Note on Pipes:** Where the original equations used the vertical bar (e.g., absolute values or set notation), LaTeX-rendered equivalents `\vert` and `\mid` have been preserved to keep markdown tables intact. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility These formulas are not just academic — they are the **decision-making language** of: - **Plant managers** justifying a ₹10 Cr CNC investment to a board. - **Quality engineers** tracking OEE on dashboards. - **Supply-chain planners** using Takt Time to balance lines. - **Government economists** computing MVA for national industrial policy. A KTU student who can quote **"moving OEE from 60% to 85% doubles effective capacity without new machines"** will score above a peer who just writes *"CIM increases efficiency."* # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU examiners frequently award **3 of the 7 marks** in a sub-question for a clean, step-by-step derivation. The following worked problems are the **highest-yield derivations** for Module 1. --- ## 3.1 Derivative Problem 1: OEE-Based Capacity Justification **Problem Statement:** A standalone CNC machine currently runs with Availability = 70%, Performance = 80%, Quality = 90%. After CIM integration, the manufacturer targets Availability = 92%, Performance = 95%, Quality = 99%. The machine has a theoretical cycle time of 12 seconds per part and operates 480 minutes/day. Calculate: - (i) Current effective parts per day. - (ii) Improved effective parts per day. - (iii) % capacity gain *without* buying a new machine. ### Step 1 — State the OEE Definition By the standard SEMI E10 definition: $$ \begin{aligned} OEE &= Availability \times Performance \times Quality \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Compute Current OEE $$ \begin{aligned} OEE_{current} &= 0.70 \times 0.80 \times 0.90 \\ &= 0.504 \\ &= 50.4\% \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Compute Improved OEE (post-CIM) $$ \begin{aligned} OEE_{CIM} &= 0.92 \times 0.95 \times 0.99 \\ &= 0.86534 \\ &= 86.53\% \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Compute Theoretical Maximum Parts per Day Available time = 480 minutes = 28,800 seconds. Cycle time = 12 s. $$ \begin{aligned} Parts_{theoretical} &= \frac{Available\ Time}{Cycle\ Time} \\ &= \frac{28{,}800}{12} \\ &= 2{,}400\ parts/day \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Compute Effective Parts (Current) $$ \begin{aligned} Parts_{current} &= Parts_{theoretical} \times OEE_{current} \\ &= 2{,}400 \times 0.504 \\ &= 1{,}209.6 \approx 1{,}210\ parts/day \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 6 — Compute Effective Parts (CIM) $$ \begin{aligned} Parts_{CIM} &= Parts_{theoretical} \times OEE_{CIM} \\ &= 2{,}400 \times 0.86534 \\ &= 2{,}076.8 \approx 2{,}077\ parts/day \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 7 — Compute Capacity Gain $$ \begin{aligned} \%\ Gain &= \frac{Parts_{CIM} - Parts_{current}}{Parts_{current}} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{2{,}077 - 1{,}210}{1{,}210} \times 100 \\ &= 71.6\% \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** The same machine produces **71.6% more good parts per day** purely through CIM-driven integration — illustrating exactly *why* modern manufacturing matters. --- ## 3.2 Derivative Problem 2: Takt Time and Line Balancing **Problem Statement:** A factory operates **2 shifts of 8 hours each, 5 days a week**. Customer demand is **18,000 units/week**. Compute the Takt Time, and check if the proposed cycle time of 90 seconds is feasible. ### Step 1 — Compute Available Production Time $$ \begin{aligned} T_{available} &= Shifts/day \times Hours/shift \times Days/week \\ &= 2 \times 8 \times 5 \\ &= 80\ hours/week \\ &= 4{,}800\ minutes/week \\ &= 288{,}000\ seconds/week \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Apply Takt Formula $$ \begin{aligned} Takt &= \frac{T_{available}}{Customer\ Demand} \\ &= \frac{288{,}000}{18{,}000} \\ &= 16\ seconds/unit \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Compare with Proposed Cycle Time $$ \begin{aligned} Cycle_{proposed} &= 90\ s \\ Takt_{required} &= 16\ s \\ \Delta &= 90 - 16 = 74\ s\ (deficit) \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Determine Number of Parallel Machines / Stations $$ \begin{aligned} N_{stations} &= \left\lceil \frac{Cycle_{proposed}}{Takt_{required}} \right\rceil \\ &= \left\lceil \frac{90}{16} \right\rceil \\ &= \lceil 5.625 \rceil = 6\ stations \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Step-by-Step Rule:** When asked *"Is the cycle time feasible?"*, the conclusion must explicitly state: *"A single station is infeasible; at least 6 parallel stations (or 1 station with 6× faster cycle) are required to meet demand at Takt = 16 s."* --- ## 3.3 Derivative Problem 3: CIM ROI Justification **Problem Statement:** A firm invests **₹2 Crore** in CIM. Annual savings = **₹60 Lakh/year** in labor + **₹25 Lakh/year** in scrap reduction + **₹15 Lakh/year** in inventory carrying. Annual operating cost of CIM (software + maintenance) = **₹20 Lakh/year**. Compute payback period and 5-year ROI (assuming no salvage value). ### Step 1 — Net Annual Savings $$ \begin{aligned} S_{net} &= (60 + 25 + 15) - 20 \\ &= 100 - 20 \\ &= 80\ Lakh/year \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Payback Period $$ \begin{aligned} Payback &= \frac{Investment}{Net\ Annual\ Savings} \\ &= \frac{2{,}00{,}00{,}000}{80{,}00{,}000} \\ &= 2.5\ years \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — 5-Year ROI $$ \begin{aligned} ROI_{5yr} &= \frac{(S_{net} \times 5) - Investment}{Investment} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{(80 \times 5) - 200}{200} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{400 - 200}{200} \times 100 \\ &= 100\% \end{aligned} $$ > [!TIP] > A 100% ROI in 5 years and a 2.5-year payback is **highly attractive** under any industry hurdle rate. KTU examiners expect this kind of numeric argument — never present CIM as "good"; present it as **"₹1 returned for every ₹1 invested within 30 months."** # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > All diagrams below use Mermaid syntax with **alphanumeric, prefixed node IDs** and **plain-text labels** (no markdown formatting inside labels) — fully compliant with the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards. --- ## 4.1 Evolution of Manufacturing Paradigms ```mermaid graph LR A1[STAGE 1: CRAFT
1700s to 1800s] --> B1[STAGE 2: MASS
1900s to 1950s] B1 --> C1[STAGE 3: LEAN
1960s to 1990s] C1 --> D1[STAGE 4: CIM
1990s to 2010s] D1 --> E1[STAGE 5: INDUSTRY 4.0
2010s to Present] E1 --> F1[STAGE 6: AUTONOMOUS FACTORY
2030 and Beyond] style A1 fill:#f5e6d3,stroke:#8b5a2b style B1 fill:#d3e5f5,stroke:#1f4e79 style C1 fill:#d3f5e0,stroke:#2e7d32 style D1 fill:#f5d3e0,stroke:#8e2e7d style E1 fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#a87b00 style F1 fill:#e0d3f5,stroke:#5b2e8e ``` **Reading the diagram:** Each paradigm solved the limitation of the previous one — Craft lacked scalability, Mass lacked flexibility, Lean lacked speed of change, CIM lacked intelligence, Industry 4.0 added connectivity, and the autonomous factory will add cognition. --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Factors Establishing the Importance of Modern Manufacturing ```mermaid graph TD P1[ECONOMIC PILLAR
GDP, Employment, Exports] --> HUB[CIM AS NATIONAL
COMPETITIVENESS ENGINE] P2[TECHNOLOGICAL PILLAR
R and D, AI, IoT, Robotics] --> HUB P3[OPERATIONAL PILLAR
OEE, Takt, Quality, Lead Time] --> HUB P4[STRATEGIC PILLAR
Mass Customization, Agility] --> HUB P5[SUSTAINABILITY PILLAR
Energy, Waste, Circular Economy] --> HUB HUB --> OUT1[Higher MVA percent of GDP] HUB --> OUT2[Lower unit cost] HUB --> OUT3[Faster time to market] HUB --> OUT4[Better product quality] HUB --> OUT5[Resilient supply chains] HUB --> OUT6[Global competitive advantage] style HUB fill:#ffe082,stroke:#f57c00,stroke-width:3px style P1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32 style P2 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0 style P3 fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#ad1457 style P4 fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0 style P5 fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#00695c ``` --- ## 4.3 Architecture — How CIM Translates Importance into Action ```mermaid graph TD L1[BUSINESS PLANE
ERP, CRM, SCM] --> M1[INTEGRATION BUS
Middleware and APIs] L2[DESIGN PLANE
CAD, CAE, CAPP] --> M1 L3[PRODUCTION PLANE
CNC, Robotics, FMS, AGVs] --> M1 L4[QUALITY PLANE
SPC, CMM, Vision Systems] --> M1 L5[CONTROL PLANE
SCADA, MES, Historian DB] --> M1 M1 --> N1[SHOP FLOOR OUTPUT
Higher OEE] M1 --> N2[ENTERPRISE OUTPUT
Higher ROI] M1 --> N3[STRATEGIC OUTPUT
Market Leadership] style M1 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f9a825,stroke-width:4px style N1 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#388e3c style N2 fill:#90caf9,stroke:#1565c0 style N3 fill:#ce93d8,stroke:#6a1b9a ``` --- ## 4.4 Comparative Matrix — Traditional vs. Modern Manufacturing ```mermaid graph LR subgraph TRAD[TRADITIONAL MANUFACTURING] T1[Islands of Automation] T2[Paper Based Planning] T3[Reactive Quality] T4[High WIP and Inventory] T5[Long Lead Times] T6[Low Flexibility] end subgraph MOD[MODERN CIM] M1[Fully Integrated Systems] M2[Digital Twin Planning] M3[Predictive Quality via AI] M4[Lean and Just in Time] M5[Agile Lead Times] M6[Mass Customization Ready] end T1 -.evolves into.-> M1 T2 -.evolves into.-> M2 T3 -.evolves into.-> M3 T4 -.evolves into.-> M4 T5 -.evolves into.-> M5 T6 -.evolves into.-> M6 style TRAD fill:#ffebee,stroke:#b71c1c style MOD fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20 ``` --- ## 4.5 The Importance Feedback Loop ```mermaid graph TD S1[CIM Investment] --> S2[Higher OEE and Quality] S2 --> S3[Lower Unit Cost] S3 --> S4[Competitive Pricing] S4 --> S5[Higher Market Share] S5 --> S6[Higher Revenue] S6 --> S7[Reinvest in CIM] S7 --> S1 S2 --> S8[Skilled Job Creation] S8 --> S9[National MVA Growth] S9 --> S10[Economic Strength] S10 --> S1 style S1 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100 style S5 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style S10 fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b ``` > [!TIP] > The **self-reinforcing loop** is the single most important conceptual diagram of this module. In a KTU 14-mark answer, drawing this loop and annotating each arrow with a **metric** (e.g., S2 → S3 = "defect rate drops from 3% to 0.5%") demonstrates examiner-level command of the topic. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!NOTE] > All questions below are modeled on **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** patterns. Mark splits follow the standard **3 marks (Part A) and 14 marks (Part B)** with **internal choice between two questions**, each with **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks** sub-structure. --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]` **Define Computer Integrated Manufacturing (CIM). List any four of its defining characteristics.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **CIM** is a manufacturing philosophy that uses **computer-based systems to integrate and control all functions of an enterprise** — from product design (CAD/CAE) and process planning (CAPP) to production (CNC, robotics), quality control (SPC, CMM), and enterprise management (ERP) — through a common digital backbone. **Four defining characteristics:** 1. **Integration** of all functional islands through a single information flow. 2. **Automation** of both physical (machines) and information (planning) processes. 3. **Flexibility** to handle product variety and volume changes. 4. **Optimization** of cost, quality, and time across the entire lifecycle. *(1 mark for clean definition + ½ mark × 4 = 2 marks for characteristics → Total 3 marks)* --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]` **State and briefly explain any three Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) used to measure the importance/effectiveness of modern manufacturing systems.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness):** Single number combining Availability, Performance, and Quality; world-class ≥ **85%**. 2. **Takt Time:** Rate at which a product must be produced to meet customer demand, computed as Available Time ÷ Customer Demand. 3. **First Pass Yield (FPY):** Percentage of products that pass quality inspection on the first attempt without rework; world-class ≥ **99%**. *(1 mark per KPI with formula + meaning = 3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the **economic importance** of manufacturing in a modern economy. Support your answer with relevant statistical indicators. **(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of CIM** and describe how the integration of business and shop-floor planes contributes to the strategic importance of modern manufacturing. --- #### Model Solution to A(a) — Economic Importance (7 marks) **Step 1 — Definition and Scope (1 mark):** Manufacturing is the **engine of value addition** in any economy. It transforms raw materials into finished goods with a measurable **value-add** (MVA). It differs from agriculture and services by being **tangible, scalable, and tradeable**. **Step 2 — Contribution to GDP (2 marks):** - Manufacturing Value Added (MVA) as a percentage of GDP: - Germany: ~22% - China: ~28% - South Korea: ~27% - India: ~14% (as of recent economic surveys) - Empirically, countries with MVA > 20% of GDP are categorized as **"industrialized"**; below 15%, the economy is "pre-industrial" or "service-dominated." **Step 3 — Employment Multiplier (2 marks):** - Each direct manufacturing job supports **2 to 3 indirect jobs** in the supply chain (logistics, components, services). - Manufacturing provides **formal, skilled employment** that lifts worker productivity and wages — unlike informal services. **Step 4 — Exports and Trade Balance (1 mark):** - Manufactured goods constitute **~70% of global merchandise exports** (WTO). - High manufacturing share = stronger current account, lower forex vulnerability. **Step 5 — Innovation Spillovers (1 mark):** - Manufacturing R&D accounts for the majority of industrial patents globally. - Drives spillovers into services (e.g., IT services for manufacturing, fintech for industrial payments). --- #### Model Solution to A(b) — CIM Architecture (7 marks) **Step 1 — Draw the Five-Layer Block Diagram (3 marks):** The CIM architecture comprises five functional planes connected via a central **integration bus (middleware / OPC-UA / industrial Ethernet):** 1. **Business Plane** — ERP, CRM, SCM systems (top-level decisions). 2. **Design Plane** — CAD, CAE, CAPP (product and process definition). 3. **Production Plane** — CNC, Robotics, FMS, AGVs, 3D printers. 4. **Quality Plane** — SPC, CMM, machine vision. 5. **Control Plane** — SCADA, MES, Historian DB. **Step 2 — Explain the Integration Bus (2 marks):** The integration bus uses standardized protocols (OPC-UA, MTConnect, MQTT) to ensure **bi-directional data flow** — e.g., a sales order in ERP automatically generates a CNC program via CAPP, schedules a job on MES, and triggers a quality inspection plan. **Step 3 — Connect to Strategic Importance (2 marks):** - **Mass Customization:** Customer orders flow seamlessly from web → ERP → MES → CNC. - **Global Competitiveness:** Real-time dashboards enable rapid response to demand changes. - **Resilience:** Sensor-driven predictive maintenance prevents unplanned downtime. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Hayes-Wheelwright Product-Process Matrix**. How does it justify the need for modern, flexible manufacturing? **(b) [7 Marks]** A manufacturing unit is currently producing **1,200 units/day** with a **defect rate of 4%** and an **OEE of 55%**. The management plans to introduce CIM. After CIM, the OEE is expected to be **82%** and the defect rate to drop to **0.8%**. Calculate: - (i) The current and projected good output per day. - (ii) The percentage improvement in effective capacity. - (iii) The annual savings if defective parts cost **₹150 each** to rework, working **300 days/year**. --- #### Model Solution to B(a) — Hayes-Wheelwright Matrix (7 marks) **Step 1 — State the Framework (2 marks):** The Hayes-Wheelwright Matrix (1979) plots **Product Life Cycle Stage** (Intro, Growth, Maturity, Decline) on one axis and **Process Type** (Job Shop, Batch, Assembly Line, Continuous Flow) on the other. The diagonal represents **alignment** — the ideal state where process capability matches product requirements. **Step 2 — Describe the Diagonal (2 marks):** - **Intro + Job Shop:** Maximum flexibility for prototypes and variants. - **Growth + Batch Line:** Volume rising, repeat orders begin. - **Maturity + Assembly Line:** High volume, low variety, dedicated lines. - **Decline + Continuous Flow / Disassembly:** Cost optimization, remanufacturing. **Step 3 — Justify CIM (3 marks):** Modern markets demand that a single factory handle products across **all life cycle stages simultaneously** (a phenomenon known as *hyper-competition*). Only CIM — with its programmable, software-defined machines, FMS, and integrated planning — can: - Rapidly reconfigure from job-shop to flow-line mode. - Switch SKUs without major hardware changeover (SMED). - Support mass customization where each unit could be a unique variant. Thus, CIM moves the firm from a single diagonal point to **operating across the entire matrix** — the strategic justification for modern manufacturing importance. --- #### Model Solution to B(b) — Numerical (7 marks) **Step 1 — Current Good Output (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} Good_{current} &= 1{,}200 \times (1 - 0.04) \\ &= 1{,}200 \times 0.96 \\ &= 1{,}152\ units/day \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Projected Good Output Using OEE (2 marks):** Theoretical maximum remains 1,200/day (theoretical capacity, not affected by CIM in this question; the OEE reflects effective rate). Effective output with improved OEE and defect rate: $$ \begin{aligned} Effective\ Capacity_{CIM} &= 1{,}200 \times OEE_{CIM} \\ &= 1{,}200 \times 0.82 \\ &= 984\ "good-equivalent" units \end{aligned} $$ But the **good units** (post-CIM) considering defect rate: $$ \begin{aligned} Good_{CIM} &= 984 \times (1 - 0.008) \\ &= 984 \times 0.992 \\ &= 976.1 \approx 976\ units/day \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation of "OEE with defect":** The Quality factor of OEE *already* folds in good-count ratio; thus applying the explicit defect rate again is **double-counting**. The cleaner approach is: $$ \begin{aligned} Good_{CIM} &= 1{,}200 \times 0.82 \times (1 - 0.008) \\ &= 984 \times 0.992 \\ &= 976\ units/day \end{aligned} $$ > **[Examiner's Note: The two-step factor is academically rigorous; the one-step OEE-only shortcut is acceptable if the question assumes "defect rate" is already inside OEE.]** **Step 3 — Capacity Improvement (1 mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} \%\ Improvement &= \frac{Good_{CIM} - Good_{current}}{Good_{current}} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{976 - 1{,}152}{1{,}152} \times 100 \\ &= -15.3\% \end{aligned} $$ > **[Important Note]:** The improvement in OEE outweighs the improvement in *good output* numerically *only* if the OEE's quality component is removed first. The OEE-based effective capacity gain is: $$ \begin{aligned} \%\ OEE\ Gain &= \frac{0.82 - 0.55}{0.55} \times 100 = 49.1\% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Annual Savings from Defect Reduction (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} Defects_{current/day} &= 1{,}200 \times 0.04 = 48\ units \\ Defects_{CIM/day} &= 1{,}200 \times 0.82 \times 0.008 = 7.87\ units \\ \Delta Defects/day &= 48 - 7.87 = 40.13\ units \\ Annual\ Savings &= 40.13 \times 150 \times 300 \\ &= ₹18{,}05{,}850\ (\approx\ ₹18.06\ Lakh/year) \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Most common loss of marks:** Writing the OEE gain and the good-output gain as the *same* number. They differ by the Quality factor — be explicit. > 2. **Second most common mistake:** Forgetting to multiply defects by working days (300) for annual savings. Always read the time-frame keywords ("per day" vs "per year") carefully. > 3. **Units trap:** Mixing up ₹/unit and ₹/year in the savings step. Always write units next to the number. > 4. **Bonus tip:** Drawing a small OEE contribution bar chart (A × P × Q) on the answer sheet earns "presentation marks" — a real KTU examiner habit. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — print this and revise 1 hour before the exam.** - ✅ **Definition of CIM (Harrington, 1973):** integration of all manufacturing functions through computers. - ✅ **Five Pillars of Importance:** Economic, Technological, Operational, Strategic, Sustainability. - ✅ **Three top KPIs to memorize:** OEE (≥ 85%), Takt Time, FPY (≥ 99%). - ✅ **OEE Formula:** $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ — write it as a product, not a sum. - ✅ **Takt Formula:** $Takt = \dfrac{Available\ Time}{Customer\ Demand}$ — must always be in seconds/unit in derivations. - ✅ **MVA Benchmark:** Manufacturing Value Added = 15–20% of GDP for healthy industrialized economies. - ✅ **Five-Plane CIM Architecture:** Business, Design, Production, Quality, Control — all connected by an integration bus. - ✅ **Hayes-Wheelwright Matrix:** Diagonal = alignment; CIM allows operating *across* the matrix. - ✅ **CIM ROI range:** Typical payback 2–5 years; 5-year ROI of 80–150% considered attractive. - ✅ **Three key shifts Modern Manufacturing enables:** 1. Mass → Mass Customization. 2. Reactive → Predictive Quality. 3. Island Automation → Fully Integrated Enterprise. - ✅ **Three exam-winning numerical demos:** 1. OEE capacity gain (without new machines). 2. Takt Time line balancing. 3. CIM ROI / payback calculation. - ✅ **Mnemonic for pillars:** **"E-T-O-S-S"** = Economic, Technological, Operational, Strategic, Sustainability. - ✅ **Number-one examiner warning:** Always **quantify** importance — never write a qualitative statement without a metric. > **End of Module 1 Notes — Importance in Modern Manufacturing**

topics.title: Manufacturing Planning and Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT613 - COMPUTER INTEGRATED MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Manufacturing Planning and Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Manufacturing Planning and Control (MPC)** is defined as a comprehensive, computer-integrated framework of policies, procedures, and decision-support systems used by manufacturing enterprises to plan, monitor, and control all factory-floor activities so that the right materials, equipment, labour, and information are available at the right time, in the right quantity, and at the right cost.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (CIM Module 1):**
> *"Manufacturing Planning and Control (MPC) is the set of all functions that coordinate the supply of materials and the operation of machines and labour so that finished goods are produced on time and at minimum cost, linking customer demand to shop-floor execution."*

The three classical functional pillars that constitute any MPC system are:

1. **Production Planning (PP)** — *What* is to be made, *when*, and *in what quantity*.
2. **Production Control (PC)** — *How* the plan is monitored, executed, and corrected.
3. **Shop Floor Execution (SFE)** — *Where* the actual transformation of raw materials into finished goods occurs.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A Restaurant during the Dinner Rush**
>
> Imagine a busy restaurant. The **waiter** receives customer orders (the *demand*). The **chef manager** decides what to cook today based on the bookings and stock of ingredients — this is the **Master Production Schedule (MPS)**. The **storekeeper** checks the pantry, finds that tomatoes are low, and tells the **purchasing agent** to buy more — this is the **Material Requirements Planning (MRP)**. The **station chef** allocates the grill, the fryer, and the pasta section so dishes are not delayed — this is **Capacity Requirements Planning (CRP)**. Finally, the **line cooks** execute the orders minute-by-minute — this is the **Shop Floor Control (SFC)**. The whole coordinated flow is **Manufacturing Planning and Control**.

Just as a restaurant cannot serve 100 customers with the wrong menu at the wrong time, a factory cannot fulfil customer orders efficiently without an MPC system that synchronises **demand, supply, capacity, and execution**.

## 1.3 Why MPC is Central to CIM

In **Computer Integrated Manufacturing (CIM)**, MPC is the *nervous system* that links the upstream **CAD/CAM/CAE** design functions to the downstream **Shop Floor Automation** through shared databases (often an **ERP / MES** backbone).

| Industry Need | MPC Function that Addresses It |
|---|---|
| Forecast next quarter's demand | Aggregate Production Planning |
| Decide what to build this week | Master Production Schedule (MPS) |
| Decide what to buy / make | Material Requirements Planning (MRP) |
| Decide if machines can cope | Capacity Requirements Planning (CRP) |
| Track actual WIP on the floor | Shop Floor Control (SFC) |
| Measure supplier performance | Purchasing & Vendor Management |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Hint:** Whenever a question mentions *"co-ordination of manufacturing resources"* or *"linking customer demand to shop-floor"* — the expected one-line answer is **Manufacturing Planning and Control (MPC)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchical Structure of MPC

MPC is not a single function; it is a **pyramid of decisions** taken at different time horizons and at different levels of detail. The classical Bedworth–Sahni hierarchical framework (also adopted by KTU) divides MPC into **three horizons**:

### A. Strategic (Long-term, 1–5 years)
- **Aggregate Production Planning (APP)** — Decides *output rate*, *inventory levels*, *manpower*, and *subcontracting* over months/quarters to balance demand and capacity.
- Decisions: Make-or-buy, capacity expansion, product mix.

### B. Tactical / Planning (Medium-term, weeks–months)
- **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** — Translates APP into a *time-phased plan* stating *what finished products are to be made, in what quantities, and by when*.
- **Material Requirements Planning (MRP)** — Explodes the MPS into a detailed schedule of raw materials, components, and sub-assemblies using the **Bill of Materials (BOM)**.
- **Capacity Requirements Planning (CRP)** — Confirms whether the factory has the machine-hours and labour-hours required by the MRP.

### C. Operational / Execution (Short-term, hours–days)
- **Shop Floor Control (SFC) / Production Activity Control (PAC)** — Issues detailed work orders, sequences jobs on machines, monitors progress, and triggers corrective action.
- **Inventory Control** — Manages buffers using **reorder point**, **safety stock**, and **EOQ** logic.
- **Purchasing** — Converts MRP planned orders into purchase orders.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Flow Direction (Top-Down, Bottom-Up Feedback):**
>
> Customer Demand → APP → MPS → MRP → CRP → SFC → Shop Floor
> Shop Floor → (Feedback: progress, exceptions) → MRP → MPS → APP

## 2.2 Master Production Schedule (MPS)

The MPS is the **backbone** of MPC. It answers: *"What specific end-items will be produced, in what quantities, and during which week?"*

Key inputs:
- Finished-goods **demand forecast** (or actual orders).
- **Available-to-Promise (ATP)** inventory logic.
- **Production lead time**.
- Current **on-hand inventory** and **scheduled receipts**.

Key outputs:
- A time-phased plan, typically week-wise.
- **Planned order releases** that feed MRP.

## 2.3 Material Requirements Planning (MRP)

MRP converts the MPS into a time-phased schedule of dependent-demand items using three primary inputs:

| Input | Description |
|---|---|
| **MPS** | What end items and when |
| **Bill of Materials (BOM)** | The product structure tree (parent–child relationships) |
| **Inventory Status File** | On-hand quantities, scheduled receipts, lead times |

The MRP **logic** is a three-step computation:

1. **Netting** — Gross requirement − On-hand inventory − Scheduled receipts = **Net Requirement**.
2. **Lot-sizing** — Combine net requirements into discrete orders (EOQ, Lot-for-Lot, etc.).
3. **Offsetting** — Subtract lead time to determine **Planned Order Release**.

> [!NOTE]
> **MRP II (Manufacturing Resource Planning)** extends MRP by closing the loop with **Capacity Planning, Shop Floor Feedback, and Finance** — making it a *closed-loop* system.

## 2.4 Capacity Requirements Planning (CRP)

CRP uses **routing files** (operation sequences and standard times) to convert the MRP into **machine-hour and labour-hour loads** per work centre, per time period. If load > capacity → **capacity adjustment** (overtime, subcontracting, or rescheduling).

## 2.5 Shop Floor Control (SFC)

SFC is the **execution arm**. It performs:
- **Order release** to the floor.
- **Sequencing and dispatching** of jobs.
- **Progress tracking** against the plan.
- **Exception reporting** (machine breakdown, scrap, shortages).
- **Feedback** to MRP for re-planning.

## 2.6 Just-in-Time (JIT) — The Lean Alternative

JIT is a *pull-based* philosophy (originated at Toyota) that complements or replaces traditional push-based MRP:

| Feature | Traditional MRP (Push) | JIT (Pull) |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Forecast / schedule | Actual downstream demand (Kanban) |
| Inventory | Buffer stock (WIP, safety) | Minimal / zero |
| Lot size | Economic (EOQ) | Small / one-piece flow |
| Lead time | Long, planned | Short, stable |
| Goal | Utilise capacity | Reduce waste (muda) |

The **Kanban** card is the visible signal that authorises production or withdrawal of a fixed quantity at the next station.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master these equations — they appear repeatedly in KTU numericals on Module 1.**

| Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) | units/order |
| $T^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2D}{HS}}$ | Optimal time between orders | time units |
| $ROP = D_{avg} \cdot L + SS$ | Reorder Point | units |
| $SS = z \cdot \sigma_{L}$ | Safety Stock (normal distribution) | units |
| $N = G - (OH + SR)$ | Net Requirement (MRP netting) | units |
| $POR = PO - L$ | Planned Order Release = Planned Order − Lead Time | time |
| $\eta = \dfrac{\sum t_{i}}{\sum T_{i}} \cdot 100\%$ | Machine / Labour utilisation | percent |
| $T_{cycle} = \sum\limits_{i=1}^{n} t_{op,i}$ | Cycle time (per unit, through serial stages) | min / unit |
| $WIP_{Little} = T_{th} \cdot TH$ | Little's Law: WIP = Throughput × Cycle Time | units |
| $C_{total} = D \cdot P + \dfrac{Q}{2} \cdot H + \dfrac{D}{Q} \cdot S$ | Total annual inventory cost | currency |

Where:
- $D$ = annual demand, $S$ = ordering cost per order, $H$ = holding cost per unit per year.
- $L$ = lead time, $\sigma_{L}$ = std. deviation of demand during lead time.
- $G$ = gross requirement, $OH$ = on-hand, $SR$ = scheduled receipts.
- $t_{op,i}$ = standard time of operation $i$, $T_{i}$ = available time at centre $i$.
- $TH$ = throughput rate (units/time), $T_{th} = T_{cycle}$ = average cycle time.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Convention:** Always carry **units** in the final numerical answer. Examiners deduct marks for unit-less answers in numerical problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) Model

The **EOQ model** (also called the *Wilson–Harris formula*) is the most frequently asked derivation in KTU Module 1 papers. It minimises the **Total Cost** of ordering + holding inventory over a planning horizon.

### 3.1.1 Assumptions of the EOQ Model

1. Demand rate $D$ is **constant and deterministic**.
2. Replenishment is **instantaneous** (infinite production rate).
3. Ordering cost $S$ is **fixed per order**.
4. Holding cost $H$ is **linear** in average inventory.
5. No **stockouts** are permitted.
6. Order quantity $Q$ is **constant** every cycle.

### 3.1.2 Cost Function

The annual total cost consists of three components:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{total} &= C_{ordering} + C_{holding} + C_{purchasing} \\
          &= \frac{D}{Q} \cdot S \;+\; \frac{Q}{2} \cdot H \;+\; D \cdot P
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical reasoning for each term:**

| Term | Origin of the Term |
|---|---|
| $\dfrac{D}{Q}$ | Number of orders placed per year (Demand ÷ Order size). |
| $\dfrac{Q}{2}$ | Average inventory. Since stock depletes linearly from $Q$ to $0$, the average level is $(Q+0)/2 = Q/2$. |
| $D \cdot P$ | Purchase cost: independent of $Q$ (a constant under fixed unit price $P$). |

Because $D \cdot P$ does not depend on $Q$, it can be dropped while minimising. The **variable cost** to be minimised is:

$$
C_{v}(Q) = \frac{D \cdot S}{Q} + \frac{H \cdot Q}{2}
$$

### 3.1.3 Minimisation

Differentiate $C_{v}(Q)$ with respect to $Q$ and set the derivative to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d C_{v}}{d Q} &= -\frac{D \cdot S}{Q^{2}} + \frac{H}{2} = 0 \\
\Rightarrow \quad \frac{H}{2} &= \frac{D \cdot S}{Q^{2}} \\
\Rightarrow \quad Q^{2} &= \frac{2 D \cdot S}{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking the positive square root (since order quantity is physically positive):

$$
\boxed{\,Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 D \cdot S}{H}}\,}
$$

### 3.1.4 Second-Order Verification (Minimum, Not Maximum)

$$
\frac{d^{2} C_{v}}{d Q^{2}} = \frac{2 D \cdot S}{Q^{3}} > 0 \quad \text{for } Q>0
$$

The second derivative is positive, confirming a **minimum**.

### 3.1.5 Optimal Cycle Time and Minimum Cost

$$
T^{*} = \frac{Q^{*}}{D} = \sqrt{\frac{2 S}{D \cdot H}}
$$

$$
C_{v}^{min} = \frac{D \cdot S}{Q^{*}} + \frac{H \cdot Q^{*}}{2} = \sqrt{2 D \cdot S \cdot H}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — MRP Netting (KTU-Style 14-Mark Style Numerical)

### Problem Statement

A company manufactures **Product X**, whose MPS calls for the following gross requirements over the next 6 weeks:

| Week | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Requirement (units) | 30 | 20 | 25 | 40 | 30 | 20 |

The item has:
- **On-hand inventory** at start of week 1 = **35 units**.
- **Scheduled receipts** in week 2 = **20 units**.
- **Lead time** = 2 weeks.
- **Lot sizing rule** = *Lot-for-Lot* (i.e., planned order quantity = net requirement of that week).

### Step 1 — Tabulate the Inputs

| Week | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Requirement $G$ | 30 | 20 | 25 | 40 | 30 | 20 |
| Scheduled Receipts $SR$ | 0 | 20 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

### Step 2 — Compute Projected On-Hand and Net Requirements

Beginning on-hand $OH_{0}=35$.

**Week 1:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
OH_{1} &= OH_{0} + SR_{1} - G_{1} = 35 + 0 - 30 = 5 \\
\text{Net}_1 &= \max(0,\, G_{1} - OH_{0} - SR_{1}) = \max(0,\, 30 - 35) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Week 2:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
OH_{2} &= OH_{1} + SR_{2} - G_{2} = 5 + 20 - 20 = 5 \\
\text{Net}_2 &= \max(0,\, 20 - 5 - 20) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Week 3:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
OH_{3} &= OH_{2} + 0 - 25 = 5 - 25 = -20 \;\;(\text{shortage}) \\
\text{Net}_3 &= 25 - 5 = 20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Week 4:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
OH_{4} &= 0 + 0 - 40 = -40 \\
\text{Net}_4 &= 40
\end{aligned}
$$

**Week 5:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
OH_{5} &= 0 + 0 - 30 = -30 \\
\text{Net}_5 &= 30
\end{aligned}
$$

**Week 6:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
OH_{6} &= 0 + 0 - 20 = -20 \\
\text{Net}_6 &= 20
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Planned Order Release (Offset by 2 Weeks)

Under *Lot-for-Lot*, Planned Order Quantity = Net Requirement. Release date = Order date − Lead time.

| Week | Net Req. | Planned Order Receipt | Planned Order Release |
|---|---|---|---|
| 3 | 20 | Week 3 | Week **1** |
| 4 | 40 | Week 4 | Week **2** |
| 5 | 30 | Week 5 | Week **3** |
| 6 | 20 | Week 6 | Week **4** |

### Step 4 — Final MRP Table

| Week | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Requirement | 30 | 20 | 25 | 40 | 30 | 20 |
| Scheduled Receipts | 0 | 20 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Projected on Hand | 5 | 5 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Net Requirement | 0 | 0 | 20 | 40 | 30 | 20 |
| Planned Order Receipt | 0 | 0 | 20 | 40 | 30 | 20 |
| Planned Order Release | 20 | 40 | 30 | 20 | 0 | 0 |

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — EOQ Numerical (KTU Pattern)

### Problem
Annual demand $D = 10\,000$ units, ordering cost $S = \text{Rs. } 200$ per order, holding cost $H = \text{Rs. } 10$ per unit per year.

### Step 1 — Apply EOQ Formula

$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 10000 \cdot 200}{10}} = \sqrt{400000} = 632.46 \;\text{units}
$$

Rounded off (KTU expects nearest integer): **$Q^{*} \approx 632$ units**.

### Step 2 — Optimal Number of Orders per Year

$$
N = \frac{D}{Q^{*}} = \frac{10000}{632.46} = 15.81 \approx 16 \;\text{orders/year}
$$

### Step 3 — Optimal Cycle Time

$$
T^{*} = \frac{Q^{*}}{D} = \frac{632.46}{10000} = 0.0632 \;\text{years} \approx 23 \;\text{days}
$$

### Step 4 — Minimum Variable Cost

$$
C_{v}^{min} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 10000 \cdot 200 \cdot 10} = \sqrt{40\,000\,000} = \text{Rs. } 6324.56
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which MPC Tool

| Manufacturing Characteristic | Recommended MPC Approach | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| High volume, low variety (e.g., oil refinery) | **Continuous flow / JIT** | Stable demand; minimising WIP saves cash. |
| High volume, high variety (e.g., car assembly) | **MRP II with line balancing** | Complex BOM needs explosion logic. |
| Low volume, high variety (e.g., job shop) | **MRP + detailed CRP + SFC** | Capacity is the bottleneck, not materials. |
| Make-to-stock with stable demand | **EOQ + ROP + Safety stock** | Independent demand. |
| Make-to-order with engineer-to-order | **Finite-capacity scheduling + CRP** | Each order is unique. |
| Mixed environment (most Indian SMEs) | **Hybrid MRP-JIT / Lean** | Combine MRP logic with Kanban visibility. |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Block Diagram of MPC (Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Strategic Planning 1 to 5 years Aggregate Production Plan Capacity Strategy Make or Buy]
    B[Tactical Planning MPS Master Production Schedule]
    C[Material Requirements Planning MRP Uses BOM and Inventory Status]
    D[Capacity Requirements Planning CRP Machine Hours Labour Hours]
    E[Shop Floor Control SFC Order Release Dispatch Sequencing]
    F[Shop Floor Execution CNC Robots Assembly Stations]
    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G[Feedback Progress Exceptions Scrap Downtime]
    G --> E
    G --> C
    G --> B
```

## 4.2 MRP Logic Loop — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[MPS Input What and When]
    IN2[Bill of Materials BOM Product Structure]
    IN3[Inventory Status File On Hand and On Order]
    PROC[MRP Processor Netting Lot Sizing Offsetting]
    OUT[Planned Order Releases Purchase Orders Work Orders]
    EXEC[Shop Floor Execution]
    INV[Inventory Update On Hand New Receipts]
    IN1 --> PROC
    IN2 --> PROC
    IN3 --> PROC
    PROC --> OUT
    OUT --> EXEC
    EXEC --> INV
    INV --> IN3
```

## 4.3 Push vs Pull (MRP vs JIT) Comparative Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PUSH[Push System MRP Forecast Driven]
        F1[Forecast] --> M1[MPS]
        M1 --> W1[Work Orders Push to Floor]
        W1 --> FG1[Finished Goods]
    end
    subgraph PULL[Pull System JIT Demand Driven]
        D1[Customer Order] --> K1[Final Assembly Kanban]
        K1 --> K2[Sub Assembly Kanban]
        K2 --> K3[Component Kanban]
        K3 --> SUP[Supplier Kanban]
    end
```

## 4.4 Closed-Loop MPC (MRP II) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DEM[Customer Demand Orders and Forecast] --> APP[Aggregate Production Planning]
    APP --> MPS2[MPS]
    MPS2 --> MRP2[MRP Explosion]
    MRP2 --> CRP2[Capacity Requirements Planning]
    CRP2 --> SFC2[Shop Floor Control]
    SFC2 --> SF[Shop Floor]
    SF --> FB[Feedback Actual Progress]
    FB --> CRP2
    FB --> MRP2
    FB --> APP
    APP --> FB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read these diagrams in the exam:** Always describe the diagram *in words* before pasting it. Example — *"MPC consists of three hierarchical levels… the strategic level feeds the tactical level, which feeds the operational level, and shop-floor feedback closes the loop."* This verbal scaffolding earns 1–2 marks even if the diagram is incomplete.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Manufacturing Planning and Control (MPC). List its three main functional components.*

**Model Answer:**

Manufacturing Planning and Control (MPC) is the integrated set of policies, procedures, and information systems used to plan, organise, and control all manufacturing activities so that the right products are produced in the right quantities, at the right time, and at minimum cost.

The three main functional components are:

1. **Production Planning** — Determination of *what*, *how much*, and *when* to produce.
2. **Production Control** — Monitoring and adjusting the plan as execution proceeds.
3. **Shop Floor Execution** — The actual physical transformation on the factory floor.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** A clean *definition + 3 components* gets full 3 marks. Do not write paragraphs — bullet points score better.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Distinguish between MRP and JIT.*

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | MRP (Push) | JIT (Pull) |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Forecast / schedule | Actual downstream demand |
| Inventory | Carries buffer stock | Minimal / zero inventory |
| Lot size | Economic (EOQ) | Small / one-piece flow |
| Lead time | Long, planned | Short, stabilised |
| Goal | Maximise utilisation | Eliminate waste |
| Tool | Bill of Materials | Kanban |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule:** Each Part B question has an **internal choice** — answer either Question A OR Question B. Each sub-question carries 7 marks. Model answers below.

---

### Q3A. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the hierarchical structure of Manufacturing Planning and Control. Discuss the role of Aggregate Production Planning (APP), Master Production Schedule (MPS), Material Requirements Planning (MRP), Capacity Requirements Planning (CRP), and Shop Floor Control (SFC). **(7 marks)**
