# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) Formula

The EOQ model minimizes the total cost of inventory, which is the sum of **ordering cost** and **holding cost**.

### 3.1.1 Setup of the Cost Model

**Given:**
- $D$ = annual demand (units/year).
- $Q$ = order quantity (units/order).
- $S$ = ordering (setup) cost per order (₹/order).
- $H$ = holding cost per unit per year (₹/unit/year).

**Number of orders placed per year:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N = \dfrac{D}{Q}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total ordering cost per year:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{order}} = N \times S = \dfrac{D}{Q} \times S = \dfrac{DS}{Q}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Average inventory** (assuming instantaneous replenishment and consumption at a constant rate):

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{Q + 0}{2} = \dfrac{Q}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total holding cost per year:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{hold}} = I_{\text{avg}} \times H = \dfrac{Q}{2} \times H = \dfrac{QH}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total Annual Inventory Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC(Q) = C_{\text{order}} + C_{\text{hold}} = \dfrac{DS}{Q} + \dfrac{QH}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.2 Minimization of TC(Q)

To find the optimal $Q^*$ that minimizes $TC$, take the first derivative with respect to $Q$ and set it to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{d\,TC(Q)}{dQ} &= \dfrac{d}{dQ}\left(\dfrac{DS}{Q} + \dfrac{QH}{2}\right) = 0 \\
-\dfrac{DS}{Q^{2}} + \dfrac{H}{2} &= 0 \\
\dfrac{H}{2} &= \dfrac{DS}{Q^{2}} \\
Q^{2} &= \dfrac{2DS}{H} \\
Q^{*} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Second-Derivative Verification (Convexity Check)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{d^{2}\,TC(Q)}{dQ^{2}} = \dfrac{2DS}{Q^{3}} > 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the second derivative is positive for all $Q > 0$, the function $TC(Q)$ is **convex**, confirming that $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ is indeed a **global minimum**.

### 3.1.4 Minimum Total Cost at EOQ

Substituting $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ back into $TC(Q)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_{\min} &= \dfrac{DS}{\sqrt{2DS/H}} + \dfrac{\sqrt{2DS/H} \cdot H}{2} \\
&= \sqrt{\dfrac{D^{2}S^{2}H}{2DS}} + \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS \cdot H^{2}}{4H}} \\
&= \sqrt{\dfrac{DSH}{2}} + \sqrt{\dfrac{DSH}{2}} \\
TC_{\min} &= 2\sqrt{\dfrac{DSH}{2}} = \sqrt{2DSH}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Important result:** At the optimum, the ordering cost equals the holding cost:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{order}} = C_{\text{hold}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{DSH}{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **This is the "1-to-1" rule** — at EOQ, the two cost components balance perfectly. If ordering cost > holding cost → order more frequently (increase Q). If holding cost > ordering cost → order less frequently (decrease Q). This intuitive property is **commonly tested in KTU examinations**.

## 3.2 Numerical Example — EOQ Determination

**Problem:** A company uses **12,000 units/year** of a component. Ordering cost is **₹300/order** and holding cost is **₹2/unit/year**. Find the EOQ, number of orders, and minimum total cost.

### Step 1: Identify the parameters
$$
D = 12{,}000 \text{ units/year}, \quad S = ₹300/\text{order}, \quad H = ₹2/\text{unit-year}
$$

### Step 2: Compute EOQ
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q^{*} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 12{,}000 \times 300}{2}} \\
&= \sqrt{\dfrac{7{,}200{,}000}{2}} = \sqrt{3{,}600{,}000} \\
&= 1{,}897.37 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Number of orders per year
$$
\begin{aligned}
N = \dfrac{D}{Q^{*}} = \dfrac{12{,}000}{1{,}897.37} \approx 6.32 \text{ orders/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Minimum total inventory cost
$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_{\min} = \sqrt{2DSH} &= \sqrt{2 \times 12{,}000 \times 300 \times 2} \\
&= \sqrt{14{,}400{,}000} \\
&= ₹3{,}794.73/\text{year}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Takt Time

**Takt Time** ensures production rate matches customer demand rate.

**Given:**
- $T_{\text{available}}$ = net available production time per day (seconds).
- $D_{\text{cust}}$ = customer demand per day (units).

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{takt}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{available}}}{D_{\text{cust}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example:** A factory operates **8 hours/day** with a **30-minute break** and must fulfill a demand of **400 units/day**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{available}} &= (8 \times 3600) - (30 \times 60) = 28{,}800 - 1{,}800 = 27{,}000 \text{ s} \\
T_{\text{takt}} &= \dfrac{27{,}000}{400} = 67.5 \text{ s/unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A new unit must come off the line every **67.5 seconds** to meet demand.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Production Type

The following decision matrix helps in **selecting a production type** based on key product/market characteristics:

| Decision Criterion | Job Shop | Batch | Mass | Continuous | Project |
|:-------------------|:--------:|:-----:|:----:|:----------:|:-------:|
| **Annual Volume** | < 100 | 100–10,000 | 10,000–1,00,000 | > 1,00,000 | 1 unit |
| **Product Variety** | Very High | High | Low | None | Unique |
| **Flexibility** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★ | ★ | ★★★★ |
| **Unit Cost** | Very High | High | Low | Very Low | Highest |
| **Lead Time** | Long | Medium | Short | Continuous | Longest |
| **WIP Inventory** | Very High | High | Low | Very Low | N/A |
| **Skill Level** | High | Medium | Low | Low | High |
| **Automation** | CNC | CNC + Cells | Transfer Line | PLC/SCADA | Limited |
| **Best For** | Tooling, R&D | Pharma, Bakery | Auto, Electronics | Petrochem, Steel | Aerospace, Dams |
| **CODP Position** | ETO/MTO | MTO/ATO | MTS | MTS | ETO |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb:** As one moves from *Project → Job → Batch → Mass → Continuous*, **volume increases** and **variety decreases**. Flexibility and unit cost move in opposite directions.

## 3.5 Comparison Matrix — Production Models

| Attribute | Fordism (Mass) | Toyotism (JIT/Lean) | Agile | CIM |
|:----------|:--------------:|:-------------------:|:-----:|:---:|
| **Volume** | Very High | High | Variable | High |
| **Variety** | None | Moderate | High | High |
| **Inventory** | High | Very Low | Low | Optimized |
| **Lead Time** | Short | Short | Very Short | Programmable |
| **Changeover Cost** | Tolerated | Minimized (SMED) | Modular | Automated |
| **Key Enabler** | Assembly line | Kanban, TQM | Modular design | Computers/Networks |
| **Weakness** | Rigidity | Supplier risk | Cost | Cyber-risk |
| **Best Era** | 1900s–1970s | 1970s–2000s | 2000s+ | 1990s+ |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Job Shop Production and Mass Production in terms of volume, variety, layout, and unit cost.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Job Shop | Mass Production |
|:----------|:--------:|:---------------:|
| **Volume** | Very Low | Very High |
| **Variety** | Very High | Very Low (standardized) |
| **Layout** | Functional/Process | Product/Line |
| **Machines** | General purpose | Dedicated/Specialized |
| **Unit Cost** | Very High | Very Low |
| **Lead Time** | Long | Short |
| **WIP** | High | Low |
| **Skill** | High | Low |
| **Example** | Custom tool | Car assembly |

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Just-in-Time (JIT) production and list its four essential elements.**

**Model Answer:**
**Just-in-Time (JIT)** is a production philosophy that seeks to produce and deliver finished goods **exactly when needed, in the exact quantity needed**, thereby minimizing inventory and associated costs.

**Four Essential Elements of JIT:**
1. **Pull System (Kanban)** — production triggered by downstream demand.
2. **Takt-Time Synchronization** — production rate aligned to customer demand.
3. **Setup Reduction (SMED)** — quick changeovers to enable small batches.
4. **Total Quality Control** — defect-free production to avoid reworks.

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (Choose either Option A or Option B)

---

### ✅ Question Option A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **five major types of production systems** with suitable industrial examples and a comparative diagram.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A manufacturer of precision bearings has an annual demand of **18,000 units**, ordering cost of **₹400 per order**, and holding cost of **₹3 per unit per year**. Determine the **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)**, the **number of orders per year**, and the **minimum total inventory cost per annum**. State the EOQ assumptions.

#### Part (a) — Detailed Model Solution (7 Marks)

The five production types are:

1. **Project Production** — one-of-a-kind, fixed location (e.g., dam, spacecraft).
2. **Job Shop** — low volume, high variety, functional layout (e.g., tool-room).
3. **Batch Production** — medium volume/variety, cell/line (e.g., pharmaceutical batches).
4. **Mass Production** — high volume, low variety, product layout (e.g., Maruti Suzuki).
5. **Continuous Production** — 24×7, fluid/bulk (e.g., IOCL refinery).

**Comparative Diagram (textual):**

| Type | Volume ▲ Variety | Layout | Example |
|:----:|:-----------------:|:------:|:--------|
| Project | 1 × Unique | Site | Satellite |
| Job | Low × High | Functional | Tooling |
| Batch | Med × Med | Cellular | Pharma |
| Mass | High × Low | Product | Auto |
| Continuous | V. High × None | Process | Refinery |

**Key Takeaway:** As we move from Project → Continuous, **volume increases** and **variety decreases**.

#### Part (b) — Detailed Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
$$
D = 18{,}000 \text{ units/yr}, \quad S = ₹400/\text{order}, \quad H = ₹3/\text{unit-yr}
$$

**Step 1: EOQ calculation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q^{*} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 18{,}000 \times 400}{3}} \\
&= \sqrt{\dfrac{14{,}400{,}000}{3}} = \sqrt{4{,}800{,}000} \\
&\approx 2{,}190.89 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Number of orders per year**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N = \dfrac{D}{Q^{*}} = \dfrac{18{,}000}{2{,}190.89} \approx 8.215 \text{ orders/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Minimum total cost**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_{\min} &= \sqrt{2DSH} = \sqrt{2 \times 18{,}000 \times 400 \times 3} \\
&= \sqrt{43{,}200{,}000} \approx ₹6{,}572.67/\text{year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: EOQ Assumptions**
- Constant demand rate.
- Instantaneous replenishment.
- No stockouts.
- Holding cost is linear in inventory.
- Ordering/setup cost is fixed per order.
- Independent items (no quantity discounts).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loss:**  
> (i) Students often forget to state the **EOQ assumptions** → −2 marks.  
> (ii) Some use wrong units (e.g., monthly demand with annual cost) → −1 mark.  
> (iii) Not converting ₹ correctly in cost calculation → −1 mark.  
> (iv) Skipping the **verification of convexity** (second derivative > 0) → −0.5 mark.

---

### ✅ Question Option B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **Toyota Production System (TPS)** and explain its two pillars: **JIT** and **Jidoka**. List the **eight wastes (Muda)** eliminated by Lean Manufacturing.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A job shop takes an average of **45 minutes** to process an order, with an average queue time of **6 hours**, transit time of **30 minutes**, and wait time of **2 hours**. The plant operates **one shift of 8 hours**. Calculate the **throughput time**, **delivery performance ratio**, and propose a TPS-based improvement.

#### Part (a) — Detailed Model Solution (7 Marks)

**TPS Block Diagram (textual):**

**FOUNDATION:** Standardized Work + 5S + Kaizen

↓ ↓

**PILLAR 1 — JIT:** Takt Time + Continuous Flow + Pull/Kanban + Heijunka

**PILLAR 2 — JIDOKA:** Stop on Defect + Poka-Yoke + Andon + Root-Cause Analysis

↓

**GOAL:** Highest Quality + Lowest Cost + Shortest Lead Time + Best Safety + Highest Morale

**JIT (Just-in-Time):** Producing only what is needed, only when needed, in the required quantity — driven by downstream demand (pull system).

**Jidoka (Autonomation):** *Automation with a human touch* — any worker can stop the production line if a defect is detected, preventing defective products from moving downstream.

**Eight Wastes (Muda):**
1. **Defects** (rework/scrap)
2. **Overproduction**
3. **Waiting**
4. **Non-utilized talent**
5. **Transportation**
6. **Inventory**
7. **Motion**
8. **Extra processing**

#### Part (b) — Detailed Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Compute Total Throughput Time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{thru}} &= T_{\text{process}} + T_{\text{queue}} + T_{\text{transit}} + T_{\text{wait}} \\
&= 45 \text{ min} + 6 \text{ h} + 30 \text{ min} + 2 \text{ h} \\
&= 0.75 \text{ h} + 6 \text{ h} + 0.5 \text{ h} + 2 \text{ h} \\
&= 9.25 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Value-Added Ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{VA Ratio} = \dfrac{T_{\text{process}}}{T_{\text{thru}}} = \dfrac{0.75}{9.25} = 0.0811 = 8.11\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Identify the Waste**

- **Process time = 8.11%** of throughput → **91.89% is waste** (queue + transit + wait).
- This is a classic **job-shop** problem with high WIP and poor flow.

**Step 4: TPS-Based Improvements**
- Implement **cellular manufacturing** (Group Technology) to reduce queue.
- Apply **SMED** for setup reduction.
- Introduce **single-piece flow** instead of batch processing.
- Use **Heijunka** for leveling.
- Apply **5S** to reduce motion and search waste.
- Expected outcome: Throughput time should drop to **~1.5–2 hours** post-Lean.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loss:**  
> (i) Forgetting to **convert all times to the same unit** (e.g., minutes to hours) → −1 mark.  
> (ii) Skipping the **value-added ratio** calculation → −1 mark.  
> (iii) Not connecting the answer to a **named TPS tool** → −1 mark.  
> (iv) Writing the eight wastes incorrectly (only 7 or 9) → −1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Production Types and Models (Module 1)**

- ⭐ **5 Production Types:** Project, Job, Batch, Mass, Continuous — arranged by **increasing volume**, **decreasing variety**.
- ⭐ **Hayes-Wheelwright Matrix** is the *canonical* classification of production systems.
- ⭐ **CODP** (Customer-Order Decoupling Point) determines inventory location — ETO → MTS spectrum.
- ⭐ **Fordism** = Standardization + Assembly Line; **Toyotism** = JIT + Jidoka.
- ⭐ **TPS Two Pillars:** **JIT** (pull) and **Jidoka** (stop-on-defect).
- ⭐ **Kanban** = visual signal card for pull production.
- ⭐ **Poka-Yoke** = mistake-proofing; **Heijunka** = production leveling; **5S** = workplace organization.
- ⭐ **EOQ Formula:** $Q^{*} = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ — must be derivable from $TC = DS/Q + QH/2$.
- ⭐ At EOQ: **Ordering Cost = Holding Cost** (the "1-to-1" rule).
- ⭐ **Takt Time** = available time ÷ customer demand; **Cycle Time** must be ≤ Takt Time.
- ⭐ **OEE = A × P × Q** — Availability, Performance, Quality (world-class ≥ 85%).
- ⭐ **Lean Eight Wastes (Muda):** DOWNTIME — Defects, Overproduction, Waiting, Non-utilized talent, Transportation, Inventory, Motion, Extra processing.
- ⭐ **CIM** = integration of CAD/CAM/CAPP/CAE/CAQ/MRP/ERP/MES through a **common database** and **network**.
- ⭐ **Agile Manufacturing** = mass customization enabled by CIM.
- ⭐ **Break-even volume** $BE = F/(p-v)$ helps decide between line and job-shop economics.
- ⭐ **Throughput time** = process + queue + transit + wait — only the *first* is value-added.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Production Types (V to C — Variety to Continuity):**  
> **"Joyful Boys Make Continuous Projects"** → **J**ob → **B**atch → **M**ass → **C**ontinuous → **P**roject (bookends).

**— End of Module 1 Notes —**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Production: Batch, Mass, and Continuous
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT613 - COMPUTER INTEGRATED MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Production: Batch, Mass, and Continuous

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a "Production System"?

In the context of **Computer Integrated Manufacturing (CIM)**, a *production system* is the organized arrangement of men, machines, materials, and information used to convert raw inputs into finished goods. The **type of production** defines *how* these resources are configured, *how often* the product changes, and *what volume* is manufactured.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact Terminology):**
> *Production systems are broadly classified by volume–variety characteristics into Job Production, Batch Production, Mass Production, and Continuous (Process) Production. The first three are discrete manufacturing types, while Continuous Production belongs to process manufacturing.*

## 1.2 The Three Focus Types of This Module

This module focuses on the **higher-volume, lower-variety** end of the production spectrum:

| # | Type | One-Line Definition |
|---|------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Batch Production** | A limited quantity (batch) of each product is made before switching to another. |
| 2 | **Mass Production** | A very large quantity of a *standardized* product is made continuously on a dedicated line. |
| 3 | **Continuous Production** | The product flows *non-stop, 24×7*, with no discrete units — typically liquids, gases, or powders. |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Bakery Analogy — easiest way to remember for KTU exams:**
> - **Batch Production** → A bakery that bakes 200 loaves of wheat bread this morning, then *switches* its oven to 200 buns in the afternoon. Same oven, different recipe, **in lots**.
> - **Mass Production** → A factory like Maruti Suzuki that builds the *same* Swift model off an assembly line, one after another, all day, every day. **Identical cars, thousands per day.**
> - **Continuous Production** → An oil refinery where crude oil flows through pipes, distillation columns, and crackers **24 hours a day, 365 days a year**. There are no "individual units" being assembled — it is a *flow*.

## 1.4 The Master Trade-off: **Volume vs. Variety**

Every production type sits on a spectrum defined by two parameters:

- **Volume** — How many units per year? (Low to High)
- **Variety** — How many different products? (High to Low)

$$
\text{Standard Product Variety} \;\propto\; \text{Flexibility Required}
$$

$$
\text{Production Rate} \;\propto\; \text{Specialization of Equipment}
$$

This single trade-off decides whether you need a general-purpose machine shop or a dedicated transfer line — the very heart of CIM planning.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory hook for KTU viva:** *More variety → more flexible equipment → lower volume per setup. Less variety → more dedicated equipment → higher volume per unit time.*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Batch Production — Deep Analysis

Batch production occupies the **middle ground** between job-shop flexibility and assembly-line speed. A *batch* is a defined lot of identical units produced together.

### 2.1.1 Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

1. **Group Technology (GT) Coding:** Similar parts are grouped into "part families" using GT codes (e.g., Opitz, DCLASS) to reduce setup changeovers.
2. **Lot Sizing:** The Economic Batch Quantity (EBQ) is calculated to minimize total cost of setup + inventory holding.
3. **Routing:** Each batch follows a defined process route through the workshop, but machines are *not* dedicated to one product.
4. **Changeover/SETUP:** When a new batch begins, machines are re-tooled. This **setup time** is the biggest cost driver in batch production.
5. **Production runs in cycles:** Product A batch → re-setup → Product B batch → re-setup → Product A batch again.

### 2.1.2 Why Batch Production Exists (The "Why")

- Real-world demand is rarely continuous for a *single* SKU, and rarely one-off.
- Capital investment is **moderate** (general-purpose CNC machines, not dedicated transfer lines).
- Inventory is held in semi-finished (WIP) form between operations.

> [!NOTE]
> **CIM Linkage:** Batch production is the *prime customer* of FMS (Flexible Manufacturing Systems), GT cells, and CNC-DNC networks — the core of Module 2 & 3 of this course.

## 2.2 Mass Production — Deep Analysis

Mass production targets a **single, standardized product** made in **very high volume**, typically on a **dedicated assembly line** or **transfer line**.

### 2.2.1 Operational Logic

1. **Product standardization** is finalized; design is frozen.
2. Work is divided into the *smallest possible tasks* (Taylor's principle of task subdivision).
3. Stations are laid out in a **line layout**; one station per operation.
4. **Line balancing** ensures the slowest station (= **bottleneck**) sets the *takt time* of the line.
5. Material flows via conveyors, AGVs, or roller tracks — *no backtracking*.

### 2.2.2 The Heartbeat: **Takt Time**

$$
T_{takt} = \frac{\text{Available Production Time per Day}}{\text{Daily Customer Demand}}
$$

**Real-world use:** Determines the speed at which the conveyor must move so that demand is exactly met — no more, no less.

### 2.2.3 Line Balancing Efficiency

$$
\eta_{balance} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}{n \times t_{max}} \times 100\%
$$

where $t_i$ is the work-element time at station *i*, $n$ is the number of stations, and $t_{max}$ is the longest station time (the bottleneck).

## 2.3 Continuous Production — Deep Analysis

Continuous production is the **highest-volume, lowest-variety** form. Output is measured in *kilograms, liters, or cubic meters* — not "units".

### 2.3.1 Operational Logic

1. Raw material enters as a **continuous flow** (liquid, gas, slurry, powder, or strip).
2. The process runs **24 × 7 × 365**, except for planned maintenance turnarounds.
3. Operations are tightly coupled — a stoppage anywhere halts the entire plant.
4. Control is dominated by **process control loops** (PID controllers, DCS, SCADA) — the CIM backbone.
5. Products are inherently **non-discrete** (petrol, steel, electricity, cement, paper).

### 2.3.2 Why and How

- Capital cost is *astronomical* (a petrochemical cracker = thousands of crores), so utilization must be maximum.
- Product cannot be economically *stopped and stored* mid-process — it must keep flowing.
- High **automation** and **instrumentation** replace human labour.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the only set of formulas you need to memorize for this topic.**

| # | Formula / Metric | Symbol / Expression | Typical Unit | Where Used |
|---|------------------|---------------------|--------------|------------|
| 1 | Economic Batch Quantity | $Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | units / batch | Batch production lot sizing |
| 2 | Takt Time | $T_{takt} = \dfrac{T_{available}}{D_{demand}}$ | seconds / unit | Mass & continuous line design |
| 3 | Line Balancing Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{\sum t_i}{n \cdot t_{max}} \times 100$ | % | Assembly line balancing |
| 4 | Production Rate | $R_p = \dfrac{1}{t_{cycle}}$ | units / hour | Mass production throughput |
| 5 | Machine Utilization | $U = \dfrac{\text{Loading Time}}{\text{Available Time}} \times 100$ | % | All three types |
| 6 | Throughput Time | $T_{th} = \displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{m} (Q_j \cdot t_j)$ | hours | Batch job-shop flow |
| 7 | Capacity (Continuous) | $C = \rho \cdot v \cdot A$ | kg / hr | Continuous flow (e.g., pipe) |

**Variable Legend (used above):**
- $D$ = Annual demand (units/yr) — $S$ = Setup cost per batch (₹) — $H$ = Holding cost per unit per year (₹/unit-yr)
- $T_{available}$ = Daily available production time (sec) — $D_{demand}$ = Daily customer demand (units)
- $Q_j$ = Lot size at operation *j* — $t_j$ = Operation time at *j* — $\rho$ = density, $v$ = velocity, $A$ = area.

> [!WARNING]
> **Absolute values inside tables:** Always use $\vert x \vert$ in KTU answer scripts to avoid markdown breakage. KTU examiners *will* deduct marks for ambiguous symbols.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Production Type | Indian Industry Example | CIM Element Used |
|-----------------|------------------------|------------------|
| Batch | Tata Motors Press Shop (multiple car panels in lots) | FMS, GT cells, DNC |
| Mass | Maruti Suzuki Manesar Assembly Line | Dedicated transfer lines, AGVs |
| Continuous | BPCL Kochi Refinery; Vizag Steel Plant | DCS, SCADA, process PLCs |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Economic Batch Quantity (EBQ)

The EBQ formula (sometimes called **Economic Lot Size, ELS**) minimizes the *total* of two opposing costs:

- **Setup cost** → falls as batch size grows (fewer setups).
- **Holding cost** → rises as batch size grows (more inventory held).

### Step 1 — Define the Cost Components

Let annual demand = $D$ units, batch size = $Q$ units.

**Number of batches per year:**
$$
N = \frac{D}{Q}
$$

**Setup cost per year:**
$$
C_{setup} = N \times S = \frac{D}{Q} \cdot S
$$

**Average inventory** (assuming stock is depleted linearly from $Q$ to 0):
$$
I_{avg} = \frac{Q}{2}
$$

**Holding cost per year:**
$$
C_{hold} = I_{avg} \times H = \frac{Q}{2} \cdot H
$$

### Step 2 — Total Annual Cost

$$
TC(Q) = C_{setup} + C_{hold} = \frac{D \cdot S}{Q} + \frac{Q \cdot H}{2}
$$

### Step 3 — Differentiate and Set to Zero

$$
\frac{d(TC)}{dQ} = -\frac{D \cdot S}{Q^{2}} + \frac{H}{2} = 0
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for Q*

$$
\frac{H}{2} = \frac{D \cdot S}{Q^{2}}
$$

$$
Q^{2} = \frac{2 D S}{H}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 D S}{H}}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU exam:** The optimum batch size grows with the *square root* of demand and setup cost, but shrinks with the square root of holding cost. **Double the demand ⇒ batch size grows by only $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$ times, not 2 times.**

## 3.2 Worked Example: EBQ for a Batch Production Problem

**Problem (KTU 2019-pattern):**
A component has annual demand $D = 10{,}000$ units. Setup cost $S = ₹400$ per batch. Holding cost $H = ₹8$ per unit per year. Find the Economic Batch Quantity and the number of setups per year.

**Solution:**

$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 10{,}000 \cdot 400}{8}} = \sqrt{\frac{8{,}000{,}000}{8}} = \sqrt{1{,}000{,}000} = 1000 \text{ units}
$$

**Number of setups per year:**
$$
N = \frac{D}{Q^{*}} = \frac{10{,}000}{1000} = 10 \text{ setups/year}
$$

**Minimum total cost:**
$$
TC_{min} = \frac{10{,}000 \cdot 400}{1000} + \frac{1000 \cdot 8}{2} = 4000 + 4000 = ₹8000
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (THE High-Yield KTU Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix has appeared in KTU 2017, 2019, 2022, and 2024 university exams in both 3-mark and 14-mark forms. Memorize it.**

| Parameter | Batch Production | Mass Production | Continuous Production |
|-----------|------------------|-----------------|----------------------|
| **Volume** | Medium | Very High | Extremely High |
| **Variety** | Medium | Very Low (often 1) | Almost Zero |
| **Product Flow** | Intermittent | Continuous line | Continuous process |
| **Layout** | Process / Functional | Product / Line | Fixed-path (pipe/column) |
| **Machine Type** | General-purpose CNC | Dedicated / Special | Process equipment (reactors, columns) |
| **Skill of Operator** | High (setups, NC coding) | Medium (repetitive) | Low (monitoring only) |
| **WIP Inventory** | High (between batches) | Low to Medium | Very Low |
| **Lead Time** | Medium to Long | Short | Very Short (in flow sense) |
| **Capital Investment** | Moderate | High | Very High |
| **Flexibility** | High | Low | Almost Nil |
| **Typical Industry** | Job-machine shops, garment lots | Cars, electronics, FMCG | Oil, steel, chemicals, power |
| **CIM Driver** | FMS, GT, DNC | Transfer line, AGV, JIT | DCS, SCADA, Process PLC |
| **Stopping Cost** | Low | Medium | Astronomical |
| **Cycle Time** | Variable per batch | Fixed (takt) | Not defined per unit |

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example: Line Balancing in Mass Production

**Problem:** An assembly line has 6 work elements with times (in seconds): $t_1 = 40, t_2 = 30, t_3 = 50, t_4 = 25, t_5 = 45, t_6 = 20$. Daily demand = 400 units in 8 hours. Find (a) takt time, (b) minimum number of stations, (c) balance efficiency.

**Step (a) — Takt Time:**
$$
T_{takt} = \frac{8 \times 3600}{400} = \frac{28{,}800}{400} = 72 \text{ sec/unit}
$$

**Step (b) — Minimum Stations:**
Total work content $\sum t_i = 40+30+50+25+45+20 = 210$ sec.

$$
n_{min} = \left\lceil \frac{\sum t_i}{T_{takt}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{210}{72} \right\rceil = \lceil 2.917 \rceil = 3 \text{ stations}
$$

**Step (c) — Balance Efficiency:**
Bottleneck station time $t_{max} = 72$ sec (constrained by takt).

$$
\eta = \frac{\sum t_i}{n \cdot t_{max}} \times 100 = \frac{210}{3 \times 72} \times 100 = \frac{210}{216} \times 100 \approx 97.22\%
$$

> [!TIP]
> **A balance efficiency > 90% is considered excellent in industry; below 70% indicates severe imbalance.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flow (All Production Types)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Production System] --> B[Discrete Manufacturing]
    A --> C[Process Manufacturing]
    B --> D[Job Production - 1 unit]
    B --> E[Batch Production - Lot of units]
    B --> F[Mass Production - High volume]
    C --> G[Continuous Production - 24x7 flow]
    E --> E1[CNC Machines + GT Cells]
    F --> F1[Dedicated Transfer Lines]
    G --> G1[DCS / SCADA Controlled]
    E1 --> E2[Moderate Volume + Variety]
    F1 --> F2[High Volume + Low Variety]
    G1 --> G2[Extreme Volume + Near Zero Variety]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Node IDs above use purely alphanumeric names (A, B, C, E1, F1) and labels contain no markdown formatting — fully KTU-print safe.**

## 4.2 Comparative Functional Architecture: Batch vs Mass vs Continuous

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BatchLayer["BATCH PRODUCTION ARCHITECTURE"]
        B_Input[Raw Material In] --> B_Store[Store Room]
        B_Store --> B_CNC[CNC Machine 1]
        B_Store --> B_CNC2[CNC Machine 2]
        B_Store --> B_CNC3[CNC Machine 3]
        B_CNC --> B_QA[Inspection Station]
        B_CNC2 --> B_QA
        B_CNC3 --> B_QA
        B_QA --> B_Out[Batch Out - Lot Q]
    end
    subgraph MassLayer["MASS PRODUCTION ARCHITECTURE"]
        M_Input[Parts Feed Line] --> M_S1[Station 1 - Press]
        M_S1 --> M_S2[Station 2 - Weld]
        M_S2 --> M_S3[Station 3 - Paint]
        M_S3 --> M_S4[Station 4 - Assemble]
        M_S4 --> M_Out[Conveyor Out]
    end
    subgraph ContLayer["CONTINUOUS PRODUCTION ARCHITECTURE"]
        C_Input[Crude Feed] --> C_Pump[Feed Pump]
        C_Pump --> C_Reactor[Reactor Column]
        C_Reactor --> C_Sep[Separator]
        C_Sep --> C_DC[Distillation Column]
        C_DC --> C_Out[Product Storage Tank]
    end
```

## 4.3 Volume-Variety Positioning Map (Draw This in Exam!)

```mermaid
quadrantChart
    title Volume vs Variety Spectrum
    x-axis Low Variety --> High Variety
    y-axis Low Volume --> High Volume
    quadrant-1 High Vol High Var: Not Possible
    quadrant-2 High Vol Low Var: Mass + Continuous
    quadrant-3 Low Vol Low Var: Not Common
    quadrant-4 Low Vol High Var: Job Shop
    Continuous: [0.05, 0.95]
    Mass: [0.20, 0.85]
    Batch: [0.55, 0.55]
    Job: [0.90, 0.20]
```

> [!TIP]
> **In your KTU answer sheet, draw a simple 2×2 grid (X = variety, Y = volume) and place each type in its quadrant. Examiners reward this 2-mark freebie consistently.**

## 4.4 Information Flow in a CIM-Enabled Batch Cell

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Host[Central CIM Host Computer] --> DB[(Shared Database)]
    DB --> DNC[DNC Server]
    DNC --> M1[CNC Lathe]
    DNC --> M2[CNC Mill]
    DNC --> M3[CNC Grinder]
    M1 --> DB
    M2 --> DB
    M3 --> DB
    Host --> Sched[Production Scheduler]
    Sched --> AGV[Automated Guided Vehicle]
    AGV --> M1
    AGV --> M2
    AGV --> M3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Mass Production and Continuous Production with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — keyword-driven for full marks):**

| Basis | Mass Production | Continuous Production |
|-------|-----------------|----------------------|
| **Output Form** | Discrete units (cars, mobiles) | Non-discrete flow (petrol, gas) |
| **Operating Hours** | Usually single/double shift; can stop | 24 × 7 × 365, stoppage not feasible |
| **Examples** | Maruti Suzuki assembly line, smartphone plants | BPCL refinery, NTPC power plant |
| **Control System** | PLC + SCADA on stations | DCS with thousands of PID loops |
| **Stopping Cost** | Moderate (line restarts) | Astronomical (whole plant at risk) |

*(1 mark for definition of each + 1 mark for examples = 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any six characteristics of Batch Production.**

**Model Answer (any 6 × ½ mark = 3 marks):**

1. Moderate production volume and moderate variety.
2. General-purpose machines are used (CNCs, lathes, mills).
3. Lot/batch size is decided by Economic Batch Quantity.
4. Setup/changeover time is a major cost component.
5. Work-in-process inventory is significant between operations.
6. Skilled labour is required for setups and NC programming.
7. Production is cyclical — same product repeats after an interval.
8. Layout is *process* or *cellular*, not strictly a line.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (ESE 14-Mark Module Choice)

> Per KTU 2024 regulations: Module 1 contains a 14-mark question with internal choice. Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Pattern]*

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the characteristics, advantages, and limitations of **Batch Production**. How does CIM (Flexible Manufacturing Systems) enhance batch production efficiency?

**(b) [7 marks]** A company produces a component with annual demand **D = 8000 units**, setup cost **S = ₹500/batch**, and holding cost **H = ₹10/unit-year**. Determine the **Economic Batch Quantity**, **number of batches per year**, and **total minimum cost**. Also comment on what happens to the optimum batch size if demand doubles.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

*(2 marks for characteristics — list at least 4)*
- Moderate volume, moderate variety.
- Batches of limited size produced periodically.
- Machines are general-purpose and shared across products.
- Significant WIP inventory between operations.
- Production is cyclical with periodic setups.

*(2 marks for advantages)*
- Lower capital investment than mass/continuous.
- Flexibility to produce many products on the same equipment.
- Better machine utilization across product mix.
- Lower risk than a dedicated line (no single-product dependence).

*(1.5 marks for limitations)*
- Higher inventory carrying cost.
- Longer lead times than mass production.
- Frequent setups cause production loss.
- Production planning complexity is high.

*(1.5 marks for CIM/FMS linkage)*
- FMS cells integrate multiple CNC machines under a central controller, reducing changeover time.
- Group Technology (GT) groups similar parts → families → reduces setups.
- DNC delivers NC programs electronically, eliminating manual loading delays.
- AGVs and automated storage reduce WIP and material handling time.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

Step 1: EBQ formula:
$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 8000 \times 500}{10}} = \sqrt{\frac{8{,}000{,}000}{10}} = \sqrt{800{,}000}
$$

$$
Q^{*} = 894.43 \approx 894 \text{ units}
$$

Step 2: Number of batches per year:
$$
N = \frac{D}{Q^{*}} = \frac{8000}{894.43} \approx 8.94 \approx 9 \text{ batches/year}
$$

Step 3: Minimum total cost:
$$
TC_{min} = \frac{D \cdot S}{Q^{*}} + \frac{Q^{*} \cdot H}{2} = \frac{8000 \times 500}{894.43} + \frac{894.43 \times 10}{2}
$$

$$
TC_{min} \approx 4472.14 + 4472.14 = ₹8944.28
$$

Step 4: Effect of doubling demand:
New demand $D' = 16000$. New $Q^{*'} = \sqrt{2 \times 16000 \times 500 / 10} = \sqrt{1{,}600{,}000} \approx 1264.9$ units.

$$
\frac{Q^{*'}}{Q^{*}} = \frac{1264.9}{894.43} \approx 1.414 = \sqrt{2}
$$

**Conclusion:** Doubling demand increases the optimal batch size by only $\sqrt{2}$ times, not 2 times — because EBQ scales with the square root of demand.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Pattern — Alternative Choice]*

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **Volume–Variety spectrum** of production systems. Place Job, Batch, Mass, and Continuous production on this spectrum and justify their positions.

**(b) [7 marks]** An assembly line is designed to produce **300 units per day** in **8 working hours**. The total work content is **540 seconds** distributed across work elements of 60, 50, 80, 70, 90, 110, and 80 seconds respectively. Calculate (i) Takt time, (ii) Minimum number of stations, (iii) Balance efficiency.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

*(2.5 marks for sketch — draw 2×2 matrix with axes Volume and Variety)*
*(2.5 marks for placement with justification)*
*(2 marks for description of each type)*

**Placement on the spectrum:**

| Quadrant | Production Type | Justification |
|----------|-----------------|---------------|
| High Vol + Low Var (top-left) | **Continuous** | Refinery output is continuous, near-zero variety. |
| High Vol + Low Var (top-left, lower) | **Mass** | Identical cars/phones in huge volumes, one design. |
| Moderate Vol + Moderate Var (center) | **Batch** | Multiple SKUs, each in medium lots. |
| Low Vol + High Var (bottom-right) | **Job** | Custom / one-off engineering work. |

**Justification text:** The *higher* the volume, the *lower* the variety that is economically viable, because dedicated equipment pays off only when spread over many units. Continuous production represents the extreme — flow rather than discrete units.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

Step 1 — Takt time:
$$
T_{takt} = \frac{8 \times 3600}{300} = \frac{28{,}800}{300} = 96 \text{ seconds}
$$

Step 2 — Total work content:
$$
\sum t_i = 60+50+80+70+90+110+80 = 540 \text{ sec}
$$

Step 3 — Minimum stations:
$$
n_{min} = \left\lceil \frac{\sum t_i}{T_{takt}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{540}{96} \right\rceil = \lceil 5.625 \rceil = 6 \text{ stations}
$$

Step 4 — Bottleneck time (= takt time constrained) = 96 sec.

Step 5 — Balance efficiency:
$$
\eta = \frac{\sum t_i}{n \cdot t_{max}} \times 100 = \frac{540}{6 \times 96} \times 100 = \frac{540}{576} \times 100 \approx 93.75\%
$$

**Conclusion:** A balance efficiency of **93.75%** is excellent — the line is well balanced.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically LOSE marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing Mass with Continuous.** Mass = *discrete units* on a line (cars). Continuous = *flow* (oil). Writing "Maruti produces continuously" is worth **0 marks** even if the rest is correct.
> 2. **Forgetting units in EBQ.** $D$ = units/year, $S$ = ₹/batch, $H$ = ₹/unit-year. Mixing units gives a numerically correct-looking but dimensionally **wrong** answer → **2 marks deducted** in valuation.
> 3. **Skipping the lot-size derivation steps.** Writing only the final formula $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ without the cost minimization derivation = **max 4 of 7 marks** in part-b.
> 4. **Ignoring the Volume-Variety diagram.** In 7-mark questions, examiners give **2 free marks** for a clean spectrum sketch. Skipping it is a 2-mark loss.
> 5. **CIM linkage missing.** In any 14-mark question, you *must* mention FMS, GT, DNC, or AGV — Module 1 of CIM must reference CIM tools explicitly. A purely mechanical engineering answer = **−2 marks** for not addressing the "Integrated" part of CIM.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (read 5 minutes before exam):**
>
> ✅ **Three types in this module:** Batch, Mass, Continuous. Job Production is *not* in this module's deep focus.
>
> ✅ **Volume–Variety** is the master trade-off. Always draw the 2×2 matrix in long-answer questions.
>
> ✅ **Batch** = moderate vol + moderate var, general-purpose machines, EBQ lot sizing, FMS-driven.
>
> ✅ **Mass** = high vol + low var, dedicated line, takt time, line balancing, transfer lines.
>
> ✅ **Continuous** = extreme vol + zero var, flow not units, DCS/SCADA-controlled, 24×7.
>
> ✅ **EBQ formula:** $Q^{*} = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ — derive, don't just state.
>
> ✅ **Takt time:** $T_{takt} = T_{available} / D_{demand}$ — use *seconds* for unit consistency.
>
> ✅ **Line balance efficiency:** $\eta = (\sum t_i) / (n \cdot t_{max}) \times 100\%$. Always use ceiling of $n_{min}$.
>
> ✅ **CIM tools per type:** Batch→FMS+GT+DNC, Mass→Transfer line+AGV+JIT, Continuous→DCS+SCADA+PID.
>
> ✅ **Capital investment order:** Batch < Mass < Continuous.
>
> ✅ **Stopping cost order:** Batch < Mass < Continuous.
>
> ✅ **Always give Indian industry examples** in KTU answers: BPCL Kochi, Maruti Manesar, Tata Press Shop, Vizag Steel — these are contextually valued.
>
> ✅ **Mention CIM integration** in *every* long answer — that is the "C" in CIM and examiners test for it.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Manufacturing Models and Metrics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT613 - COMPUTER INTEGRATED MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Manufacturing Models and Metrics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Manufacturing Models — The Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Manufacturing Model** is a mathematical, logical, or computer-simulated representation of a real manufacturing system. It abstracts the physical flow of materials, information, and control signals into a structured analytical framework that allows engineers to predict system behavior, optimize performance, and evaluate "what-if" scenarios without disturbing the actual production line.

In **Computer Integrated Manufacturing (CIM)**, models are classified into four primary families:

1. **Mathematical / Analytical Models** — Closed-form equations representing system behavior.
2. **Network Models** — Graphical models for project planning (PERT, CPM).
3. **Queueing Models** — Stochastic models using birth-death processes.
4. **Simulation Models** — Discrete-event or continuous numerical experimentation.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Manufacturing Model like a "Weather Forecast" for a factory.**
> Just as meteorologists don't wait for a cyclone to hit land to know its path — they simulate the atmosphere using equations — manufacturing engineers don't want to shut down a real factory to test a new layout. They build a *digital twin* (a model) of the shop floor and run experiments. The **metrics** are the "instruments" on this weather station: throughput, cycle time, and WIP are the *temperature, pressure, and humidity* of your production system.

## 1.3 Manufacturing Metrics — The Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> **Manufacturing Metrics** are quantifiable performance indicators used to measure the efficiency, productivity, quality, flexibility, and cost-effectiveness of a manufacturing system. They convert abstract operational goals (e.g., "be faster," "be cheaper") into objective, trackable numerical values.

The **five canonical KTU metric families** are:

- **Productivity Metrics** (Output / Input)
- **Flexibility Metrics** (Range, Changeover)
- **Quality Metrics** (Defect rates, Six Sigma levels)
- **Cost Metrics** (Unit cost, conversion cost)
- **Time Metrics** (Cycle time, lead time, Takt time)

## 1.4 The Fundamental Link

A *model* without *metrics* is a brain without senses — you cannot measure what you have modeled. A *metric* without a *model* is a number without context. **CIM unites them**: the model generates the data, the metrics interpret it.

![Conceptual Bridge: Model to Metric](attachment://bridge.png)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Manufacturing Models

### Pillar A — Mathematical (Analytical) Models
These are **deterministic, equation-based** models used when the system is simple and predictable.

**Core 'Why':** When the relationships between variables (machine speed, batch size, defect rate) are well understood and constant, closed-form math gives an *exact* answer.

**Core 'How':** Use variables like production rate $R$, number of machines $m$, utilization $U$, and downtime $d$ to derive output equations.

**Engineering Utility:** Used for capacity planning, line balancing, and break-even analysis in mass production environments (e.g., automotive assembly lines).

---

### Pillar B — Network Models (PERT / CPM)
**Project Evaluation and Review Technique (PERT)** and **Critical Path Method (CPM)** model the *sequence and dependencies* of manufacturing tasks.

- **CPM** uses *deterministic* time estimates.
- **PERT** uses *three-time estimates*: Optimistic ($t_o$), Most Likely ($t_m$), Pessimistic ($t_p$).

**Core 'Why':** Manufacturing projects (new product introduction, plant setup) involve hundreds of interdependent activities. A network model reveals the *bottleneck path*.

**Engineering Utility:** Tool introduction, plant commissioning, and maintenance scheduling in CIM environments.

---

### Pillar C — Queueing Models
These are **stochastic** models that represent machines as "servers" and parts as "customers" waiting in line.

**Core 'Why':** Real manufacturing is random — breakdowns, variable processing times, and erratic demand create queues. Queueing theory quantifies *waiting time, queue length, and server utilization*.

**Engineering Utility:** Sizing buffer zones, deciding number of AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles), and designing cellular layouts.

---

### Pillar D — Simulation Models
When the system is **too complex for closed-form math**, engineers build a **Discrete Event Simulation (DES)** model.

**Core 'Why':** Captures the *dynamic, time-dependent, non-linear* interactions between machines, conveyors, robots, and human operators.

**Engineering Utility:** Validating CIM layouts before capital investment, testing scheduling algorithms (e.g., Johnson's rule, genetic algorithms).

---

## 2.2 The Five KTU Metric Families — Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are KTU Board Exam high-yield. Memorize the bold-marked variables.**

### Table 2.1: Productivity & Throughput Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Variables | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Productivity** | $P = \dfrac{O}{I}$ | $O$ = Output, $I$ = Input | units / hr |
| **Machine Utilization** | $U = \dfrac{\text{Busy Time}}{\text{Available Time}}$ | Ratio | % |
| **Throughput** | $TH = \dfrac{1}{T_c}$ | $T_c$ = Cycle Time | parts / hr |
| **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | $A$=Availability, $P$=Performance, $Q$=Quality | % |
| **Line Efficiency** | $\eta_L = \dfrac{\sum t_i}{n \cdot t_{\max}}$ | $t_i$=task time, $n$=stations, $t_{\max}}$=bottleneck | % |

### Table 2.2: Time Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Cycle Time ($T_c$)** | $T_c = \dfrac{\text{Available Time}}{\text{Demand}}$ | Time per unit at one station |
| **Lead Time ($T_L$)** | $T_L = \text{Queue Time} + \text{Process Time} + \text{Move Time}$ | Total order-to-delivery |
| **Takt Time ($T_t$)** | $T_t = \dfrac{\text{Available Production Time}}{\text{Customer Demand}}$ | Pace of production |
| **Work-In-Process (WIP)** | $WIP = TH \cdot T_L$ | Little's Law | parts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Little's Law (Board Favorite):**
> $$WIP = \text{Throughput} \times \text{Lead Time}$$
> This is the *fundamental identity* of flow systems. KTU often gives 3-mark questions asking you to compute one of the three variables.

### Table 2.3: Quality Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $FPY = \dfrac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units Produced}}$ | Zero-rework quality |
| **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} FPY_i$ | Yield across $n$ processes |
| **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** | $DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 10^6}{U \times O}$ | $D$=defects, $U$=units, $O$=opportunities |
| **Sigma Level** | $\sigma = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{s}$ | Process capability |

### Table 2.4: Flexibility & Cost Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Range Flexibility** | $F_R = N_{\text{product types}}$ | Number of products handled |
| **Changeover Time** | $SMED$ (Single Minute Exchange of Dies) | Setup reduction target $<10$ min |
| **Conversion Cost** | $C_c = \text{Direct Labor} + \text{Manufacturing Overhead}$ | Cost of transformation |
| **Unit Cost** | $C_u = \dfrac{C_c + C_m}{N}$ | $C_m$ = material, $N$ = units |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Model Type | Industrial Application |
|---|---|
| Mathematical | Break-even analysis for FMS investment |
| Network (CPM) | New product launch scheduling |
| Queueing | AGV fleet sizing in automated warehouses |
| Simulation | Validating digital twin before commissioning |
| OEE Metric | Benchmarking in Industry 4.0 plants |
| Little's Law | WIP reduction in lean manufacturing |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Little's Law (The Heart of Flow Metrics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Little's Law** connects three of the most important metrics in any manufacturing system: inventory, throughput, and flow time. KTU frequently tests this as a 7 or 14-mark problem.

### Step 1 — Define the System
Consider a production line in **steady state** (long-run equilibrium). At any instant, there are $L$ parts inside the system (the average number of customers in a queueing system). Parts leave the system at a long-run average rate of $\lambda$ per unit time.

### Step 2 — Set Up the Cumulative Count
Let $A(t)$ = the number of arrivals (parts entering) up to time $t$. Let $D(t)$ = the number of departures (parts leaving) up to time $t$.

The average number of parts in the system at time $t$ is:
$$L(t) = A(t) - D(t)$$

### Step 3 — Define the Throughput
The **long-run throughput** $\lambda$ is defined as the average rate of arrival:
$$\lambda = \lim_{t \to \infty} \frac{A(t)}{t}$$

### Step 4 — Define the Lead Time
The average **lead time** $W$ is the average time a part spends in the system:
$$W = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i$$

where $T_i$ is the time the $i$-th part spends in the system.

### Step 5 — The Fundamental Relationship
By the **conservation principle**, in the long run the number of parts that have entered equals the number that have left. Therefore:
$$\lim_{t \to \infty} \frac{A(t)}{t} = \lim_{t \to \infty} \frac{D(t)}{t}$$

### Step 6 — Combine via Area Interpretation
The integral $\int_{0}^{t} L(\tau) \, d\tau$ represents the **cumulative residence time** of all parts in the system. Dividing by $t$ gives the average lead time, and recognizing that $\frac{1}{t} \int_{0}^{t} L(\tau) \, d\tau \to W$ as $t \to \infty$, we obtain the celebrated identity:

$$\boxed{\,L = \lambda W\,}$$

In manufacturing notation, this is:

$$WIP = \text{Throughput} \times \text{Lead Time}$$

**Engineering Insight:** This law is *universal* — it holds for any system in steady state, regardless of complexity, arrival distribution, or service discipline. KTU examiners love this.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: OEE Breakdown

The **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** metric is a product of three sub-efficiencies.

### Step 1 — Availability ($A$)
$$A = \frac{\text{Planned Production Time} - \text{Downtime}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}$$

*Logic:* Downtime (breakdowns, setup) reduces actual run time.

### Step 2 — Performance ($P$)
$$P = \frac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces Produced}}{\text{Run Time}}$$

*Logic:* Even when running, the machine may operate slower than its theoretical best speed.

### Step 3 — Quality ($Q$)
$$Q = \frac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces Produced}}$$

*Logic:* Defective output is waste; only good pieces count as value created.

### Step 4 — Combine
$$OEE = A \times P \times Q$$

A **World-Class OEE benchmark is 85%** (i.e., $A=90\%$, $P=95\%$, $Q=99\%$).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: PERT Expected Time

For network models, PERT uses three estimates. The expected time $t_e$ is the *mean* of the Beta distribution:

$$t_e = \frac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$$

**Variance of the estimate:**
$$\sigma^2 = \left( \frac{t_p - t_o}{6} \right)^2$$

**Variance of the project (sum of critical path variances):**
$$\sigma^2_{\text{project}} = \sum_{i \in CP} \sigma^2_i$$

**Probability of meeting a deadline $T_d$:**
$$Z = \frac{T_d - T_e}{\sigma_{\text{project}}}$$

This $Z$-score is then looked up in the standard normal table to obtain probability.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Model-to-Metric Mapping

| Manufacturing Goal | Best-Fit Model | Best-Fit Metric |
|---|---|---|
| Maximize output | Mathematical | Throughput, Productivity |
| Minimize delay | Queueing | Lead Time, Queue Length |
| Hit delivery date | Network (PERT/CPM) | Slack, Critical Path |
| Validate new layout | Simulation | OEE, Cycle Time |
| Reduce waste | Lean / Six Sigma | RTY, DPMO, Sigma Level |
| Cut inventory cost | Flow Model | WIP (via Little's Law) |
| Improve responsiveness | Cellular Mfg | Changeover Time (SMED) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Manufacturing Models

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Manufacturing Models] --> rootB[Mathematical Analytical]

    rootE --> rootE1[Discrete Event Simulation]

```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — CIM Feedback Loop

```mermaid

    inputA[Customer Demand Signal] --> inputB[Order Processing Module]

```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define manufacturing model. List any four types of manufacturing models used in CIM.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A manufacturing model is a mathematical, logical, or computer-simulated abstraction of a real production system used to predict performance, optimize processes, and test scenarios without physical intervention.
Four types:
1. Mathematical / Analytical Models
2. Network Models (PERT, CPM)
3. Queueing Models
4. Simulation Models (Discrete Event Simulation)

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing with brief description.)*

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State Little's Law. What is its significance in manufacturing?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Little's Law states that in a steady-state system:
$$WIP = \text{Throughput} \times \text{Lead Time}$$
**Significance (2 marks):**
- It is a universal relationship valid for any flow system.
- It enables managers to reduce inventory (WIP) by controlling throughput or lead time.
- It forms the mathematical foundation of lean manufacturing and Theory of Constraints.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Always state units** in your final numerical answer. Marks are deducted when units are missing.
> 2. **For Little's Law problems**, students often confuse *cycle time* with *lead time*. Cycle time is the time per unit at a single station; lead time is the total time from order to delivery.
> 3. **In OEE calculations**, never multiply three percentages and forget to convert to decimals. OEE = 0.90 × 0.95 × 0.99 = 0.846 (i.e., 84.6%), NOT 90 × 95 × 99.
> 4. **PERT problems** require you to compute the *critical path* before computing project variance. Marks are lost when students skip this step.

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the classification of manufacturing models used in CIM. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing cell produces 240 units per shift (8 hours). Downtime due to breakdowns and setup is 45 minutes per shift. The ideal cycle time is 1.5 minutes, and the actual average cycle time is 1.8 minutes. The quality inspection rejects 6 units per shift. Calculate:
1. Availability
2. Performance
3. Quality
4. Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Classification of Manufacturing Models (7 marks)

Manufacturing models are classified into four major categories:

1. **Mathematical / Analytical Models (2 marks):**
   These are closed-form, equation-based models representing deterministic relationships. Used for capacity planning, line balancing, and break-even analysis. Example: Production function $R = m \cdot t / T_c$ where $R$ is output, $m$ is number of machines, $t$ is available time, and $T_c$ is cycle time.

2. **Network Models (1.5 marks):**
   Represent activities and their dependencies as a graph. PERT handles uncertainty with three time estimates; CPM uses a single deterministic time. Used for project planning, new product introduction, and plant commissioning.

3. **Queueing Models (1.5 marks):**
   Stochastic models treating machines as servers and parts as customers. Characterized by Kendall's notation (e.g., M/M/1). Used for buffer sizing, AGV fleet planning, and WIP optimization.

4. **Simulation Models (2 marks):**
   Numerical experimentation using Discrete Event Simulation (DES) when the system is too complex for analytical solutions. Tools: Arena, FlexSim, Tecnomatix. Used to validate CIM layouts and digital twins before capital investment.

*(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram for a visual representation.)*

---

#### Part (b) — OEE Calculation (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- Shift duration = 8 hours = 480 minutes
- Production = 240 units / shift
- Downtime = 45 minutes
- Ideal cycle time = 1.5 min/unit
- Actual cycle time = 1.8 min/unit
- Rejected units = 6

**Step 1 — Availability ($A$) (2 marks):**
Planned Production Time = 480 min
Downtime = 45 min
Run Time = 480 - 45 = 435 min
$$A = \frac{435}{480} = 0.9063 = 90.63\%$$

**Step 2 — Performance ($P$) (2 marks):**
Total Pieces = 240
Ideal Cycle Time = 1.5 min
$$\text{Ideal Run Time} = 240 \times 1.5 = 360 \text{ min}$$
$$P = \frac{360}{435} = 0.8276 = 82.76\%$$

**Step 3 — Quality ($Q$) (1.5 marks):**
Good Pieces = 240 - 6 = 234
$$Q = \frac{234}{240} = 0.975 = 97.5\%$$

**Step 4 — OEE (1.5 marks):**
$$OEE = 0.9063 \times 0.8276 \times 0.975$$
$$OEE = 0.7314 = 73.14\%$$

**Interpretation:** The cell is operating at **73.14% OEE**, which is below the world-class benchmark of 85%. Major improvement opportunity exists in *Performance Efficiency*.

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the five categories of manufacturing performance metrics with two examples each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A factory has a throughput of 50 units/day and an average lead time of 4 days. Due to process improvement, the throughput increases to 75 units/day while lead time reduces to 2 days.
1. Calculate the WIP before and after improvement using Little's Law.
2. What is the percentage reduction in WIP? **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Five Categories of Performance Metrics (7 marks)

1. **Productivity Metrics (1.4 marks):** Measure output per unit of input.
   - Examples: Machine Utilization, Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)

2. **Flexibility Metrics (1.4 marks):** Measure the ability to adapt to changes.
   - Examples: Changeover Time (SMED), Range Flexibility (number of product types)

3. **Quality Metrics (1.4 marks):** Measure conformance to specifications.
   - Examples: First Pass Yield (FPY), Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)

4. **Cost Metrics (1.4 marks):** Measure economic efficiency.
   - Examples: Unit Cost, Conversion Cost

5. **Time Metrics (1.4 marks):** Measure the temporal efficiency of flow.
   - Examples: Cycle Time, Lead Time, Takt Time, WIP

---

#### Part (b) — Little's Law Application (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Before: $TH_1 = 50$ units/day, $T_{L1} = 4$ days
- After: $TH_2 = 75$ units/day, $T_{L2} = 2$ days

**Step 1 — WIP Before Improvement (2.5 marks):**
Using Little's Law:
$$WIP_1 = TH_1 \times T_{L1} = 50 \times 4 = 200 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2 — WIP After Improvement (2.5 marks):**
$$WIP_2 = TH_2 \times T_{L2} = 75 \times 2 = 150 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3 — Percentage Reduction in WIP (2 marks):**
$$\text{Reduction} = \frac{WIP_1 - WIP_2}{WIP_1} \times 100$$
$$\text{Reduction} = \frac{200 - 150}{200} \times 100 = 25\%$$

**Interpretation:** The improvement program reduced WIP by **25%** while simultaneously increasing throughput by 50% and halving lead time. This is a classic *lean transformation* outcome.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1**

- **Manufacturing Model** = abstract representation (math, network, queue, simulation) of a real production system.
- **Four Model Types:** Mathematical, Network (PERT/CPM), Queueing (M/M/1, M/M/c), Simulation (DES).
- **Five Metric Families:** Productivity, Flexibility, Quality, Cost, Time.
- **Little's Law:** $WIP = TH \times T_L$ — *universal, no assumptions, always true in steady state.*
- **OEE = A × P × Q**, World-class benchmark = **85%**.
- **Takt Time** = Available Time ÷ Customer Demand (sets the production *pace*).
- **Cycle Time** = time per unit at *one station*; **Lead Time** = total time *order to delivery*.
- **RTY** = product of FPYs across all processes (compounds defects geometrically).
- **DPMO** = $\dfrac{D \times 10^6}{U \times O}$ — basis of Six Sigma.
- **PERT Expected Time:** $t_e = \dfrac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$.
- **PERT Variance:** $\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{t_p - t_o}{6}\right)^2$.
- **Always include units** in your final answer for full marks.
- **Convert percentages to decimals** before multiplying in OEE/compound metric problems.
- **Critical Path** in PERT/CPM is the longest-duration path; project variance = sum of variances *on* the critical path.
- **SMED** (Single Minute Exchange of Dies) target = setup in **less than 10 minutes** (single-digit minute).
- **Industry 4.0 connection:** All these models and metrics are now embedded in **digital twins** for real-time plant optimization.

---

*End of Module 1 Notes — Manufacturing Models and Metrics*
*Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech OERUT613 — Computer Integrated Manufacturing*
