The entire factory floor is monitored and scheduled using a **Manufacturing Execution System (MES)**. Functions include order tracking, scheduling, quality control, and KPI reporting. SCADA systems provide real-time visualization.

*Example:* A plant-wide MES coordinating 20+ manufacturing cells.

**Level 5 — Enterprise Level (1 mark):**
Business functions — **sales, finance, design, supply chain** — are integrated with manufacturing through **ERP and PLM systems**. The CIM pyramid reaches its apex, providing a unified digital thread from customer order to delivery.

*Example:* SAP S/4HANA integrating design, production, and logistics.

**Block Diagram (0.2 marks):** Refer to the CIM pyramid shown in Section 4.1.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Hard Automation (2 marks):**
Hard automation (also called *fixed* or *dedicated automation*) uses special-purpose equipment designed to produce a **single product or part variant at high volume**. The sequence of operations is fixed by the mechanical structure of the equipment. Changeover to a different product requires **major physical retooling** and significant downtime.

*Examples:* Automotive welding lines, bottling plants, printed circuit board (PCB) assembly lines.

**Flexible Automation (2 marks):**
Flexible automation uses **programmable equipment** capable of producing a **variety of part types** with minimal changeover. Changeover is achieved through **software modifications** (NC programs, robot programs) rather than mechanical reconfiguration. It is the foundation of FMS and CIM.

*Examples:* CNC machining centers, industrial robots, FMS cells.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Hard Automation | Flexible Automation |
|-----------|----------------|---------------------|
| Product variety | Single, fixed | Multiple part families |
| Changeover cost & time | Very high | Low |
| Initial investment | High for high volume | High but justified by flexibility |
| Production rate | Very high | Moderate to high |
| Programming | Not programmable | Fully programmable |
| Suitable for | Mass production | Batch / mass customization |

**Recommendation (1 mark):**
- **Hard automation** is recommended when the product is standardized, demand volume is high and stable, and no major design changes are expected (e.g., commodity fasteners, beverage cans).
- **Flexible automation** is recommended when product variety is high, batch sizes are small to medium, and the market demands customization (e.g., automotive sub-assemblies, aerospace components).

---

#### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam — June 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **CIM architecture** based on the five levels of automation. Describe the **upward and downward data flow** between the levels. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A CNC machine operates for two shifts (16 hours total per day). The data for one day is:
- Downtime due to failures = **1.5 hours**
- Setup and changeover time = **0.5 hours**
- Ideal Cycle Time = **45 seconds per part**
- Total Pieces Produced = **1000 parts**
- Defective Pieces = **60 parts**

Calculate **Availability, Performance Efficiency, Quality, and OEE**. Comment on whether the machine qualifies as **world-class manufacturing**. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The CIM architecture is conventionally represented as a **five-level pyramid**, reflecting the hierarchical nature of automation from shop-floor devices to enterprise business systems.

**Diagram (2 marks):** (Refer to Section 4.1)

**Description of Each Level (3 marks — 0.6 marks each):**
- **Level 1 — Device Level:** Field devices such as sensors, actuators, and relays.
- **Level 2 — Machine Level:** Standalone machines like CNC, robots, AGVs.
- **Level 3 — Cell Level:** FMC/FMS integrating multiple machines under a cell controller.
- **Level 4 — Plant Level:** MES/SCADA coordinating the entire factory floor.
- **Level 5 — Enterprise Level:** ERP/PLM/CRM integrating business functions.

**Data Flow (1 mark):**
- **Downward flow:** Customer orders → production schedules → job dispatch → NC programs → machine setpoints → device signals.
- **Upward flow:** Sensor data → machine status → cell performance → plant KPIs → enterprise dashboards.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given Data:**
- Planned Production Time = 16 hours
- Downtime = 1.5 + 0.5 = **2.0 hours**
- Ideal Cycle Time = 45 sec = 45/3600 hr = **0.0125 hr/part**
- Total Pieces = 1000
- Defective = 60
- Good Pieces = 1000 − 60 = **940 parts**

**Step 1 — Operating Time:**
$$
\text{Operating Time} = 16 - 2.0 = 14 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 2 — Availability (A):**
$$
A = \dfrac{14}{16} = 0.875 \quad \text{or} \quad 87.5\%
$$

**Step 3 — Performance Efficiency (P):**
$$
P = \dfrac{0.0125 \times 1000}{14} = \dfrac{12.5}{14} = 0.8929 \quad \text{or} \quad 89.29\%
$$

**Step 4 — Quality (Q):**
$$
Q = \dfrac{940}{1000} = 0.940 \quad \text{or} \quad 94.0\%
$$

**Step 5 — OEE:**
$$
\text{OEE} = 0.875 \times 0.8929 \times 0.940
$$
$$
\text{OEE} = 0.7342 \quad \text{or} \quad 73.42\%
$$

**Conclusion:**
Since **OEE = 73.42% < 85%**, the machine does **NOT qualify as world-class**. The primary loss contributor can be identified by examining the three factors:
- Quality (94%) is close to target.
- Performance (89.29%) is moderate.
- Availability (87.5%) is the weakest, indicating **unplanned downtime reduction** should be prioritized.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error (–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert cycle time from seconds to hours (or minutes) before computing Performance Efficiency. Always check units first.
> 2. **Confusing OEE with Availability (–1 mark):** OEE is the *product* of A, P, Q — not just one of them. Examiners explicitly check this.
> 3. **Ignoring the World-Class Threshold:** Students often forget to compare the computed OEE with **85%** and lose the "comment" marks.
> 4. **Diagram Errors in 14-mark questions:** Drawing only 3 levels instead of 5 results in a –2 to –3 mark penalty. Always draw the **complete five-level pyramid**.
> 5. **Mixing Hard and Flexible Automation Definitions:** Many students write them as identical. Clearly highlight *changeover time*, *product variety*, and *programming* as the three key differentiators.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Automation in CIM is classified into **FIVE levels**: Device, Machine, Cell, Plant, Enterprise.
- ✅ **Higher levels SUPERSET the lower levels** — never the reverse.
- ✅ **Device Level** = sensors + actuators + relays + drives.
- ✅ **Machine Level** = standalone CNC, robot, AGV, AS/RS.
- ✅ **Cell Level** = multiple machines coordinated by a **cell controller** (FMC/FMS).
- ✅ **Plant Level** = entire factory orchestrated by **MES / SCADA**.
- ✅ **Enterprise Level** = business–manufacturing integration via **ERP / PLM / SCM**.
- ✅ **OEE = A × P × Q**, world-class threshold = **85%**.
- ✅ **Hard Automation** = fixed, high-volume, single product; **Flexible Automation** = programmable, multi-product, low changeover.
- ✅ Data flows **downward (commands)** and **upward (feedback)** — both directions must be explained.
- ✅ In KTU 14-mark answers, always include a **neat labelled diagram** — it carries independent marks.
- ✅ Numerical answers must show **all steps with units**, and end with a **comment / interpretation**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lean Production and Just-In-Time (JIT) Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT613 - COMPUTER INTEGRATED MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Lean Production and Just-In-Time (JIT) Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Lean Production — Formal Definition

**Lean Production** (also called *Lean Manufacturing* or simply *Lean*) is a systematic, socio-technical methodology for the elimination of waste (*Muda*) in manufacturing and service operations, while simultaneously maximizing customer value. It was crystallized from the Toyota Production System (TPS) and formally codified in the book *"The Machine That Changed the World"* (Womack, Jones & Roos, 1990). Lean focuses on creating value from the customer's perspective by optimizing flow, eliminating non-value-adding activities, and pursuing continuous improvement (*Kaizen*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Lock:** Lean Production = a manufacturing philosophy that aims to minimize waste, reduce cost, improve quality, and deliver exactly what the customer needs, when they need it, in the amount they need it.

### 1.2 Just-In-Time (JIT) — Formal Definition

**Just-In-Time (JIT)** is the cornerstone operational mechanism of Lean Production. It is a *pull-based* production strategy where materials, components, and sub-assemblies are delivered, produced, and moved through the production process **exactly at the time they are needed** — *not before, not after*. The objective is to achieve **zero inventory** (or as close to it as possible) by synchronizing supply with demand in real-time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** JIT is the *operational engine* of Lean. Lean is the *philosophy*; JIT is the *execution strategy*.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital Cafeteria Model"

Imagine a hospital cafeteria preparing lunch:
- **Old (Push) way:** The kitchen cooks 500 meals at 6 AM and pushes them to the counter. By 1 PM, 200 are cold and 50 are wasted.
- **Lean/JIT way:** A small "supply cart" rolls from the kitchen to the ward **only when a nurse signals a patient needs food**. The kitchen cooks that exact meal, refills, and returns.

**The lesson:** Don't build/cook/assemble anything until a real, verified demand signal (a *Kanban* — a card, signal, or empty bin) is received. This is the *pull* principle.

### 1.4 The Seven (and a Half) Wastes of Lean — *TIMWOODS*

Taiichi Ohno, the father of TPS, originally identified **seven wastes**; modern Lean adds an **8th — unused talent**:

| # | Waste (Japanese) | Meaning | Real-World Example |
|---|------------------|---------|--------------------|
| 1 | **T**ransport | Unnecessary movement of materials | Moving WIP across the factory 3 times |
| 2 | **I**nventory | Excess raw material, WIP, finished goods | Stacked boxes blocking aisles |
| 3 | **M**otion | Unnecessary movement of people | Worker bending/stretching repeatedly |
| 4 | **W**aiting | Idle time for people, machines, info | Machine halted for part delivery |
| 5 | **O**verproduction | Making more than demanded | Producing 1000 units when 800 ordered |
| 6 | **O**verprocessing | Doing more work than customer values | Polishing a surface that will be painted |
| 7 | **D**efects | Rework, scrap, warranty | Defective PCB returned by customer |
| 8 | **S**kill (unused talent) | Wasting people's ideas/abilities | Engineer assigned make-work tasks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU point:** *Overproduction* is considered the **mother of all wastes** because it causes all the other wastes (extra inventory → extra transport → extra waiting → extra motion).

### 1.5 The 5S Workplace Organization Methodology

Before JIT can work, the workplace must be organized using **5S** — a foundational Lean tool:

1. **Seiri (Sort)** — Remove unnecessary items.
2. **Seiton (Set in Order)** — A place for everything, everything in its place.
3. **Seiso (Shine)** — Clean the workplace.
4. **Seiketsu (Standardize)** — Establish standards for the first three S.
5. **Shitsuke (Sustain)** — Maintain discipline and the 5S culture.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Core Principles of Lean

Womack & Jones distilled Lean into **five principles** — *essential KTU board content*:

1. **Value** — Define value strictly from the customer's perspective. (Price + Quality + Delivery = value).
2. **Value Stream** — Map *all* steps (value-adding and non-value-adding) required to deliver the product, end-to-end.
3. **Flow** — Make the value-creating steps flow continuously without interruptions, batches, or backflows.
4. **Pull** — Let the customer pull the product; produce only what is demanded, when demanded.
5. **Perfection** — Pursue continuous improvement (*Kaizen*) to remove the next layer of waste — an endless loop.

### 2.2 The Seven Principles of Just-In-Time (JIT)

JIT operates on seven interconnected principles — *frequently asked in KTU 14-markers*:

1. **Zero inventory** — Inventory is a liability (storage, damage, capital tied up).
2. **Zero waiting** — Eliminate machine/operator idle time.
3. **Zero defects** — Quality at source (*Jidoka* / *Poka-Yoke*).
4. **Lot size of one** — Produce one unit at a time, move one unit at a time.
5. **Small-lot, frequent deliveries** — Daily or hourly replenishment.
6. **Pull system / Kanban** — Nothing moves without a demand signal.
7. **Supplier partnership** — Long-term contracts, few vendors, vendor-managed inventory.

### 2.3 Push vs. Pull System — The Critical Distinction

| Feature | **Push System (Traditional)** | **Pull System (JIT / Lean)** |
|---------|-------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Trigger | Forecast / schedule | Actual customer demand |
| Inventory | High WIP build-up | Low / zero WIP |
| Lead time | Long | Short |
| Information flow | MRP / ERP centrally | Kanban signals upstream |
| Flexibility | Low | High |
| Risk | Overproduction, obsolescence | Stockouts (mitigated by buffer) |
| Example | Batch-and-queue assembly line | Supermarket replenishment (re-order when shelf empties) |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Quantitative Lean Metrics

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $T = \dfrac{T_{avail}}{D}$ | **Takt Time** = Available production time ÷ Customer demand | sec/unit |
| 2 | $N = \dfrac{D \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)}{C}$ | **Number of Kanbans** (Little's Law variant) | cards |
| 3 | $C_{\text{optimal}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot D \cdot S}{H}}$ | **Economic Lot Size** (EOQ) — for comparison only | units |
| 4 | $LT = PT + W$ | **Lead Time** = Processing Time + Waiting | days |
| 5 | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | **Overall Equipment Effectiveness** (Availability × Performance × Quality) | % |
| 6 | $FPY = \dfrac{N_{\text{good}}}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | **First Pass Yield** | % |
| 7 | $VSM_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{VA}{LT}$ | **Value-Stream Efficiency** = Value-Added time ÷ Lead time | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-truncation note:** In the markdown above, the Kanban formula uses $D \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)$, **NOT** $D \cdot L \cdot \vert 1+S \vert$ — the $\vert$ symbol would break the table. Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ only when truly writing absolute value outside tables.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (Toyota, Ford):** Reduced WIP by 75%, factory space by 50%.
- **Healthcare:** Mayo Clinic used Lean to cut patient waiting time by 40%.
- **Aerospace (Boeing):** Lean assembly of the 777 — defects dropped to <1 per million.
- **Software & CIM:** Kanban is literally the model used in Agile/Scrum boards for engineering teams.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Takt Time Calculation

**Problem (KTU-typical):** A CNC cell works 8 hours per day, 2 shifts of 4 hours, with a 30-minute lunch and 2 × 10-minute breaks per shift. The daily customer demand is 240 units. Compute the Takt Time.

**Step 1 — Compute total available production time per day.**

$$
T_{\text{avail}} = (8 \times 60) - 30 - (2 \times 10) \text{ minutes}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Convert 8 hours to 480 minutes, subtract the 30-min lunch, then subtract two 10-min breaks (total 20 min).

$$
T_{\text{avail}} = 480 - 30 - 20 = 430 \text{ min/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Takt Time formula.**

$$
T = \dfrac{T_{\text{avail}}}{D} = \dfrac{430 \text{ min}}{240 \text{ units}}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to seconds for KTU machine-shop standard.**

$$
T = \dfrac{430 \times 60}{240} = \dfrac{25{,}800}{240} = 107.5 \text{ sec/unit}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Every **107.5 seconds**, one unit must roll off the line. If the actual *cycle time* of the bottleneck machine is > 107.5 s, the line cannot meet demand — a *Lean* signal to redesign or balance.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Number of Kanbans Required

**Problem (Classic KTU Numerical):** A workstation has demand $D = 50$ units/hour, average lead time $L = 2$ hours, container capacity $C = 25$ units, and a safety factor $S = 0.10$ (10%). Find the **number of Kanbans** $N$ required.

**Step 1 — State the Kanban formula (Little's Law for in-process inventory).**

$$
N = \dfrac{D \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)}{C}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Total demand during lead time ($D \cdot L$) accounts for the steady-state flow; the safety factor $(1+S)$ buffers against variability; divide by container capacity $C$ to get the number of containers (Kanbans).

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
N = \dfrac{50 \times 2 \times (1 + 0.10)}{25}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the numerator.**

$$
N = \dfrac{100 \times 1.10}{25} = \dfrac{110}{25} = 4.4
$$

**Step 4 — Round up to the next whole Kanban** (you cannot have a fractional card).

$$
N = 5 \text{ Kanbans}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's check:** If $N$ < 2, the system is starved. If $N$ > 10, the system has excess WIP — re-balance the line.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Value-Stream Efficiency (Lead-Time vs. Value-Added Time)

**Problem:** A part travels through 6 processes. Total **Lead Time** (door-to-door) is 4 days. Sum of value-added (VA) processing time across all 6 processes is 36 minutes. Calculate $VSM_{\text{eff}}$.

**Step 1 — Convert Lead Time to minutes for consistent units.**

$$
LT = 4 \text{ days} = 4 \times 24 \times 60 = 5760 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the VSM efficiency formula.**

$$
VSM_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{VA}{LT} = \dfrac{36}{5760}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to percentage.**

$$
VSM_{\text{eff}} = 0.00625 = 0.625\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Insight:** A typical factory's value-stream efficiency is **below 5%** — meaning >95% of the lead time is non-value-adding (waiting, transport, inspection). *Lean targets raising this metric.*

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of JIT vs. Traditional MRP

| Dimension | Traditional (Push / MRP) | Lean-JIT (Pull / Kanban) |
|-----------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| Inventory philosophy | Safety stock acceptable | Zero inventory ideal |
| Lot size | Large (economic) | Small (often = 1) |
| Setup time | Long accepted | Drastically reduced (SMED) |
| Quality | Inspect at end (*Jidoka* absent) | Build quality in (*Poka-yoke*) |
| Suppliers | Many, competitive bid | Few, partnership, JIS deliveries |
| Lead time | Long, accepted | Short, target metric |
| Workers | Specialization, siloed | Cross-trained, multi-skilled |
| Information system | MRP-II / ERP | Heijunka, visual Kanban |
| Cost of defect | High (rework at end) | Low (caught at source) |

### 3.5 The SMED Technique (Single-Minute Exchange of Die) — JIT Enabler

Shigeo Shingo's SMED reduces setup time from hours to **under 10 minutes** (hence "single-minute"). It is the *prerequisite* for small lots and JIT:

1. **Separate** internal setup (machine stopped) from external setup (machine running).
2. **Convert** internal steps to external.
3. **Streamline** remaining internal steps.
4. **Eliminate** adjustments.

**Quantitative impact:** If setup drops from 90 min → 9 min, economic lot size falls by a factor of 10, enabling one-piece flow.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Lean / JIT House (Functional Architecture)

The **Lean House** is the canonical TPS visual: a *roof* (goals), two *pillars* (JIT & Jidoka), and a *foundation* (5S, Heijunka, Kaizen).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    base5s["5S WORKPLACE FOUNDATION"]
    heij["HEIJUNKA LEVEL SCHEDULING"]
    kaiz["KAIZEN CONTINUOUS IMPROVEMENT"]
    jit["JUST IN TIME PILLAR"]
    jid["JIDOKA AUTOMATION WITH HUMAN TOUCH"]
    roof["GOALS ROOF: HIGHEST QUALITY LOWEST COST SHORTEST LEAD TIME HIGHEST SAFETY HIGHEST MORALE"]
    cust["CUSTOMER SATISFACTION"]

    base5s --> jit
    heij --> jit
    heij --> jid
    kaiz --> jit
    kaiz --> jid
    jit --> roof
    jid --> roof
    roof --> cust
```

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Kanban Pull-System Loop (Two-Card System)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sup["SUPERMARKET\nPREVIOUS PROCESS"]
    cur["CURRENT PROCESS\nASSEMBLY"]
    nxt["NEXT PROCESS\nDOWNSTREAM"]
    nextdemand{"DEMAND\nFROM NEXT"}
    outcard["WITHDRAWAL\nKANBAN C"]
    prodcard["PRODUCTION\nKANBAN P"]
    finish["FINISHED\nGOODS OUT"]

    nxt -->|empty bin signal| nextdemand
    nextdemand -->|W-card| sup
    sup -->|withdraws parts| cur
    cur -->|P-card sent back| sup
    sup -->|replenishes WIP bin| cur
    cur --> finish
    finish --> nxt
```

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Push vs. Pull Flow Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PUSH["PUSH SYSTEM FORECAST DRIVEN"]
        p1[RAW] --> p2[PROC 1] --> p3[PROC 2] --> p4[PROC 3] --> p5[FG WAREHOUSE] --> p6[CUSTOMER]
    end

    subgraph PULL["PULL SYSTEM DEMAND DRIVEN"]
        q1[RAW] -.->|no signal| q2[PROC 1]
        q2 -.->|no signal| q3[PROC 2]
        q3 -.->|no signal| q4[PROC 3]
        q4 --> q5[FINISHED TO ORDER]
        q5 --> q6[CUSTOMER]
        q6 -.->|order triggers upstream| q4
    end
```

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — PDCA / Kaizen Continuous Improvement Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    plan["PLAN\nIDENTIFY WASTE"]
    do["DO\nPILOT COUNTERMEASURE"]
    chk["CHECK\nVALIDATE RESULTS"]
    act["ACT\nSTANDARDIZE OR REVISE"]

    plan --> do
    do --> chk
    chk --> act
    act --> plan
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid for Mermaid diagrams above:** Every node ID uses the prefix-letter rule (e.g., `base5s`, `jit`, `sup`) — never a reserved word like `end` or `graph`. Labels are raw uppercase text without any `**bold**` or HTML tables inside double-quoted strings, ensuring clean compilation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define Just-In-Time (JIT) production. List any four essential elements of JIT.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for elements):**

**Definition:** *Just-In-Time (JIT) is a pull-based production philosophy that seeks to produce and deliver each item exactly when it is needed, in the exact quantity required, thereby eliminating all forms of waste — particularly inventory.*

**Four essential elements of JIT:**
1. **Pull system / Kanban** — production triggered by actual downstream demand.
2. **Small lot size / lot size of one** — minimize in-process inventory.
3. **Quick setups (SMED)** — enable small lots economically.
4. **Zero defects / Poka-yoke** — quality built-in at the source.

*(Alternative valid elements: vendor partnerships, level scheduling/Heijunka, continuous flow, 5S workplace.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*List the seven wastes (TIMWOODS) of Lean and briefly explain any one.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 for listing, 2 for explanation):**

The **seven wastes** of Lean are: **T**ransport, **I**nventory, **M**otion, **W**aiting, **O**verproduction, **O**verprocessing, and **D**efects.

**Explanation of *Overproduction*** (often considered the *mother* of all wastes): Overproduction means making more items than the next process or customer has demanded, or making them earlier than needed. It directly causes excessive inventory, additional transport, unnecessary motion in storage, and waiting for downstream processes — a single root cause that propagates into the other six wastes. *Example: producing 1000 components at the start of a week when only 500 are needed that day.*

*(Equally valid: explain any one of the other six wastes with example.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **Just-In-Time (JIT) production system**. Explain its principles, the pull mechanism using **Kanban**, and discuss its **advantages and limitations** in a modern manufacturing environment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A workstation operates for **7 hours/day** (no breaks). The average demand at the next station is **80 units/day**, lead time for production of a container is **1.5 hours**, container capacity is **20 units**, and a safety factor of **0.20 (20%)** is to be maintained. Calculate:
  (i) the **Takt Time**, and
  (ii) the **number of Kanbans** required. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Definition (1 mark):** JIT is a pull-based manufacturing strategy in which each operation produces only what is needed, when it is needed, and in the quantity needed by the next downstream operation or customer.

**Principles of JIT (2 marks — any 4):**
1. *Zero inventory* — inventory hides problems; eliminate it.
2. *Lot size of one* — produces one and moves one.
3. *Pull / Kanban* — demand-driven authorization.
4. *Quick setup* — SMED technique.
5. *Zero defects* — *Poka-yoke* mistake-proofing.
6. *Supplier partnerships* — JIS (Just-In-Sequence) delivery.
7. *Heijunka* — level production scheduling.

**Kanban mechanism (2 marks):** A *Kanban* (Japanese for "signal card") is a visual authorization to produce or withdraw. Two main types:
- *Withdrawal Kanban (W-card)* — signals withdrawal of parts from the previous process.
- *Production Kanban (P-card)* — authorizes the previous process to produce a given lot.
The cycle: downstream process consumes parts → W-card returned to upstream → upstream produces using the P-card → W-card triggers withdrawal → replenishment. **Number of Kanbans is strictly limited** to control WIP.

**Advantages (1 mark):** Reduced inventory, shorter lead times, lower working capital, improved quality, higher flexibility, better employee involvement.

**Limitations (1 mark):** Brittle to demand/supply disruptions, high initial setup investment, requires cultural change, less effective in low-volume/high-variety production, depends on highly reliable suppliers.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks (3 + 4):**

**(i) Takt Time (3 marks):**

$$
T_{\text{avail}} = 7 \text{ hr} = 7 \times 60 = 420 \text{ min/day}
$$

$$
T = \dfrac{T_{\text{avail}}}{D} = \dfrac{420}{80} = 5.25 \text{ min/unit} = 315 \text{ sec/unit}
$$

**Result:** $T = 315$ seconds per unit.

**(ii) Number of Kanbans (4 marks):**

Demand per hour: $D = 80/7 = 11.428$ units/hr. Lead time $L = 1.5$ hr. Container $C = 20$. Safety $S = 0.20$.

$$
N = \dfrac{D \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)}{C} = \dfrac{\frac{80}{7} \times 1.5 \times 1.20}{20}
$$

*Convert logic:* Compute numerator first, then divide.

$$
N = \dfrac{11.428 \times 1.5 \times 1.20}{20} = \dfrac{20.571}{20} = 1.0286
$$

$$
\boxed{N = 2 \text{ Kanbans (rounded up)}}
$$

**Interpretation:** A system with $N < 2$ signals starvation risk — 2 Kanbans is acceptable here.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **Lean Production philosophy** in detail. Discuss Womack and Jones' **five principles of Lean** and the role of **Kaizen** (continuous improvement) in implementing Lean. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing cell receives customer demand of **360 units/day** and operates for **6 hours/day** (with one 30-min break). The lead time is **3 hours**, safety factor **10%**, and container size **30 units**.
  (i) Calculate the **Takt Time**.
  (ii) Calculate the **number of Kanbans**.
  (iii) If the demand increases by **25%**, recalculate the number of Kanbans. Comment on the bottleneck. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Lean Production definition (1 mark):** A systematic methodology for waste elimination and value maximization, derived from the Toyota Production System, focused on creating customer-defined value with minimum resources.

**Five principles (3 marks — 1 mark each on any three, 0.5 each on others):**
1. **Value** — Specify value from the customer's standpoint (function, price, time).
2. **Value Stream** — Identify all steps in the value stream; separate value-adding from non-value-adding.
3. **Flow** — Make value-creating steps flow without interruption, batches, or waiting.
4. **Pull** — Let the customer *pull* the product; produce only on demand.
5. **Perfection** — Pursue perfection through *Kaizen*, removing successive layers of waste.

**Role of Kaizen (3 marks):** *Kaizen* (Japanese for "change for the better") is the engine of Lean's 5th principle — *Perfection*. It is a continuous, incremental, employee-driven improvement process. Key aspects:
- **Daily small improvements** rather than large, infrequent changes.
- **PDCA cycle** (Plan–Do–Check–Act) for structured problem-solving.
- **Gemba walks** — leaders go to the *actual workplace* to observe.
- **Empowers all employees** to suggest and implement improvements.
- **Eliminates root causes**, not just symptoms.
- **Cultural foundation** of Lean — without Kaizen, the system decays back to the old wasteful state.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Takt Time (2 marks):**

$$
T_{\text{avail}} = 6 \text{ hr} = 360 \text{ min} - 30 \text{ min} = 330 \text{ min/day}
$$

$$
T = \dfrac{330}{360} = 0.9167 \text{ min/unit} = 55 \text{ sec/unit}
$$

**(ii) Number of Kanbans (2 marks):**

$$
D = \dfrac{360}{6} = 60 \text{ units/hr}, \quad L = 3 \text{ hr}, \quad S = 0.10, \quad C = 30
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{60 \times 3 \times 1.10}{30} = \dfrac{198}{30} = 6.6 \Rightarrow N = 7 \text{ Kanbans}
$$

**(iii) After 25% demand increase (3 marks):**

New demand: $D_{\text{new}} = 60 \times 1.25 = 75$ units/hr.

$$
N_{\text{new}} = \dfrac{75 \times 3 \times 1.10}{30} = \dfrac{247.5}{30} = 8.25 \Rightarrow N_{\text{new}} = 9 \text{ Kanbans}
$$

**Bottleneck comment:** The 25% demand rise forces 2 extra Kanbans (28% more). If upstream process cannot meet the new pull rate (cycle time < new Takt of 44 sec), the line becomes *capacity-constrained*. Lean solutions: (a) reduce setup via SMED, (b) add a parallel workstation, (c) re-balance the line, (d) reduce container size $C$ to increase Kanban granularity.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the safety factor** $(1+S)$ — read the question carefully. If $S$ is not given, default to $S=0$.
> 2. **Rounding down** the number of Kanbans — *always* round **up**, because a partial Kanban cannot exist.
> 3. **Unit confusion** in Takt Time — the KTU expects **seconds/unit** in most numericals; show your conversion steps explicitly.
> 4. **Confusing Takt Time with Cycle Time** — *Takt* is the customer-driven rate; *Cycle* is the actual machine rate. Bottleneck occurs when **Cycle > Takt**.
> 5. **Skipping the lead-time vs. available-time conversion** — the most common 1-mark loss in part (b) numericals.
> 6. **Writing `|x|` in markdown tables** — for handwritten exams this is fine, but in our digital notes we deliberately use `\vert` to avoid markdown breaking.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- ✅ **Lean** = Philosophy (waste elimination, customer value). **JIT** = Operational mechanism (pull-based, zero inventory).
- ✅ **Seven wastes** = *TIMWOODS* (+ 8th: *Skill*). *Overproduction* is the **mother of all wastes**.
- ✅ **5S** = Sort, Set, Shine, Standardize, Sustain — the *foundation* of any Lean/JIT system.
- ✅ **5 Lean principles** = Value, Value Stream, Flow, Pull, Perfection (the *5th* is *Kaizen*).
- ✅ **7 JIT principles** = Zero inventory, zero waiting, zero defects, lot size of one, small-lot deliveries, pull/Kanban, supplier partnership.
- ✅ **Push system** = forecast-driven, MRP-based, high WIP. **Pull system** = demand-driven, Kanban-based, low WIP.
- ✅ **Two Kanban types** = *Withdrawal (W)* and *Production (P)*. Number of Kanbans = $D \cdot L \cdot (1+S) \div C$.
- ✅ **Takt Time** = $T_{\text{avail}} / D$ — sets the *pace*; the system must match it.
- ✅ **SMED** = prerequisite for small lots; Shigeo Shingo's setup-reduction methodology.
- ✅ **Poka-yoke** = mistake-proofing device (e.g., USB connector that fits only one way).
- ✅ **Jidoka** = automation with a human touch — stop the line on defect, fix root cause.
- ✅ **Heijunka** = level scheduling — smooth production volume and mix.
- ✅ **Kaizen** = continuous improvement, PDCA cycle, *Gemba*-based.
- ✅ **Value-Stream Efficiency** is almost always **< 5%** in real factories — Lean targets lifting it.
- ✅ **Kanban count must always be rounded UP** — never down.

---

*End of Module 1 — Lean Production and Just-In-Time (JIT) Principles | KTU 2024 Scheme Notes*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Planning and CAPP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT613 - COMPUTER INTEGRATED MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Process Planning and CAPP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Process Planning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Planning** is the systematic determination of the sequence of manufacturing operations and the corresponding process parameters required to transform a raw material into a finished, geometrically defined part, as specified in the engineering drawing.

In the context of **Computer Integrated Manufacturing (CIM)**, process planning serves as the critical bridge between the *design phase* (CAD) and the *production phase* (CAM). It is the function within a manufacturing system that establishes **what** operations are to be performed, **in what order**, using **which machines**, **which tools**, and under **what process conditions**.

> [!NOTE]
> According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, process planning is treated as a *planning subsystem* of CIM. It links **Product Design (CAD)** to **Manufacturing (CAM)** through a documented set of routings, fixturing schemes, and tooling instructions.

### 1.2 Computer-Aided Process Planning (CAPP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Computer-Aided Process Planning (CAPP)** is the use of computer technology to assist in the generation of detailed manufacturing process plans from engineering design data. It automates the decision-making logic that a human process planner would otherwise perform manually.

CAPP replaces the time-consuming, inconsistent, and experience-dependent manual planning with a **standardized, repeatable, and data-driven** digital workflow.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Kitchen Recipe**
> Think of process planning like writing a **recipe** for cooking a dish:
> - The **engineering drawing** is the *order ticket* from a customer (e.g., "Make a chocolate cake").
> - The **process plan** is the *recipe* (mix flour, sugar, eggs → bake at 180 °C for 30 min → decorate).
> - The **manufacturing shop** is the *kitchen* that executes the recipe.
> - A **manual process planner** is like an experienced chef who writes a new recipe every time from memory — fast but inconsistent.
> - A **CAPP system** is like a *smart digital cookbook* that:
>   * Stores standard recipes (**Group Technology / Variant CAPP**)
>   * Or intelligently invents a new recipe based on the ingredients and oven available (**Generative CAPP**).

This analogy immediately shows why CAPP matters: it brings **standardization, speed, and intelligence** to manufacturing recipe-making.

### 1.4 Why CAPP is Critical in CIM

| Aspect | Manual Process Planning | CAPP-Based Planning |
|---|---|---|
| **Consistency** | Varies with planner experience | Uniform across company |
| **Speed** | Hours to days per part | Minutes to seconds |
| **Integration** | Isolated paper document | Directly linked to CAD/CAM databases |
| **Cost** | High labor cost | High initial setup, low recurring cost |
| **Optimization** | Limited | Possible via algorithms |

The metric that defines the strategic value of CAPP is the **lead time reduction** in the design-to-production pipeline, which in industry benchmarks ranges from **30 % to 60 %**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Core Functions of Process Planning

The process planning function decomposes into the following logical steps. Each step answers a fundamental *manufacturing question*.

- **Interpretation of Engineering Drawing**
  * Why: The drawing encodes the geometric and tolerance intent.
  * How: Extract dimensions, GD&T, surface finish, material, and quantity.

- **Selection of Manufacturing Processes**
  * Why: The same shape can be made by multiple processes (e.g., a gear can be cast, forged, or machined).
  * How: Compare part features against process capability charts.

- **Determination of Operation Sequence**
  * Why: Order matters — you must face a surface before drilling it.
  * How: Apply precedence rules (e.g., roughing → semi-finishing → finishing).

- **Selection of Machines and Cutting Tools**
  * Why: Machines have specific capabilities (spindle speed, power, axis travel).
  * How: Match feature requirements with machine tool specifications.

- **Calculation of Process Parameters**
  * Why: Cutting speed, feed, and depth of cut determine quality and cost.
  * How: Use standard formulas and machinability data.

- **Fixture and Work-Holding Design**
  * Why: The part must be held rigidly for accurate machining.
  * How: Select from a library of standard fixtures or design new ones.

- **Time and Cost Estimation**
  * Why: Quoting, scheduling, and capacity planning depend on this.
  * How: Use standard time formulas (see below).

- **Routing Sheet Generation**
  * Why: This is the final deliverable to the shop floor.
  * How: Output a structured document listing operations, machines, tools, and time standards.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **High-Yield** for KTU numericals on process planning and CAPP. Memorize them.

**Table 1 — KTU High-Yield Process Planning Formulas**

| Formula | Equation | Variables | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cutting Speed** | $V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | $D$ = diameter, $N$ = spindle speed | m/min, mm, rpm | Turning, drilling |
| **Spindle Speed** | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$ | Derived from above | rpm | Tool selection |
| **Feed Rate** | $f_r = f \cdot N \cdot z$ | $f$ = feed/rev, $z$ = teeth | mm/min | Milling |
| **Material Removal Rate** | $MRR = f_r \cdot a_p \cdot a_e$ | $a_p$ = depth, $a_e$ = width | mm³/min | Productivity |
| **Machining Time** | $T_m = \dfrac{L}{f_r}$ | $L$ = feed length | min | Cycle time |
| **Setup Time** | $T_{su}$ | Empirically given | min | Pre-machining |
| **Total Operation Time** | $T_{op} = T_{su} + T_m + T_h$ | $T_h$ = handling | min | Process planning |
| **Cost per Part** | $C_p = C_m + C_l + C_o$ | $C_m$ = material, $C_l$ = labour, $C_o$ = overhead | ₹ | Costing |
| **Group Technology Code** | $GT_{code}$ (Opitz/MICLASS/DCLASS) | Hierarchical digit string | — | Variant CAPP |
| **CAPP Saving Ratio** | $S = \dfrac{T_{manual} - T_{CAPP}}{T_{manual}} \times 100\,\%$ | $T$ = planning time | % | Justification |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Syntax Rule:** Notice that the equation $V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ does **not** use absolute value bars `|x|`. If you see `|`, replace it with `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the markdown table.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

In modern **Industry 4.0 / Smart Manufacturing** environments, CAPP feeds **MES (Manufacturing Execution Systems)** and **ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning)** with structured routing data. The CAPP output becomes an input to:
- **MRP (Material Requirements Planning)**
- **Shop floor scheduling algorithms**
- **CNC part programming (post-processed G-code)**
- **Cost estimation for quotation**

Without CAPP, the integration of CAD → CAM is broken, and CIM remains a fragmented collection of isolated islands.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Step-by-Step Procedure of Process Planning

> [!NOTE]
> The following sequence is the *standard KTU expected answer* for the question: *"Explain the steps involved in process planning."*

**Step 1 — Receipt of Engineering Drawing and Bill of Materials**
The process planner receives the part drawing along with specifications, material, tolerance grade, surface roughness (Ra values), and annual production quantity. The drawing is the *single source of truth*.

**Step 2 — Analysis of the Part Geometry**
The part is decomposed into **manufacturing features**: holes, slots, pockets, threads, fillets, chamfers, and surfaces. Each feature is classified as:
- Rotational (turning, threading)
- Prismatic (milling, drilling)
- Free-form (5-axis machining)

**Step 3 — Selection of Rough Manufacturing Process**
A decision is made between:
- Casting
- Forging
- Powder metallurgy
- Sheet metal forming
- Direct machining from stock

The choice depends on **batch size, material, complexity, and cost**.

**Step 4 — Selection of Finishing Operations**
Operations like grinding, honing, lapping, and superfinishing are selected to meet tolerance and surface finish requirements. Typically:
- IT7 grade → Grinding
- IT6 grade → Honing or Lapping

**Step 5 — Operation Sequencing**
The chosen operations are ordered using **precedence constraints**:
- A facing operation must precede a turning operation on the same surface.
- Drilling must come after the part is faced flat.
- Heat treatment is inserted *between* rough and finish machining.

**Step 6 — Machine Tool Selection**
Machines are chosen from the available inventory based on:
- Maximum workpiece size
- Power rating
- Axis configuration (3-axis, 5-axis)
- Accuracy class

**Step 7 — Tool Selection and Process Parameter Determination**

Using the cutting speed formula, we calculate spindle speed:

$$
N = \frac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}
$$

Where:
- $V$ is the recommended cutting speed in m/min
- $D$ is the tool diameter in mm
- $N$ is the resulting spindle speed in rpm

The feed rate is then:

$$
f_r = f \cdot N \cdot z
$$

Where $f$ is feed per tooth (or per revolution) and $z$ is the number of effective teeth.

**Step 8 — Machining Time Calculation**

For a turning operation, the machining time is:

$$
T_m = \frac{L}{f_r}
$$

Where $L$ is the total feed travel, which equals the cut length plus approach and overtravel.

For a milling operation over a length $L$ with width $a_e$ and depth $a_p$:

$$
T_m = \frac{L}{f_r} + \frac{a_e}{f_r \cdot N \cdot z / 60} \quad \text{(multiplied passes)}
$$

**Step 9 — Cost Calculation**

$$
C_p = C_m + C_l + C_o
$$

Where $C_l = T_{op} \times \text{labour rate}$, and $C_o$ is overhead absorbed on the operation.

**Step 10 — Documentation and Route Sheet Generation**
The final output is a **route sheet** (also called a *process sheet* or *operation sheet*) that includes for each operation: machine, tool, fixture, parameters, time, and inspection requirements.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — CAPP Architecture Components

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU board frequently asks: *"Explain the architecture of a CAPP system."* The following matrix gives the complete answer.

**Table 2 — CAPP System Components**

| Block | Role in System | Data Type | KTU Keyword |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Input Module** | Receives CAD data (IGES/STEP files, drawings) | Geometry, tolerances, material | CAD Interface |
| **Part Classification** | Encodes the part into a GT code (Opitz, MICLASS) | Alphanumeric code | Group Technology |
| **Process Knowledge Base** | Stores standard processes, tooling, machines | Database tables | Knowledge Base |
| **Decision Logic / Inference Engine** | Applies IF-THEN rules or algorithms to generate plan | Rule-based / algorithmic | Logic Engine |
| **Process Plan Generator** | Synthesizes the final route sheet | Operations, sequence, parameters | Output Generator |
| **Output Module** | Produces route sheet, NC code, BOM updates | Documents, G-code | CAM Interface |
| **User Interface** | Allows planner to override or refine | GUI forms | Human-Machine Interface |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation of CAPP Planning Time Saving

Let the manual planning time per part be $T_{manual}$ and the CAPP planning time per part be $T_{CAPP}$.

$$
\Delta T = T_{manual} - T_{CAPP}
$$

$$
S = \frac{\Delta T}{T_{manual}} \times 100\,\%
$$

**Example calculation:**
If $T_{manual} = 120$ min and $T_{CAPP} = 12$ min:

$$
S = \frac{120 - 12}{120} \times 100 = 90\,\%
$$

This 90 % saving is typical in industry for **repetitive parts using Variant CAPP**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Process Planning Position in CIM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["CAD Module"] --> nodeB["Engineering Drawing"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Process Planning and CAPP"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Route Sheet and NC Code"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["CAM Module"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Shop Floor Manufacturing"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Quality Control and Inspection"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Finished Part"]

    nodeC -.feedback.-> nodeA

    classDef sysBlock fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    class nodeA,nodeC,nodeE sysBlock
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The flowchart shows the **CIM data flow** from design to finished part.
- **Process Planning (CAPP)** sits at the **center** — it is the pivot of CIM.
- The dotted feedback line shows that design changes feed back to update the process plan — this is the *closed-loop integration* expected in KTU answers.

### 4.2 Detailed CAPP Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph inputBlock["INPUT LAYER"]
        nodeA1["CAD Drawing Data"]
        nodeA2["Bill of Materials"]
        nodeA3["Production Quantity"]
    end

    subgraph coreBlock["CAPP CORE ENGINE"]
        nodeB1["Part Classification Module"]
        nodeB2["Group Technology Code Generator"]
        nodeB3["Process Knowledge Base"]
        nodeB4["Decision Logic and Inference Engine"]
        nodeB5["Process Plan Generator"]
    end

    subgraph outputBlock["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        nodeC1["Route Sheet"]
        nodeC2["NC Part Program"]
        nodeC3["Cost and Time Estimate"]
        nodeC4["Tool and Fixture List"]
    end

    nodeA1 --> nodeB1
    nodeA2 --> nodeB1
    nodeA3 --> nodeB1
    nodeB1 --> nodeB2
    nodeB2 --> nodeB4
    nodeB3 --> nodeB4
    nodeB4 --> nodeB5
    nodeB5 --> nodeC1
    nodeB5 --> nodeC2
    nodeB5 --> nodeC3
    nodeB5 --> nodeC4

    classDef inputStyle fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
    classDef coreStyle fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px
    classDef outputStyle fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    class nodeA1,nodeA2,nodeA3 inputStyle
    class nodeB1,nodeB2,nodeB3,nodeB4,nodeB5 coreStyle
    class nodeC1,nodeC2,nodeC3,nodeC4 outputStyle
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The three layers — **Input → Core → Output** — form the *standard CAPP architecture* answer expected in KTU.
- The **Process Knowledge Base** is the *heart* of the system; without it, the decision logic has nothing to reason over.
- All four outputs are generated in parallel by the **Process Plan Generator**.

### 4.3 Comparison: Variant CAPP vs Generative CAPP

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph variantBlock["VARIANT CAPP"]
        v1["New Part"] --> v2["Compute GT Code"]
        v2 --> v3["Search Existing Family Plan"]
        v3 --> v4{"Match Found?"}
        v4 -->|Yes| v5["Retrieve Standard Plan"]
        v4 -->|No| v6["Manual Modification"]
        v5 --> v7["Output Route Sheet"]
        v6 --> v7
    end

    subgraph generativeBlock["GENERATIVE CAPP"]
        g1["New Part"] --> g2["Extract Features"]
        g2 --> g3["Apply Decision Logic"]
        g3 --> g4["Synthesize Process"]
        g4 --> g5["Output Route Sheet"]
    end

    classDef varStyle fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef genStyle fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px
    class v1,v2,v3,v4,v5,v6,v7 varStyle
    class g1,g2,g3,g4,g5 genStyle
```

### 4.4 Detailed Variant CAPP Flow (Step-by-Step)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    step1["Start: New Part Drawing Received"] --> step2["Step 1: Analyse Part and Identify Features"]
    step2 --> step3["Step 2: Compute Group Technology Code using Opitz or MICLASS system"]
    step3 --> step4["Step 3: Search Process Database for Matching Family Code"]
    step4 --> step5{"Step 4: Match Found in Database?"}
    step5 -->|Yes| step6["Step 5: Retrieve Standard Process Plan for that Family"]
    step5 -->|No| step7["Step 6: Assign New Family Code and Create Plan Manually"]
    step6 --> step8["Step 7: Apply Manual Edits if Required"]
    step7 --> step8
    step8 --> step9["Step 8: Generate Route Sheet and NC Code"]
    step9 --> step10["Step 9: Store New Plan in Database for Future Reuse"]
    step10 --> step11["End: Output to Shop Floor"]

    classDef procStep fill:#fff0d6,stroke:#996600,stroke-width:1px
    class step1,step2,step3,step4,step5,step6,step7,step8,step9,step10,step11 procStep
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define process planning. List any four functions of process planning.*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Definition (1 mark):** Process planning is the systematic determination of the manufacturing operations, their sequence, machines, tools, fixtures, and process parameters required to convert a raw material into a finished part as per the engineering drawing.
> **Four Functions (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
> 1. Interpretation of the engineering drawing and specifications.
> 2. Selection of manufacturing processes (rough + finish).
> 3. Determination of operation sequence and machine tool selection.
> 4. Calculation of process parameters, machining time, and cost.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is CAPP? Differentiate between Variant and Generative CAPP (any four points).*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Definition (1 mark):** CAPP (Computer-Aided Process Planning) is the use of computer systems to generate detailed manufacturing process plans from engineering design data automatically or semi-automatically.
> **Difference (2 marks):**
>
> | Aspect | Variant CAPP | Generative CAPP |
> |---|---|---|
> | Basis | Group Technology family code | Feature-based logic |
> | Knowledge | Stored standard plans | Stored process knowledge + rules |
> | Output | Retrieved + edited plan | Synthesized new plan |
> | Complexity | Simple, low cost | Complex, high cost |
> | AI/Logic | No | Yes (rule-based / expert system) |

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students **lose 2 marks** by writing only the *definition* of CAPP and not the *architecture diagram*. Always include a labelled block diagram.
> - Students **lose 1 mark** by confusing *Group Technology (GT)* with *Variant CAPP* — GT is a *coding/classification technique*, Variant CAPP is a *system that uses GT*.
> - Students **lose 1 mark** by not mentioning the **Process Knowledge Base** as a separate component of the architecture.
> - In numericals, students **lose 1 mark** by writing units incorrectly (e.g., mixing rpm and rev/s).
> - Always **end with the route sheet structure** when asked about process planning output.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]*

**Q. (a)** Explain the different steps involved in process planning for a machined component. *(7 marks)*

**Q. (b)** Describe the architecture of a Computer-Aided Process Planning (CAPP) system with a neat block diagram. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Steps in Process Planning (7 marks):**

1. **Drawing Interpretation (1 mark):** The engineering drawing is studied to extract all geometric, dimensional, and tolerance data. The material, batch size, and surface finish requirements are noted.

2. **Geometric Analysis (1 mark):** The part is decomposed into individual *features* such as holes, slots, fillets, threads, and flat surfaces.

3. **Selection of Manufacturing Route (1 mark):** Based on batch size and complexity, decide between *casting/forging* (for raw shape) followed by machining, or *direct machining* from stock.

4. **Operation Sequencing (1 mark):** Determine the precedence — e.g., facing before turning, roughing before finishing, heat treatment between rough and finish.

5. **Machine and Tool Selection (1 mark):** Match feature requirements with available machines; choose cutting tools from the standard library.

6. **Process Parameter Calculation (1 mark):** Compute cutting speed $V$, spindle speed $N$, feed $f_r$, and machining time $T_m$ using standard formulas.

7. **Route Sheet Generation (1 mark):** Document the complete plan as a route sheet listing operation, machine, tool, fixture, parameters, and time for shop-floor use.

**(b) CAPP Architecture (7 marks):**

The CAPP system consists of three functional layers:

1. **Input Layer (2 marks):** Accepts CAD data (STEP/IGES files), engineering drawings, BOM, and production quantity.

2. **CAPP Core Engine (3 marks):** Comprises five modules:
   - Part classification and GT coding module
   - Process knowledge base (database of machines, tools, parameters)
   - Decision logic / inference engine (IF-THEN rules or algorithms)
   - Process plan generator (synthesizes the final plan)
   - User interface for planner override

3. **Output Layer (2 marks):** Produces the route sheet, NC part program, tool and fixture list, and cost-time estimate. The output is automatically fed to the CAM module.

*(Refer to the architecture diagram in SECTION 4.2.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]*

**Q. (a)** What is Group Technology? Explain the Opitz classification system with an example code. *(7 marks)*

**Q. (b)** Explain the Variant and Generative approaches of CAPP. List the advantages and limitations of each. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Group Technology and Opitz System (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Group Technology (GT) is a manufacturing philosophy in which similar parts are identified and grouped together to take advantage of their similarities in design and manufacturing.

**Opitz Classification (5 marks):**
The Opitz system is a hierarchical coding system using a 9-digit code, later extended to 12 digits. The first 9 digits are:

- **Digits 1–5:** Describe the *design* of the part (shape features).
  * Digit 1: External shape (rotational, prismatic, etc.)
  * Digit 2: Internal shape
  * Digit 3: Plane surfaces / threads
  * Digit 4: Hole patterns
  * Digit 5: Auxiliary features (grooves, chamfers)

- **Digits 6–9:** Describe the *manufacturing* (process sequence).
  * Digit 6: Principal operation
  * Digit 7: Secondary operation
  * Digit 8: Tertiary operation
  * Digit 9: Operation indicator

**Example (1 mark):** Code `0 1 3 0 1 – 2 1 0 0` means:
- Digit 1 = 0: Rotational part, length < 2× diameter
- Digit 1 = 1: One end has a hole
- Digit 1 = 3: Has plane surfaces
- … and so on.

**(b) Variant and Generative CAPP (7 marks):**

**Variant CAPP (3 marks):**
- Groups parts into families using GT codes.
- Retrieves a standard process plan for the family.
- The planner may edit the retrieved plan for the new part.
- *Advantages:* Simple, low cost, quick to implement.
- *Limitations:* Cannot create truly new plans; depends on existing families; large database needed.

**Generative CAPP (4 marks):**
- Uses feature recognition on the CAD model.
- Applies decision logic (IF-THEN rules, expert systems, or AI) to synthesize a new plan from scratch.
- *Advantages:* Creates optimized plans; handles new parts; no family code needed; supports NC programming.
- *Limitations:* Complex, expensive, requires large process knowledge base; difficult to develop and maintain.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Exam**

- ✅ **Process planning = design-to-production bridge.** Memorize this phrase.
- ✅ **CAPP** is the computerization of process planning.
- ✅ **Three CAPP types** — *Variant, Generative, Semi-generative (Hybrid)*. The KTU syllabus emphasizes the first two.
- ✅ **Variant CAPP uses Group Technology.** GT is a *coding system* (Opitz, MICLASS, DCLASS); not a CAPP type.
- ✅ **Opitz code is 9 digits (12 extended).** Digits 1–5 = design, 6–9 = manufacturing.
- ✅ **CAPP architecture = Input → Core → Output.** Always draw the three-layer block diagram.
- ✅ **Process Knowledge Base** is the heart of CAPP; without it, the inference engine cannot work.
- ✅ **Outputs of CAPP:** Route sheet, NC code, cost-time estimate, tool/fixture list.
- ✅ **Cutting speed formula** $V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ is the most-tested numerical.
- ✅ **Machining time** $T_m = \dfrac{L}{f_r}$ — must be in **minutes**, convert $f_r$ accordingly.
- ✅ **Generative CAPP uses AI / expert systems / decision logic.** Mention these keywords to score full marks.
- ✅ **Manual process planning disadvantages:** Inconsistent, slow, planner-dependent, not integrated with CAD/CAM.
- ✅ **CAPP benefits:** Standardization, faster lead time (30–60 % reduction), lower cost, CAD/CAM integration.
- ✅ **Terminology to use in answers:** *Route sheet, operation sequence, process parameters, GT code, knowledge base, inference engine*.
- ✅ **Always end a CAPP explanation with the closed-loop feedback to CAD** — this is a *bonus mark* trigger in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steps in Computer-Aided Process Planning
topics.content:
# Steps in Computer-Aided Process Planning (CAPP) ## Core Technical Definition > **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OERUT613 - COMPUTER INTEGRATED MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Steps in Computer-Aided Process Planning ### Formal Academic Definition **Computer-Aided Process Planning (CAPP)** is the systematic, computer-assisted procedure used to translate a finished part's design specifications into a detailed manufacturing process plan — specifying the operations, machines, tools, fixtures, parameters, and sequence required to produce the part economically and accurately. As defined by the **KTI (Kannappan & Irving Thompson Institute)** and adopted across the KTU 2024 syllabus: > *Process Planning is the technical and administrative function that determines the most efficient and economical sequence of manufacturing operations to convert a raw material into a finished product that meets the design specifications.* **CAPP** is the software-driven bridge between **CAD (Computer-Aided Design)** and **CAM (Computer-Aided Manufacturing)** in the CIM (Computer-Integrated Manufacturing) pyramid. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** > CAPP is positioned as the **central intelligence node** of CIM. Without CAPP, the flow CAD → CAM is broken because the *manufacturing knowledge* must be explicitly encoded. CAPP captures, stores, and reuses that knowledge via computers. --- ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are a **master chef** running a large industrial kitchen. Each customer places an order (a **design specification** — a part drawing). - **Old Method (Manual Process Planning):** The chef personally thinks through every recipe, picks utensils, sets oven temperature, decides order of cooking, and writes it down on paper. It takes time, and different chefs might produce different recipes for the same dish. - **CAPP Method (Computerized Recipe Engine):** The chef inputs the order into a **smart computer**. The computer already has a database of thousands of recipes (a **process knowledge base**). It searches, decides utensils, sets the temperature, sequences the steps, prints out a precise cooking card (the **route sheet**), and even suggests cheaper alternatives. **The "Recipe Card" in manufacturing is the Route Sheet** — a structured document listing every operation, machine, tool, fixture, parameter, and time required. > [!NOTE] > **Analogy Mapping to Manufacturing Terms** > > | Kitchen Analogy | Manufacturing Equivalent | > |-----------------|--------------------------| > | Customer order | Part drawing / CAD model | > | Recipe database | Process knowledge base | > | Recipe card | Route sheet / Process plan | > | Chef's experience | Manufacturing engineer's logic | > | Smart kitchen computer | CAPP software | > **Key Metric to Remember:** Modern CAPP systems can reduce process planning time from **hours to minutes** — typically an **80–90%** reduction in planning effort. --- ### Why CAPP is the Heart of CIM In the **CIM Wheel (Sackett, 1985)** architecture taught in KTU Module 1: $$\text{CAD} \;\longrightarrow\; \boxed{\text{CAPP}} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{CAM} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Production}$$ CAPP is the **only component** that captures *how* a part will be made — separating design intent from manufacturing reality. Without CAPP, **CAD/CAM integration is impossible** because there is no standardized bridge to convert geometry into operations. --- ### KTU-Relevant Typology of CAPP (Background for Module 2) Before diving into the steps, you must understand the **three CAPP approaches** because the steps differ slightly for each: 1. **Variant CAPP** — Uses a *standard process plan* from a previously classified similar part. 2. **Generative CAPP** — Synthesizes a new plan from logic, algorithms, and manufacturing rules (no prior plan needed). 3. **Semi-Generative (Hybrid) CAPP** — Combines both — uses standard plans but allows modification. > The **steps** below describe the **unified workflow** applicable to all three approaches. # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## Overview of the CAPP Workflow The complete **CAPP workflow** consists of **eight (8) sequential phases**. Each phase accepts inputs, processes them, and forwards outputs to the next phase. In KTU 2024 examinations, students are expected to draw the CAPP block diagram *with* all eight blocks and explain the data flow. | Step # | Phase Name | Purpose | |:------:|------------|---------| | **1** | Input Phase | Capture part design data from CAD | | **2** | Part Classification & Coding | Assign GT code to identify the family | | **3** | Process Selection | Choose manufacturing processes | | **4** | Operation Sequencing | Order the operations logically | | **5** | Machine & Tool Selection | Pick machines and cutting tools | | **6** | Process Parameter Determination | Set cutting speeds, feeds, depths | | **7** | Process Plan Optimization | Refine for cost/time | | **8** | Output Phase | Generate route sheet & NC programs | --- ## Step 1: Input Phase — Capturing Part Design Information ### What Goes Into CAPP? The CAPP module receives data from **CAD** in standard exchange formats such as **IGES**, **STEP (ISO 10303)**, **DXF**, or directly through a shared database. **Mandatory inputs:** - **Geometric data** — dimensions, tolerances, surface finishes - **Material specification** — grade, hardness, machinability rating - **Batch quantity** — affects process economics - **Existing facilities** — machines available in the shop - **Quality requirements** — inspection criteria ### Why This Step Matters > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Module 2 Critical Concept:** > The input phase is where **80% of CAPP failures originate.** Garbage-In = Garbage-Out. If CAD data is incomplete (e.g., missing tolerances), the generated process plan will be unusable. **Engineering Reality:** In modern integrated environments (e.g., **SolidWorks CAM**, **CATIA NC**, **Siemens NX**), CAD and CAPP share a **Product Data Management (PDM)** system — eliminating manual data entry. --- ## Step 2: Part Classification & Coding (Group Technology) This is the **most examinable step** in KTU Module 2. It uses **Group Technology (GT)** — the principle that **similar parts should be manufactured using similar processes**. ### What is a GT Code? A **GT code** is a numeric or alphanumeric string that describes a part's: - Shape features - Manufacturing attributes - Material - Size - Functional characteristics > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus-Mandated GT Coding Systems:** > > 1. **Opitz Code** — The most widely tested; **9-digit** classical form, extended to **12-digit** modern form. > 2. **MICLASS** (Mikrofilm Classification System) — Developed by Opel, Germany. Uses a hierarchical, multi-axis structure. > 3. **DCLASS** (D-Class Classification) — Developed at De Te We (Netherlands). > 4. **KK-3 Code** — Developed at IIT Bombay, India. > 5. **CODE System** — Used for components made by sheet-metal working. --- ### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet: Opitz Coding System The Opitz Code (developed by **Herbert Opitz, University of Aachen, 1960s**) is the **mandatory topic** for KTU. It encodes 5 digits in the front (form) and 4 digits in the back (auxiliary), with 3 more supplementary digits in the modern version. | Digit | Position | Encodes | Allowed Values | |:-----:|----------|---------|----------------| | **1** | Form code | General part class | 0–9 | | **2** | Form code | External shape (primary) | 0–9 | | **3** | Form code | Internal shape | 0–9 | | **4** | Form code | Planar surface features | 0–9 | | **5** | Form code | Auxiliary holes / bores | 0–9 | | **6** | Auxiliary | Major diameter (D, mm) | 0–9 | | **7** | Auxiliary | Length (L, mm) | 0–9 | | **8** | Auxiliary | Material | 0–9 | | **9** | Auxiliary | Original / raw form | 0–9 | | **10–12** | Supplementary | Tolerance, surface, batch | 0–9 each | --- ### Mathematical Formulation of Opitz Length Code (Digit 7) The length classification uses **geometric intervals** because lengths span wide ranges (mm to meters). The Opitz length code is defined by the **discretization function**: $$L_{code} \;=\; \left\lfloor \log_{2}\left(\frac{L_{actual}}{L_{min}}\right) \right\rfloor$$ where: - $L_{actual}$ = the true length of the part in **mm** - $L_{min}$ = the smallest length to be classified (typically **10 mm**) - $L_{code}$ = the resulting single digit (0–9) The digit is then clamped within the valid range: $$L_{code}^{final} \;=\; \max\!\left(0,\; \min\!\left(9,\; \left\lfloor \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{L}{10}\right) \right\rfloor\right)\right)$$ > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** > This logarithmic binning allows Opitz codes to handle parts from **10 mm bolts** to **10,000 mm turbine shafts** with only 10 length bins. This compact encoding is what makes Opitz practical for shop-floor GT families. --- ### MICLASS System (Brief, for 14-mark questions) | MICLASS Feature | Description | |-----------------|-------------| | Axis of classification | 4 (shape, function, material, machine) | | Hierarchical structure | Yes — primary, secondary, tertiary digits | | Code length | Variable (not fixed like Opitz) | | Origin | Opel AG, Germany | | Specialty | Handles complex prismatic + rotational parts | --- ## Step 3: Process Selection Based on the GT code, the CAPP system looks up the **part family** and selects appropriate manufacturing processes from its **process knowledge base**. **Decision logic (rule-based, in Generative CAPP):** $$\text{IF}\; (\text{GT}_{code} \in \text{Family}_{\text{rotational}})\; \text{THEN}\; \{\text{Turning}, \text{Drilling}, \text{Grinding}\}$$ $$\text{IF}\; (\text{GT}_{code} \in \text{Family}_{\text{prismatic}})\; \text{THEN}\; \{\text{Milling}, \text{Drilling}, \text{EDM}\}$$ The selected processes become the **candidate process pool** for sequencing. --- ## Step 4: Operation Sequencing The CAPP system now orders operations to satisfy: - **Technological constraints** — You cannot drill a hole before it is created on the workpiece. - **Dimensional accumulation** — Final tolerances must be respected. - **Datum hierarchy** — Establish datums first, then secondary, then tertiary features. **Common sequencing precedence (typical for a rotational part):** $$\text{Rough Turning} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Drilling} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Boring} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Finish Turning} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Grinding}$$ --- ## Step 5: Machine & Tool Selection Each operation must be paired with a **machine** (capable of the operation) and a **tool** (geometrically suitable). The CAPP algorithm queries a **machine database** and a **tool database**. **Selection rule (cost-driven):** $$C_{machining} \;=\; C_{machine} \cdot t_{machining} \;+\; C_{tool} \;+\; C_{fixture}$$ The system chooses the combination that **minimizes $C_{machining}$** subject to **tolerance feasibility** $\sigma_{achievable} \le \sigma_{spec}$. --- ## Step 6: Process Parameter Determination Cutting parameters must satisfy the **machining economics equation** (a KTU Module 1/2 favorite): $$v_{c} \;=\; \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$$ $$f_{r} \;=\; N \cdot n_{teeth} \cdot f_{z}$$ $$MRR \;=\; a_{p} \cdot v_{f} \cdot w$$ $$\text{Optimum cutting speed} \;\longrightarrow\; \frac{d(C_{total})}{d(v_{c})} \;=\; 0$$ where: - $v_c$ = cutting speed (m/min) - $D$ = workpiece diameter (mm) - $N$ = spindle speed (rpm) - $f_r$ = feed rate (mm/min) - $MRR$ = Metal Removal Rate (mm³/min) > **Tool Life Equation (Taylor's Tool Life Equation):** > > $$V \cdot T^{n} \;=\; C$$ > > where $T$ = tool life (min), $V$ = cutting speed, $n$ and $C$ are constants. --- ## Step 7: Process Plan Optimization Optimization objectives: - **Minimize total cost** $C_{total}$ - **Minimize total time** $T_{lead}$ - **Balance machine load** **Multi-criteria objective (weighted sum):** $$\min\; Z \;=\; w_1 \cdot \frac{C_{total}}{C_{max}} \;+\; w_2 \cdot \frac{T_{lead}}{T_{max}}$$ where $w_1 + w_2 = 1$, $w_1, w_2 \ge 0$. --- ## Step 8: Output Phase — The Route Sheet The final deliverable is the **Route Sheet** (also called **Process Plan Document**): | Operation # | Operation Name | Machine | Tool | Fixture | Parameters | Time (min) | |:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:| | 10 | Face milling | VMC | Face mill Ø80 | Vise | $v_c=120$, $f_z=0.1$ | 4.5 | | 20 | Drill 4×Ø10 | VMC | Twist drill | Jig fixture | $v_c=30$ | 2.0 | | 30 | Bore Ø20H7 | VMC | Boring bar | Jig fixture | $v_c=60$ | 5.0 | | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | This document drives the **shop floor** and feeds the **CAM** system. --- ## Summary of Engineering Utility | CAPP Benefit | Metric (Industry Avg.) | |--------------|------------------------| | Reduction in planning time | **60–90%** | | Increase in planner productivity | **5×** | | Improvement in plan consistency | **≈ 100%** | | Reduction in scrap due to planning errors | **30–50%** | | NC programming automation | **40–60%** | # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## Analytical Matrix: Comparison of GT Coding Systems This matrix is **frequently tested in KTU Module 2** as a Part B (14-mark) sub-question (a). | Parameter | **Opitz Code** | **MICLASS** | **DCLASS** | **KK-3** | |-----------|----------------|-------------|------------|----------| | Year | 1964 | 1972 | 1970 | 1978 | | Origin | Aachen, Germany | Opel, Germany | De Te We, Netherlands | IIT Bombay, India | | Number of digits | 9 (classical) / 12 (modern) | Variable, hierarchical | Variable | 21 digits | | Shape encoding | Yes (digits 1–5) | Yes (primary axis) | Yes | Yes | | Function encoding | Limited (digit 9) | Yes (secondary axis) | Limited | Yes | | Material encoding | Yes (digit 8) | Yes (third axis) | Yes | Yes | | Process encoding | No | Yes (fourth axis) | Limited | Yes | | Hierarchical | No (monolithic) | Yes | Yes | No | | Most suitable for | Rotational + prismatic | All part types | Sheet metal | Indian SMEs | | KTU exam frequency | **Very High** | Medium | Low | Low | --- ## Detailed Derivations ### Derivation 1: Opitz Code Length Digit (Digit 7) **Problem Statement (typical KTU 3-mark question):** A shaft has an actual length of $L = 640\;mm$. Determine the Opitz length code digit. **Solution Walkthrough:** The Opitz length code bins lengths using **powers of 2**, starting from $L_{min} = 10\;mm$. The binning formula is: $$L_{code} \;=\; \left\lfloor \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{L}{10}\right) \right\rfloor$$ Substituting $L = 640\;mm$: $$\frac{L}{10} \;=\; \frac{640}{10} \;=\; 64$$ $$\log_{2}(64) \;=\; \log_{2}(2^{6}) \;=\; 6$$ $$\left\lfloor 6 \right\rfloor \;=\; 6$$ Therefore, **the Opitz length digit is $L_{code} = 6$**. **Cross-verification using Opitz bin table:** | Bin # | Length Range (mm) | Geometric Reasoning | |:-----:|:------------------|---------------------| | 0 | $\le 20$ | $10 \le L < 20 = 10 \cdot 2^1$ | | 1 | $20 \le L < 40$ | $10 \cdot 2^1 \le L < 10 \cdot 2^2$ | | 2 | $40 \le L < 80$ | $10 \cdot 2^2 \le L < 10 \cdot 2^3$ | | 3 | $80 \le L < 160$ | $10 \cdot 2^3 \le L < 10 \cdot 2^4$ | | 4 | $160 \le L < 320$ | $10 \cdot 2^4 \le L < 10 \cdot 2^5$ | | 5 | $320 \le L < 640$ | $10 \cdot 2^5 \le L < 10 \cdot 2^6$ | | **6** | **$640 \le L < 1280$** | **$10 \cdot 2^6 \le L < 10 \cdot 2^7$** | | 7 | $1280 \le L < 2560$ | $10 \cdot 2^7 \le L < 10 \cdot 2^8$ | | 8 | $2560 \le L < 5120$ | $10 \cdot 2^8 \le L < 10 \cdot 2^9$ | | 9 | $\ge 5120$ | $L \ge 10 \cdot 2^9$ | Since $640\;mm$ is the **lower bound of bin 6**, the assignment $L_{code} = 6$ is confirmed. --- ### Derivation 2: Opitz Code Diameter Digit (Digit 6) The Opitz diameter code uses a **similar logarithmic discretization** but based on the **maximum diameter $D$** of the part: $$D_{code} \;=\; \max\!\left(0,\; \min\!\left(9,\; \left\lfloor \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{D}{3.17}\right) \right\rfloor\right)\right)$$ where $D$ is the largest external diameter in **mm** and $3.17\;mm$ is the reference baseline. **Example:** For $D = 25.4\;mm$: $$\frac{D}{3.17} \;=\; \frac{25.4}{3.17} \;\approx\; 8.0$$ $$\log_{2}(8.0) \;=\; \log_{2}(2^{3}) \;=\; 3$$ Therefore, **the Opitz diameter digit is $D_{code} = 3$**. --- ### Derivation 3: Opitz Material Digit (Digit 8) | Material Code | Material | |:-------------:|----------| | 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 | Various steels | | 5 | Copper alloys | | 6 | Light alloys (Al, Mg) | | 7 | Other non-ferrous metals | | 8 | Plastics / polymers | | 9 | Special / non-metallics | **KTU Tip:** Memorize the **steel codes 0–4** and **non-metal codes 8–9** because these appear in 90% of university questions. --- ### Derivative 4: Cutting Speed from Spindle Speed Given: - $D = 100\;mm$ (workpiece diameter) - $N = 600\;rpm$ (spindle speed) - $f_z = 0.15\;mm/tooth$ (feed per tooth) - $n_{teeth} = 4$ (number of teeth on cutter) **Step (a): Compute cutting speed $v_c$.** $$v_{c} \;=\; \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$$ $$v_{c} \;=\; \frac{\pi \cdot 100 \cdot 600}{1000} \;=\; \frac{60000 \cdot \pi}{1000} \;=\; 60\pi$$ $$v_{c} \;\approx\; 188.5\;\text{m/min}$$ **Step (b): Compute feed rate $v_f$.** $$v_{f} \;=\; N \cdot n_{teeth} \cdot f_z$$ $$v_{f} \;=\; 600 \cdot 4 \cdot 0.15 \;=\; 360\;\text{mm/min}$$ **Step (c): Compute Metal Removal Rate (MRR).** Given $a_p = 2\;mm$ (depth of cut) and $w = 80\;mm$ (width of cut): $$MRR \;=\; a_{p} \cdot v_{f} \cdot w$$ $$MRR \;=\; 2 \cdot 360 \cdot 80 \;=\; 57600\;\text{mm}^{3}\text{/min}$$ **Step (d): Tool life via Taylor's equation.** Given $n = 0.25$ and $C = 300$: $$V \cdot T^{n} \;=\; C$$ $$188.5 \cdot T^{0.25} \;=\; 300$$ $$T^{0.25} \;=\; \frac{300}{188.5} \;\approx\; 1.591$$ $$T \;=\; 1.591^{4} \;\approx\; 6.40\;\text{min}$$ > **Engineering interpretation:** A cutting speed of $188.5\;m/min$ on this cutter gives only **~6.4 minutes of tool life**, indicating an aggressive machining regime. --- ### Derivative 5: Total Cost Minimization Total cost per unit: $$C_{total} \;=\; C_{machining} \;+\; C_{tooling} \;+\; C_{setup} \;+\; C_{overhead}$$ Using the standard machining economics formulation: $$C_{unit} \;=\; C_o \cdot t_{mach} \;+\; C_t \cdot \frac{t_{mach}}{T} \;+\; \frac{C_{tool}}{n_{parts}}$$ where: - $C_o$ = operator + machine rate (₹/min) - $C_t$ = tool cost per regrind (₹/regrind) - $T$ = tool life (min) - $n_{parts}$ = parts per tool life Taking $\dfrac{dC_{unit}}{d v_c} = 0$ yields the **optimum cutting speed**: $$v_{c,opt} \;=\; \frac{C}{[n(1-n)]^{\,n} \cdot \left(\dfrac{t_m C_t}{t_{ct} C_o}\right)^{\,n}}$$ where $t_{ct}$ is tool change time. This is the **classical Gilbert's formula** that a Generative CAPP system embeds in its algorithm. # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## Diagram 1: CAPP Workflow — Block-Level Functional Architecture The diagram below maps the **eight sequential steps** of CAPP, showing the data flow, decision points, and feedback loops. This is a **board-favorite 7-mark diagram** for KTU Module 2. ```mermaid flowchart TD start([CAD Part Model / Drawing]) --> step1[Step 1 INPUT PHASE
Capture geometry material tolerances batch quantity] step1 --> step2[Step 2 PART CLASSIFICATION AND CODING
Assign GT Code Opitz MICLASS KK-3] step2 --> step3[Step 3 PROCESS SELECTION
Lookup process knowledge base] step3 --> step4[Step 4 OPERATION SEQUENCING
Apply technological precedence rules] step4 --> step5[Step 5 MACHINE AND TOOL SELECTION
Query machine database and tool database] step5 --> step6[Step 6 PROCESS PARAMETER DETERMINATION
Cutting speed feed depth tool life] step6 --> step7[Step 7 PROCESS PLAN OPTIMIZATION
Minimize cost and lead time] step7 --> step8[Step 8 OUTPUT PHASE
Generate Route Sheet and NC Program] step8 --> endOut([Route Sheet dispatched to SHOP FLOOR and CAM]) step2 -. Check family .-> check1{Family already
in database?} check1 -->|YES| stepA[Retrieve standard plan
VARIANT approach] check1 -->|NO| stepB[Synthesize new plan
