Feedback from shop .-> step1 style start fill:#FFD580,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style endOut fill:#80FF80,stroke:#008000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style step1 fill:#E0E0FF,stroke:#000080 style step2 fill:#E0E0FF,stroke:#000080 style step3 fill:#E0E0FF,stroke:#000080 style step4 fill:#E0E0FF,stroke:#000080 style step5 fill:#E0E0FF,stroke:#000080 style step6 fill:#E0E0FF,stroke:#000080 style step7 fill:#FFE0E0,stroke:#800000 style step8 fill:#80FFFF,stroke:#006666 style check1 fill:#FFFF80,stroke:#808000 ``` --- ## Diagram 2: CAPP Position in CIM — Pyramid Architecture This diagram shows CAPP as the **central node** connecting CAD, CAM, and Production. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CIM_PYRAMID[COMPUTER INTEGRATED MANUFACTURING ARCHITECTURE] L4[LEVEL 4 - Enterprise Resource Planning ERP] L3[LEVEL 3 - Manufacturing Execution System MES] L2[LEVEL 2 - Shop Floor Control SFC] L1[LEVEL 1 - Direct Process Control CNC DNC] end CAD[CAD
Design] -- Design Data --> CAPP CAPP[FA CAPP COMPUTER AIDED PROCESS PLANNING FA
8 Step Workflow] -- Process Plan --> CAM CAM[CAM
NC Programming] -- NC Code --> SFC SFC[Shop Floor Production] -- Feedback Data --> CAPP subgraph CAPP_INTERNAL[Inside CAPP Module] KM[Knowledge Base
Process Rules Part Families] DB[(Manufacturing Database
Machines Tools Materials)] ALG[Process Generation
Algorithms] end CAPP --> KM CAPP --> DB CAPP --> ALG style CAPP fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF style CAD fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#006666,color:#000 style CAM fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#006666,color:#000 style SFC fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#006666,color:#000 style L4 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000 style L3 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000 style L2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000 style L1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000 ``` --- ## Diagram 3: Variant vs Generative CAPP — Logic Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph VARIANT[VARIANT CAPP] V1[Part Family F] --> V2[Existing Standard Plan P F] V2 --> V3[Edit P F to P New] end subgraph GENERATIVE[GENERATIVE CAPP] G1[Part Description] --> G2[Process Logic
Rule Based AI Based] G2 --> G3[Synthesize Plan P New] end VARIANT --> outV((Plan Output)) GENERATIVE --> outV style V1 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B style V2 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B style V3 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B style G1 fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#8B4513 style G2 fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#8B4513 style G3 fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#8B4513 style outV fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ## Diagram 4: Opitz Code Structure ```mermaid flowchart LR A[DIGIT 1
General Class] --> B[DIGIT 2
External Shape] B --> C[DIGIT 3
Internal Shape] C --> D[DIGIT 4
Planar Surfaces] D --> E[DIGIT 5
Auxiliary Holes] E --> F[DIGIT 6
Diameter] F --> G[DIGIT 7
Length] G --> H[DIGIT 8
Material] H --> I[DIGIT 9
Raw Form] I --> J[DIGIT 10
Tolerance] J --> K[DIGIT 11
Surface Finish] K --> L[DIGIT 12
Batch Size] style A fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000 style B fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000 style C fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000 style D fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000 style E fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000 style F fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000 style G fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000 style H fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style I fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style J fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000 style K fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000 style L fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000 ``` > The **5 leftmost digits** (orange) encode **Form** — the part's geometry. The **4 middle digits** (blue + green) encode **Auxiliary** information — size, material, raw form. The **3 rightmost digits** (purple) are **Supplementary** — precision metrics. # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Process Planning. List any four major steps in CAPP. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]** **Model Answer:** **Process Planning** is the engineering function that determines the procedures, machines, tools, parameters, and operations required to transform raw material into a finished part that meets the design specifications. **Four Major Steps in CAPP:** 1. **Input Phase** — Capturing part design data (geometry, material, tolerances, batch) from CAD. 2. **Part Classification & Coding** — Assigning a GT (Group Technology) code to identify the part family using systems such as Opitz or MICLASS. 3. **Process Selection & Operation Sequencing** — Choosing the manufacturing processes and ordering them based on technological precedence. 4. **Output Phase** — Generating the route sheet and transmitting it to the shop floor and CAM system. --- ### Question 2: What is the difference between Variant CAPP and Generative CAPP? **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | **Variant CAPP** | **Generative CAPP** | |--------|------------------|---------------------| | Source of plan | Retrieves from existing standard plans of similar parts | Synthesizes a new plan from logic and algorithms | | Knowledge needed | Part family classification only | Complete manufacturing rules + AI/logic | | Speed | Faster (lookup-based) | Slower (computation-heavy) | | Flexibility | Limited to known families | Handles any new part | | Implementation difficulty | Easy | Complex | | KTU example | OPITZ-coded families + standard plans | Expert system using if-then rules | **One-line memory hook:** *Variant = "find and edit"; Generative = "create from scratch".* --- ## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **INTERNAL CHOICE:** KTU allows the student to answer either **Question A** *or* **Question B**. --- ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) #### (a) Explain the eight major steps in Computer-Aided Process Planning with a neat block diagram. **[7 Marks]** #### (b) Describe the Opitz classification system in detail. How are the diameter and length digits determined? Illustrate with an example. **[7 Marks]** --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) #### (a) Discuss in detail the different approaches to CAPP — Variant, Generative, and Semi-Generative. Compare their merits and demerits. **[7 Marks]** #### (b) A shaft has the following specifications: - Maximum diameter $D = 50.8\;mm$ - Length $L = 1280\;mm$ - Material = Mild Steel - Material code = 0 **Compute the Opitz diameter and length digits. Hence write the partial Opitz code (digits 6, 7, 8) and state its engineering significance.** **[7 Marks]** --- ## Detailed Model Solutions ### Question A — Part (a) Model Solution: 8 Steps in CAPP The eight sequential steps of Computer-Aided Process Planning are: **Step 1: Input Phase** The CAPP module receives part design information from the **CAD system**. Data includes geometric dimensions, tolerances, surface finish requirements, material specification, batch quantity, and available machines in the shop floor. Exchange formats: **STEP, IGES, DXF**. **Step 2: Part Classification & Coding** The part is analyzed and assigned a **Group Technology (GT) code** using systems such as **Opitz, MICLASS, KK-3, DCLASS**. This code uniquely identifies the part family. **Step 3: Process Selection** Based on the GT code, candidate manufacturing processes are retrieved from the **process knowledge base**. Example: A rotational part family → turning, drilling, grinding. **Step 4: Operation Sequencing** The selected processes are arranged in a **technologically valid sequence** respecting datum hierarchy and tolerance accumulation. **Step 5: Machine & Tool Selection** For each operation, the system selects the **most suitable machine and cutting tool** from its database, minimizing cost while meeting tolerance. **Step 6: Process Parameter Determination** **Cutting speed ($v_c$), feed rate ($f_r$), depth of cut ($a_p$), spindle speed ($N$)** are computed using machining equations (Taylor's tool life, etc.). **Step 7: Process Plan Optimization** The plan is refined to **minimize total cost and lead time**, often using weighted multi-objective optimization. **Step 8: Output Phase** The final **route sheet** is printed and dispatched to the shop floor; **NC programs** are generated for CAM execution. > A flow diagram should accompany this answer with **8 sequential blocks**, **feedback loops**, and a **knowledge base** attached to the CAPP core. *(See Diagram 1 in Section 4.)* --- ### Question A — Part (b) Model Solution: Opitz Code with Worked Example **The Opitz Classification System** was developed by **Herbert Opitz at the Technical University of Aachen, Germany, in 1964.** It encodes each part using a **9-digit classical code** or a **12-digit modern code**. **Structure of the 9-Digit Opitz Code:** - **Digits 1–5: Form Code** — describes shape features. - Digit 1: General part class (rotational, prismatic, etc.) - Digit 2: External shape (primary form features) - Digit 3: Internal shape (holes, cavities) - Digit 4: Planar surfaces (slots, faces) - Digit 5: Auxiliary holes / bores - **Digits 6–9: Auxiliary Code** — describes size and material. - Digit 6: Diameter - Digit 7: Length - Digit 8: Material - Digit 9: Original raw form (bar, casting, forging) **Supplementary Digits 10–12** carry tolerance, surface finish, and batch size information. **Diameter Digit Formula:** $$D_{code} \;=\; \left\lfloor \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{D}{3.17}\right) \right\rfloor$$ where $D$ is in mm and $3.17$ mm is the reference baseline. The result is clamped to the valid range [0, 9]. **Length Digit Formula:** $$L_{code} \;=\; \left\lfloor \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{L}{10}\right) \right\rfloor$$ where $L$ is in mm and $10$ mm is the reference baseline. The result is clamped to the valid range [0, 9]. **Worked Example:** For a shaft with $D = 50.8\;mm$ and $L = 1280\;mm$: $$D_{code} \;=\; \left\lfloor \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{50.8}{3.17}\right) \right\rfloor \;=\; \left\lfloor \log_{2}(16) \right\rfloor \;=\; \left\lfloor 4 \right\rfloor \;=\; 4$$ $$L_{code} \;=\; \left\lfloor \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{1280}{10}\right) \right\rfloor \;=\; \left\lfloor \log_{2}(128) \right\rfloor \;=\; \left\lfloor 7 \right\rfloor \;=\; 7$$ **Engineering Significance:** - Opitz codes enable **part family identification** for group technology. - They provide a **compact numeric identity** for hundreds of thousands of parts. - They drive **variant CAPP** by linking similar parts to a shared standard plan. - They reduce **planning time, tooling redundancy, and inventory.** --- ### Question B — Part (a) Model Solution: Comparison of CAPP Approaches | Feature | **Variant CAPP** | **Generative CAPP** | **Semi-Generative CAPP** | |---------|------------------|---------------------|--------------------------| | Origin / concept | GT-family + standard plans | Logic / AI synthesis | Hybrid (variant + generative) | | Knowledge required | Part families, standard plans | Complete process rules, AI logic | Partial knowledge | | Plan generation speed | Fast (lookup) | Slow (compute) | Medium | | Flexibility to new parts | Limited | Very high | Medium | | Implementation cost | Low | Very high | Medium | | Suitable for | Repetitive parts in known families | Complex one-off parts | Mixed production | | Example system | CAM-I CAPP, DCLASS | MIPLAN, XCAPP | Custom in-house systems | | Maintenance effort | Medium | High | Medium-High | | KTU exam weight | **High** | **Very High** | Medium | **Engineering Insight:** - **Variant** is the *evolutionary first step* for a CIM company migrating from manual planning. - **Generative** is the *ideal end-state* — fully automated, AI-driven, expert-rule based. - **Semi-Generative** is the *practical middle ground* adopted by most Indian SMEs (and is what KTU expects students to design in lab courses). --- ### Question B — Part (b) Model Solution: Opitz Calculation for a Shaft **Given Data:** - Maximum diameter $D = 50.8\;mm$ - Length $L = 1280\;mm$ - Material = Mild Steel with material code = 0 **Required:** Compute Opitz diameter and length digits. Write the partial Opitz code (digits 6, 7, 8). State its engineering significance. --- **Step 1: Compute Diameter Digit (Digit 6)** Using the Opitz diameter formula: $$D_{code} \;=\; \left\lfloor \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{D}{3.17}\right) \right\rfloor$$ $$\frac{D}{3.17} \;=\; \frac{50.8}{3.17}$$ $$\frac{D}{3.17} \;\approx\; 16.025$$ $$\log_{2}(16.025) \;\approx\; \log_{2}(2^{4}) \;\approx\; 4.00$$ $$\left\lfloor 4.00 \right\rfloor \;=\; 4$$ **Therefore, Diameter Digit = 4.** --- **Step 2: Compute Length Digit (Digit 7)** Using the Opitz length formula: $$L_{code} \;=\; \left\lfloor \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{L}{10}\right) \right\rfloor$$ $$\frac{L}{10} \;=\; \frac{1280}{10} \;=\; 128$$ $$\log_{2}(128) \;=\; \log_{2}(2^{7}) \;=\; 7$$ $$\left\lfloor 7 \right\rfloor \;=\; 7$$ **Therefore, Length Digit = 7.** --- **Step 3: Material Digit (Digit 8)** Material = Mild Steel → Opitz material code = **0**. --- **Step 4: Write the Partial Opitz Code** The partial code for digits 6, 7, 8 is: $$\boxed{\;\text{Opitz partial code (D, L, Mat)} \;=\; \mathbf{4 \; 7 \; 0}\;}$$ The full 9-digit code might look like: $$\mathbf{0 \; 1 \; 1 \; 0 \; 0 \; 4 \; 7 \; 0 \; 2}$$ (if we assume a rotational stepped shaft with internal bore from bar stock — for example). --- **Step 5: Engineering Significance** 1. **Part Family Identification:** Code **4-7-0** places this shaft in the *large shaft family* (diameter bin 4 covers roughly 51–101 mm; length bin 7 covers 1280–2559 mm) of **mild-steel components**. 2. **Standard Plan Retrieval:** A **Variant CAPP** system will retrieve the standard route sheet for family 4-7-0 — typically: *Saw → Lathe (rough + finish) → Drill → Bore → Keyway mill → Grind → Inspect.* 3. **Tool & Fixture Selection:** The system will auto-select machines with spindle bores ≥ $\sim 80\;mm$ and tool holders sized for large-diameter tooling. 4. **Cost & Time Estimation:** Routing through known family data enables rapid **cost estimation** for quotation. 5. **Inventory Benefits:** Standard tooling for family 4-7-0 is kept ready — reducing **setup time**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to clamp** the bin number to the range [0, 9]. If a student calculates $\log_2$ > 9, they must clamp to 9. KTU examiners **deduct 1 mark** if the clamp step is missing. > 2. **Using the wrong reference baseline** — diameter uses $3.17$ mm but length uses $10$ mm. Confusing these two is the most common error and costs **2 marks**. > 3. **Forgetting units** — the diameter $D$ and length $L$ must be in mm before applying the formula. If $D$ is mistakenly entered in inches, the answer will be off by 25.4 and **all subsequent marks are lost**. > 4. **Not stating engineering significance** — even if the calculation is correct, the model answer requires a brief paragraph on "what the code means" to score full marks. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 Exam Day:** - [x] **CAPP = bridge between CAD and CAM** in the CIM architecture. - [x] CAPP converts **part design data** into a **manufacturing route sheet**. - [x] **Eight steps** of CAPP: Input → Classification & Coding → Process Selection → Operation Sequencing → Machine & Tool Selection → Parameter Determination → Optimization → Output. - [x] **Variant CAPP** = lookup + edit; **Generative CAPP** = synthesize from rules; **Semi-Generative** = hybrid. - [x] **GT (Group Technology)** is the foundation of CAPP — "similar parts need similar plans." - [x] **Opitz Code** has **9 classical digits** (5 form + 4 auxiliary) and **3 supplementary digits** (tolerance, surface, batch). - [x] **Opitz Diameter formula:** $D_{code} = \lfloor \log_2(D / 3.17) \rfloor$ — reference baseline = **3.17 mm**. - [x] **Opitz Length formula:** $L_{code} = \lfloor \log_2(L / 10) \rfloor$ — reference baseline = **10 mm**. - [x] Both digits must be **clamped to [0, 9]** before writing the final code. - [x] **Material code 0** = steel, **5** = copper, **6** = light alloys, **8** = plastics. - [x] **MICLASS** is hierarchical, **DCLASS** is Dutch, **KK-3** is Indian (IIT Bombay). - [x] **Cutting speed** $v_c = \pi D N / 1000$ (m/min), **feed rate** $v_f = N \cdot n_t \cdot f_z$. - [x] **MRR** = $a_p \cdot v_f \cdot w$. - [x] **Taylor's Tool Life:** $V \cdot T^n = C$ — recall that **optimum $v_c$** comes from $dC_{total} / dv_c = 0$. - [x] CAPP reduces planning time by **60–90%** — a frequent 1-mark "bonus" answer. - [x] Modern CAD/CAM suites (**Siemens NX, CATIA, SolidWorks CAM**) integrate CAD + CAPP + CAM under one PDM shell. > **One-Line Memory Hook for the Exam:** > *"**I**nput → **C**ode → **P**rocess → **S**equence → **M**achine → **P**arameters → **O**ptimize → **O**utput = **ICPSMPOO** — read as **I Can Plan Smart Manufacturing Process Orders!**"*

topics.title: Benefits and Implementation of CAPP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT613 - COMPUTER INTEGRATED MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Benefits and Implementation of CAPP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Computer-Aided Process Planning (CAPP)** is the use of computer technology to assist process planners in developing detailed manufacturing process plans for converting a designed part (CAD geometry and specifications) into a finished product. When an organisation strategically deploys CAPP across its production engineering functions and re-engineers the planning workflow around it, the activity is referred to as **CAPP Implementation**.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for CIM (OERUT613), the implementation of CAPP bridges the critical information gap between **CAD (design domain)** and **CAM (manufacturing domain)** by computerising the selection of machines, tools, fixtures, sequencing of operations, determination of cutting parameters, and calculation of cycle times.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Under the KTU 2024 OBE framework, students must be able to **evaluate the benefits of CAPP** and **describe the systematic implementation strategy** of a CAPP system in a manufacturing organisation. This maps to **CO2 (Module 2)** of the OERUT613 course.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Smart Kitchen Recipe Generator"**
>
> Imagine you are a head chef in a large hotel kitchen. Every day, hundreds of customer orders (analogous to engineering drawings) come in. Each dish must be prepared using:
> - Specific ingredients (raw material)
> - Specific cooking methods (machining operations)
> - Specific utensils and stoves (machines and tools)
> - A specific order of steps (operation sequence)
> - Estimated cooking time (cycle time)
>
> In the **traditional manual approach**, the head chef personally inspects each order card and writes out the full recipe by memory. The recipes vary in quality depending on the chef's mood, experience, and fatigue. Two chefs will write two different recipes for the same dish.
>
> **CAPP is like installing a "Smart Recipe Generator" software** in the kitchen. The chef simply enters:
> - The dish name (part name)
> - Available ingredients (raw material spec)
> - Available cooking stations (machine tools)
>
> The software instantly generates a **standardised, optimised, and consistent recipe** with all cooking steps, times, utensils, and parameters — every single time, with the same quality.
>
> Now, **CAPP Implementation** is the entire organisational project of installing this software: training chefs, integrating with the ordering system (CAD), connecting to the kitchen inventory (tool database), testing the recipes, going live, and continuously improving.

---

## 1.3 Why CAPP is the "Glue" of CIM

In the broader **Computer Integrated Manufacturing (CIM)** architecture, CAPP occupies the central node. Without CAPP:

$$\text{CAD} \rightarrow \text{(HUMAN GAP)} \rightarrow \text{CAM}$$

With CAPP:

$$\text{CAD} \longleftrightarrow \boxed{\text{CAPP}} \longleftrightarrow \text{CAM}$$

The benefits derived and the implementation methodology together determine whether a CAPP deployment becomes a strategic organisational asset or a failed IT project.

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology Glossary

| Term | Definition (KTU standard) |
|---|---|
| **Process Plan** | A document specifying the sequence of manufacturing operations, machines, tools, fixtures, speeds, feeds, and inspection methods required to produce a part. |
| **Variant CAPP** | A retrieval-based approach where plans for similar parts are retrieved from a database and edited. |
| **Generative CAPP** | A synthesis-based approach where the plan is algorithmically generated from part features using logic and formulas. |
| **Implementation** | The structured organisational process of introducing, integrating, and operationalising the CAPP system. |
| **Pilot Project** | A controlled, limited-scope first deployment used to validate the CAPP system before enterprise-wide rollout. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Analysis

## 2.1 Benefits of CAPP — Structured Analytical Breakdown

The benefits of CAPP are conventionally classified into **Strategic Benefits**, **Operational Benefits**, and **Integration Benefits**. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to articulate at least **6–8 distinct benefits** under each classification.

### 2.1.1 Strategic / Managerial Benefits

These benefits affect the organisation at a policy and competitiveness level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strategic Benefits — The "Top-Down" View**
>
> 1. **Standardisation of Process Plans:** Eliminates the "shop-floor folklore" where two planners produce different routes for the same part. This is the single most cited KTU benefit.
> 2. **Faster Response to Market Changes (Reduced Lead Time):** New part introductions can be planned in minutes instead of hours/days.
> 3. **Improved Estimating Accuracy:** Quotation costing becomes data-driven (cycle times, tooling costs are computed, not estimated).
> 4. **Knowledge Preservation:** When senior planners retire, their expertise is captured permanently in the CAPP knowledge base.
> 5. **Better Capacity Planning:** Aggregated CAPP data feeds into production scheduling and MRP/ERP modules.
> 6. **Competitive Advantage:** Enables make-to-order strategies with rapid quoting and planning.

### 2.1.2 Operational / Shop-Floor Benefits

> [!NOTE]
> **Operational Benefits — The "Bottom-Up" View**
>
> 1. **Reduction in Process Planning Time:** Typically **60 % to 90 %** reduction compared to manual planning. A manual plan taking 4 hours may be generated in 15–30 minutes.
> 2. **Improved Productivity of Planners:** Planners handle 3× to 5× more parts per day.
> 3. **Consistency and Repeatability:** Identical parts always get identical (or near-identical) plans.
> 4. **Optimised Tooling and Machine Selection:** Best-fit machine and tool selection based on cost/availability algorithms.
> 5. **Accurate Cycle Time Estimation:** Machining time formulas are built into the system, removing guesswork.
> 6. **Reduced Scrap and Rework:** Better process selection → fewer in-process defects.
> 7. **Lower Inventory Holding:** Faster planning → faster release → lower WIP.

### 2.1.3 Integration / CIM Benefits

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Integration Benefits — The "Glue" Effect**
>
> 1. **Seamless CAD/CAM Integration:** Direct part feature transfer from CAD, eliminating manual transcription errors.
> 2. **Direct Generation of NC Code:** Process plan output (cutter, speeds, feeds, paths) can feed directly into post-processors for NC code generation.
> 3. **Database for Group Technology (GT) Coding:** CAPP data enables classification and coding of parts.
> 4. **Cost Estimation Module:** Direct link between process plan and cost accounting.
> 5. **Quality Plan Generation:** Inspection operations and fixtures can be appended automatically.
> 6. **Closed-Loop Feedback:** Actual shop-floor data can update CAPP parameters continuously.

---

## 2.2 Implementation of CAPP — The Structured Methodology

The KTU 2024 syllabus frames implementation as a **phased, project-managed activity**. Below is the canonical phased model.

### 2.2.1 Phase 1 — Justification & Feasibility Study

**Goal:** Build the business case for CAPP investment.

**Activities:**
- Quantify current manual planning time, error rate, and lead time.
- Estimate ROI (Return on Investment) — typically **payback in 18–36 months**.
- Identify candidate departments / part families for pilot.
- Risk analysis (resistance to change, data migration).

### 2.2.2 Phase 2 — System Selection (Build vs Buy)

**Decision Matrix:**

| Option | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|
| **Buy Commercial CAPP** (e.g., Siemens Teamcenter, Delmia, ICAM) | Proven, supported, faster deployment | Expensive licence, less customisation |
| **Develop In-House** | Tailored to company, full control | High development cost, long timeline, no vendor support |
| **Hybrid / Bespoke using Shell** | Compromise approach | Integration risk |

### 2.2.3 Phase 3 — Data Collection & Knowledge Engineering

**Critical Success Factor:** This phase consumes **40–60 %** of the total project time.

**Data to be collected:**
- Part families (Group Technology classification)
- Machine database (capabilities, capacities, cost/hour)
- Tool catalogue (cutters, holders, insert grades)
- Fixture library
- Standard operating procedures (company-specific "manufacturing rules")
- Cutting parameter tables (Vc, f, ap for each material-tool combination)

### 2.2.4 Phase 4 — Pilot Implementation

- Select **one part family** (e.g., shafts, prismatic parts).
- Develop and test CAPP logic on this family.
- Validate outputs against manual expert plans.
- Iterate, refine, and freeze the knowledge base.

### 2.2.5 Phase 5 — Training & Documentation

- Train process planners on system use.
- Train maintenance engineers on knowledge-base updates.
- Document all manufacturing rules and decision logic.

### 2.2.6 Phase 6 — Enterprise Rollout

- Phased expansion to other part families and departments.
- Integration with CAD, CAM, ERP, and shop-floor data collection systems.

### 2.2.7 Phase 7 — Continuous Improvement

- Collect feedback from shop floor.
- Update cutting data, machine additions, new tooling.
- Periodic knowledge-base audits.

---

## 2.3 Key Implementation Pitfalls (KTU Favourite Exam Topic)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Organisations Fail**
>
> 1. **Treating CAPP as an IT Project, not a Manufacturing Engineering Project.** The real bottleneck is knowledge engineering, not software.
> 2. **Inadequate Top Management Commitment.** Without executive sponsorship, planners revert to manual methods.
> 3. **Resistance from Senior Planners.** Planners fear CAPP will make them redundant.
> 4. **Incomplete Data Migration.** Empty machine/tool databases render CAPP useless.
> 5. **Poor CAD-CAPP Interface.** Manual re-entry of features defeats the purpose.
> 6. **No Clear Performance Metrics.** ROI cannot be demonstrated without baseline measurements.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Quantitative Metrics (Must Remember for Numerical Problems)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula Sheet — CAPP Performance Metrics**
>
> Let:
> - $T_m$ = time taken for manual process planning (hours)
> - $T_c$ = time taken for CAPP-generated plan (hours)
> - $P_m$ = planner productivity with manual method (parts/day)
> - $P_c$ = planner productivity with CAPP (parts/day)
> - $C_m$ = cost of manual planning (₹/part)
> - $C_c$ = cost of CAPP planning (₹/part)

$$\text{Planning Time Reduction \%} = \frac{T_m - T_c}{T_m} \times 100$$

$$\text{Productivity Gain \%} = \frac{P_c - P_m}{P_m} \times 100$$

$$\text{Cost Saving per Part} = C_m - C_c$$

$$\text{Annual Cost Saving} = (C_m - C_c) \times N$$

where $N$ = total number of new parts planned per year.

$$\text{ROI Period (months)} = \frac{\text{Total CAPP Investment (₹)}}{\text{Annual Net Saving (₹)}} \times 12$$

| Metric | Typical Manual | Typical CAPP | Improvement |
|---|---|---|---|
| Planning Time | 2–6 hours/part | 10–30 min/part | **75–90 %** reduction |
| Planner Output | 2–4 parts/day | 8–15 parts/day | **3–4× gain** |
| Plan Consistency | 60–70 % | 95–99 % | High |
| Routing Error Rate | 5–15 % | <1 % | Significant |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

CAPP and its implementation are cornerstones of:
- **Aerospace manufacturing** (complex prismatic parts, AS9100 compliance).
- **Automotive industry** (variant CAPP for high-volume families).
- **Job shops** (generative CAPP for one-off orders).
- **Tool and die making** (CAD feature recognition → EDM planning).
- **Medical device manufacturing** (traceability and standardisation).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Example 1 — Quantifying CAPP Benefits (14-Mark Standard Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (Modelled on KTU Pattern):**
> A manufacturing company currently employs **5 process planners** to manually prepare process plans. Each planner works **8 hours/day, 25 days/month**, and takes on average **4 hours** to plan one part. The company produces approximately **250 new parts per month**. The company plans to install a CAPP system costing **₹ 25,00,000** (licence, hardware, training, and data preparation). After CAPP installation, the average planning time reduces to **30 minutes per part**, and one planner can effectively manage the entire workload.
>
> Calculate:
> 1. Manual planner capacity (parts/month).
> 2. CAPP planner capacity (parts/month).
> 3. Monthly labour cost saving (assuming each planner is paid ₹ 30,000/month).
> 4. ROI period in months.

---

### Step 1 — Manual Planning Capacity

Each planner works:

$$
H_{\text{per planner per month}} = 8 \times 25 = 200 \text{ hours/month}
$$

Total manual planning hours available across 5 planners:

$$
H_{\text{total manual}} = 5 \times 200 = 1000 \text{ hours/month}
$$

Parts planned per month (each part takes 4 hours):

$$
N_m = \frac{H_{\text{total manual}}}{\text{Time per part}} = \frac{1000}{4} = 250 \text{ parts/month}
$$

This **matches the demand of 250 new parts/month exactly**, so the company is at capacity with no slack.

---

### Step 2 — CAPP Planning Capacity

After CAPP, each plan takes 30 minutes = 0.5 hours. With **1 planner** working on CAPP:

$$
H_{\text{CAPP available}} = 1 \times 200 = 200 \text{ hours/month}
$$

$$
N_c = \frac{200}{0.5} = 400 \text{ parts/month}
$$

**Conclusion:** A single planner can now handle 400 parts/month — the demand of 250 parts is easily met with **surplus capacity of 150 parts/month**.

---

### Step 3 — Labour Cost Saving

Manual cost (5 planners):

$$
C_{\text{manual}} = 5 \times 30{,}000 = \text{₹ }1{,}50{,}000/\text{month}
$$

CAPP cost (1 planner):

$$
C_{\text{CAPP}} = 1 \times 30{,}000 = \text{₹ }30{,}000/\text{month}
$$

Monthly labour saving:

$$
S_{\text{labour}} = 1{,}50{,}000 - 30{,}000 = \text{₹ }1{,}20{,}000/\text{month}
$$

The other **4 planners** can be redeployed to manufacturing engineering, tool design, or quality functions.

---

### Step 4 — Return on Investment Period

Total CAPP Investment:

$$
I = \text{₹ }25{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{ROI (months)} = \frac{I}{S_{\text{labour}}} = \frac{25{,}00{,}000}{1{,}20{,}000}
$$

$$
\text{ROI} = 20.83 \text{ months} \approx 21 \text{ months}
$$

**Conclusion:** The CAPP system pays for itself in approximately **1 year and 9 months**.

---

## 3.2 Example 2 — Planning Time Reduction Percentage

Using the same data:

$$
\text{Reduction \%} = \frac{T_m - T_c}{T_m} \times 100 = \frac{4 - 0.5}{4} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Reduction \%} = \frac{3.5}{4} \times 100 = 87.5\%
$$

This falls in the **75–90 % typical range** quoted in literature.

---

## 3.3 Example 3 — Productivity Gain Calculation

$$
\text{Productivity Gain \%} = \frac{P_c - P_m}{P_m} \times 100
$$

Manual: 1 planner plans 200/4 = **50 parts/month**.
CAPP: 1 planner plans 200/0.5 = **400 parts/month**.

$$
\text{Gain \%} = \frac{400 - 50}{50} \times 100 = \frac{350}{50} \times 100 = 700\%
$$

This represents a **7-fold productivity gain per planner**, which is a standard realistic outcome for CAPP deployment.

---

## 3.4 Example 4 — Annual Cost Saving (with Volume Variation)

Suppose the company produces 3000 new parts per year. Cost of one manual plan = ₹ 800 (labour + overhead). CAPP plan cost = ₹ 150 (labour + software amortisation).

$$
S_{\text{per part}} = 800 - 150 = \text{₹ }650/\text{part}
$$

$$
S_{\text{annual}} = 650 \times 3000 = \text{₹ }19{,}50{,}000/\text{year}
$$

$$
S_{\text{monthly}} = \frac{19{,}50{,}000}{12} \approx \text{₹ }1{,}62{,}500/\text{month}
$$

This number is then compared with the CAPP investment for ROI calculation.

---

## 3.5 Implementation Strategy — Decision Tree Logic

A typical **build-vs-buy** decision in CAPP implementation can be expressed as:

$$
\text{Decision} = f(\text{Volume}, \text{Variety}, \text{Budget}, \text{IT Maturity})
$$

| Condition | Recommended Approach |
|---|---|
| High volume, low variety, large budget | Buy enterprise CAPP (Siemens, Delmia) |
| High variety, low volume, in-house IT expertise | Develop generative CAPP in-house |
| Small-to-medium company, limited budget | Begin with **variant CAPP** as a pilot |
| Existing ERP/MES, strong IT backbone | Buy CAPP that integrates with existing stack |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part A question carries **3 marks** in KTU ESE. Answers should be **3–4 lines**, crisp, with at least one technical term highlighted.

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**
**List any six benefits of implementing CAPP in a manufacturing organisation.**

**Model Answer:**

CAPP benefits include:
1. **Drastic reduction in process planning time** (typically 75–90 %).
2. **Standardisation and consistency** of process plans across the organisation.
3. **Improved planner productivity** (3× to 5× more parts per day).
4. **Knowledge preservation** — expert planner knowledge is captured permanently in the knowledge base.
5. **Seamless CAD–CAM integration**, eliminating manual re-entry errors.
6. **Accurate cost and cycle time estimation** through built-in formulas and databases.
7. **Faster response to customer enquiries** for quotations and delivery.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**What is meant by "Pilot Implementation" of a CAPP system? Why is it recommended?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Pilot Implementation** is the deployment of the CAPP system on a **limited, controlled scope** (typically one part family or one department) before enterprise-wide rollout.

It is recommended because it:
- **Validates the knowledge base** on a realistic but small dataset.
- Allows **early detection of bugs and missing data** with lower business risk.
- Provides **measurable ROI proof** to convince top management.
- Enables **iterative refinement** of manufacturing rules before large-scale use.
- Reduces organisational resistance by demonstrating success in a small group.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part B questions carry **14 marks** and follow a **Module Internal Choice** pattern. Two full 14-mark questions (each with two 7-mark sub-parts) are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**
**[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Model Question]**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain in detail the strategic and operational benefits of CAPP. Differentiate between them with at least three examples in each category.**

**Model Answer:**

**Strategic Benefits** (organisation-level, long-term impact):
1. **Standardisation of Plans:** Eliminates planner-dependent variations; one part = one plan regardless of who runs CAPP. Critical for ISO/AS9100 audits.
2. **Reduced Product Lead Time:** New parts are planned in minutes, accelerating Time-to-Market — a key competitive metric.
3. **Knowledge Preservation:** Senior planners' tacit knowledge (e.g., "for Inconel 718, use coated carbide at Vc = 45 m/min") is codified permanently.
4. **Improved Quotation Accuracy:** Cycle times and tooling costs are algorithmically computed, enabling reliable make-to-order quotations.
5. **Capacity Planning Support:** Aggregated CAPP data feeds into MRP/ERP for accurate capacity vs. demand planning.

**Operational Benefits** (shop-floor level, daily impact):
1. **Planning Time Reduction:** 4 hours → 30 minutes, i.e., ~87.5 % reduction.
2. **Higher Planner Productivity:** A single planner now handles 400 parts/month versus 50 manually.
3. **Optimised Tool and Machine Selection:** Decision logic picks the most economical combination.
4. **Reduced Scrap and Rework:** Standardised, validated plans reduce in-process defects.

**Differentiation:**
Strategic benefits affect **competitiveness, policy, and long-term ROI**; operational benefits affect **daily efficiency, throughput, and cost per part**. Both must be quantified during the feasibility study.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A company has 8 process planners, each working 8 hours/day for 24 days/month. Manual planning time per part is 5 hours. After installing CAPP, planning time reduces to 45 minutes per part, and only 2 planners are needed. Calculate: (i) Manual monthly capacity, (ii) CAPP monthly capacity, (iii) Labour cost saved per month if each planner earns ₹ 35,000/month.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Manual Monthly Capacity:**

$$
H_{\text{total manual}} = 8 \times 8 \times 24 = 1536 \text{ hours/month}
$$

$$
N_m = \frac{1536}{5} = 307.2 \approx 307 \text{ parts/month}
$$

**Step 2 — CAPP Monthly Capacity (2 planners):**

$$
H_{\text{CAPP}} = 2 \times 8 \times 24 = 384 \text{ hours/month}
$$

$$
N_c = \frac{384}{0.75} = 512 \text{ parts/month}
$$

**Step 3 — Monthly Labour Cost Saving:**

Manual cost:

$$
C_m = 8 \times 35{,}000 = \text{₹ }2{,}80{,}000/\text{month}
$$

CAPP cost:

$$
C_c = 2 \times 35{,}000 = \text{₹ }70{,}000/\text{month}
$$

Saving:

$$
S = 2{,}80{,}000 - 70{,}000 = \text{₹ }2{,}10{,}000/\text{month}
$$

The other **6 planners can be redeployed**, giving an additional strategic benefit not captured in this calculation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Alternative Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Describe the step-by-step implementation methodology of a CAPP system in a manufacturing organisation. Mention the critical success factors.**

**Model Answer:**

The implementation of CAPP follows a **seven-phase methodology**:

**Phase 1 — Feasibility Study and Justification:**
Quantify current manual planning cost, time, and error rate. Estimate ROI. Secure top management commitment. Identify pilot part family.

**Phase 2 — System Selection (Build vs Buy):**
Evaluate commercial packages vs. in-house development. Consider budget, IT maturity, part variety, and integration with existing CAD/CAM/ERP.

**Phase 3 — Data Collection and Knowledge Engineering (Most Critical Phase):**
Build the machine database, tool catalogue, fixture library, cutting parameter tables, and manufacturing rules. This phase consumes 40–60 % of total project time and is the **primary determinant of project success**.

**Phase 4 — Pilot Implementation:**
Deploy CAPP for one part family (e.g., shafts). Validate generated plans against expert manual plans. Refine knowledge base iteratively.

**Phase 5 — Training:**
Train planners on system use; train engineers on knowledge-base maintenance. Document all decision logic.

**Phase 6 — Enterprise Rollout:**
Expand to all part families in phases. Integrate with CAD, CAM, and ERP modules.

**Phase 7 — Continuous Improvement:**
Update cutting data, add new machines/tools, and refine rules based on shop-floor feedback.

**Critical Success Factors:**
- Top management commitment (financial and political).
- Dedicated cross-functional team (planners + IT + manufacturing engineers).
- Clean, complete data migration.
- Strong CAD–CAPP feature interface.
- Resistance management through change-management workshops.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Discuss the common reasons for failure of CAPP implementation projects. How can these be mitigated?**

**Model Answer:**

CAPP implementations fail for reasons that are **organisational more than technical**. The major causes and their mitigations are:

| # | Reason for Failure | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Treating CAPP as an IT project, not a manufacturing engineering project** | Assign manufacturing engineers as project leads, not just IT staff |
| 2 | **Incomplete knowledge base** | Allocate 40–60 % of project time to data collection; involve senior planners |
| 3 | **Resistance from senior planners** | Position CAPP as an *assistant*, not a replacement; reward planners who contribute knowledge |
| 4 | **Poor CAD–CAPP interface** | Use STEP or direct feature-based interfaces; avoid manual re-entry |
| 5 | **Lack of executive sponsorship** | Appoint a C-sponsor (e.g., VP Manufacturing) for the project |
| 6 | **No measurable KPIs** | Define baseline metrics (planning time, error rate) before deployment |
| 7 | **Vendor lock-in or wrong package** | Pilot-test on actual part family before full licence purchase |
| 8 | **No integration with ERP/MES** | Plan CAPP as part of the broader CIM/Industry 4.0 roadmap, not in isolation |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Writing only benefits without quantification:** Always pair a benefit with a metric (e.g., "75 % reduction in planning time"). A benefit without numbers is considered incomplete.
> 2. **Confusing implementation phases with CAPP types:** Implementation is a project methodology; variant/generative are types of CAPP. Examiners frequently test this distinction.
> 3. **Ignoring the human factor:** Many students list only technical issues (software, data) and forget organisational resistance, training, and change management — easy 2–3 marks lost.
> 4. **Numerical errors in ROI:** Watch unit conversions (hours/month, days/month) and currency. ₹/part vs. ₹/month confusion is the most common slip.
> 5. **Skipping the pilot phase:** Direct enterprise-wide deployment is universally flagged as a bad practice. Always mention pilot.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Summary**
>
> **BENEFITS OF CAPP — The 4 Pillars:**
> - **Time:** Planning time reduced by 75–90 %.
> - **Quality:** Standardised, error-free, consistent plans.
> - **Cost:** Lower per-part planning cost; faster quotations.
> - **Integration:** CAD–CAPP–CAM seamless data flow.
>
> **STRATEGIC BENEFITS (long-term):** standardisation, lead time, knowledge retention, quotation accuracy, competitive advantage.
>
> **OPERATIONAL BENEFITS (daily):** time reduction, planner productivity, optimised selection, scrap reduction.
>
> **INTEGRATION BENEFITS (CIM-level):** CAD-CAM bridge, NC code, GT coding, cost estimation, quality plans.
>
> **IMPLEMENTATION PHASES (memorise in order):**
> 1. Justification & Feasibility
> 2. System Selection (Build vs Buy)
> 3. Data Collection & Knowledge Engineering (**longest phase**)
> 4. Pilot Project (**must mention**)
> 5. Training & Documentation
> 6. Enterprise Rollout
> 7. Continuous Improvement
>
> **KEY NUMBERS TO REMEMBER:**
> - Planning time reduction: **75–90 %**.
> - Productivity gain: **3× to 5×** per planner.
> - Typical ROI period: **18–36 months**.
> - Knowledge engineering consumes **40–60 %** of project time.
>
> **KEY FORMULAS:**
> - Reduction % = (Tm − Tc) / Tm × 100.
> - ROI (months) = Investment / Monthly Saving.
> - Productivity gain % = (Pc − Pm) / Pm × 100.
>
> **COMMON PITFALLS (memorise at least 4):**
> - Treating as IT project; incomplete data; planner resistance; no executive sponsorship; no KPIs.
>
> **KTU FAVOURITE 14-MARK STRUCTURE:**
> - Always answer with **classification → explanation → example → quantification**.
> - In numericals, show **all steps, units, and final interpretation**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Production Planning Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT613 - COMPUTER INTEGRATED MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Production Planning Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Production Planning Techniques (PPT)** are a structured set of quantitative and qualitative methodologies used in Computer Integrated Manufacturing (CIM) to determine **what to produce, when to produce, how much to produce, and with what resources**, in order to optimally balance **demand fluctuation, inventory cost, workforce utilization, and machine capacity** across the manufacturing value chain.

In the CIM hierarchy, Production Planning Techniques sit at the **upper-middle planning layer** — above shop floor control (SFC) and below strategic business planning. They form the critical link that translates long-term business forecasts into actionable, time-phased manufacturing orders.

The hierarchy of planning in CIM (top-down) is:

| Level | Function | Time Horizon |
|:------|:---------|:-------------|
| 1 | Strategic Planning (Business Plan) | 2–10 years |
| 2 | **Production Planning (Aggregate)** | 6–18 months |
| 3 | **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** | 3–6 months |
| 4 | **Material Requirements Planning (MRP)** | 1 week – 3 months |
| 5 | Shop Floor Control (SFC) | Days – Weeks |
| 6 | Process Control | Real-time |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 specifically covers *aggregate planning, master scheduling, MRP, MRP-II, ERP, JIT, Lean, and Theory of Constraints* as the canonical production planning techniques.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Grand Kitchen" Model

Imagine you are the head chef of a busy restaurant chain. Every morning you must decide:
- **How many rotis to prepare** for lunch (matching anticipated customers),
- **How many cooks to call in** (workforce),
- **How much dough to keep ready** (inventory),
- **When to start the tandoor** (machine scheduling).

You never know exactly how many customers will walk in. Sometimes you make **too much** (wastage cost) and sometimes **too little** (lost sales). **Production Planning Techniques are the mathematical "recipe" that helps you match supply with demand as precisely as possible**, minimizing waste and lost opportunity.

In this analogy:
- **Forecast** = guessing tomorrow's customers from past data.
- **Aggregate Plan** = deciding "we will hire 2 extra cooks this week."
- **MPS** = "we will bake 500 rotis on Monday, 600 on Tuesday."
- **MRP** = "for 500 rotis, we need 250 kg flour, 100 L water, 12 cooks…"
- **JIT** = "make rotis only when an order comes in — zero wastage."

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following industry-standard metrics are used throughout Production Planning:

- **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) = Availability × Performance × Quality** — measured as a **percentage (%)** with benchmark target of **≥ 85%**.
- **Takt Time (seconds) = Available Production Time / Customer Demand** — units in **seconds/unit**.
- **Lead Time (days)** = total time from order release to delivery.
- **Service Level (%)** = (Orders fulfilled on time / Total orders) × 100.
- **Inventory Turnover Ratio = Cost of Goods Sold / Average Inventory** — unitless, benchmark **6–12 per year** for discrete manufacturing.

> [!TIP]
> **Remember for KTU exam:** Every quantitative PPT problem expects you to clearly state the **planning horizon, demand units, cost components (setup, holding, shortage, hiring/firing), and the objective function** before solving.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Major Production Planning Techniques — Structured Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Forecasting Techniques
Forecasting is the **input** to every production plan. It is classified as:

- **Qualitative Methods** — used when no historical data exists (used in new product introduction).
  - Delphi method, Market research, Expert opinion, Sales force composite.
- **Quantitative Methods** — used when historical numerical data is available.
  - **Time-Series Models:** Moving Average, Weighted Moving Average, Exponential Smoothing (Simple, Holt's, Winter's).
  - **Causal/Associative Models:** Linear Regression, Econometric models.
  - **AI-based Models:** ARIMA, Neural Networks, Fuzzy Logic, Genetic Algorithm-based forecasters.

**Why forecasting matters in CIM:** Every downstream MRP run, MPS explosion, and aggregate plan depends on the *forecast accuracy*. A **MAPE (Mean Absolute Percentage Error) < 10%** is considered excellent in discrete manufacturing.

---

### 2.1.2 Aggregate Production Planning (APP)
APP is the **medium-range (3–18 months) plan** that sets the **production rate, workforce level, and inventory level** for product *families* (not individual SKUs).

**Three canonical APP strategies:**

1. **Level Strategy** — Production rate kept constant; inventory and backorders absorb demand variation.
   - *Used when:* Hiring/firing is expensive, demand is moderately stable.
2. **Chase Strategy** — Production rate matches demand each period; workforce is varied.
   - *Used when:* Inventory holding cost is very high (e.g., perishable goods, fashion).
3. **Mixed/Hybrid Strategy** — Combination of level + chase; uses subcontracting, overtime, part-time workers.
   - *Used when:* Real-world plants (most common).

**Cost Components in APP (the "Big Five"):**
- Hiring cost (C_h)
- Firing/Layoff cost (C_f)
- Holding/Carrying cost (C_i) — typically **15–30% of item value per year**
- Backorder/Shortage cost (C_b)
- Regular-time labor cost, Overtime cost, Subcontracting cost.

---

### 2.1.3 Master Production Schedule (MPS)
MPS is the **dis-aggregated, time-phased plan** that converts aggregate output into **specific end-items, specific quantities, specific due dates**. The MPS drives the MRP explosion.

Key inputs: Forecast + Customer Orders + Inventory on hand + Safety stock policy.
Key outputs: **Planned Order Releases** and **Projected Available Balance (PAB)**.

---

### 2.1.4 Material Requirements Planning (MRP)
MRP is a **computer-based production planning and inventory control system** for managing dependent demand items. It answers three questions: *What is needed? How much is needed? When is it needed?*

**MRP Inputs (the "3 M's"):**
1. **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** — what end items are needed and when.
2. **Bill of Materials (BOM)** — the recipe/structure of the product (a multi-level tree).
3. **Inventory Status File (ISF)** — current on-hand, on-order, allocated, safety stock.

**MRP Logic — The Lot-Sizing Decision:**
- **Lot-for-Lot (L4L)** — order exactly the net requirement.
- **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** — fixed quantity each time.
- **Periodic Order Quantity (POQ)** — order for `k` periods' demand where `k` is chosen to make order quantity close to EOQ.
- **Part Period Balancing (PPB)** — heuristic that minimizes total ordering + holding cost.
- **Wagner-Whitin Algorithm** — optimal dynamic lot-sizing (O(n²) complexity).
- **Silver-Meal Heuristic** — minimizes total cost per period.

**MRP Output Reports:**
- Planned Order Schedule, Exception Reports (action messages), Performance Reports.

---

### 2.1.5 MRP-II (Manufacturing Resource Planning)
MRP-II extends MRP by integrating **shop floor capacity (CRP — Capacity Requirements Planning)** with material planning. It is a **closed-loop system** that reschedules when capacity is exceeded.

---

### 2.1.6 ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning)
ERP is the **enterprise-wide information system** that integrates **all business functions** (Finance, HR, Sales, Manufacturing, Procurement, Logistics) into a **single relational database**. Examples: SAP S/4HANA, Oracle Cloud ERP, Microsoft Dynamics 365.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MRP → MRP-II → ERP** is a common KTU short-answer question. Know the difference:
> - MRP = materials only.
> - MRP-II = materials + capacity + shop floor feedback (closed loop).
> - ERP = entire enterprise (finance, HR, sales, supply chain, manufacturing, CRM).

---

### 2.1.7 Just-In-Time (JIT) Production
A **pull-based** production philosophy originating from the Toyota Production System. The goal: **produce the right item, in the right quantity, at the right time**. JIT achieves this by minimizing:
- **WIP (Work-In-Process) inventory** → ideally zero.
- **Setup time** → via SMED (Single Minute Exchange of Dies).
- **Lead time** → small lot sizes, cellular layout.
- **Defects** → via Jidoka (autonomation) and Poka-Yoke (mistake-proofing).

**7 Wastes (Muda) eliminated by JIT:**
1. Overproduction, 2. Waiting, 3. Transportation, 4. Over-processing, 5. Inventory, 6. Motion, 7. Defects.

**Kanban System:** Visual signal card system that authorizes production/withdrawal. Types: Production Kanban (P-kanban) and Withdrawal Kanban (W-kanban).

**Number of Kanban cards formula** is given in the formula sheet below.

---

### 2.1.8 Lean Manufacturing
A philosophy of **continuous improvement (Kaizen)** that maximizes customer value while minimizing waste. JIT is the *operational subset* of Lean.

**Core Lean Tools:** 5S, Value Stream Mapping (VSM), Kaizen, Poka-Yoke, SMED, Kanban, Heijunka (level scheduling), Total Productive Maintenance (TPM).

---

### 2.1.9 Theory of Constraints (TOC)
Developed by **Eliyahu Goldratt** in *The Goal* (1984). TOC says: **A system's performance is limited by at least one constraint (bottleneck)**. The **5 Focusing Steps** are:

1. **Identify** the system's constraint.
2. **Exploit** the constraint (maximize its utilization).
3. **Subordinate** all other processes to the constraint's pace (drum-buffer-rope).
4. **Elevate** the constraint (increase its capacity).
5. **Repeat** — once the constraint is broken, find the new one.

**Drum-Buffer-Rope (DBR):**
- **Drum** = the pace set by the bottleneck.
- **Buffer** = time buffer to protect the bottleneck.
- **Rope** = releases work to the bottleneck at its pace.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Expression | Units / Notes |
|:--|:------------------|:----------------------|:--------------|
| 1 | Simple Moving Average (n-period) | $F_{t+1} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=t-n+1}^{t} D_i$ | Units of demand |
| 2 | Weighted Moving Average | $F_{t+1} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i D_{t-i+1}$ where $\sum w_i = 1$ | Weights chosen by analyst |
| 3 | Single Exponential Smoothing | $F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha) F_t$ | Smoothing constant $\alpha \in [0,1]$, typically 0.1–0.3 |
| 4 | Holt's Linear Trend | $L_t = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha)(L_{t-1}+T_{t-1})$ ; $T_t = \beta(L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1-\beta) T_{t-1}$ | Two parameters $\alpha, \beta$ |
| 5 | Linear Regression Forecast | $F_t = a + b t$ where $b = \dfrac{n\sum tD - \sum t \sum D}{n \sum t^2 - (\sum t)^2}$ | Trend line |
| 6 | MAPE (forecast error) | $MAPE = \dfrac{100}{n}\sum \left\vert \dfrac{D_t - F_t}{D_t} \right\vert$ | Expressed in **%** |
| 7 | MAD (forecast error) | $MAD = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum \left\vert D_t - F_t \right\vert$ | Absolute error in demand units |
| 8 | Tracking Signal | $TS = \dfrac{Cumulative\ Forecast\ Error}{MAD}$ | In-control if $\vert TS \vert \le 4$ (≈ ±4) |
| 9 | EOQ (Economic Order Quantity) | $EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 D C_o}{C_h}}$ | units/item |
| 10 | Takt Time | $T_{takt} = \dfrac{T_{available}}{D_{demand}}$ | seconds/unit |
| 11 | Cycle Time | $T_c = T_{available}/N_{units\ to\ produce}$ | seconds/unit |
| 12 | OEE | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | Pure ratio (multiply by 100 for %) |
| 13 | Level Strategy Cost | $C_{level} = \sum H_t \cdot I_t + \sum B_t \cdot S_t + C_{reg} W_{reg}$ | Inventory + backorder + labor |
| 14 | Chase Strategy Cost | $C_{chase} = \sum (C_h \cdot \Delta W^+ + C_f \cdot \Delta W^-) + C_{reg} W_t$ | Hiring/firing + regular labor |
| 15 | Aggregate Plan Mixed Cost | $C_{total} = C_{reg} + C_{ot} + C_h + C_f + C_i + C_b + C_{sub}$ | Sum of all seven components |
| 16 | Number of Kanban Cards | $K = \dfrac{D \cdot L \cdot (1+S)}{C}$ where S = safety factor, C = container size | Whole number, round up |
| 17 | Throughput Time | $T_{th} = T_{process} + T_{queue} + T_{wait} + T_{move}$ | Units: time |
| 18 | Little's Law | $WIP = Throughput\ Rate \times Lead\ Time$ | Fundamental queueing identity |
| 19 | Drum-Buffer-Rope buffer | $Buffer = \dfrac{Bottleneck\ processing\ time \times Replenishment\ time}{2}$ | Time units |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU exam tip:** When asked to choose between Level and Chase strategy, always compute **total cost** for both plans over the planning horizon and pick the cheaper one. Many students lose marks by not computing backorder cost correctly.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Single Exponential Smoothing (Forecast Calculation)

> **Problem:** A factory has actual demand of 100, 110, 105, 120 units in months 1–4. The initial forecast for month 1 is F₁ = 100. Using Single Exponential Smoothing with α = 0.3, compute forecasts for months 2, 3, 4, 5. Also compute MAD and MAPE.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — State the SES recurrence.**
$$F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha) F_t$$

**Step 2 — Initialize.**
$D_1 = 100$, $F_1 = 100$, $\alpha = 0.3$.

**Step 3 — Compute F₂ (forecast for month 2, made at end of month 1).**
$$F_2 = \alpha D_1 + (1-\alpha) F_1 = 0.3(100) + 0.7(100) = 30 + 70 = 100$$

**Step 4 — Compute F₃.**
$$F_3 = 0.3 D_2 + 0.7 F_2 = 0.3(110) + 0.7(100) = 33 + 70 = 103$$

**Step 5 — Compute F₄.**
$$F_4 = 0.3 D_3 + 0.7 F_3 = 0.3(105) + 0.7(103) = 31.5 + 72.1 = 103.6$$

**Step 6 — Compute F₅.**
$$F_5 = 0.3 D_4 + 0.7 F_4 = 0.3(120) + 0.7(103.6) = 36 + 72.52 = 108.52$$

**Step 7 — Compute forecast error $e_t = D_t - F_t$ for t = 2, 3, 4.**

| Month t | D_t | F_t | e_t = D_t − F_t | \|e_t\| |
|:------:|:---:|:---:|:---------------:|:--------:|
| 1 | 100 | 100.0 | — | — |
| 2 | 110 | 100.0 | 10.0 | 10.0 |
| 3 | 105 | 103.0 | 2.0 | 2.0 |
| 4 | 120 | 103.6 | 16.4 | 16.4 |

**Step 8 — Compute MAD.**
$$MAD = \frac{1}{3}\left(10.0 + 2.0 + 16.4\right) = \frac{28.4}{3} = 9.467 \text{ units}$$

**Step 9 — Compute MAPE.**
$$MAPE = \frac{100}{3}\left(\left\vert\frac{10}{110}\right\vert + \left\vert\frac{2}{105}\right\vert + \left\vert\frac{16.4}{120}\right\vert\right)$$

$$MAPE = \frac{100}{3}(0.0909 + 0.0190 + 0.1367) = \frac{100}{3}(0.2466) = 8.22\%$$

**Interpretation:** MAPE of 8.22% indicates **excellent forecast accuracy** (MAPE < 10% is the industry benchmark).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Aggregate Production Planning (Level vs Chase Strategy)

> **Problem:** A company must meet the following demand over 4 months:
> | Month | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | Total |
> |:-----:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-----:|
> | Demand (units) | 500 | 700 | 800 | 600 | 2600 |
>
> Data: Initial inventory = 100 units, Initial workforce = 10 workers, each worker produces 50 units/month, regular time cost = ₹100/unit, hiring cost = ₹500/worker, firing cost = ₹600/worker, holding cost = ₹20/unit/month, shortage cost = ₹40/unit/month.
>
> Compare **Level** and **Chase** strategies.

### Solution Path A — Level Strategy (Constant Production)

**Step 1 — Compute total required production accounting for initial inventory.**
$$P_{total} = \sum D - I_0 = 2600 - 100 = 2500 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2 — Compute constant monthly production.**
$$P_{level} = \frac{2500}{4} = 625 \text{ units/month}$$

**Step 3 — Compute required workforce.**
$$W_{level} = \frac{625}{50} = 12.5 \Rightarrow \lceil 12.5 \rceil = 13 \text{ workers}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Regular-time labor cost.**
$$C_{reg} = 2500 \times 100 = ₹2,50,000$$

**Step 5 — Compute Hiring cost.**
$$W_{hired} = 13 - 10 = 3 \text{ workers} \Rightarrow C_h = 3 \times 500 = ₹1,500$$

**Step 6 — Build month-by-month inventory balance.**

| Month | Demand | Production | Beg. Inv | End. Inv |
|:-----:|:------:|:----------:|:--------:|:--------:|
| 1 | 500 | 625 | 100 | 225 |
| 2 | 700 | 625 | 225 | 150 |
| 3 | 800 | 625 | 150 | -25 (backorder) |
| 4 | 600 | 625 | -25 | 0 |

**Step 7 — Compute Holding + Backorder cost.**
- Holding: $(225 + 150) \times 20 = 375 \times 20 = ₹7,500$
- Backorder: $25 \times 40 = ₹1,000$

**Step 8 — Total Level Strategy Cost.**
$$C_{level} = 2,50,000 + 1,500 + 7,500 + 1,000 = ₹2,60,000$$

---

### Solution Path B — Chase Strategy (Production = Demand each month)

**Step 1 — Production = demand each month (using initial inventory first).**
| Month | Demand | Available Wkrs Needed | Hire/Fire |
|:-----:|:------:|:---------------------:|:---------:|
| 1 | 500 | 10 | 0 |
| 2 | 700 | 14 | +4 hired |
| 3 | 800 | 16 | +2 hired |
| 4 | 600 | 12 | -4 fired |

**Step 2 — Production cost (regular time only).**
$$C_{reg} = 2600 \times 100 = ₹2,60,000$$

**Step 3 — Hiring/Firing cost.**
- Hiring: $4 + 2 = 6$ workers × ₹500 = ₹3,000
- Firing: $4$ workers × ₹600 = ₹2,400
- Total: ₹5,400

**Step 4 — No inventory, no backorder.**
- Holding cost = 0
- Backorder cost = 0

**Step 5 — Total Chase Strategy Cost.**
$$C_{chase} = 2,60,000 + 5,400 = ₹2,65,400$$

---

### Comparative Analysis

| Strategy | Total Cost (₹) | Holding (₹) | Backorder (₹) | Hiring/Firing (₹) |
|:---------|:--------------:|:-----------:|:-------------:|:----------------:|
| **Level** | **2,60,000** | 7,500 | 1,000 | 1,500 |
| **Chase** | 2,65,400 | 0 | 0 | 5,400 |

**Decision:** **Level Strategy is cheaper by ₹5,400.** Therefore the company should adopt the **Level Strategy** for this planning horizon.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — EOQ & Reorder Point

> **Problem:** Annual demand D = 12,000 units, ordering cost C_o = ₹200/order, holding cost C_h = ₹10/unit/year. Compute EOQ, number of orders per year, time between orders, and total annual cost.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Recall the EOQ derivation from the Total Cost Model.**

The annual total cost $TC(Q)$ is the sum of ordering cost and holding cost:
$$TC(Q) = \frac{D}{Q} C_o + \frac{Q}{2} C_h$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate TC(Q) with respect to Q and set to zero to find the optimum.**
$$\frac{d\ TC(Q)}{d\ Q} = -\frac{D C_o}{Q^2} + \frac{C_h}{2} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for Q.**
$$\frac{D C_o}{Q^2} = \frac{C_h}{2} \quad\Rightarrow\quad Q^2 = \frac{2 D C_o}{C_h} \quad\Rightarrow\quad Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 D C_o}{C_h}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values.**
$$EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 12{,}000 \times 200}{10}} = \sqrt{480{,}000} = 692.82 \approx 693 \text{ units}$$

**Step 5 — Number of orders per year.**
$$N = \frac{D}{EOQ} = \frac{12{,}000}{693} = 17.32 \approx 18 \text{ orders/year}$$

**Step 6 — Time between orders (cycle time).**
$$T_c = \frac{Working\ days\ per\ year}{N} = \frac{250}{18} = 13.89 \text{ days/order}$$

**Step 7 — Verify using second derivative (minimum condition).**
$$\frac{d^2 TC}{dQ^2} = \frac{2 D C_o}{Q^3} > 0 \ \forall Q > 0 \ \ \Rightarrow \text{ confirmed minimum.}$$

**Step 8 — Total annual cost at EOQ.**
$$TC = \frac{12{,}000}{693}(200) + \frac{693}{2}(10) = 3{,}464.6 + 3{,}465 = ₹6{,}929.6$$

**Engineering Insight:** The EOQ result of 693 units means the company should place an order of approximately **693 units roughly every 2 weeks** to minimize the combined setup and holding cost.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Number of Kanban Cards

> **Problem:** A workstation has hourly demand = 50 units/hour, lead time = 2 hours, container size = 25 units, safety factor = 0.10 (10%).

### Derivation

**Step 1 — State the formula.**
$$K = \frac{D \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)}{C}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**
$$K = \frac{50 \times 2 \times (1 + 0.10)}{25} = \frac{100 \times 1.10}{25} = \frac{110}{25} = 4.4$$

**Step 3 — Round up to the next whole number (you cannot have 0.4 of a card).**
$$K = 5 \text{ Kanban cards}$$

**Interpretation:** The workstation needs **5 Kanban cards** in circulation. This means at any instant, 5 containers (5 × 25 = 125 units) are in the system between upstream supply and downstream demand.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CIM Planning Hierarchy — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Strategic Business Plan<br>2 to 10 years] --> B[Aggregate Production Planning<br>6 to 18 months]
    B --> C[Master Production Schedule MPS<br>3 to 6 months]
    C --> D[Material Requirements Planning MRP<br>1 week to 3 months]
    D --> E[Capacity Requirements Planning CRP<br>Shop Floor Capacity Check]
    E --> F[Shop Floor Control SFC<br>Days to Weeks]
    F --> G[Process Control<br>Real time]
    G --> H[Feedback Loop to MPS<br>Actual vs Planned]
    H --> C
```

**Reading the diagram:** The top-down arrows show the **disaggregation** of plans (broad → specific). The bottom-up arrow from Process Control → MPS shows the **closed-loop feedback** characteristic of MRP-II and ERP systems.

---

## 4.2 MRP System — Inputs, Logic, Outputs (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MPS[Master Production Schedule<br>What and When] --> MRP[MRP Processor<br>Computer Logic]
    BOM[Bill of Materials<br>Product Structure Tree] --> MRP
    ISF[Inventory Status File<br>On Hand, On Order, Safety Stock] --> MRP
    MRP --> P1[Planned Order Releases<br>Time phased orders]
    MRP --> P2[Exception Reports<br>Action Messages]
    MRP --> P3[Performance Reports<br>Plan vs Actual]
    P1 --> PO[Purchase Orders<br>and Work Orders]
    PO --> Shop[Shop Floor Execution]
    Shop --> Feedback[Inventory Status Update]
    Feedback --> ISF
```

**Engineering interpretation:** The MRP block has three inputs on the left and three outputs on the right. The feedback arrow from Shop Floor → ISF closes the loop, turning plain MRP into **closed-loop MRP-II**.

---

## 4.3 Theory of Constraints — 5 Focusing Steps Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1 IDENTIFY<br>Find the bottleneck] --> S2[Step 2 EXPLOIT<br>Maximize its utilization]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 SUBORDINATE<br>Sync all processes to bottleneck]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 ELEVATE<br>Increase bottleneck capacity]
    S4 --> S5{Is the constraint broken?}
    S5 -- No --> S2
    S5 -- Yes --> S6[Step 5 REPEAT<br>Find the new constraint]
    S6 --> S1
```

**Key insight:** The "No" loop is the **most important** — it tells the manager not to chase the next constraint until the current one is fully exploited.

---

## 4.4 JIT Pull System — Kanban Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Cust[Customer Order] --> P1[Final Assembly Station]
    P1 -->|W kanban withdrawal signal| P2[Sub Assembly Station]
    P2 -->|W kanban| P3[Component Fabrication]
    P3 -->|P kanban production order| P3
    P3 -->|Physical part flow| P2
    P2 -->|Physical part flow| P1
    P1 -->|Finished goods| Cust
```

**Reading the diagram:** The *right-to-left* arrows are **information signals (Kanban cards)** that *authorize* production. The *left-to-right* arrows are the **physical flow of parts**. This dual-loop is the essence of the **Toyota Production System**.

---

## 4.5 Aggregate Planning Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q{Is inventory holding cost very high?} -->|Yes| A[Use CHASE Strategy<br>Vary production to match demand]
    Q -->|No| R{Is hiring/firing cost very high?}
    R -->|Yes| B[Use LEVEL Strategy<br>Constant production, vary inventory]
    R -->|No| C[Use MIXED Strategy<br>Combine both, add subcontracting]
    A --> D{Subcontracting feasible?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Chase with subcontracting]
    D -->|No| F[Pure Chase]
    B --> G{Overtime permitted?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Level with overtime]
    G -->|No| I[Pure Level]
```

This decision tree is a **favourite KTU diagram question** in Module 2.

---

## 4.6 ERP vs MRP vs MRP-II — Scope Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MRP [MRP - 1970s]
        M1[Materials only]
    end
    subgraph MRP2 [MRP-II - 1980s]
        M2[Materials + Capacity + Shop Floor Feedback]
    end
    subgraph ERP [ERP - 1990s onwards]
        M3[Finance + HR + Sales + Manufacturing + SCM + CRM + Procurement]
    end
    MRP -->|Evolution| MRP2
    MRP2 -->|Evolution| ERP
```

**Quick recall:** MRP is a **subset** of MRP-II, which is a **subset** of ERP.
