# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**Define Aggregate Production Planning. List the three major strategies with one-line examples.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Aggregate Production Planning (APP)** is the medium-range (3–18 months) planning process that establishes **production rates, inventory levels, workforce size, and backlog** for product families, in order to balance demand and capacity at minimum total cost.
>
> The three major strategies are:
> 1. **Level Strategy** — Production kept constant; inventory + backorders absorb demand variation. *Example: Automobile assembly.*
> 2. **Chase Strategy** — Production rate matches demand each period; workforce is varied. *Example: Fast-food restaurant.*
> 3. **Mixed Strategy** — Combines level + chase + overtime + subcontracting. *Example: Electronics manufacturing.*

*(3 marks — definition 1.5, strategies 1.5)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**Distinguish between MRP, MRP-II, and ERP in a tabular form.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | MRP | MRP-II | ERP |
|:--------|:----|:-------|:----|
| **Scope** | Materials only | Materials + Capacity + Shop Floor | Entire enterprise |
| **Closed Loop?** | No (open loop) | Yes (closed loop) | Yes (fully integrated) |
| **Year Emerged** | 1960s–70s | 1980s | 1990s onwards |
| **Function** | Dependent demand explosion | Capacity-validated material plan | Finance, HR, SCM, CRM, Mfg. |
| **Data Source** | Standalone | Single database (early) | Single unified database |
| **Example** | Initial IBM PICS | IBM COPICS, early SAP R/2 | SAP S/4HANA, Oracle Cloud |

*(3 marks — three rows of 1 mark each)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A manufacturing firm uses Single Exponential Smoothing with α = 0.4. Initial forecast F₁ = 200. Actual demand for months 1–6 is: 200, 220, 210, 240, 230, 250. Compute:
- (i) Forecasts F₂ through F₇.
- (ii) MAD and MAPE for the 6 months.
- (iii) Tracking Signal at end of month 6. Comment on the forecast.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **5 Focusing Steps of Theory of Constraints**. Briefly state the concept of **Drum-Buffer-Rope**.

---

#### Solution to (a)

**Recurrence:** $F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha) F_t$, with α = 0.4.

| t | D_t | F_t (rounded) | Error e_t = D_t − F_t | \|e_t\| | \|e_t\|/D_t (%) |
|:-:|:---:|:-------------:|:--------------------:|:--------:|:-----------------:|
| 1 | 200 | 200.0 | — | — | — |
| 2 | 220 | 200.0 | 20.0 | 20.0 | 9.09 |
| 3 | 210 | 208.0 | 2.0 | 2.0 | 0.95 |
| 4 | 240 | 208.8 | 31.2 | 31.2 | 13.00 |
| 5 | 230 | 221.3 | 8.7 | 8.7 | 3.78 |
| 6 | 250 | 224.8 | 25.2 | 25.2 | 10.08 |

**Computation details (sample):**
- $F_2 = 0.4(200) + 0.6(200) = 200$
- $F_3 = 0.4(220) + 0.6(200) = 88 + 120 = 208$
- $F_4 = 0.4(210) + 0.6(208) = 84 + 124.8 = 208.8$
- $F_5 = 0.4(240) + 0.6(208.8) = 96 + 125.28 = 221.28$
- $F_6 = 0.4(230) + 0.6(221.28) = 92 + 132.77 = 224.77$
- $F_7 = 0.4(250) + 0.6(224.77) = 100 + 134.86 = 234.86$

**MAD calculation** (using months 2–6, n = 5):
$$MAD = \frac{20 + 2 + 31.2 + 8.7 + 25.2}{5} = \frac{87.1}{5} = 17.42 \text{ units}$$

**MAPE calculation:**
$$MAPE = \frac{1}{5}(9.09 + 0.95 + 13.00 + 3.78 + 10.08) = \frac{36.90}{5} = 7.38\%$$

**Tracking Signal at end of month 6:**
$$CFE = 20 + 2 + 31.2 + 8.7 + 25.2 = 87.1$$
$$TS = \frac{CFE}{MAD} = \frac{87.1}{17.42} = 5.0$$

**Comment:** Since |TS| = 5.0 **exceeds ±4 control limit**, the forecast exhibits a **persistent positive bias** (systematically under-forecasting). The forecaster should consider switching to a **trended model (Holt's method)** or increase α.

---

#### Solution to (b)

**The 5 Focusing Steps of TOC (with diagram):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1 IDENTIFY the constraint bottleneck] --> S2[Step 2 EXPLOIT the constraint maximize its use]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 SUBORDINATE all non-constraint processes to bottleneck pace]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 ELEVATE the constraint increase its capacity]
    S4 --> S5{Constraint broken?}
    S5 -- No --> S2
    S5 -- Yes --> S6[Step 5 REPEAT identify new constraint]
    S6 --> S1
```

**Drum-Buffer-Rope (DBR):**
- **Drum** = the schedule of the bottleneck dictates the pace of the entire plant.
- **Buffer** = a time buffer (usually one-third to one-half of the bottleneck processing time) is placed before the bottleneck to protect it from starvation.
- **Rope** = the release of raw material/work to the system is "tied" to the drum — material is released only at the rate the bottleneck can consume it.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A company has the following demand forecast for 6 months and must compare two aggregate planning strategies.

| Month | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | Total |
|:-----:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-----:|
| Demand (units) | 400 | 500 | 600 | 700 | 500 | 300 | 3000 |

- Initial inventory = 50 units; Initial workforce = 8 workers.
- Each worker produces 50 units/month.
- Regular-time cost = ₹80/unit, Overtime cost = ₹120/unit, Hiring cost = ₹400/worker, Firing cost = ₹500/worker, Holding cost = ₹15/unit/month, Backorder cost = ₹30/unit/month.
- Overtime is limited to 20% of regular production per month.
- Subcontracting not allowed.

**Compare Level Strategy (using overtime) with Chase Strategy. Recommend the cheaper one.**

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **MRP explosion process** with a suitable example product structure (BOM tree). List the **four lot-sizing techniques** with one-line merits.

---

#### Solution to (a)

**Level Strategy with Overtime**

- Total demand = 3000; Initial inventory = 50; Production required = 3000 − 50 = 2950 units.
- Spread equally: 2950/6 = 491.67 ≈ 492 regular units/month.
- Workers = 492/50 = 9.84 → 10 workers (hire 2).
- Overtime needed in months 3, 4 (peak demand).

Compute monthly production = 492 (regular) + OT as needed.

| Month | Demand | Reg Prod | OT Needed | End Inv |
|:-----:|:------:|:--------:|:---------:|:-------:|
| 1 | 400 | 492 | 0 | +142 |
| 2 | 500 | 492 | 0 | +134 |
| 3 | 600 | 492 | 0 (492 < 600) | Use 108 from inv → 26 left |
| 4 | 700 | 492 | +200 OT | 26 − 700 + 692 = 18 |
| 5 | 500 | 492 | 0 | 10 |
| 6 | 300 | 492 | 0 | 202 |

(Holding positive throughout, no backorder if OT of 200 used in month 4.)

**Costs:**
- Regular labor: 2950 × 80 = ₹2,36,000
- Overtime: 200 × 120 = ₹24,000
- Hiring: 2 × 400 = ₹800
- Holding: (142+134+26+18+10+202) × 15 = 532 × 15 = ₹7,980
- Backorder: 0

$$C_{level} = 2,36,000 + 24,000 + 800 + 7,980 + 0 = ₹2,68,780$$

**Chase Strategy**

| Month | Demand | Wkrs needed | Action |
|:-----:|:------:|:------------|:-------|
| 1 | 400 | 8 | 0 |
| 2 | 500 | 10 | +2 hire |
| 3 | 600 | 12 | +2 hire |
| 4 | 700 | 14 | +2 hire |
| 5 | 500 | 10 | -4 fire |
| 6 | 300 | 6 | -4 fire |

**Costs:**
- Regular labor: 3000 × 80 = ₹2,40,000
- Hiring: 6 × 400 = ₹2,400
- Firing: 8 × 500 = ₹4,000
- Holding: 0, Backorder: 0

$$C_{chase} = 2,40,000 + 2,400 + 4,000 = ₹2,46,400$$

**Recommendation:** **Chase Strategy is cheaper by ₹22,380.** The company should adopt the Chase strategy because holding cost savings outweigh hiring/firing cost in this scenario.

---

#### Solution to (b)

**MRP Explosion Process — Example:**

Consider a finished product **"Bicycle"** with the following Bill of Materials (BOM):

```
Bicycle (Level 0) — 1 unit required in Week 5
├── Frame Assembly (Level 1) — qty 1 per bicycle
│   ├── Frame Weldment (Level 2) — qty 1
│   │   ├── Steel Tube (Level 3) — qty 4
│   │   └── Welding Rod (Level 3) — qty 1
│   └── Handlebar (Level 2) — qty 1
│       └── Rubber Grip (Level 3) — qty 2
├── Wheel Assembly (Level 1) — qty 2
│   ├── Tire (Level 2) — qty 1
│   ├── Tube (Level 2) — qty 1
│   └── Spoke Set (Level 2) — qty 36
└── Seat Assembly (Level 1) — qty 1
    ├── Seat Post (Level 2) — qty 1
    └── Saddle (Level 2) — qty 1
```

**MRP Explosion Logic (for 100 bicycles, Week 5 due date):**
- Gross requirement of **Bicycle = 100** in Week 5.
- Gross requirement of **Frame Assembly = 100 × 1 = 100** in Week 4 (offset by lead time).
- Gross requirement of **Wheel Assembly = 100 × 2 = 200** in Week 4.
- Gross requirement of **Frame Weldment = 100 × 1 = 100** in Week 3.
- Gross requirement of **Steel Tube = 100 × 4 = 400** in Week 2.
- Gross requirement of **Tire = 200 × 1 = 200** in Week 3.
- ... and so on recursively for every level.

**Netting step:** At each level, Net Requirement = Gross Requirement − (On Hand + On Order) + Safety Stock.

**Four Lot-Sizing Techniques:**

| # | Technique | One-line Merit |
|:-:|:----------|:---------------|
| 1 | **Lot-for-Lot (L4L)** | Order exactly the net requirement — minimum holding cost. |
| 2 | **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** | Minimizes ordering + holding cost (assumes constant demand). |
| 3 | **Periodic Order Quantity (POQ)** | Covers a fixed number of periods' demand; aligns with review cycle. |
| 4 | **Part-Period Balancing (PPB)** | Heuristic that adds periods until the holding cost ≈ setup cost. |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (common mark-losers):**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract initial inventory** in aggregate planning → wrong production target.
> 2. **Mixing up Tracking Signal sign** — bias can be positive (under-forecast) or negative (over-forecast). State the bias direction explicitly.
> 3. **MRP BOM explosion:** Many students fail to **multiply gross requirements across levels** correctly. Always multiply the parent's gross requirement by the per-unit quantity in the BOM.
> 4. **EOQ units:** Don't forget to state the unit ("units/order") and to **round to a whole number** only at the final answer, not intermediate steps.
> 5. **Kanban formula:** You must **round UP** the number of cards — never round down, as this would cause line starvation.
> 6. **TOC:** The 5 steps are sequential, but Step 5 loops back to Step 1 — drawing a loop arrow earns full marks.
> 7. **Not stating assumptions:** Always list assumptions (constant demand, no backorders in JIT, etc.) — examiners award 1–2 marks for them.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Production Planning Techniques**

### Core Concepts to Memorize
- ☐ **Forecasting** = qualitative vs quantitative; SES, MA, WMA, Holt's, Regression.
- ☐ **α (smoothing constant)** in SES: high α ≈ 0.7 = reactive; low α ≈ 0.1 = stable. Choose 0.1–0.3 normally.
- ☐ **MAD** = average of absolute errors; **MAPE** = average of percentage errors; **Tracking Signal** = CFE/MAD.
- ☐ **Aggregate Planning** = 3–18 months plan, 3 strategies (Level, Chase, Mixed).
- ☐ **Level strategy cost** = Regular labor + Holding + Backorder + small hiring.
- ☐ **Chase strategy cost** = Regular labor + Heavy hiring/firing + no inventory.
- ☐ **MPS** = time-phased, end-item-specific; feeds MRP.
- ☐ **MRP Inputs** = MPS + BOM + Inventory Status File. (Memorize as "3 M's" or "M-B-I").
- ☐ **MRP Outputs** = Planned Order Releases + Exception Reports + Performance Reports.
- ☐ **Lot Sizing** = L4L, EOQ, POQ, PPB, Silver-Meal, Wagner-Whitin (optimal but O(n²)).
- ☐ **MRP-II** = MRP + Capacity Requirements Planning (CRP) + closed-loop feedback.
- ☐ **ERP** = enterprise-wide, single database, integrates Finance + HR + Mfg + SCM + CRM.
- ☐ **JIT** = pull, zero WIP, Kanban, SMED, Jidoka, Poka-Yoke, 7 Wastes (Muda).
- ☐ **Kanban formula** = K = (D × L × (1 + S)) / C, **round up**.
- ☐ **TOC** = 5 Focusing Steps; DBR; identifies bottleneck; Goldratt.
- ☐ **Lean** = continuous improvement (Kaizen), VSM, 5S, value vs waste, customer-pull.

### Key Numerical Formulas to Memorize
- ☐ $F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha) F_t$
- ☐ $EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 D C_o}{C_h}}$
- ☐ $K = \dfrac{D L (1+S)}{C}$
- ☐ $T_{takt} = \dfrac{T_{available}}{D_{demand}}$
- ☐ $OEE = A \times P \times Q$
- ☐ $\text{Little's Law: } WIP = TH \times LT$

### Exam Strategy Reminders
- ☐ Always **tabulate** month-wise inventory/production balance in APP.
- ☐ Always **state units** in numerical answers.
- ☐ For comparative questions (Level vs Chase), compute **both totals** then conclude.
- ☐ Diagrams: Mermaid is acceptable; in pen-paper exam use clear **labelled boxes + arrows**.
- ☐ Mention **assumptions** at the start of every quantitative problem (constant demand, single product, no lead time unless specified, etc.).
- ☐ Practice at least **one numerical** from each of: SES, EOQ, Aggregate Plan, MRP explosion, Kanban count.
- ☐ For 14-mark Part B questions, allocate roughly: 3 marks diagram + 8 marks computation + 3 marks discussion/recommendation.

### Common Mistake Patterns
- ☐ Confusing **independent vs dependent demand** — MRP is for *dependent* demand.
- ☐ Thinking JIT means **zero inventory** (it means *minimized* inventory, allowing safety buffer).
- ☐ Saying MRP-II is a software (it's a *methodology*; software examples are SAP, Oracle).
- ☐ Forgetting that **EOQ assumes constant demand rate** — if demand varies, use POQ or Wagner-Whitin.
- ☐ Treating "level" and "chase" as the only options — the **mixed strategy** is the most common in real plants.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aggregate Production Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT613 - COMPUTER INTEGRATED MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Aggregate Production Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Aggregate Production Planning (APP)** is a **medium-range (tactical) planning** activity — typically covering a horizon of **3 to 18 months** — that establishes general levels of **production output, employment levels, finished-goods inventory, subcontracting, backorders, and pricing** in order to meet forecasted aggregate demand at minimum total cost, while respecting capacity constraints.

The term *aggregate* signifies that the plan is developed for **product families** (groups of similar items) rather than for individual SKUs. The planning horizon is decomposed into **time buckets** (e.g., weeks or months), and decisions are taken on a per-bucket basis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 2):** APP sits *between* long-term strategic capacity planning and short-term scheduling (Master Production Scheduling / MRP). It translates the **Aggregate Plan / Production Plan** into actionable decisions for the next 12 months.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bus Booking Counter"

Imagine you run a **bus ticket counter** for a 6-month tourist season. You don't book every *individual seat* months in advance — instead, you decide:

1. *How many buses to deploy each month?* (workforce / production rate)
2. *How many spare buses to keep parked?* (inventory)
3. *Should you hire private buses on surge days?* (subcontracting / overtime)
4. *Should you refund & delay passengers to the next month?* (backorders)

That **monthly resource balancing** is exactly what **APP does for a factory**. It decides *how much* to produce, *when* to produce, and *from which source* — without worrying yet about the exact SKU mix.

## 1.3 Key Planning Variables

| Variable | Symbol (typical) | KTU Notation |
|---|---|---|
| Demand forecast (period $t$) | $D_t$ | $D_t$ |
| Regular-time production | $R_t$ | $P_t$ |
| Overtime production | $O_t$ | $O_t$ |
| Subcontracted production | $S_t$ | $S_t$ |
| Inventory at end of period $t$ | $I_t$ | $I_t$ |
| Backlog at end of period $t$ | $B_t$ | $B_t$ |
| Workforce size | $W_t$ | $W_t$ |
| Hiring / Firing | $H_t, F_t$ | $H_t, F_t$ |

## 1.4 Why APP Matters (Industry Utility)

- **Bridges the gap** between long-range strategy and the Master Production Schedule (MPS).
- Minimizes the **total cost** of meeting demand across a planning horizon.
- Establishes **feasibility** of the demand forecast against plant capacity.
- Provides a **framework for testing** "what-if" scenarios (e.g., 20% demand surge).

> [!TIP]
> In the CIM hierarchy, APP feeds the **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** → which feeds **MRP** → which feeds **Shop-Floor Scheduling**. A flawed APP propagates errors downstream.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The APP Decision Framework

APP decisions are grouped into **three decision categories**, each of which determines a specific cost:

1. **Production decisions** → Regular, Overtime, Subcontracted units.
2. **Workforce decisions** → Hire, Fire, Layoff, Idle-time.
3. **Demand-management decisions** → Backorders, Lost sales, Pricing, Promotion.

The cost function is the **sum of all these decisions** across all periods.

## 2.2 Cost Components (Step-by-Step Logic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every term in the APP cost function maps to a real factory decision.** Memorize the *units* — examiners love testing whether you associate cost type with cost unit.

| # | Cost Term | Symbol | Unit of Cost | Cost Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Regular-time production | $C_R$ | ₹ / unit | $C_R$ |
| 2 | Overtime production | $C_O$ | ₹ / unit ( $> C_R$ ) | $C_O$ |
| 3 | Subcontracting | $C_S$ | ₹ / unit ( $> C_O$ ) | $C_S$ |
| 4 | Inventory holding | $C_H$ | ₹ / unit / period | $C_H$ |
| 5 | Backorder / shortage | $C_B$ | ₹ / unit / period | $C_B$ |
| 6 | Hiring (or increasing workforce) | $C_H^{HR}$ | ₹ / worker hired | $C_{Hire}$ |
| 7 | Firing (or laying off) | $C_F$ | ₹ / worker fired | $C_{Fire}$ |
| 8 | Idle time (under-utilization) | $C_I$ | ₹ / unit short of capacity | $C_{Idle}$ |

## 2.3 Aggregate Planning Strategies

> [!NOTE]
> **Three canonical strategies** are tested every KTU cycle. Learn them as a *cost-tradeoff triangle*.

### (a) Chase Strategy
- Workforce is **flexed every period** to exactly match demand.
- Inventory is held near zero; backorders are minimal.
- **Cost drivers:** Hiring + Firing costs.
- *Best when:* Hiring/firing is cheap; inventory holding is expensive.

### (b) Level Strategy
- Workforce + production rate are kept **constant** across all periods.
- Demand fluctuations absorbed by **inventory buildup** and **backorders**.
- **Cost drivers:** Inventory holding + Backorder costs.
- *Best when:* Hiring/firing is expensive; production is smooth-able.

### (c) Mixed / Hybrid Strategy
- A **judicious combination** of chase and level — most realistic in industry.
- Uses a stable base workforce, with overtime/subcontracting + limited inventory.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **exam-day formula sheet**. Each equation is examinable. Do not skip any row.

| # | Equation | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sum_{t=1}^{T} P_t \;\geq\; \sum_{t=1}^{T} D_t$ | Total production must cover total demand (including initial inventory offset) |
| 2 | $I_t \;=\; I_{t-1} \;+\; P_t \;-\; D_t$ | Inventory balance (no backorders) |
| 3 | $I_t^+ \;-\; I_t^- \;=\; I_{t-1} \;+\; P_t \;-\; D_t$ | Inventory + backorder balance |
| 4 | $W_t \;=\; W_{t-1} \;+\; H_t \;-\; F_t$ | Workforce balance |
| 5 | $P_t \;\leq\; p \cdot W_t$ | Production capped by workforce × productivity |
| 6 | $O_t \;\leq\; O_{\max}$ | Overtime capacity limit |
| 7 | $S_t \;\leq\; S_{\max}$ | Subcontracting capacity limit |
| 8 | $\min Z \;=\; \sum_{t} \left[ C_R R_t + C_O O_t + C_S S_t + C_H I_t^+ + C_B I_t^- + C_{Hire} H_t + C_{Fire} F_t \right]$ | **APP Objective Function** |
| 9 | $W_{\text{avg}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} W_t$ | Average workforce (level strategy) |
| 10 | $R_{\text{avg}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} R_t$ | Average production rate |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "HICS-OBF":** Holding, Idle, Contracting, Subcontracting, Overtime, Backorder, Firing. These are the **seven** cost families KTU examiners will rotate through.

## 2.5 Methods for Solving APP Problems

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Discuss the methods of aggregate production planning."* Use this checklist.

| Method | Type | Best for | KTU Tag |
|---|---|---|---|
| Graphical / Charting technique | Informal | Visualization, comparison | Common |
| Transportation Method | Linear Programming | Multi-period multi-source | **High-yield** |
| Linear Programming (LP) | Optimization | Cost minimization, many variables | **High-yield** |
| Simplex Method (on LP) | Optimization | Solving LP form | Common |
| Linear Decision Rule (LDR) | Heuristic (quadratic cost) | Workforce smoothing | Advanced |
| Management Coefficients Model | Regression-based | Real historical data fitting | Advanced |
| Simulation | Heuristic | Complex stochastic systems | Rare |

## 2.6 Transportation Method Formulation of APP (The KTU Power Topic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Transportation Method is the single most-tested APP technique in KTU.** You will be given a demand/cost table and asked to find the minimum-cost plan.

### Setup
- **Sources (rows):** Production options → Regular-time Period-1, Regular-time Period-2, ..., Overtime, Subcontract, Beginning Inventory.
- **Destinations (columns):** Demand in Period-1, Period-2, ..., Final Inventory, Backorders.
- **Cell cost:** $C_{ij} =$ regular production cost $+$ inventory holding (or $-$ backorder saving) from source period $i$ to demand period $j$.

### Decision rule
**Allocate** to the cell with the **lowest unit cost** first, subject to source-supply and destination-demand limits, until all demand is satisfied. This is solved by **MODI method** or **Stepping-Stone method** to test optimality.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> We will solve a **fully worked KTU-style Transportation Method problem** from first principles. *No step is skipped.*

## 3.1 Problem Statement (KTU-Style)

A manufacturing firm forecasts demand for the next **3 periods** as:

$$
D_1 = 100 \text{ units}, \quad D_2 = 150 \text{ units}, \quad D_3 = 200 \text{ units}
$$

**Production options (per period):**
- Regular time: up to 100 units @ **₹ 10 / unit**
- Overtime: up to 50 units @ **₹ 15 / unit**
- Subcontract: up to 50 units @ **₹ 20 / unit**

**Other parameters:**
- Beginning inventory: $I_0 = 0$ units
- Inventory holding cost: $C_H =$ **₹ 1 / unit / period**
- Backorder cost: $C_B =$ **₹ 2 / unit / period**
- No hiring/firing cost (regular-time capacity is the binding constraint).

**Find** the minimum-cost aggregate plan using the **Transportation Method**.

## 3.2 Step 1 — Total Demand vs. Total Supply

Total demand:

$$
D_{\text{total}} \;=\; 100 + 150 + 200 \;=\; 450 \text{ units}
$$

Regular-time capacity over 3 periods:

$$
R_{\text{total}} \;=\; 3 \times 100 \;=\; 300 \text{ units}
$$

Therefore, we **must** use overtime or subcontract for at least:

$$
450 - 300 \;=\; 150 \text{ units}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Regular time alone is **insufficient**. A dummy (or excess-capacity) destination is *not* needed because we already have more capacity than demand once overtime and subcontracting are included.

## 3.3 Step 2 — Construct the Transportation Tableau

We define **sources** (production options in each period) and **destinations** (demand in each period + a final-inventory + backorder column).

> [!NOTE]
> The transportation tableau for APP has **2 extra columns** beyond pure demand columns: **Final Inventory** and **Backorders**. These allow production in the last period to be absorbed.

| **Source** | **Capacity** | **D1 (100)** | **D2 (150)** | **D3 (200)** | **Final Inv.** | **Backorder** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Regular-P1 | 100 | **10** | $10+1=11$ | $10+2=12$ | $10+3=13$ | $10+2$ |
| Regular-P2 | 100 | — | **10** | $10+1=11$ | $10+2=12$ | $10+2$ |
| Regular-P3 | 100 | — | — | **10** | $10+1=11$ | $10+2$ |
| Overtime-P1 | 50 | **15** | $15+1=16$ | $15+2=17$ | $15+3=18$ | $15+2$ |
| Overtime-P2 | 50 | — | **15** | $15+1=16$ | $15+2=17$ | $15+2$ |
| Overtime-P3 | 50 | — | — | **15** | $15+1=16$ | $15+2$ |
| Subcontract-P1 | 50 | **20** | $20+1=21$ | $20+2=22$ | $20+3=23$ | $20+2$ |
| Subcontract-P2 | 50 | — | **20** | $20+1=21$ | $20+2=22$ | $20+2$ |
| Subcontract-P3 | 50 | — | — | **20** | $20+1=21$ | $20+2$ |
| **Demand** |  | 100 | 150 | 200 | — | — |

> Total supply = $3(100) + 3(50) + 3(50) = 600$. Total demand for *product* = 450. Remaining **150 units** of dummy demand must be assigned → we use a **dummy destination** with zero cost for 150 units, OR an explicit **Final Inventory** column with cost = $C_H$ per period. For this problem, since period-3 final inventory has cost $10 + 3(\text{holding}) = 13$ at most, we **add a dummy "excess" column with cost 0** to absorb the 150 surplus units.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU examiner trick:** When total supply $>$ total demand, add a **dummy demand column of cost 0** equal to the surplus. When total demand $>$ total supply, add a **dummy supply row of cost 0** (representing lost sales).

After adding the dummy column (capacity 150, cost 0), apply the **Minimum-Cost Allocation Rule**.

## 3.4 Step 3 — Minimum-Cost Allocation

Sorting cost cells in ascending order:

| Cost | Cell | Allocation Logic |
|---|---|---|
| **10** | Regular-P1 → D1 (cap 100, dem 100) | All 100 units here |
| **10** | Regular-P2 → D2 (cap 100, dem 150) | 100 to D2 → D2 remaining = 50 |
| **10** | Regular-P3 → D3 (cap 100, dem 200) | 100 to D3 → D3 remaining = 100 |
| **11** | Regular-P2 → D3 | 50 to D3 → D3 remaining = 50 |
| **15** | Overtime-P1 → D1 (cap 50, dem 0) | All 50 → dummy (cost 0) |
| **15** | Overtime-P2 → D3 (remaining 50) | 50 to D3 → D3 satisfied |
| **15** | Overtime-P3 → dummy (cap 50) | 50 → dummy |
| **20** | Subcontract-P1 → dummy | 50 → dummy |
| **20** | Subcontract-P2 → dummy | 50 → dummy |
| **20** | Subcontract-P3 → dummy | 50 → dummy |

The dummy absorbs: $50 + 50 + 50 + 50 + 50 = 250$ units, but we have dummy capacity of 150. So the *last* 100 units of dummy allocation are **wasted** (representing unused capacity). The actual solution is valid because we satisfy all real demand.

## 3.5 Step 4 — Cost Calculation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Cost} \;&= \; (100 \times 10) \;+\; (100 \times 10) \;+\; (100 \times 10) \;+\; (50 \times 11) \;+\; (50 \times 15) \\
&= \; 1000 \;+\; 1000 \;+\; 1000 \;+\; 550 \;+\; 750 \\
&= \; 4300
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Minimum Aggregate Plan Cost = ₹ 4,300**

## 3.6 Step 5 — Verify Optimality (MODI Method)

The **Modified Distribution (MODI) Method** computes opportunity costs $u_i + v_j - c_{ij}$ for all unoccupied cells. If **all** opportunity costs are $\leq 0$, the solution is optimal. Otherwise, the cell with the most positive opportunity cost is the **entering variable**, and a closed loop is traced to find the leaving variable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU**, the examiner may not require a full MODI execution. They expect you to state that the *least-cost method* produces a starting BFS which is then tested via MODI / Stepping-Stone.

## 3.7 Step 6 — Analytical Insight

The optimal plan uses **300 units of regular time** and **50 units of overtime in P2** — *not* a single unit of subcontract. **Why?**

- Subcontracting costs ₹ 20/unit, the highest.
- Overtime (₹ 15) is cheaper than subcontracting, but more expensive than regular time.
- The 50 units of overtime fill the gap between regular capacity (300) and demand (450) without holding inventory or paying backorder costs.

## 3.8 Quick Analytical Matrix (Decision Routing)

> [!TIP]
> **Use this routing table in the exam** to instantly classify an APP scenario.

| If you observe... | Use the strategy... |
|---|---|
| Hiring/firing cost $<$ Holding cost | **Chase** |
| Holding cost $<$ Hiring/firing cost | **Level** |
| Demand variability is high + capacity is rigid | **Mixed with subcontracting** |
| Perishable / seasonal product | **Chase with subcontracting** |
| Capital-intensive plant (e.g., steel) | **Level with backorders** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — APP Position in the CIM Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["Long Range Planning 3 to 5 years"] --> A2["Aggregate Production Planning 3 to 18 months"]
    A2 --> A3["Master Production Schedule MPS 1 to 3 months"]
    A3 --> A4["Material Requirements Planning MRP"]
    A4 --> A5["Shop Floor Scheduling Short term"]
    A2 -.feedback.- B1["Demand Forecast"]
    B1 -.input.- A2
    A2 --> C1["Capacity Plan"]
    A2 --> C2["Workforce Plan"]
    A2 --> C3["Inventory Plan"]
    A2 --> C4["Subcontracting Plan"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** APP receives demand forecast as input, balances it against four sub-plans, and feeds the MPS. The dashed feedback loop represents the iterative reconciliation between forecast and capacity.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — APP Cost Component Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Decision Production Quantity"] --> C1["Regular Time Cost CR"]
    P1 --> C2["Overtime Cost CO"]
    P1 --> C3["Subcontracting Cost CS"]
    P2["Decision Workforce Size"] --> C4["Hiring Cost CHire"]
    P2 --> C5["Firing Cost CFire"]
    P3["Decision Inventory Level"] --> C6["Holding Cost CH"]
    P3 --> C7["Backorder Cost CB"]
    C1 --> Z["Total Cost Z"]
    C2 --> Z
    C3 --> Z
    C4 --> Z
    C5 --> Z
    C6 --> Z
    C7 --> Z
    Z --> OUT["Minimize Z subject to Demand and Capacity constraints"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Strategy Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Demand Forecast Available"] --> Q2{"Holding Cost vs Hiring Firing Cost"}
    Q2 -->|"Holding less than Hire Fire"| R1["Use Chase Strategy"]
    Q2 -->|"Hire Fire less than Holding"| R2["Use Level Strategy"]
    R1 --> S1["Flex workforce period by period"]
    R1 --> S2["Allow zero inventory"]
    R2 --> T1["Constant workforce"]
    R2 --> T2["Build inventory in low demand"]
    R2 --> T3["Backorder in high demand"]
    S1 --> X["Solution by Linear Programming"]
    T1 --> Y["Solution by Transportation Method"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Transportation Method Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1["Define Sources Regular OT Subcontract"] --> M2["Define Destinations Demand Periods Final Inv Backorder"]
    M2 --> M3["Build Cost Matrix production cost plus holding per period"]
    M3 --> M4{"Supply equals Demand"}
    M4 -->|"No"| M5["Add Dummy Row or Column to balance"]
    M4 -->|"Yes"| M6["Apply Least Cost Method to allocate"]
    M5 --> M6
    M6 --> M7["Initial Basic Feasible Solution BFS"]
    M7 --> M8["Test Optimality via MODI or Stepping Stone"]
    M8 --> M9{"All opportunity costs are non positive"}
    M9 -->|"No"| M10["Pivot to improve reallocate via closed loop"]
    M10 --> M8
    M9 -->|"Yes"| M11["Optimal Minimum Cost Plan"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define Aggregate Production Planning. List its key strategies.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Aggregate Production Planning (APP) is a medium-range (3–18 months) tactical planning process that determines the overall production rate, workforce size, inventory levels, subcontracting, and backorder quantities for *product families* so as to meet forecasted aggregate demand at the minimum total cost, subject to capacity constraints.
- **Strategies (1 mark):** The three canonical strategies are:
  1. **Chase Strategy** — vary workforce to match demand.
  2. **Level Strategy** — keep workforce constant; absorb fluctuations with inventory.
  3. **Mixed / Hybrid Strategy** — combination of the above.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**List any six cost components considered in aggregate production planning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark each):**
1. Regular-time production cost.
2. Overtime production cost.
3. Subcontracting cost.
4. Inventory holding cost.
5. Backorder / shortage cost.
6. Hiring cost (and/or firing cost).

*(Idle time cost is a 7th, optional component.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU mandates an **internal choice** in Part B. You are given **either** Question A **or** Question B. We provide *both* below with full model solutions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 — Adapted]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **Transportation Method** of aggregate production planning. How is a balanced transportation tableau constructed when supply exceeds demand?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A company must meet the following demand over 4 periods: P1 = 400, P2 = 600, P3 = 500, P4 = 700. Beginning inventory = 50 units. The regular-time capacity is 500 units/period at ₹ 8/unit, overtime capacity is 200 units/period at ₹ 12/unit, and subcontracting capacity is 150 units/period at ₹ 18/unit. Inventory holding cost = ₹ 1/unit/period. Backorder cost = ₹ 3/unit/period. Find the **minimum-cost plan** using the Transportation Method.

---

#### **Model Solution — (a) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Concept (3 marks):**
- The Transportation Method is a **Linear Programming** formulation where production options in each period are treated as *sources* and period-wise demand is treated as *destinations*.
- A unit produced in period $i$ for use in period $j > i$ incurs an **inventory holding cost** of $(j-i) \cdot C_H$ in addition to the production cost. A unit produced in period $i$ for use in period $j < i$ incurs a **backorder cost** of $(i-j) \cdot C_B$.
- The cost of cell $(i, j)$ is:
$$
c_{ij} \;=\; \text{Production cost at } i \;+\; (j-i) \cdot C_H \quad \text{if } j \geq i
$$
$$
c_{ij} \;=\; \text{Production cost at } i \;+\; (i-j) \cdot C_B \quad \text{if } j < i
$$

**Step 2 — Balanced Tableau when Supply > Demand (4 marks):**
- Compute total supply and total demand.
- If supply $>$ demand, add a **dummy destination column** with demand equal to the surplus supply and with **zero unit cost** in every cell.
- Any allocation to this dummy represents *unused capacity* (or final inventory that is never consumed).
- If demand $>$ supply, add a **dummy source row** with supply equal to the deficit and zero cost — it represents *lost sales* or unmet demand.

> **Closing sentence (1 mark):** The resulting balanced tableau is then solved using the **Least-Cost Method** for the initial BFS and the **MODI method** for optimality testing.

---

#### **Model Solution — (b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Total Demand and Supply (1 mark):**
$$
D_{\text{total}} = 400 + 600 + 500 + 700 = 2200 \text{ units}
$$
$$
\text{Supply} = 4 \times (500 + 200 + 150) = 3400 \text{ units}
$$
Surplus $= 3400 - 2200 = 1200$ units → add a **dummy column of 1200 units with cost 0**.

(Subtract beginning inventory: net production needed $= 2200 - 50 = 2150$ units; the dummy still absorbs surplus capacity.)

**Step 2 — Build the Transportation Tableau (3 marks):**

| Source | Cap | D1(400) | D2(600) | D3(500) | D4(700) | Dummy(1200) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Reg-P1 | 500 | **8** | 8+1=9 | 8+2=10 | 8+3=11 | **0** |
| Reg-P2 | 500 | — | **8** | 8+1=9 | 8+2=10 | **0** |
| Reg-P3 | 500 | — | — | **8** | 8+1=9 | **0** |
| Reg-P4 | 500 | — | — | — | **8** | **0** |
| OT-P1 | 200 | **12** | 12+1=13 | 12+2=14 | 12+3=15 | **0** |
| OT-P2 | 200 | — | **12** | 12+1=13 | 12+2=14 | **0** |
| OT-P3 | 200 | — | — | **12** | 12+1=13 | **0** |
| OT-P4 | 200 | — | — | — | **12** | **0** |
| Sub-P1 | 150 | **18** | 18+1=19 | 18+2=20 | 18+3=21 | **0** |
| Sub-P2 | 150 | — | **18** | 18+1=19 | 18+2=20 | **0** |
| Sub-P3 | 150 | — | — | **18** | 18+1=19 | **0** |
| Sub-P4 | 150 | — | — | — | **18** | **0** |

**Step 3 — Least-Cost Allocation (3 marks):**

Priority cells: all **0-cost dummy cells** are absorbed first. Each source has 0 in the dummy column, so the algorithm allocates surplus supply to the dummy first. Once all "free" allocations are made, the cheapest *real* cells are filled:

- Reg-P1 → D1: cost 8, capacity 500, demand 400 → allocate **400**, demand D1 satisfied.
- Reg-P1 → D2: cost 9, demand 600 → allocate **100**, D2 remaining 500.
- Reg-P2 → D2: cost 8, demand 500 → allocate **500**, D2 satisfied.
- Reg-P3 → D3: cost 8, demand 500 → allocate **500**, D3 satisfied.
- Reg-P4 → D4: cost 8, demand 700 → allocate **500**, D4 remaining 200.
- Overtime-P4 → D4: cost 12, demand 200 → allocate **200**, D4 satisfied.
- All other production capacities → dummy (cost 0).

**Step 4 — Compute Total Cost (1 mark):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Cost} &= 400(8) + 100(9) + 500(8) + 500(8) + 500(8) + 200(12) \\
&= 3200 + 900 + 4000 + 4000 + 4000 + 2400 \\
&= \mathbf{18{,}500}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Optimal Plan Cost = ₹ 18,500.** All remaining capacities (overtime, subcontract) are routed to the dummy at zero cost.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023 — Adapted]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the **Chase, Level, and Mixed strategies** of aggregate production planning. Under what conditions is each strategy preferred?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A firm has the following data for the next 3 periods:

| Period | Demand |
|---|---|
| 1 | 800 |
| 2 | 1000 |
| 3 | 1200 |

Workforce at start = **40 workers**; each worker produces **25 units/period**. Hiring cost = ₹ 500/worker; Firing cost = ₹ 800/worker; Inventory holding cost = ₹ 5/unit/period; Backorder cost = ₹ 10/unit/period. Initial inventory = 0. There is no overtime or subcontracting.

Compute the cost of the **Chase Strategy** and the **Level Strategy**, and recommend the better one.

---

#### **Model Solution — (a) [7 Marks]**

| Strategy | Workforce | Production | Inventory | Hiring / Firing | When Preferred |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Chase** | Flexes every period | Matches demand | Near zero | **High** | Low hiring/firing cost, perishable goods, very high holding cost. |
| **Level** | Constant | Constant average | **Builds up in low-demand, draws down in high-demand** | **Zero** | High hiring/firing cost (e.g., unionized plants), stable demand, low holding cost. |
| **Mixed** | Mostly stable with adjustments | Constant + overtime/subcontract as needed | Moderate | Moderate | Realistic industry case — combines stability with flexibility. |

*Marking split:* 1 mark per row of comparison + 1 mark for the "when preferred" column summary = 7 marks.

---

#### **Model Solution — (b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Workforce needed per period (1 mark):**
$$
W_1 = \frac{800}{25} = 32, \quad W_2 = \frac{1000}{25} = 40, \quad W_3 = \frac{1200}{25} = 48
$$

**Step 2 — Chase Strategy Cost (3 marks):**
- Period 1: Need 32 workers, have 40 → **fire 8** workers. Cost = $8 \times 800 =$ ₹ 6,400.
- Period 2: Need 40, have 32 → **hire 8** workers. Cost = $8 \times 500 =$ ₹ 4,000.
- Period 3: Need 48, have 40 → **hire 8** workers. Cost = $8 \times 500 =$ ₹ 4,000.
- Production = demand exactly, so inventory cost = 0.

$$
\text{Chase Total Cost} = 6400 + 4000 + 4000 = \mathbf{₹\;14{,}400}
$$

**Step 3 — Level Strategy Cost (3 marks):**
- Average workforce needed:
$$
W_{\text{avg}} = \frac{32 + 40 + 48}{3} = 40 \text{ workers}
$$
- So keep workforce at **40** throughout. Production per period = $40 \times 25 = 1000$ units.
- Period 1: $P_1 = 1000$, $D_1 = 800$ → inventory built up = 200 units.
- Period 2: $P_2 = 1000$, $D_2 = 1000$ → inventory = 200 units.
- Period 3: $P_3 = 1000$, $D_3 = 1200$ → inventory drops to 0 (draw down 200).

Inventory holding cost:
$$
C_H \cdot \left[ 200 \cdot 1 + 200 \cdot 1 \right] = 5 \times 400 = \mathbf{₹\;2{,}000}
$$
*(Period 3 ending inventory is 0, so only 2 periods of holding cost apply.)*

Hiring/firing cost = 0 (workforce is constant).

$$
\text{Level Total Cost} = \mathbf{₹\;2{,}000}
$$

**Step 4 — Recommendation (1 mark):**
$$
\text{Level Cost (₹ 2,000)} \;\ll\; \text{Chase Cost (₹ 14,400)}
$$

> **Recommendation:** Use the **Level Strategy** — it is dramatically cheaper because the hiring/firing costs (₹ 500–800 per worker) dominate the holding cost (₹ 5/unit/period).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes in APP problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert time units.** If productivity is "units/worker/month" and demand is quarterly, **always convert to a common period** first. (–2 marks typical)
> 2. **Miscounting the dummy row/column.** If total supply $\neq$ total demand, an unbalanced tableau gives **infeasible** results. (–1 to –2 marks)
> 3. **Forgetting inventory holding cost in transportation cells.** Cell cost = production cost + (j−i)·$C_H$, not just production cost. (–2 marks)
> 4. **Confusing *backorder* with *lost sales*.** Backorder is **deferred** demand; lost sale is **gone forever** — they have different cost treatments in APP.
> 5. **Ignoring initial inventory.** $I_0$ is often a free supply source that must be used in the tableau.
> 6. **Writing strategy names without justification.** Always answer *why* a strategy is preferred, not just *which* one.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **APP** is the **tactical, medium-range** (3–18 months) plan that translates forecasts into production / workforce / inventory / subcontracting decisions for **product families**.
- It sits between **strategic capacity planning** and the **Master Production Schedule (MPS)**.
- **Three strategies:** Chase, Level, Mixed — selection is driven by relative cost of hiring/firing vs. holding inventory.
- **Key cost components (HICS-OBF):** Holding, Idle, Contracting (regular), Subcontracting, Overtime, Backorder, Firing.
- **Inventory balance equation:** $I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t - D_t$ (or $\pm B_t$ with backorders).
- **Workforce balance:** $W_t = W_{t-1} + H_t - F_t$.
- **Transportation Method:** Construct a cost matrix with production cost + holding/backorder per period between source-period and demand-period. Add a dummy row/column to balance supply and demand. Solve by **Least-Cost Method** + **MODI / Stepping-Stone** optimality test.
- **LP Formulation:** Minimize the sum of all 7 cost terms subject to inventory, workforce, capacity, and non-negativity constraints.
- **Lower bound on cost:** Always use **cheapest sources first** (regular time before overtime before subcontracting).
- **Verify optimality:** The opportunity cost in every unoccupied cell must be $\leq 0$ (MODI method).
- **In chase strategy** → no inventory, but high hire/fire cost.
- **In level strategy** → no hire/fire, but inventory + backorder cost.
- **Mixed strategy** is the *practical* default in industry.
- **Graphical method** is a quick visualization tool, but not a substitute for LP / Transportation in formal exams.
- **Linear Decision Rule (LDR)** minimizes a quadratic cost function; **Management Coefficients** uses regression on past data — both are advanced KTU topics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Master Production Schedule
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT613 - COMPUTER INTEGRATED MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Master Production Schedule

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Production Schedule (MPS):** A Master Production Schedule is a detailed time-phased plan that specifies *what finished products (end items) are to be produced, in what quantities, and when they are to be completed*. It serves as the central coordinating link between demand management (forecasts and customer orders) and the detailed shop-floor execution systems (MRP, CRP, and Shop Floor Control) in a Computer Integrated Manufacturing (CIM) environment.

According to the **APICS / ASCM (Association for Supply Chain Management)** dictionary, the MPS is a *plan for the production of end-items to meet the demand derived from the forecast and the actual customer orders, while maintaining a planned inventory level and the production plan for finished goods*.

In the KTU 2024 scheme framework, the MPS is positioned as a **decision-making tool** in the production planning hierarchy that bridges the **Aggregate Production Plan (APP)** and the **Material Requirements Planning (MRP)** system.

## 1.2 Hierarchical Position in CIM Planning

The production planning hierarchy in CIM is structured as follows (from long-term to short-term):

1. **Long-Range Plan (1–5 years):** Business plan, capacity strategy.
2. **Aggregate Production Plan (APP) (3–18 months):** Product families, total volume.
3. **Master Production Schedule (MPS) (1–3 months):** Specific end-items, weekly buckets.
4. **Material Requirements Planning (MRP) (days–weeks):** Component-level scheduling.
5. **Shop Floor Control (SFC) (hours–days):** Machine and labour execution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The MPS is the *only* plan that drives MRP. Without a stable, realistic MPS, the entire downstream material, capacity, and shop-floor planning collapses — this is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question** topic.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen Analogy:**
> 
> Imagine you run a busy restaurant. At the start of the day, your Head Chef (the **Master Scheduler**) writes a schedule on the kitchen board. This schedule lists:
> - **What** dishes (end items) will be prepared today.
> - **How many** portions of each dish (quantity).
> - **When** each batch must be ready (timing — breakfast rush at 9 AM, lunch at 12:30 PM, dinner at 7 PM).
> 
> This kitchen board is the **MPS**. It does not say "how to chop onions" (that is **MRP** and **Shop Floor Work Orders**). It only commits the kitchen to delivering specific finished dishes at specific times. If a customer places an extra order, the chef consults the board to decide whether the kitchen can *promise* that dish (this is the **Available-to-Promise / ATP** concept).
> 
> In a factory, the MPS plays this exact role — committing the manufacturing system to deliver specific finished goods at specific dates, while balancing customer demand, available inventory, and production capacity.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Terminology Used in MPS

The following standard industrial metrics are critical for KTU examinations and must be memorised:

- **Planning Horizon:** The total span of time covered by the MPS, typically **rolling 12 to 18 months** into the future, with the first few weeks frozen.
- **Planning Bucket:** The time period used in the MPS, usually **weekly** in the near term and **monthly** in the far term.
- **Time Fence (Demand Time Fence / Planning Time Fence):** A boundary within the MPS that separates the **frozen zone** (no changes allowed to quantities) from the **flexible zone** (quantities and dates may be adjusted based on real demand).
- **Available-to-Promise (ATP):** The uncommitted portion of inventory and planned production in a period, available for new customer orders.
- **Projected Available Balance (PAB):** The expected on-hand inventory at the end of each period after considering scheduled receipts and gross requirements.
- **Safety Stock:** A buffer quantity held to protect against demand and supply variability. Standard value ranges between **5%–15%** of average demand depending on demand variability (CIM literature standard).
- **Lot Size:** The quantity in which a product is produced or ordered (L4L, EOQ, Fixed Period, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Terminology Block:**
> - *End Item:* A finished product sold to a customer (the lowest level of the MPS).
> - *Independent Demand:* Demand for end items (driven by customers/forecast).
> - *Dependent Demand:* Demand for components (derived from end items via BOM explosion).
> - *Make-to-Stock (MTS):* Producing finished goods in anticipation of demand.
> - *Make-to-Order (MTO):* Producing only after a customer order is received.
> - *Assemble-to-Order (ATO):* A hybrid where key subassemblies are stocked and final assembly is triggered by order.
> - *Engineer-to-Order (ETO):* Product is designed and built only after order.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Objectives of the Master Production Schedule

The MPS is designed to achieve the following five engineering objectives, each frequently appearing in KTU 14-mark descriptive questions:

1. **Drive Material Planning:** The MPS explodes into the MRP system to generate component-level purchase orders and work orders.
2. **Drive Capacity Planning:** Through Rough Cut Capacity Planning (RCCP), the MPS validates whether the planned production can be executed by the available work centres.
3. **Maintain Customer Service Levels:** Ensures finished goods are available when customers demand them, balancing inventory carrying cost and stock-out penalties.
4. **Provide a Basis for Promising Delivery Dates:** The ATP quantity allows the sales team to commit to customers with confidence.
5. **Act as a Communication Tool:** Between sales, production, procurement, and finance departments in a CIM environment.

## 2.2 Inputs to the Master Production Schedule

The MPS accepts five major categories of input data, as illustrated in the flow diagram in Section 4:

- **Demand Forecasts:** Statistical projections of future demand (moving average, exponential smoothing, etc.).
- **Customer Orders (Actual):** Confirmed purchase orders received from real customers.
- **Inventory Status:** Current on-hand stock, allocated stock, and safety stock levels.
- **Production Capacity:** Available machine hours and labour hours from the Resource Planning module.
- **Business Plan & Aggregate Plan Constraints:** The strategic targets handed down from higher-level planning.

## 2.3 Outputs of the Master Production Schedule

The MPS produces three critical outputs that drive the rest of the CIM hierarchy:

- **Planned Order Releases (for end items):** Authoritative production orders for finished goods.
- **Exception Reports:** Flags for planners when PAB drops below safety stock, demand exceeds forecast, or capacity is overloaded.
- **Available-to-Promise (ATP) Quantities:** Used by sales order promising (SOP) systems.

## 2.4 Time Fence Concept — A KTU Favourite

The MPS is divided into **three temporal zones**, each governed by different rules:

| Zone | Period | Rule for Modification |
|------|--------|-----------------------|
| **Frozen Zone** | Inside the **Demand Time Fence (DTF)** (typically 1–2 weeks ahead) | No quantity changes allowed. Only engineering / quality changes permitted. |
| **Slushy Zone** | Between DTF and the **Planning Time Fence (PTF)** (typically 3–6 weeks ahead) | Quantities and dates can be modified within limits, requires planner approval. |
| **Liquid Zone** | Beyond the PTF (6+ weeks to 18 months) | Fully flexible. Forecasts can be replaced with new forecasts, lot sizes can be changed. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Demand Time Fence** is generally equal to the **cumulative lead time** of the end item. The **Planning Time Fence** is generally equal to the **cumulative lead time plus the lot-sizing period** of the end item.

## 2.5 Lot Sizing Techniques in MPS

Lot sizing decides *how much* to produce when the decision to produce is made. The five common techniques are:

- **Lot-for-Lot (L4L):** Produce exactly the net requirement in each period. Lowest inventory carrying cost, highest ordering cost. Default in MRP-II / ERP systems.
- **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) / Economic Batch Quantity (EBQ):** Minimises total cost of ordering + carrying.
- **Fixed Order Quantity (FOQ):** A pre-determined constant lot size is repeated whenever replenishment is needed.
- **Fixed Period Requirements (FPR):** Order covers the demand for a fixed number of future periods.
- **Part Period Balancing (PPB):** Algorithmically balances ordering cost with carrying cost by evaluating the Economic Part Period (EPP).

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following markdown table summarises every KTU-relevant formula, equation, and rule required to solve numerical problems on MPS. (Note: all absolute value / magnitude symbols are written using `\vert` to protect table integrity.)

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Typical Value | Notes |
|---|---------|---------------|----------------------|-------|
| 1 | Projected Available Balance (PAB) | $PAB_t = PAB_{t-1} + SR_t + PR_t - GR_t$ | Units (pieces, kg, etc.) | $SR_t$ = Scheduled Receipts, $PR_t$ = Planned Receipts, $GR_t$ = Gross Requirements |
| 2 | Net Requirement (NR) | $NR_t = \max(0, \, GR_t - PAB_{t-1} + SS)$ | Units | $SS$ = Safety Stock |
| 3 | Planned Order Receipt (POR) | $POR_t = NR_t$ (if NR > 0) | Units | The lot size scheduled for receipt in period $t$ |
| 4 | Planned Order Release (PORel) | $PORel_t = POR_{t+LT}$ | Units | Released $LT$ periods before the receipt date |
| 5 | Available-to-Promise (ATP) | $ATP = (PAB_{prev} + MPS_{t}) - Committed_{t}$ | Units | Calculated at the start of each bucket |
| 6 | Cumulative ATP (discrete order) | $CumATP = \sum_{i=t}^{t+k} ATP_i$ until next MPS receipt | Units | Used when an order exceeds a single bucket |
| 7 | EOQ Formula | $EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | Units / order | $D$ = annual demand, $S$ = setup cost, $H$ = holding cost/unit/year |
| 8 | EOQ Total Cost | $TC_{EOQ} = \sqrt{2DSH}$ | Currency/year | Minimum total annual inventory cost |
| 9 | Reorder Point (ROP) | $ROP = \bar{d} \cdot L + SS$ | Units | $\bar{d}$ = average daily demand, $L$ = lead time in days |
| 10 | Safety Stock (variable demand) | $SS = z \cdot \sigma_{L}$ | Units | $z$ = service-level z-score, $\sigma_{L}$ = std. dev. of demand over lead time |
| 11 | Inventory Turnover Ratio | $ITR = \dfrac{COGS}{Avg.\ Inventory}$ | times/year | Higher is better (typical: 6–12 for manufacturing) |
| 12 | Cumulative Lead Time | $CLT = \sum_{i=1}^{n} LT_i$ | Days / Weeks | Sum of lead times along the BOM path |
| 13 | Demand Time Fence | $DTF = LT_{cumulative\ end\ item}$ | Days | Frozen zone boundary |
| 14 | Planning Time Fence | $PTF = DTF + L$ (lot-sizing period) | Days | Slushy zone boundary |

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

The MPS is the operational backbone of every modern discrete manufacturing enterprise:

- **Automotive Industry:** Schedules vehicle models and option packages at assembly plants weeks in advance; ATP determines if a customer can be promised a specific colour/trim combination.
- **Electronics (Smartphones / PCs):** High-volume MTS, where MPS batches of millions of units must align with global component supply chains (semiconductors, displays).
- **Aerospace:** Mixed MTS / MTO / ATO strategies — engines and avionics may be MTS, while airframes are MTO/ETO.
- **FMCG (Fast-Moving Consumer Goods):** Pure MTS with very short demand time fences (often daily) due to high product velocity and shelf-life constraints.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** MPS must integrate with regulatory batch-release timing (Quality Assurance holds), creating complex time fences.

> [!TIP]
> In CIM, the MPS is the **single point of integration** between the **Sales/CRM system**, the **Production/ERP system**, and the **Supply Chain / Procurement system**. A delay or inaccuracy in MPS ripples through the entire digital manufacturing backbone.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Projected Available Balance (PAB) Algorithm

The PAB is calculated iteratively across the planning horizon. The derivation from first principles begins with the basic inventory identity and extends it across multiple periods.

**Step 1 — Basic Inventory Identity (for a single period):**

The ending inventory of a period must equal the beginning inventory plus all inflows (receipts) minus all outflows (gross requirements / demand).

$$
IB_t + R_t - O_t = IE_t
$$

Where:
- $IB_t$ = Inventory at the **Beginning** of period $t$
- $R_t$ = Inflows (Scheduled Receipts + Planned Receipts) in period $t$
- $O_t$ = Outflows (Gross Requirements / Demand) in period $t$
- $IE_t$ = Inventory at the **End** of period $t$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to Solve for Ending Inventory:**

$$
IE_t = IB_t + R_t - O_t
$$

**Step 3 — Recognise that $IB_t = IE_{t-1}$:**

The beginning inventory of the current period equals the ending inventory of the previous period. Substituting:

$$
IE_t = IE_{t-1} + R_t - O_t
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Safety Stock Constraint:**

The PAB must never fall below the safety stock level. The standard form in KTU / APICS literature is:

$$
PAB_t = PAB_{t-1} + SR_t + PR_t - GR_t
$$

This is the canonical recurrence relation used to build an MPS table.

**Step 5 — Iterative Application:**

Starting with $PAB_0$ = current on-hand inventory, the planner computes $PAB_1, PAB_2, \ldots, PAB_n$ sequentially. Whenever $PAB_t$ falls below the safety stock, a planned order receipt is inserted, which becomes a planned order release earlier in the schedule (offset by lead time).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Available-to-Promise (ATP) Calculation

The ATP logic ensures that sales cannot over-commit production capacity.

**Step 1 — Discrete ATP Within a Bucket (Consumption Order = 1 bucket):**

If the customer order quantity is less than or equal to the inventory and MPS quantity in the current bucket:

$$
ATP_t = (PAB_{t-1} + MPS_t) - C_{t-1,committed}
$$

Where $C_{t-1,committed}$ is the sum of all confirmed customer orders allocated to bucket $t$ *before* the current uncommitted order.

**Step 2 — Cumulative ATP (Consumption Order spans multiple buckets):**

When a single customer order quantity exceeds the ATP of one bucket, the calculation extends to subsequent buckets until the next MPS receipt:

$$
CumATP = \sum_{j=t}^{t+k} \left[ (PAB_{j-1} + MPS_j) - Committed_j \right]
$$

Where $k$ is the smallest integer such that the cumulative sum first becomes non-negative.

**Step 3 — Worked Numerical Demonstration:**

Suppose a product has the following 4-week MPS profile:

- Week 1: Beginning inventory = 100 units, MPS receipt = 200 units, Committed orders = 180 units.
- Week 2: MPS receipt = 200 units, Committed orders = 50 units.
- Week 3: MPS receipt = 0 units (no production), Committed orders = 0 units.
- Week 4: MPS receipt = 300 units, Committed orders = 220 units.

**ATP for Week 1:**

$$
ATP_1 = (100 + 200) - 180 = 120 \text{ units}
$$

This means **120 units** of new customer orders can be promised for delivery in Week 1.

**ATP for Week 2:**

If the 120 units from Week 1 are NOT consumed by the new order:

$$
ATP_2 = (PAB_1 + 200) - 50
$$

We need $PAB_1$ first:

$$
PAB_1 = 100 + 200 - 180 = 120 \text{ units}
$$

$$
ATP_2 = (120 + 200) - 50 = 270 \text{ units}
$$

This gives a **cumulative ATP** of $120 + 270 = 390$ units if the order can be delivered across Weeks 1 and 2.

**Step 4 — Interpretation in CIM:**

In a CIM environment, this calculation is performed *automatically* by the ATP/CTP (Capable-to-Promise) engine, often using real-time shop floor data, and is exposed to the sales team via a web-based configuration and quotation tool.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: EOQ and the Square-Root Economic Lot Size

The **Economic Order Quantity** (also called the **Economic Batch Quantity** in production contexts) is derived by minimising the total annual inventory cost.

**Step 1 — Define the Cost Functions:**

Let $Q$ = order quantity (lot size), $D$ = annual demand in units, $S$ = setup (ordering) cost per order, and $H$ = holding cost per unit per year.

- **Annual ordering cost** $\left( TC_{order} \right)$: Number of orders per year $\times$ cost per order.

$$
TC_{order}(Q) = \dfrac{D}{Q} \cdot S
$$

- **Annual holding cost** $\left( TC_{hold} \right)$: Average inventory $\times$ cost per unit. The average inventory is $Q / 2$ (linear depletion assumption).

$$
TC_{hold}(Q) = \dfrac{Q}{2} \cdot H
$$

- **Total annual inventory cost** $TC(Q)$:

$$
TC(Q) = \dfrac{D \cdot S}{Q} + \dfrac{H \cdot Q}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate $TC(Q)$ with Respect to $Q$ and Set to Zero:**

$$
\dfrac{d\,TC(Q)}{dQ} = -\dfrac{DS}{Q^2} + \dfrac{H}{2} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the First-Order Optimality Condition:**

$$
\dfrac{DS}{Q^2} = \dfrac{H}{2}
$$

$$
Q^2 = \dfrac{2DS}{H}
$$

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}
$$

This is the **EOQ formula**, also valid as the **Economic Batch Quantity (EBQ)** in an MPS context.

**Step 4 — Minimum Total Cost at EOQ:**

Substituting $Q^*$ back into $TC(Q)$:

$$
TC_{EOQ} = \dfrac{DS}{\sqrt{2DS/H}} + \dfrac{H}{2} \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}
$$

$$
TC_{EOQ} = \sqrt{DSH/2} + \sqrt{DSH/2} = 2 \cdot \sqrt{DSH/2}
$$

$$
TC_{EOQ} = \sqrt{2DSH}
$$

**Step 5 — Time Between Orders (TBO):**

$$
TBO = \dfrac{EOQ}{D} \text{ (in years)}
$$

Or in working days: $TBO_{days} = \dfrac{EOQ}{D} \times 250$ (assuming 250 working days/year).

**Step 6 — Numerical Example (KSEB-style KTU numerical):**

A manufacturing plant produces a component with annual demand $D = 10{,}000$ units. Setup cost $S = \text{₹}2{,}500$ per batch. Holding cost $H = \text{₹}50$ per unit per year. Find EOQ, total cost, and number of orders per year.

$$
EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 2{,}500}{50}} = \sqrt{1{,}000{,}000} = 1{,}000 \text{ units}
$$

$$
TC_{EOQ} = \sqrt{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 2{,}500 \times 50} = \sqrt{2{,}500{,}000{,}000} = \text{₹}50{,}000 \text{ per year}
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{D}{EOQ} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{1{,}000} = 10 \text{ orders per year}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Master Production Schedule Table Construction Algorithm

The algorithm to construct an MPS table is as follows — this is the **single most-tested 14-mark topic** in KTU 2024 scheme ESE on this module.

**Step 1 — Initialise the Table:**

Create columns for: Period (Week 1, Week 2, ..., Week $n$), Forecast, Customer Orders, Gross Requirements, Scheduled Receipts, Projected Available Balance (PAB), Planned Order Receipts, Planned Order Releases.

**Step 2 — Set the Initial PAB:**

Enter the current on-hand inventory as $PAB_0$ (before Period 1). Subtract safety stock to get the available portion.

**Step 3 — Calculate Gross Requirements (GR):**

$$
GR_t = \max(Forecast_t, CustomerOrders_t)
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate PAB Recursively:**

$$
PAB_t = PAB_{t-1} + SR_t + PR_t - GR_t
$$

**Step 5 — Detect Net Requirement and Trigger Planned Order:**

If $PAB_t$ would fall below zero (or below safety stock), insert a **Planned Order Receipt** in period $t$ equal to the lot size (L4L, EOQ, etc.). This becomes a **Planned Order Release** at period $t - LT$.

**Step 6 — Repeat Across Horizon:**

Continue iteratively through all periods. Any PAB that goes negative triggers a new planned order. The PAB must remain $\geq$ safety stock at all times.

**Step 7 — Validate via Rough Cut Capacity Planning (RCCP):**

The completed MPS is exploded against the Bill of Labour (or routing file) to verify that the work-centre load does not exceed available capacity. If overload exists, the MPS is **leveled** (re-sequenced) or capacity is augmented (overtime, subcontracting).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Make-to-Stock vs Make-to-Order vs Assemble-to-Order vs Engineer-to-Order

| Dimension | Make-to-Stock (MTS) | Assemble-to-Order (ATO) | Make-to-Order (MTO) | Engineer-to-Order (ETO) |
|-----------|---------------------|-------------------------|---------------------|-------------------------|
| **Demand Trigger** | Forecast | Customer Order (key options) | Customer Order | Customer Order |
| **Inventory Held** | Finished Goods | Subassemblies / Modules | Raw Materials | Nothing |
| **Lead Time to Customer** | Shortest | Medium | Long | Longest |
| **MPS Focus** | Quantity & timing of end items | Module-level production plan | Component-level release | Engineering / design plan |
| **Planning Horizon** | Short (days–weeks) | Medium (weeks) | Medium–Long | Long (months) |
| **Customisation Level** | Low | Medium | High | Total |
| **Inventory Risk** | Highest (overproduction) | Medium | Low | Negligible |
| **Examples** | FMCG, smartphones, automobiles (base models) | Cars with options, configurable PCs | Industrial machinery, machine tools | Power plants, ships, custom aerospace |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The choice of MTS/ATO/MTO/ETO strategy **drastically changes the structure of the MPS**. KTU questions frequently ask students to identify the appropriate strategy for a given scenario and justify the MPS design.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: MPS Position in the CIM Planning Hierarchy

This diagram illustrates how the MPS sits as the central coordinator between high-level planning and detailed execution within a Computer Integrated Manufacturing system.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Business Plan] --> A2[Aggregate Production Plan]
    A2 --> A3[Master Production Schedule MPS]
    A3 --> A4[Material Requirements Planning MRP]
    A3 --> A5[Rough Cut Capacity Planning RCCP]
    A4 --> A6[Shop Floor Control SFC]
    A5 --> A6
    A4 --> A7[Purchase Orders]
    A6 --> A8[Finished Goods Inventory]
    A8 --> A9[Customer Order Promising using ATP]
    A9 --> A3
    A3 -.feedback loop.-> A2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - The solid arrows (downward) represent the *explosion* of plans from long-term to short-term.
> - The dashed arrow (back to A2) is the *feedback loop* — when MPS is infeasible, the Aggregate Plan must be revised.
> - ATP (Available-to-Promise) closes the loop by feeding customer order acceptance back into MPS adjustments.

## 4.3 Mermaid Timeline: MPS Time Fence Zones

This Gantt-style schematic represents the three temporal zones that govern MPS modification rules.

```mermaid
gantt
    title MPS Time Fence Zones Across Planning Horizon
    dateFormat  YYYY-MM-DD
    axisFormat  %b-%d

    section Past
    Actual Production and Shipments    :done, p1, 2024-09-01, 7d

    section Frozen Zone
    Demand Time Fence Region No Quantity Changes :active, f1, 2024-09-08, 14d

    section Slushy Zone
    Planning Time Fence Region Limited Changes : s1, 2024-09-22, 21d

    section Liquid Zone
    Flexible Forecast Driven Zone Fully Adjustable : l1, 2024-10-13, 90d
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Gantt chart:**
> - The **Frozen Zone** spans approximately 2 weeks — quantities are locked.
> - The **Slushy Zone** spans approximately 3 weeks — changes need planner approval.
> - The **Liquid Zone** covers the remaining 3 months — fully flexible and forecast-driven.
> - Total planning horizon: **~4 months** rolling.

## 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Order Promising Using ATP

This diagram shows the interaction between a sales user, the ATP engine, and the MPS during a customer order promise.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sales as Sales User
    participant CRM as Order Entry System
    participant ATP as ATP Engine
    participant MPS as Master Schedule DB
    participant ERP as ERP Production Module

    Sales->>CRM: Enter customer order quantity 250 units
    CRM->>ATP: Request ATP check for delivery in Week 4
    ATP->>MPS: Read current PAB and committed orders Week 4
    MPS-->>ATP: PAB = 400, Committed = 300
    ATP->>ATP: Compute ATP = 400 - 300 = 100 units insufficient
    ATP->>MPS: Check cumulative ATP across Week 4 to Week 6
    MPS-->>ATP: Cumulative ATP = 100 + 250 + 300 = 650 units
    ATP-->>CRM: ATP available 650 units across Weeks 4 to 6
    CRM->>Sales: Promise delivery split Week 4 to 6
    Sales->>CRM: Accept promise and confirm order
    CRM->>ERP: Release order to MRP and update MPS
    ERP->>MPS: Decrement ATP by confirmed quantity
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Flowchart: MPS Construction Algorithm

This diagram visualises the iterative decision process used by a planner (or an ERP heuristic) to construct an MPS bucket-by-bucket.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start of MPS period] --> S2{Is PAB below safety stock}
    S2 -- No --> S3[Continue to next bucket with current PAB]
    S2 -- Yes --> S4[Trigger Planned Order Receipt of lot size Q]
    S4 --> S5[Offset by Lead Time to get Planned Order Release]
    S5 --> S6[Update PAB with new receipt]
    S6 --> S3
    S3 --> S7{Is this the last bucket}
    S7 -- No --> S2
    S7 -- Yes --> S8[Run RCCP capacity check]
    S8 --> S9{Is capacity within limits}
    S9 -- No --> S10[Level the schedule or increase capacity]
    S10 --> S8
    S9 -- Yes --> S11[Finalize MPS and release to MRP]
```
