<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define capacity planning. Differentiate between design capacity, effective capacity, and actual output. Illustrate the relationship among utilization, efficiency, and design capacity with a single expression.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Capacity planning is the systematic process of determining the production capability required to meet the anticipated demand, along with the timing and sizing of resources to deliver that capability.

**Three Definitions (1 Mark):**
- *Design Capacity* — Maximum theoretical output under ideal conditions.
- *Effective Capacity* — Output achievable under realistic constraints (80–90 % of design).
- *Actual Output* — Realized output after losses due to breakdowns and rejections.

**Mathematical Relationship (1 Mark):**

$$U = E \times \dfrac{C_{\text{effective}}}{C_{\text{design}}}$$

Where $U$ is utilization and $E$ is efficiency. This is the single KTU-favoured identity that links all three capacities.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Compare the lead, lag, and match strategies of capacity planning. Under what industry conditions is the match strategy most appropriate?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Strategy | Capacity vs Demand | Risk Profile | Best Suited For |
|----------|--------------------|---------------|-----------------|
| Lead | Capacity added *before* demand | High inventory cost, obsolescence | New products, seasonal demand |
| Lag | Capacity added *after* demand | Lost sales, poor service | Monopoly, mature products |
| Match | Capacity grows *with* demand | Requires accurate forecast | CIM, FMS, agile environments |

(2 marks for the comparison table, 1 mark for the specific justification of the Match strategy in a CIM environment with high product variety and short lead times.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. Always show the **EMV calculation step-by-step** in decision-tree questions — most students lose 2 marks by skipping the *cost-of-choice subtraction* step.
> 2. In Break-Even numericals, **label the units clearly** (₹/unit, units/month). Mixing units forfeits the method marks.
> 3. In Learning Curve problems, the slope $b$ **must be negative** for a normal learning curve. A positive $b$ means a *forgetting curve* — examiners deduct 2 marks.
> 4. Always state the **M/M/1 stability condition** $\rho < 1$ before computing queue parameters; this is a 1-mark freebie.
> 5. In Capacity Requirement Planning flow answers, the examiner expects the **closed-loop feedback path** (CRP → Adjustment → MRP). Forgetting the feedback loop costs 2 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**

A precision engineering company is evaluating two alternative capacity-expansion strategies for the next 4 years:

- **Strategy S1 — Install a new CNC machining centre**: Capital cost ₹20,00,000; expected annual profit ₹8,00,000; life = 4 years; salvage ₹2,00,000.
- **Strategy S2 — Subcontract the entire operation**: Pay ₹3,00,000 per year; expected revenue ₹7,00,000 per year; no salvage.

The MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return) is **10 % per annum**. The company also wants a *Break-Even* and a *Learning Curve* assessment:

**(a)** Use **NPV analysis** to recommend the better strategy. (7 marks)
**(b)** If the first unit of a new component takes **120 hours** and the company operates on an **80 % learning curve**, calculate the **time required for the 8th, 16th, and 32nd unit**, and estimate the **total man-hours** for the first 32 units. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — NPV Analysis (7 Marks):**

The Present Worth factor for a uniform series at 10 % over 4 years is:

$$P/A, i=10\%, n=4 = \dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i(1+i)^{n}} = \dfrac{(1.1)^{4}-1}{0.1 \times (1.1)^{4}} = 3.1699$$

**Strategy S1 (Step 1 — NPV of Profit Stream):**

$$PW_{\text{profit}} = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 3.1699 = 25{,}35{,}920$$

$$PW_{\text{salvage}} = 2{,}00{,}000 \times (1.1)^{-4} = 2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6830 = 1{,}36{,}600$$

$$NPV_{S1} = 25{,}35{,}920 + 1{,}36{,}600 - 20{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}6{,}72{,}520$$

**Strategy S2 (Step 2 — NPV of Net Annual Cash Flow):**

Net annual cash flow = Revenue − Subcontract cost = 7,00,000 − 3,00,000 = ₹4,00,000.

$$NPV_{S2} = 4{,}00{,}000 \times 3.1699 - 0 = \text{₹}12{,}67{,}960$$

**Decision (Step 3):** Since $NPV_{S2} > NPV_{S1}$, **Strategy S2 (Subcontract)** is the financially superior choice. (1 mark for final recommendation, 1 mark for the reason — higher NPV and lower risk.)

---

**Part (b) — Learning Curve (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute the slope $b$:**

$$b = \dfrac{\log_{10}(0.80)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \dfrac{-0.09691}{0.30103} = -0.3219$$

**Step 2 — Unit times for the 8th, 16th, 32nd units:**

$$Y_{x} = 120 \cdot x^{-0.3219}$$

- $Y_{8} = 120 \cdot 8^{-0.3219} = 120 \cdot 0.5 = 60.0$ hours.
- $Y_{16} = 120 \cdot 16^{-0.3219} = 120 \cdot 0.4 = 48.0$ hours.
- $Y_{32} = 120 \cdot 32^{-0.3219} = 120 \cdot 0.32 = 38.4$ hours.

(1 mark each for the three computations, 1 mark for stating the formula.)

**Step 3 — Cumulative Average Time for the first 32 units:**

$$\bar{Y}_{32} = \dfrac{120 \cdot 32^{(1 - 0.3219)}}{(1 - 0.3219)} = \dfrac{120 \cdot 32^{0.6781}}{0.6781}$$

Since $32^{0.6781} \approx 11.32$:

$$\bar{Y}_{32} = \dfrac{120 \times 11.32}{0.6781} = \dfrac{1358.4}{0.6781} \approx 2003 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 4 — Total Man-Hours for 32 Units:**

$$T_{32} = 32 \times \bar{Y}_{32} = 32 \times 2003 \approx 64{,}096 \text{ man-hours}$$

(2 marks for the cumulative average and final total.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** A work centre has **single-server M/M/1 queuing characteristics** with arrival rate $\lambda = 9$ jobs/hour and service rate $\mu = 12$ jobs/hour. Compute the (i) traffic intensity, (ii) expected number of jobs in the system, (iii) expected number in the queue, and (iv) expected waiting time in the queue. The manager wants to add a second identical server. Recompute (i) and (iv) for the M/M/2 system and comment on the capacity improvement. (7 marks)

**(b)** A factory must choose between *Add Capacity* (₹4,00,000 cost) and *No Addition* (₹0 cost). With high demand (probability 0.55), the payoff is ₹10,00,000; with low demand (probability 0.45), the payoff is ₹3,00,000. With No Addition, the payoffs are ₹5,00,000 and ₹3,00,000 respectively. Construct the decision tree, compute the EMVs, and recommend the action. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Queuing Analysis (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — M/M/1 Traffic Intensity:**

$$\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu} = \dfrac{9}{12} = 0.75$$

Since $\rho < 1$, the system is stable. (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Expected number in the system:**

$$L = \dfrac{\rho}{1 - \rho} = \dfrac{0.75}{0.25} = 3.0 \text{ jobs}$$

**Step 3 — Expected number in the queue:**

$$L_{q} = \dfrac{\rho^{2}}{1 - \rho} = \dfrac{0.5625}{0.25} = 2.25 \text{ jobs}$$

**Step 4 — Expected waiting time in the queue:**

$$W_{q} = \dfrac{L_{q}}{\lambda} = \dfrac{2.25}{9} = 0.25 \text{ hours} = 15 \text{ minutes}$$

(3 marks for the four M/M/1 metrics.)

**Step 5 — M/M/2 Re-Analysis:**

For M/M/2, the traffic intensity is re-defined per server: $\rho = \lambda / (c \cdot \mu) = 9 / (2 \times 12) = 0.375$.

Using the Erlang-C formula or M/M/c table, the probability of waiting is:

$$P_{w} = \dfrac{\dfrac{(c\rho)^{c}}{c!} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1-\rho}}{\sum_{n=0}^{c-1} \dfrac{(c\rho)^{n}}{n!} + \dfrac{(c\rho)^{c}}{c!} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1-\rho}}$$

With $c = 2$, $c\rho = 0.75$:

$$P_{w} = \dfrac{\dfrac{(0.75)^{2}}{2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{0.625}}{1 + 0.75 + \dfrac{0.5625}{2} \times 1.6} = \dfrac{0.5625 \times 0.8}{1.75 + 0.45} = \dfrac{0.45}{2.20} = 0.2045$$

The M/M/2 average wait in queue:

$$W_{q}^{(2)} = \dfrac{P_{w}}{c\mu - \lambda} = \dfrac{0.2045}{24 - 9} = \dfrac{0.2045}{15} = 0.0136 \text{ hours} = 0.82 \text{ minutes}$$

(2 marks for the M/M/2 computation.)

**Comment:** Adding a second server reduces the average wait from **15 minutes to 0.82 minutes** — a **~94 % reduction**, dramatically improving the system's capacity to handle bursts. (1 mark)

---

**Part (b) — Decision Tree (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Payoff Table Construction (1 mark):**

| Action | High Demand (P = 0.55) | Low Demand (P = 0.45) | Action Cost |
|--------|-------------------------|-----------------------|-------------|
| Add Capacity | ₹10,00,000 | ₹3,00,000 | ₹4,00,000 |
| No Addition | ₹5,00,000 | ₹3,00,000 | ₹0 |

**Step 2 — EMV of "Add Capacity" (2 marks):**

$$EMV_{\text{Add}} = (0.55 \times 10{,}00{,}000) + (0.45 \times 3{,}00{,}000) - 4{,}00{,}000 = 5{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}35{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}2{,}85{,}000$$

**Step 3 — EMV of "No Addition" (2 marks):**

$$EMV_{\text{No}} = (0.55 \times 5{,}00{,}000) + (0.45 \times 3{,}00{,}000) - 0 = 2{,}75{,}000 + 1{,}35{,}000 = \text{₹}4{,}10{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Decision (2 marks):**

Since $EMV_{\text{No}} (\text{₹}4{,}10{,}000) > EMV_{\text{Add}} (\text{₹}2{,}85{,}000)$, the recommended action is **"No Addition"** to capacity. The expected value loss of the *Add Capacity* decision is:

$$EVPI = \text{EMV}_{\text{with perfect information}} - \text{EMV}_{\text{No}}$$

With perfect information: $(0.55 \times 10{,}00{,}000) + (0.45 \times 3{,}00{,}000) - 4{,}00{,}000$ (only when high) → For high, save the cost; so the best payoff per branch.

$$EMV_{\text{perfect}} = 0.55 \times (10{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000) + 0.45 \times 3{,}00{,}000 = 3{,}30{,}000 + 1{,}35{,}000 = \text{₹}4{,}65{,}000$$

$$EVPI = 4{,}65{,}000 - 4{,}10{,}000 = \text{₹}55{,}000$$

This ₹55,000 is the *maximum* the company should pay for a perfect market forecast.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (High-Density Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Fire KTU Revision Points — Memorize for the Last Hour**

- **Three Hierarchies:** Long-Range (Strategic) → Intermediate (Tactical) → Short-Range (Operational).
- **Three Capacity Metrics:** Design Capacity > Effective Capacity > Actual Output.
- **Identity to lock in:** $U = E \times (C_{\text{effective}} / C_{\text{design}})$.
- **Three Strategies:** Lead, Lag, Match — *Match* is the CIM-favoured approach.
- **Break-Even Unit Formula:** $Q_{BE} = F / (p - v)$.
- **Break-Even Revenue Formula:** $BEP_{₹} = F / (1 - v/p)$.
- **Learning Curve Unit Equation:** $Y_{x} = a \cdot x^{b}$ with $b = \log_{2}(L) = \log_{10}(L)/\log_{10}(2)$.
- **Learning Curve Cumulative Average:** $\bar{Y}_{x} = a \cdot x^{1+b} / (1+b)$.
- **M/M/1 Stability:** $\rho = \lambda / \mu < 1$.
- **M/M/1 Expected Wait in Queue:** $W_{q} = \lambda / [\mu(\mu - \lambda)]$.
- **M/M/1 Expected Number in Queue:** $L_{q} = \lambda^{2} / [\mu(\mu - \lambda)]$.
- **Decision Tree EMV:** $EMV = \sum p_i \cdot V_i - C_{\text{choice}}$.
- **NPV Decision Rule:** Accept project if NPV $\geq 0$ and choose the highest NPV among alternatives.
- **Present Worth Factor:** $(P/A, i, n) = [(1+i)^{n} - 1] / [i(1+i)^{n}]$.
- **EVPI:** $EVPI = EMV_{\text{perfect info}} - EMV_{\text{best decision under uncertainty}}$.
- **CRP Loop:** MRP → Load Work Centre → Compare to Capacity → Adjust → Release.
- **RCCP** operates at *product family* level; **CRP** operates at *individual work centre* level.
- **Leading strategy** favours *make-to-stock*; **lagging strategy** favours *engineering-to-order* with monopolistic products.
- **Capacity Adjustment Factor:** $C_{\text{new}} = C_{\text{old}} \times (1 + d_{d}) / (1 + d_{c})$.
- **Always state units** in numerical answers (₹/month, units/day, hours/unit).
- **In the Learning Curve, $b$ is always negative** for a normal learning curve ($L < 1$).
- **M/M/1 model assumptions** to list in the exam: Poisson arrivals, exponential service times, single server, FIFO discipline, infinite queue.
- **Capacity Planning** in CIM is *closed-loop* — feedback from shop-floor performance metrics (OEE, MTBF, MTTR) updates the Master Production Schedule.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Strategy:** For any 14-mark numerical, write the formula symbolically on the *first line* of your answer. Examiners award 1–2 method marks even if the arithmetic goes wrong. Always close numerical answers with a *one-line engineering interpretation* (e.g., "Hence the firm should add 1 CNC machine to meet the 4-year demand forecast of 50,000 units").

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Systems and Shop Floor Control: Overview of Manufacturing Control Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT613 - COMPUTER INTEGRATED MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Control Systems and Shop Floor Control: Overview of Manufacturing Control Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Manufacturing Control System** is an integrated hierarchy of hardware, software, communication networks, and human procedures that collectively monitor, coordinate, and regulate all activities required to transform raw materials into finished products on the shop floor. In the broader context of **Computer Integrated Manufacturing (CIM)**, it acts as the "nerve center" that bridges the *enterprise/business planning layer* (top) with the *physical process layer* (bottom), translating strategic plans into actionable machine instructions.

According to the **ISA-95 / IEC 62264** international standard (which KTU 2024 syllabus references), the manufacturing control architecture is structured into **five distinct hierarchical levels**, often visualized as the *Automation Pyramid* or *Computer-Integrated Manufacturing Pyramid*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The Manufacturing Control System is **not** a single computer program. It is a **layered, distributed architecture** where decisions become progressively more detailed and time-critical as one moves from Level 4 (Business) down to Level 0 (Process).

### 1.2 The Five-Level CIM Control Hierarchy (KTU Standard Model)

| Level | Functional Name | Time Horizon | Typical Systems / Hardware | Primary Decisions |
|-------|----------------|--------------|----------------------------|-------------------|
| **Level 4** | Enterprise Planning | Months / Years | **ERP** (SAP, Oracle) | What to make, when, in what quantity |
| **Level 3** | Manufacturing Operations Management | Weeks / Days | **MES**, SCADA-Reporting | Order release, resource scheduling |
| **Level 2** | Supervisory Control | Hours / Minutes | **SCADA, HMI, DCS** | Set-points, recipe management |
| **Level 1** | Basic Control | Seconds / Milliseconds | **PLC, CNC, DCS Controllers** | Start/stop, open/close valves |
| **Level 0** | Physical Process | Continuous | Sensors, Actuators, Motors | Physical transformations |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Human Body Analogy — KTU Memory Trick**
> 
> Imagine the manufacturing enterprise as a **human body**:
> - **Level 4 (ERP)** = The **conscious mind and long-term goals** ("I want to become an engineer next year").
> - **Level 3 (MES)** = The **daily planner / project manager** ("Today I will study Chapter 2 from 4 PM to 6 PM").
> - **Level 2 (SCADA / HMI)** = The **eyes and ears** that continuously monitor the environment and feed information back.
> - **Level 1 (PLC / CNC)** = The **spinal cord reflexes** — instant, automatic responses (e.g., pulling hand away from a hot stove).
> - **Level 0 (Sensors/Actuators)** = The **muscles, skin, and nerve endings** that physically interact with the world.
> 
> Just as the body cannot function if the brain cannot send signals to the hand, a factory cannot produce goods if the control hierarchy is broken or poorly integrated.

## 1.4 What is Shop Floor Control (SFC)?

**Shop Floor Control (SFC)** is the *core real-time operational function* of **Manufacturing Execution Systems (MES)** residing at **Level 3** of the CIM pyramid. It is responsible for the *dynamic* execution of production orders on the shop floor — managing work-in-progress (WIP), dispatching operations, tracking resources, and reacting to disruptions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distinction often tested in KTU exams:**
> - **Production Planning (Level 4)** = *"What should happen?"* (long-term, off-line)
> - **Shop Floor Control (Level 3)** = *"What IS happening, and what should happen NEXT?"* (real-time, on-line)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Decomposition — The "Why" and "How"

The CIM control hierarchy is **not arbitrary**; it exists because different decisions have vastly different time-scales, data volumes, and levels of abstraction. Let us break down the rationale:

### Why a Hierarchy Exists
- **Decentralization of time-critical decisions** — A temperature controller must react in milliseconds; an inventory reorder can wait hours.
- **Reduction of data overload** — A PLC generates thousands of data points per second; the CEO only needs a daily summary.
- **Isolation of faults** — A failure at Level 1 should not crash the entire ERP system.
- **Modularity and scalability** — New machines or work-cells can be added at the appropriate level without redesigning the whole system.

### How Information Flows
1. **Downward (Vertical Integration)**: Top-level plans → detailed instructions → machine commands.
2. **Upward (Vertical Integration)**: Machine data → aggregated KPIs → corporate dashboards.
3. **Horizontal (Horizontal Integration)**: Communication between systems at the same level (e.g., PLC-to-PLC, ERP-to-supplier).

## 2.2 Types of Control System Architectures

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **frequently asked 3-mark question** in KTU exams.

| Architecture Type | Description | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|-------------------|-------------|------------|---------------|
| **Centralized** | One central computer controls all machines | Simple, easy to coordinate | Single point of failure, slow for large plants |
| **Decentralized** | Each machine has its own independent controller | Robust, fault-isolated | Hard to coordinate globally |
| **Distributed (Hierarchical)** | A multi-level network with local + central control | Best of both worlds — fast local response + global coordination | Complex design and integration |
| **Open-Loop Control** | Output is not measured/feedback | Simple, cheap | Inaccurate, cannot correct errors |
| **Closed-Loop Control** | Output is measured and fed back to the controller (feedback control) | Self-correcting, accurate, stable | More complex, can become unstable |

## 2.3 Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop Control — Mathematical Foundation

For a **closed-loop (feedback) control system**, the standard control law is:

$$
e(t) = r(t) - y(t)
$$

$$
u(t) = K_c \cdot e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\,dt + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

Where:
- $e(t)$ = **error signal** (difference between desired set-point and actual measured value)
- $r(t)$ = **reference set-point** (desired value)
- $y(t)$ = **measured process variable** (actual output from sensor)
- $u(t)$ = **control signal** sent to the actuator
- $K_c$ = **proportional gain**
- $K_i$ = **integral gain** (eliminates steady-state error)
- $K_d$ = **derivative gain** (anticipates future error)

The three-term controller above is the famous **PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) controller** — the workhorse of Level 1 basic control in nearly every KTU-relevant manufacturing scenario.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Control System Metrics

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Typical Range / Unit | KTU Use Case |
|---------------|---------|----------------------|--------------|
| $r(t)$ | Set-point / Reference | Engineering units (°C, mm, bar) | Target temperature for a furnace |
| $y(t)$ | Process variable (PV) | Same units as $r(t)$ | Actual temperature from thermocouple |
| $e(t)$ | Error signal | Same units as PV | Difference $r(t) - y(t)$ |
| $K_c$ | Proportional gain | Dimensionless | Determines response speed |
| $T_i$ | Integral time constant | **seconds (s)** | $K_i = K_c / T_i$ |
| $T_d$ | Derivative time constant | **seconds (s)** | $K_d = K_c \cdot T_d$ |
| $T_s$ | Sampling time | **milliseconds (ms)** | PLC scan cycle (typically 1–50 ms) |
| OEE | Overall Equipment Effectiveness | **0 to 1 (or 0% to 100%)** | MES-level KPI |
| MTBF | Mean Time Between Failures | **hours** | Reliability metric |
| MTTR | Mean Time To Repair | **hours** | Maintainability metric |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Discrete Manufacturing (Automotive, Electronics)**: SFC systems dispatch Kanban signals to assembly lines and track every part by serial number.
- **Process Manufacturing (Oil & Gas, Pharma)**: DCS at Level 2 maintains reactor temperature within $\pm 0.5°\text{C}$ using PID loops.
- **Job-Shop Manufacturing (Aerospace, Tool Rooms)**: SFC dynamically re-sequences operations based on machine availability and due dates.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Steady-State Error in a Unity Feedback System

Consider the classic **unity feedback closed-loop system** widely tested in KTU:

> A CNC milling machine's spindle speed controller. The set-point $r(t)$ is the desired RPM, the actual measured speed $y(t)$ comes from a tachometer, and the controller $G_c(s)$ drives the motor with signal $u(t)$.

The forward path transfer function is $G_c(s) \cdot G_p(s)$, and the feedback path is unity $H(s) = 1$.

### Step 1 — Define the Closed-Loop Transfer Function

Using block-diagram reduction, the relationship between output $Y(s)$ and reference $R(s)$ is:

$$
\frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = T(s) = \frac{G_c(s)\,G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s)\,G_p(s)}
$$

### Step 2 — Express the Error Signal in the s-Domain

The error is $E(s) = R(s) - Y(s)$. Substituting $Y(s) = T(s)\,R(s)$:

$$
E(s) = R(s) - T(s)\,R(s) = R(s)\bigl[1 - T(s)\bigr]
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the Transfer Function

$$
E(s) = R(s)\left[1 - \frac{G_c(s)\,G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s)\,G_p(s)}\right]
$$

### Step 4 — Simplify the Bracket

$$
1 - \frac{G_c(s)\,G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s)\,G_p(s)} = \frac{1 + G_c(s)G_p(s) - G_c(s)G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s)G_p(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + G_c(s)G_p(s)}
$$

### Step 5 — Final Error Expression

$$
E(s) = \frac{R(s)}{1 + G_c(s)\,G_p(s)}
$$

### Step 6 — Apply the Final Value Theorem for Steady-State Error

$$
e_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty} e(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} s\,E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s\,R(s)}{1 + G_c(s)\,G_p(s)}
$$

### Step 7 — Evaluate for a Unit Step Input $R(s) = 1/s$

For a step change in set-point (the most common KTU scenario):

$$
e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s \cdot (1/s)}{1 + G_c(s)\,G_p(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p}
$$

where $K_p = G_c(0)\,G_p(0)$ is the **position loop-gain** (DC gain of the open-loop system).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This derivation proves a fundamental result — *a pure proportional controller always has a non-zero steady-state error for a step input.* This is exactly why **integral action** $K_i$ is added in the PID controller, which forces $e_{ss} \to 0$.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — KTU Comparison of Control Hierarchy Levels

| Parameter | Level 4 (ERP) | Level 3 (MES / SFC) | Level 2 (SCADA) | Level 1 (PLC) | Level 0 (Process) |
|-----------|---------------|----------------------|-----------------|----------------|-------------------|
| **Time horizon** | Years → Months | Weeks → Days | Hours → Minutes | Seconds → ms | Continuous |
| **Data volume** | Low (aggregated) | Medium | High | Very High | Extremely High |
| **Decision type** | Strategic | Tactical | Supervisory | Regulatory | Physical |
| **Communication** | Ethernet / WAN | Ethernet / LAN | Fieldbus, OPC-UA | Fieldbus (Profibus, Modbus) | 4–20 mA, voltage |
| **Software** | SAP, Oracle | MES suites | SCADA HMI | Ladder logic, Structured Text | None (hardware) |
| **Failure impact** | Business loss | Production delay | Quality issues | Machine damage | Safety hazard |
| **KTU example** | Annual production plan | Order release to a work-cell | HMI displaying tank level | PID loop in PLC | Thermocouple + heater |

## 3.3 Worked Example — Computing OEE for a KTU Exam Question

> **Question (typical 7-mark problem):**
> A CNC machine operates for one 8-hour shift. Planned production time = 7 hours (1 hr scheduled maintenance). It actually produced 280 good parts out of a theoretical maximum of 400. The ideal cycle time is 0.8 minutes per part. Compute the **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)**.

**Step 1 — Availability**
Availability measures actual runtime vs. planned runtime.

$$
\text{Availability} = \frac{\text{Actual Run Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}
$$

Assume actual run time (after stoppages) = 5.6 hours:

$$
\text{Availability} = \frac{5.6\,\text{hr}}{7.0\,\text{hr}} = 0.800
$$

**Step 2 — Performance**
Performance compares theoretical max output to actual output for the run time.

$$
\text{Performance} = \frac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Actual Run Time}}
$$

Convert actual run time to minutes: $5.6\,\text{hr} = 336\,\text{min}$.

$$
\text{Performance} = \frac{0.8 \times 280}{336} = \frac{224}{336} = 0.667
$$

**Step 3 — Quality**
Quality measures good pieces vs. total pieces produced.

$$
\text{Quality} = \frac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces}} = \frac{280}{400} = 0.700
$$

**Step 4 — OEE**

$$
\text{OEE} = \text{Availability} \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality}
$$

$$
\text{OEE} = 0.800 \times 0.667 \times 0.700 = 0.3735 \approx 37.4\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **World-class OEE benchmark = 85%**. A KTU answer would comment on whether the machine is performing well and suggest improvements (e.g., reduce changeover time to improve Availability).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The CIM Automation Pyramid — Functional Block Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **5-level CIM control hierarchy**, including the dominant information flow directions and representative systems at each tier.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L4["LEVEL 4: ENTERPRISE PLANNING - ERP System"]
    L3["LEVEL 3: MANUFACTURING OPERATIONS - MES and Shop Floor Control"]
    L2["LEVEL 2: SUPERVISORY CONTROL - SCADA and HMI"]
    L1["LEVEL 1: BASIC CONTROL - PLC and CNC and DCS"]
    L0["LEVEL 0: PHYSICAL PROCESS - Sensors and Actuators and Motors"]

    L4 -- "Production Plan and KPIs" --> L3
    L3 -- "Order Release and Resource Allocation" --> L2
    L2 -- "Setpoints and Recipes" --> L1
    L1 -- "On Off Signals and Pulse Trains" --> L0

    L0 -- "Raw Sensor Data 4 to 20 mA" --> L1
    L1 -- "Process Variables" --> L2
    L2 -- "Production Reports" --> L3
    L3 -- "Cost and Yield Data" --> L4

    classDef strategic fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef tactical fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef supervisory fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#b53d00,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef basic fill:#c62828,stroke:#7f0000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef physical fill:#4a148c,stroke:#2a0854,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px

    class L4 strategic
    class L3 tactical
    class L2 supervisory
    class L1 basic
    class L0 physical
```

## 4.2 Generic Closed-Loop Control System — Block Diagram

This diagram represents the **canonical feedback control loop** used at Level 1 of the hierarchy (e.g., temperature control, speed control, position control).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R["R(S) Setpoint Reference"] --> SUM1(("SUM: Plus or Minus"))
    SUM1 --> E["E(S) Error Signal"]
    E --> GC["GC(S) PID Controller"]
    GC --> U["U(S) Control Signal"]
    U --> PLANT["GP(S) Plant or Process"]
    PLANT --> Y["Y(S) Measured Output"]
    Y --> H["H(S) Feedback Sensor Gain Equals 1"]
    H --> SUM1

    classDef sigBox fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000000
    classDef ctrlBox fill:#ffe082,stroke:#ff6f00,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000000
    classDef plantBox fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000000

    class R,E,U,Y sigBox
    class GC,ctrlBox
    class PLANT,H plantBox
```

## 4.3 Shop Floor Control (SFC) — Functional Decomposition Diagram

The SFC module is *not* a monolithic block. It comprises **eight core functions** (per the ISA-95 standard). The diagram below shows how these functions interlink within Level 3.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    INPUT["INPUT: Production Orders from Level 4 ERP"]
    INPUT --> ORDREL["FUNCTION 1: Order Release Management"]
    ORDREL --> SCHED["FUNCTION 2: Detailed Scheduling"]
    SCHED --> DISP["FUNCTION 3: Dispatching Operations to Machines"]
    DISP --> EXEC["FUNCTION 4: Execution and Tracking at Workstations"]
    EXEC --> ANALYZE{"FUNCTION 5: Exception and Performance Analysis"}
    ANALYZE -- "On Track" --> QUAL["FUNCTION 6: Quality Data Collection"]
    ANALYZE -- "Exception Detected" --> RESP["FUNCTION 7: Exception Response and Recovery"]
    QUAL --> LAB["FUNCTION 8: Labour and Resource Tracking"]
    RESP --> DISP
    LAB --> OUTPUT["OUTPUT: Actual Order Status and KPIs to Level 4"]

    classDef inout fill:#90caf9,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef func fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef logic fill:#ffcc80,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    class INPUT,OUTPUT inout
    class ORDREL,SCHED,DISP,EXEC,QUAL,LAB,func
    class ANALYZE,RESP logic
```

## 4.4 Information Flow Summary (Bidirectional Vertical Integration)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Downward Flow: Strategic to Tactical to Operational to Physical"]
    B["Upward Flow: Physical Data to Status to Reports to KPIs"]
    A ~~~ B
    A -- "Plans" --> B
    B -- "Feedback" --> A
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse ERP with MES** — ERP is at Level 4 (planning), MES is at Level 3 (execution). Many students write them interchangeably and lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Always state the level number** when asked about a control system. Writing only "SCADA" is incomplete — you must write "Level 2 SCADA system".
> 3. **OEE calculations require all three sub-metrics** — A common mistake is computing only Availability or only Quality. The KTU model answer explicitly shows $A \times P \times Q$.
> 4. **Mention both directions of vertical integration** — top-down (commands) AND bottom-up (data). One-direction answers are marked incomplete.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Differentiate between ERP (Level 4) and MES (Level 3) in a CIM environment.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | ERP (Level 4) | MES (Level 3) |
|--------|---------------|---------------|
| **Purpose** | Enterprise-wide resource planning and long-term scheduling | Real-time execution of production orders on the shop floor |
| **Time horizon** | Months to years | Days to weeks |
| **Function** | *"What to make?"* — order intake, finance, supply chain | *"What is happening now?"* — dispatching, WIP tracking |
| **Example systems** | SAP, Oracle, Microsoft Dynamics | Wonderware, Apriso, Siemens Opcenter |

*(1 mark each for: definition/purpose, time horizon difference, and function/example. 1 mark for clarity.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Define "Shop Floor Control" and list any four of its primary functions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Shop Floor Control (SFC) is the real-time operational function within the Manufacturing Execution System (MES) that manages, executes, tracks, and reports on production activities on the shop floor, ensuring that the actual production aligns with the planned schedule.

**Any four functions (0.5 mark each = 2 marks):**
1. **Order release** — authorizing the start of production orders
2. **Dispatching** — assigning operations to specific machines and operators
3. **Resource allocation** — managing labour, tools, and materials
4. **Production tracking** — monitoring WIP and operation completion in real time
5. **Exception management** — detecting and responding to delays, breakdowns, or quality issues
6. **Performance analysis** — computing KPIs such as OEE and cycle time adherence

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Both are compulsory. **Internal choice** = Question A or Question B.

---

### 📘 QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **five-level CIM control hierarchy** clearly stating the functions, time horizons, and typical systems at each level. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A manufacturing cell operates for a 10-hour shift with 1 hour of planned downtime. The actual operating time is 8 hours. During this time, the cell produced 500 units. The ideal cycle time is 0.5 minutes per unit. After quality inspection, 450 units were found to be conforming. Calculate **Availability, Performance, Quality, and OEE**. Comment on the result. *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution

### Part (a) — The Five-Level CIM Control Hierarchy (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):** The CIM control hierarchy is a layered architecture defined by the **ISA-95** standard, in which decisions become progressively more detailed, more time-critical, and more localized as one moves from Level 4 down to Level 0.

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** *(Draw the Automation Pyramid — see SECTION 4.1 for the structure.)*

The five levels from top to bottom are:

1. **Level 4 — Enterprise Planning (ERP)** *(0.5 mark)*
   - **Function:** Long-term business planning, demand forecasting, master production scheduling.
   - **Time horizon:** Months to years.
   - **Systems:** SAP, Oracle, Microsoft Dynamics.

2. **Level 3 — Manufacturing Operations Management (MES / SFC)** *(0.5 mark)*
   - **Function:** Order release, detailed scheduling, shop floor control, quality management, resource tracking.
   - **Time horizon:** Days to weeks.
   - **Systems:** MES suites (Apriso, Siemens Opcenter, Wonderware).

3. **Level 2 — Supervisory Control (SCADA / HMI)** *(0.5 mark)*
   - **Function:** Real-time monitoring, supervisory set-point control, recipe management, alarming.
   - **Time horizon:** Hours to minutes.
   - **Systems:** SCADA software, HMI panels, DCS supervisory stations.

4. **Level 1 — Basic Control (PLC / CNC / DCS Controllers)** *(0.5 mark)*
   - **Function:** Direct regulatory control using PID loops, sequential logic, motion control.
   - **Time horizon:** Seconds to milliseconds.
   - **Systems:** PLCs (Siemens S7, Allen-Bradley), CNC controllers, DCS controllers.

5. **Level 0 — Physical Process** *(0.5 mark)*
   - **Function:** Physical transformations of raw materials using actuators, and measurement using sensors.
   - **Time horizon:** Continuous.
   - **Hardware:** Motors, valves, heaters, thermocouples, pressure transmitters.

**Vertical Integration (0.5 mark):** Information flows in *both* directions — top-down for commands and bottom-up for data — making the pyramid a fully integrated closed-loop enterprise.

---

### Part (b) — OEE Calculation (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- Total shift time = 10 hr
- Planned downtime = 1 hr → Planned Production Time = 9 hr
- Actual operating time = 8 hr
- Total pieces produced = 500
- Good pieces = 450
- Ideal cycle time = 0.5 min/unit

**Step 1 — Availability (1.5 marks)**

$$
A = \frac{\text{Actual Run Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}} = \frac{8}{9} = 0.889
$$

**Step 2 — Performance (2 marks)**

$$
P = \frac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Actual Run Time}}
$$

Convert actual run time to minutes: $8\,\text{hr} = 480\,\text{min}$.

$$
P = \frac{0.5 \times 500}{480} = \frac{250}{480} = 0.521
$$

**Step 3 — Quality (1.5 marks)**

$$
Q = \frac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces}} = \frac{450}{500} = 0.900
$$

**Step 4 — OEE (1 mark)**

$$
\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q = 0.889 \times 0.521 \times 0.900 = 0.4167 \approx 41.67\%
$$

**Step 5 — Comment (1 mark)**

> The OEE of 41.67% is significantly below the world-class benchmark of 85%. The primary loss is in **Performance** (only 52.1%), suggesting the cell is running much slower than its ideal cycle — likely due to minor stoppages, idling, or reduced speed operation. The Availability (88.9%) and Quality (90%) are reasonable. **Recommendation:** Investigate the causes of speed losses using SFC performance analysis tools and apply SMED (Single-Minute Exchange of Die) and overall equipment maintenance to push OEE above 75%.

---

### 📗 QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** Explain the **closed-loop (feedback) control system** with a neat block diagram. Derive the expression for **steady-state error** of a unity feedback system subjected to a unit step input. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **eight core functions of Shop Floor Control (SFC)** as per the ISA-95 model. How does SFC differ from the supervisory control level (Level 2 SCADA)? *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution

### Part (a) — Closed-Loop Control and Steady-State Error (7 marks)

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** *(Refer to the closed-loop block diagram in SECTION 4.2.)*

The standard unity-feedback closed-loop system has:
- Reference $R(s)$ → Comparator → Error $E(s)$ → Controller $G_c(s)$ → Plant $G_p(s)$ → Output $Y(s)$
- Output $Y(s)$ is fed back through $H(s) = 1$ to the comparator.

**Closed-Loop Transfer Function (1.5 marks):**

$$
T(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G_c(s)\,G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s)\,G_p(s)}
$$

**Error Signal Derivation (2 marks):**

$$
E(s) = R(s) - Y(s) = R(s)\left[1 - T(s)\right] = \frac{R(s)}{1 + G_c(s)\,G_p(s)}
$$

**Steady-State Error for Unit Step (1.5 marks):**

Using the Final Value Theorem with $R(s) = 1/s$:

$$
e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} sE(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s(1/s)}{1 + G_c(s)G_p(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p}
$$

where $K_p = G_c(0)G_p(0)$ is the DC loop-gain. A higher $K_p$ reduces the steady-state error, motivating the use of integral action in the PID controller to drive $e_{ss} \to 0$.

---

### Part (b) — Eight Core Functions of SFC (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):** As per the **ISA-95** standard, Shop Floor Control within MES comprises eight core functions that collectively transform planned orders into completed products on the shop floor.

**Eight Functions (4 marks — 0.5 mark each):**

1. **Resource Allocation and Tracking** — assigning and monitoring labour, machines, tools, and materials.
2. **Dispatching and Production** — sequencing and releasing operations to specific work centres.
3. **Execution Management** — tracking the actual start, progress, and completion of operations.
4. **Detailed Scheduling** — producing short-term schedules in sync with available capacity.
5. **Product Definition Management** — managing routing, recipes, and work instructions.
6. **Performance Analysis** — computing OEE, throughput, cycle-time adherence.
7. **Exception Management** — detecting deviations and triggering recovery actions.
8. **Labour Management** — tracking operator activities, skills, and attendance.

**Difference from Level 2 SCADA (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Level 3 SFC | Level 2 SCADA |
|--------|-------------|---------------|
| **Scope** | Production-wide (orders, resources, quality) | Machine or process-unit specific |
| **Time horizon** | Hours to days | Seconds to minutes |
| **Data handled** | Order status, WIP, labour | Real-time process variables (temperature, pressure) |
| **Decision type** | Operational / dispatching | Regulatory / supervisory |
| **Example** | Re-sequence orders after a breakdown | Adjust PID set-points on a reactor |

> **In short:** SFC decides *what to make and in what order*; SCADA decides *how to run the equipment to execute that plan*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute KTU revision checklist — read this 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- ✅ **The CIM pyramid has exactly 5 levels** (L4–L0). Memorize the order: **E**RP, **M**ES, **S**CADA, **P**LC, **P**rocess.
- ✅ **L4 = Planning; L3 = Execution; L2 = Supervision; L1 = Basic Control; L0 = Physical Hardware.**
- ✅ **SFC lives at Level 3 (MES)** and is the *operational* counterpart to ERP's *planning* function.
- ✅ **Vertical integration is bidirectional** — never say only "top-down".
- ✅ **Open-loop control has NO feedback**; **closed-loop has feedback** with a comparator summing junction.
- ✅ **PID control** = Proportional + Integral + Derivative — used at Level 1.
- ✅ **Steady-state error for unit step** in a unity feedback system = $\dfrac{1}{1+K_p}$.
- ✅ **OEE = Availability × Performance × Quality**. World-class benchmark is **85%**.
  - $A = \dfrac{\text{Actual Run Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}$
  - $P = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Actual Run Time}}$
  - $Q = \dfrac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces}}$
- ✅ **The eight SFC functions** (per ISA-95) are: Resource Allocation, Dispatching, Execution, Detailed Scheduling, Product Definition, Performance Analysis, Exception Management, Labour Management.
- ✅ **SFC ≠ SCADA.** SFC operates at *operational level* (hours/days); SCADA operates at *supervisory level* (seconds/minutes).
- ✅ **MTBF, MTTR, OEE** are the three most common Level-3 KPIs the examiner will test.
- ✅ In a 14-mark question, always include a **neat block diagram** — KTU examiners award 2–3 marks purely for correct, well-labelled diagrams.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shop Floor Control Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT613 - COMPUTER INTEGRATED MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Shop Floor Control Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shop Floor Control (SFC)** — also referred to as **Production Activity Control (PAC)** in modern CIM literature — is the *real-time, operational-level function* of a manufacturing organization that **initiates, executes, monitors, and dynamically regulates** the flow of work-in-progress (WIP) through the shop floor so that the **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** is fulfilled with minimum deviation in cost, time, and quality.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, SFC is the **bridge between planning (MRP II / ERP)** and **physical execution on the shop floor**. It encompasses a closed-loop set of activities: **Order Release → Dispatching → Execution Monitoring → Exception Handling → Feedback to Planning**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (KTU Board Favourite):**
> - **Planning (MRP)** answers: *WHAT* to make, *WHEN*, and *HOW MUCH*?
> - **Shop Floor Control (SFC)** answers: *HOW* to execute it, *WHICH JOB* goes next, and *WHAT TO DO* when things go wrong?

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **busy airport control tower**. The **flight plan** (analogous to MRP) tells pilots where they *should* fly and at what time. But the actual **air traffic controllers** in the tower (analogous to SFC) make the *real-time* decisions: which plane lands first, which is held, which runway to use, what to do when a plane reports a technical snag. They constantly monitor, re-prioritize, and re-route.

Similarly, on a manufacturing shop floor:
- **MRP** says "Job J-101 must finish by Friday on Machine M3 after going through M1 and M2."
- **SFC** decides *right now*: of the 12 jobs waiting at Machine M1, *which one* enters first, *what priority* each carries, *what happens* if Job J-101's tool breaks, and *how* to communicate this to the planning department for re-planning.

> [!TIP]
> Think of SFC as the **"traffic police of manufacturing"** — it does not decide the destination (planning does), but it controls the *flow*, the *speed*, and the *exceptions*.

## 1.3 Primary Objectives of Shop Floor Control

| # | Objective | Practical Meaning |
|---|-----------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Schedule Adherence** | Ensure jobs complete close to planned due dates. |
| 2 | **Minimize WIP** | Avoid pile-up of semi-finished goods (reduces inventory cost). |
| 3 | **Maximize Resource Utilization** | Keep machines and labour productive (target **> 85% OEE**). |
| 4 | **Reduce Lead Time** | Compress order-to-delivery cycle. |
| 5 | **Real-Time Visibility** | Provide status information to MRP/ERP at any instant. |
| 6 | **Exception Handling** | Detect & resolve deviations (breakdowns, scrap, shortages). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Performance Metric — OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness):**
> $$\text{OEE} = \text{Availability} \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality}$$
> World-class benchmark: **OEE ≥ 85%**.

## 1.4 Position of SFC in the CIM Hierarchy

SFC sits in the **middle layer** of the **CIMOSA / Purdue Reference Model** architecture, sitting *below* planning (MRP II) and *above* the actual physical processes (sensors, machines, PLCs).

> **Hierarchy:** `ERP (Strategic) → MRP II (Tactical) → MES / SFC (Operational) → Machine Control (Real-Time)`
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Core Functions of Shop Floor Control

Shop Floor Control is not a single activity; it is a **cyclic 5-step process** that runs continuously (often every shift, hour, or in real-time in Industry 4.0 setups).

### 🔹 Function 1 — **Order Release**
- The act of **releasing planned orders** from MRP into the active shop floor.
- Acts as a **gating mechanism** to control WIP inflow.
- **Techniques:**
  - *Push Release (Traditional MRP):* Orders released based on plan dates.
  - *Pull Release (JIT/Kanban):* Orders released only when downstream demand signal arrives.
  - *CONWIP (Constant Work-In-Process):* Fixed WIP cap; new order released only when one exits.

### 🔹 Function 2 — **Order Scheduling (Detailed / Finite Scheduling)**
- Determines **exact start and finish times** of each operation on specific machines.
- Two fundamental approaches:
  - **Forward Scheduling:** Start ASAP, push tasks forward. Best for *project* or *expedite* jobs.
  - **Backward Scheduling:** Start from due date, push tasks backward. Best for *standard* jobs to avoid early completion & inventory.

### 🔹 Function 3 — **Dispatching**
- The **real-time, moment-of-decision** action of selecting the *next job* to be processed at a work centre from the queue.
- Done using **Priority Sequencing Rules** (see Section 2.2).
- Output: **Dispatch List** (sequence of jobs at each work centre).

### 🔹 Function 4 — **Order Progress Monitoring (Tracking)**
- **Data collection** from the floor via:
  - Manual logs / Shop papers (traditional)
  - Bar codes / RFID
  - CNC DNC networks
  - IoT sensors / SCADA
- Tracks: *Start, Finish, Quantities, Scrap, Downtime, Tool usage.*

### 🔹 Function 5 — **Exception Management & Feedback**
- Detects deviations (machine breakdown, quality failure, material shortage).
- Generates **exception reports** → triggers **re-planning** in MRP.
- Closes the **closed-loop** of SFC.

## 2.2 Priority Sequencing Rules (The "Heart" of Dispatching)

These rules are mathematical heuristics that assign a **priority number** to each waiting job. **Lower number = Higher priority**.

| Rule | Formula | Logic | Favours |
|------|---------|-------|---------|
| **FCFS** (First Come First Served) | Arrival Date | Oldest job first | Fairness |
| **EDD** (Earliest Due Date) | Due Date | Soonest deadline first | Due-date performance |
| **SPT** (Shortest Processing Time) | Processing Time (p) | Quickest job first | WIP & Flow Time ↓ |
| **LPT** (Longest Processing Time) | p (largest first) | Biggest job first | Machine Utilization |
| **SLACK** | Due Date − Today − Σp_remaining | Least slack first | Due-date tightness |
| **S/OPN** (Slack per Operation) | Slack / # operations remaining | Most urgent per op. | Multi-op jobs |
| **CR** (Critical Ratio) | (Due Date − Today) / Σp_remaining | Dynamic urgency | Balance of both |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Ratio Interpretation (Board Exam Hot-Question):**
> - **CR > 1** → Job is **ahead** of schedule.
> - **CR = 1** → Job is **exactly on schedule**.
> - **CR < 1** → Job is **behind schedule** (highest priority).
> - **CR < 0** → Job is **overdue** (critical alert).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Units | Application |
|---------|------------|-------|-------------|
| Critical Ratio (CR) | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{D - T}{R}$ | dimensionless | Dispatch priority |
| Slack | $\text{Slack} = D - T - R$ | days | Urgency measure |
| Slack per Operation | $\text{S/OPN} = \dfrac{D - T - R}{N}$ | days/op | Multi-op scheduling |
| Machine Utilization | $\text{MU} = \dfrac{\text{Busy Time}}{\text{Available Time}} \times 100$ | % | Resource efficiency |
| OEE | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | % | World-class ≥ 85% |
| Average Flow Time | $\bar{F} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum (C_i - R_i)$ | days | Lead time metric |
| Makespan | $C_{\max} = \max(C_i)$ | days | Schedule length |
| Tardiness (job i) | $T_i = \max(0,\ C_i - D_i)$ | days | Lateness penalty |
| WIP (Little's Law) | $\text{WIP} = \text{TH} \times \text{LT}$ | units | Flow control |

> Where: $D$ = Due Date, $T$ = Today's Date, $R$ = Remaining processing time, $N$ = No. of remaining operations, $C_i$ = Completion time, $R_i$ = Release time, TH = Throughput, LT = Lead Time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Little's Law** is a foundational result by John Little (1961): in any *stable* production system, the long-term average number of items in the system equals the long-term average arrival rate multiplied by the average time an item spends in the system. **This is universally tested in KTU exams.**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Assembly:** SFC enables Toyota's *Andon* system — pulling the cord stops the line and triggers exception handling.
- **Aerospace Machining:** Real-time dispatching based on SPT keeps expensive 5-axis CNC machines loaded with optimal job mix.
- **Electronics (PCB SMT lines):** CR-based dispatching ensures time-critical customer orders jump the queue.
- **Industry 4.0:** SFC is now the *operational kernel* inside **MES (Manufacturing Execution Systems)** like Siemens Opcenter, SAP ME, and Dassault DELMIA.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **exhaustive, line-by-line worked derivations** of the most examination-relevant SFC techniques. No step is skipped.

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Critical Ratio (CR) Computation

**Problem [KTU University Exam — June 2023 style]:**
On a shop floor, four jobs are waiting at a milling machine. Determine the dispatch order using the **Critical Ratio (CR)** rule. *Today's date = Day 0.*

| Job | Due Date (Day) | Remaining Processing Time (days) |
|-----|----------------|----------------------------------|
| A   | 10             | 5                                |
| B   | 8              | 6                                |
| C   | 15             | 3                                |
| D   | 6              | 4                                |

### **Step 1 — Recall the CR Formula**
$$\text{CR} = \frac{D - T}{R}$$

where $D$ = Due Date, $T$ = Today = 0, $R$ = Remaining Processing Time.

### **Step 2 — Substitute values for each job**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CR}_A &= \frac{10 - 0}{5} = \frac{10}{5} = 2.0 \\[4pt]
\text{CR}_B &= \frac{8 - 0}{6} = \frac{8}{6} \approx 1.33 \\[4pt]
\text{CR}_C &= \frac{15 - 0}{3} = \frac{15}{3} = 5.0 \\[4pt]
\text{CR}_D &= \frac{6 - 0}{4} = \frac{6}{4} = 1.5
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 3 — Interpret each CR value**

- **Job A (CR = 2.0):** Ahead of schedule (2× more time available than needed).
- **Job B (CR = 1.33):** Slightly ahead.
- **Job C (CR = 5.0):** Far ahead — has 5× the time it needs (lowest urgency).
- **Job D (CR = 1.5):** Modestly ahead.

### **Step 4 — Rank by CR (Ascending = Highest Priority First)**

$$
\text{Priority Order: } B\ (1.33) \;\rightarrow\; D\ (1.5) \;\rightarrow\; A\ (2.0) \;\rightarrow\; C\ (5.0)
$$

> **Dispatch Sequence at the milling machine: B → D → A → C.**

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Slack per Operation (S/OPN)

**Problem:**
Using data below and assuming *Today = Day 0*, find the dispatch sequence using the **S/OPN** rule.

| Job | Due Date (Day) | Remaining Processing Time | Operations Remaining |
|-----|----------------|---------------------------|----------------------|
| P   | 12             | 8                         | 4                    |
| Q   | 14             | 6                         | 2                    |
| R   | 10             | 9                         | 3                    |

### **Step 1 — Recall the S/OPN formula**
$$\text{S/OPN} = \frac{D - T - R}{N}$$

### **Step 2 — Compute Slack first, then divide by N**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Slack}_P &= 12 - 0 - 8 = 4 \;\text{days} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{S/OPN}_P = \frac{4}{4} = 1.00 \\[6pt]
\text{Slack}_Q &= 14 - 0 - 6 = 8 \;\text{days} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{S/OPN}_Q = \frac{8}{2} = 4.00 \\[6pt]
\text{Slack}_R &= 10 - 0 - 9 = 1 \;\text{day} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{S/OPN}_R = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.33
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 3 — Dispatch Order (Lowest S/OPN first)**
$$\boxed{\text{R (0.33)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{P (1.00)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Q (4.00)}}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three Most Tested Rules

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb for the Board Exam:**
> - If the question mentions **"balance between urgency and work remaining"** → use **CR**.
> - If the question mentions **"multi-operation job, urgency per step"** → use **S/OPN**.
> - If the question mentions **"simple urgency, single value"** → use **SLACK**.

## 3.4 Forward vs. Backward Scheduling — Numerical Proof

**Problem:** A job has 3 operations: Op-1 (3 days) → Op-2 (4 days) → Op-3 (2 days). Today's date = Day 0, Due Date = Day 12.

### Forward Scheduling (start ASAP, finish ASAP)
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Op-1:} \quad & \text{Start} = 0,\ \text{Finish} = 3 \\
\text{Op-2:} \quad & \text{Start} = 3,\ \text{Finish} = 3+4 = 7 \\
\text{Op-3:} \quad & \text{Start} = 7,\ \text{Finish} = 7+2 = 9
\end{aligned}
$$
**Completion = Day 9** (3 days early, no penalty).

### Backward Scheduling (start from due date, move backward)
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Op-3:} \quad & \text{Finish} = 12,\ \text{Start} = 12-2 = 10 \\
\text{Op-2:} \quad & \text{Finish} = 10,\ \text{Start} = 10-4 = 6 \\
\text{Op-1:} \quad & \text{Finish} = 6,\ \text{Start} = 6-3 = 3
\end{aligned}
$$
**Latest Start = Day 3** (job must start by Day 3 to finish on time).

> **Inference:** Backward scheduling answers the *commercial* question: *"When is the LAST moment we can start?"* — which is the question SFC must answer under JIT philosophy.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Closed-Loop Shop Floor Control Cycle

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical SFC cycle** — it appears in nearly every CIM textbook and is a KTU 2024 Scheme Module-2 high-yield figure.


## 4.4 Push vs. Pull Release — Comparative Block View

```mermaid

        P1["PLAN RELEASES ORDERS TO SHOP FLOOR BASED ON FORECAST"] --> P2["ORDERS PUSHED DOWNSTATION BY DOWNSTATION"]
        P2 --> P3["WIP ACCUMULATES HIGH INVENTORY RISK"]
    end
    subgraph PULL["PULL SYSTEM JIT KANBAN"]
        Q1["CUSTOMER OR DOWNSTREAM DEMAND TRIGGERS SIGNAL"] --> Q2["KANBAN CARD AUTHORIZES UPSTREAM PRODUCTION"]

```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *Define Shop Floor Control (SFC). List its primary functions.*

**Model Answer:**
Shop Floor Control (SFC), also called Production Activity Control (PAC), is the operational function of a manufacturing enterprise that initiates, executes, monitors, and regulates the flow of work on the shop floor to fulfil the master production schedule. Its **five primary functions** are:
1. Order Release
2. Order Scheduling
3. Dispatching
4. Order Progress Monitoring
5. Exception Management & Feedback to Planning

(3 marks — Definition 1.5, Functions 1.5)

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2022]**
> *Explain the Critical Ratio (CR) rule. How is it interpreted?*

**Model Answer:**
The Critical Ratio (CR) is a dynamic priority sequencing rule for shop floor dispatching. The formula is:

$$\text{CR} = \frac{\text{Due Date} - \text{Today}}{\text{Remaining Processing Time}}$$

**Interpretation:**
- **CR > 1** → Job is ahead of schedule.
- **CR = 1** → Job is exactly on schedule.
- **CR < 1** → Job is behind schedule (must be prioritized).
- **CR < 0** → Job is overdue.

The job with the **lowest CR** is dispatched next because it is the most urgent. (3 marks — Formula 1.5, Interpretation 1.5)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students write "FCFS rule" when the question asks for a "dynamic rule" — examiners deduct **2 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** In numericals, students forget to subtract "Today" from Due Date while computing CR — leads to a **3-mark** deduction.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Students confuse SFC with MRP — remember: **MRP = planning, SFC = execution**.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Sketches of SFC cycle are drawn without arrows showing the *closed loop* — full marks require a **clear feedback arrow** back to Planning.

---

### ✅ **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *Choose A OR B*

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
> **(a)** Explain the five functions of Shop Floor Control with a neat block diagram of the closed-loop SFC cycle. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Four jobs are waiting at a CNC turning centre. Use the **Critical Ratio (CR)** rule to determine the dispatch order. State any two limitations of CR. *(Today = Day 0)*

| Job | Due Date (Day) | Remaining Processing Time (days) |
|-----|----------------|----------------------------------|
| J1  | 9              | 5                                |
| J2  | 14             | 6                                |
| J3  | 6              | 4                                |
| J4  | 11             | 3                                |

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

The five functions of SFC are:

1. **Order Release (1 mark):** Activating planned orders from MRP. Push, Pull, and CONWIP are release strategies.

2. **Order Scheduling (1 mark):** Determining start/finish times of each operation. **Forward scheduling** (start ASAP) and **backward scheduling** (start from due date) are the two main methods.

3. **Dispatching (1 mark):** Real-time selection of the *next job* from a queue at a work centre. Done via priority rules and the **Dispatch List**.

4. **Progress Monitoring (1.5 marks):** Collecting real-time data on job progress, machine status, scrap, and downtime using bar codes, RFID, DNC, or IoT sensors.

5. **Exception Management (1.5 marks):** Detecting and resolving deviations (breakdowns, quality failures, material shortages); reporting back to MRP for **re-planning**.

**Block Diagram (1 mark — sketch):**
```
MRP II → [Order Release] → [Scheduling] → [Dispatching] → [Monitoring] → [Exception Mgmt] → (feedback to MRP)
```

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Write the formula (1 mark):**
$$\text{CR} = \frac{D - T}{R},\quad T = 0$$

**Step 2 — Compute CR for each job (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CR}_{J1} &= \frac{9 - 0}{5} = \frac{9}{5} = 1.80 \\[4pt]
\text{CR}_{J2} &= \frac{14 - 0}{6} = \frac{14}{6} \approx 2.33 \\[4pt]
\text{CR}_{J3} &= \frac{6 - 0}{4} = \frac{6}{4} = 1.50 \\[4pt]
\text{CR}_{J4} &= \frac{11 - 0}{3} = \frac{11}{3} \approx 3.67
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sort ascending (lowest CR = highest priority) (1 mark):**
$$\text{J3 (1.50)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{J1 (1.80)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{J2 (2.33)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{J4 (3.67)}$$

**Dispatch Sequence at CNC Turning Centre: J3 → J1 → J2 → J4**

**Step 4 — Limitations of CR (2 marks):**
1. It assumes a single due date for the entire job; for multi-operation jobs, the rule loses accuracy.
2. It is a *static* ratio at the moment of computation; job priorities can become outdated if processing time is mis-estimated.

---

### ✅ **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]**
> **(a)** Compare **Push** and **Pull** systems of order release in SFC. Which is more suitable for high-variety, low-volume manufacturing? Justify. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Three jobs A, B, C are in a queue. Use the **S/OPN** rule to determine priority. Also compute **Slack per Operation** in tabular form. *(Today = Day 0)*

| Job | Due Date (Day) | Remaining Processing Time (days) | Operations Remaining |
|-----|----------------|----------------------------------|----------------------|
| A   | 11             | 7                                | 2                    |
| B   | 13             | 5                                | 4                    |
| C   | 9              | 6                                | 3                    |

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

**Push System (3 marks):**
- Order release driven by **forecast & MRP schedule**.
- Jobs are released based on *planned* start dates.
- Risk: **WIP explosion**, high inventory, poor visibility of actual demand.
- Example: Traditional MRP-based discrete manufacturing.

**Pull System (3 marks):**
- Order release triggered by **actual downstream demand** (Kanban card, signal, customer order).
- Only produces what is consumed upstream.
- Benefits: **low WIP, low inventory, high responsiveness, quick issue detection (Andon)**.
- Example: Toyota Production System.

**Suitability for HVL-LV (1 mark):**
For **high-variety, low-volume (HVL-LV)** manufacturing (e.g., job shops, custom engineering), a **hybrid Pull-with-MRP approach** is most suitable — pure Kanban fails because of long setup times and varied routings, but Push alone causes WIP bloat. Use MRP for *planning* and a *limited pull* mechanism for *release control*.

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

**Formula (1 mark):**
$$\text{S/OPN} = \frac{D - T - R}{N}$$

**Computation (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{S/OPN}_A &= \frac{11 - 0 - 7}{2} = \frac{4}{2} = 2.00 \\[4pt]
\text{S/OPN}_B &= \frac{13 - 0 - 5}{4} = \frac{8}{4} = 2.00 \\[4pt]
\text{S/OPN}_C &= \frac{9 - 0 - 6}{3} = \frac{3}{3} = 1.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Tabular Summary (1 mark):**

| Job | Due Date | Processing Time | Operations | Slack | S/OPN |
|-----|----------|-----------------|------------|-------|-------|
| A   | 11       | 7               | 2          | 4     | 2.00  |
| B   | 13       | 5               | 4          | 8     | 2.00  |
| C   | 9        | 6               | 3          | 3     | 1.00  |

**Dispatch Order (lowest S/OPN first) (1 mark):**
$$\text{C (1.00)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{A and B (2.00 — tie, break by EDD: A first)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{B}$$
$$\boxed{\text{C → A → B}}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this checklist before entering the exam hall.**

- ☐ SFC = *Production Activity Control* = operational-level execution of MRP plans.
- ☐ SFC is the **closed loop** with five functions: Release → Schedule → Dispatch → Monitor → Exception (→ feedback).
- ☐ SFC sits **between MRP II (planning) and Machine Control (PLC/CNC)** in the CIM hierarchy.
- ☐ **Push** = forecast-driven release; **Pull** = demand-signal-driven (Kanban).
- ☐ **CONWIP** = a hybrid using a fixed WIP cap with pull-like release.
- ☐ **Forward scheduling** → start ASAP (push logic). **Backward scheduling** → start from due date (JIT logic).
- ☐ **Priority Rules (lowest value = highest priority):**
  - FCFS, EDD, SPT, LPT, SLACK, S/OPN, CR
- ☐ **Critical Ratio:**
  - $\text{CR} = (D - T)/R$
  - CR < 1 = behind schedule; CR = 1 = on time; CR > 1 = ahead.
- ☐ **Slack per Operation:**
  - $\text{S/OPN} = (D - T - R)/N$
- ☐ **Little's Law:** $\text{WIP} = \text{TH} \times \text{LT}$ — universally tested.
- ☐ **OEE** = Availability × Performance × Quality; world-class ≥ **85%**.
- ☐ SFC in modern Industry 4.0 = **core of MES** (Manufacturing Execution Systems).
- ☐ Always draw the **closed-loop arrow** back to MRP when sketching the SFC cycle.
- ☐ In numericals, **always subtract "Today"** from the due date before dividing.
- ☐ Tie-breaking between equal priorities: use EDD, then SPT, then FCFS (in that order of examiner preference).

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Shop Floor Control Techniques**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inventory Control and ERP: Basic Principles of Inventory Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT613 - COMPUTER INTEGRATED MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inventory Control and ERP: Basic Principles of Inventory Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Inventory** is the stock of raw materials, work-in-progress (WIP), finished goods, and other supplies held by an organization at any given point in time to meet future production and demand requirements. In the context of Computer Integrated Manufacturing (CIM), inventory control refers to the systematic regulation of these stocks to minimize total cost while ensuring uninterrupted production and customer service.

**Inventory Control** is the process of managing and regulating the ordering, storage, and use of inventory so that associated costs are minimized, and the supply of materials is maintained at the desired level. It forms the operational backbone of any manufacturing enterprise and is tightly integrated with the larger **Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP)** framework.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Expected):** "Inventory control is the scientific method of determining what, when, and how much to order, and how much to keep in stock, while balancing the conflict between the cost of holding inventory and the cost of stock-outs."

### 1.2 Types of Inventory in a Manufacturing System

| Type | Description | Example |
|------|-------------|---------|
| **Raw Materials (RM)** | Inputs awaiting processing | Steel coils in an auto plant |
| **Work-In-Progress (WIP)** | Partially completed items | Half-assembled PCB |
| **Finished Goods (FG)** | Completed products ready for dispatch | Packed laptops in warehouse |
| **MRO (Maintenance, Repair, Operating)** | Items for plant upkeep | Lubricants, spare bearings |
| **Safety/Buffer Stock** | Held to absorb demand/supply variability | Extra stock of critical items |
| **Pipeline/In-transit** | Goods in transit between stages | Shipment on a truck |
| **Anticipation Stock** | Built up for seasonal demand | Air conditioners before summer |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Tank Model"

Imagine your home has a **water tank** on the roof.
- The **tank capacity** = Maximum inventory you can hold.
- The **water tap usage rate** = Rate of consumption (demand rate, $D$).
- The **water filling rate from the municipal pump** = Replenishment rate.
- The **delay between placing a refill order and water arriving** = Lead time ($L$).
- The **small reserve kept at the bottom for emergencies** = Safety stock.
- The **overflow and the dry-tank situations** = Overstocking and stock-outs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inventory Control = The science of deciding WHEN to switch the pump ON, HOW MUCH water to let in, and how much emergency reserve to keep — so that the household never runs out of water and the pump is not running unnecessarily.** This is exactly the optimization problem we solve in EOQ, ROP, and ABC analysis.

### 1.4 Objectives of Inventory Control

1. Ensure **uninterrupted supply** of materials to production.
2. Minimize **carrying (holding) costs** and **ordering costs**.
3. Keep **capital locked up in inventory** at the lowest possible level.
4. Maintain adequate **safety stock** to absorb demand and lead-time variability.
5. Provide **buffer** against price fluctuations and supply chain disruptions.
6. Enable smooth **just-in-time (JIT)** operations when integrated with ERP.

### 1.5 Standard Metrics (Board-Favorite)

- **Demand Rate ($D$):** Units consumed per year (always in **bold = items/year**).
- **Ordering Cost ($C_o$ or $S$):** Cost incurred per order placed (₹/order).
- **Holding Cost ($C_h$ or $H$):** Cost to carry one unit for one year (₹/unit/year). Typically **15% to 30%** of unit cost.
- **Lead Time ($L$):** Time between placing and receiving an order (**days or weeks**).
- **Reorder Point (ROP):** Inventory level that triggers a new order (**units**).
- **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ):** Optimal order size that minimizes total cost (**units**).
- **Safety Stock ($SS$):** Buffer inventory to handle uncertainty (**units**).
- **Inventory Turnover Ratio:** $D / \text{Average Inventory}$ (**times/year**).

> [!TIP]
> In the KTU 2024 Scheme, a typical 3-mark question in Module 2 asks: *"List the types of inventory with examples."* Memorize the 5–6 types above with one example each.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Core Costs in Inventory

The fundamental challenge of inventory control arises from the **trade-off between three competing cost components**:

**A. Ordering Cost (also called Setup Cost, $C_o$)**
- Costs incurred every time an order is placed, regardless of order size.
- Components: Purchase order preparation, transportation, receiving, inspection, clerical work, supplier follow-up.
- **Behavior:** Decreases as order size increases (fewer orders per year).

**B. Holding (Carrying) Cost ($C_h$)**
- Costs of keeping inventory in storage.
- Components: Storage space (rent/electricity), insurance, taxes, depreciation, obsolescence, pilferage, opportunity cost of capital tied up.
- **Behavior:** Increases as order size increases (more average inventory).

**C. Shortage (Stock-out) Cost ($C_s$)**
- Cost when demand cannot be met from stock.
- Components: Lost sales, backorder processing, loss of goodwill, production downtime.
- **Behavior:** Decreases as safety stock and order size increase.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Trade-off Principle:** The optimal order quantity is the point where the *annual ordering cost* equals the *annual holding cost*. This elegant equality gives rise to the famous **EOQ formula**.

### 2.2 Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) — Wilson's Formula

The **EOQ model** (developed by Ford W. Harris in 1913, popularized by R.H. Wilson) determines the optimal order quantity $Q^*$ that minimizes the **Total Relevant Cost (TRC)**, which is the sum of ordering and holding costs.

**Assumptions of the Basic EOQ Model (KD Must Know):**
1. Demand rate is **constant** and **deterministic** (known and uniform).
2. Lead time is **constant** and **known**.
3. Order is received **instantaneously** (infinite replenishment rate) — also called the *Instantaneous Receipt* model.
4. **No stock-outs** are permitted.
5. Ordering cost and holding cost are **constant** and **independent** of order quantity.
6. The item is procured **independently** (no joint ordering, no quantity discounts).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|----------|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | **EOQ ($Q^*$)** | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 D C_o}{C_h}}$ | Economic Order Quantity (Wilson's) | units/order |
| 2 | **Optimal # of Orders ($N^*$)** | $N^* = D / Q^*$ | Orders per year | orders/year |
| 3 | **Optimal Time Between Orders ($T^*$)** | $T^* = Q^* / D$ | Cycle time | years |
| 4 | **Reorder Point (ROP)** | $\text{ROP} = D \times L$ | Reorder level for constant $D, L$ | units |
| 5 | **Safety Stock ($SS$)** | $SS = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | With $z$=service level factor | units |
| 6 | **ROP with Safety Stock** | $\text{ROP} = D \cdot L + SS$ | Reorder level with buffer | units |
| 7 | **Min Total Cost ($TC_{\min}$)** | $TC_{\min} = \sqrt{2 D C_o C_h}$ | Total annual relevant cost | ₹/year |
| 8 | **Average Inventory (EOQ)** | $\bar{I} = Q^*/2$ | Mean inventory level | units |
| 9 | **Inventory Turnover** | $\text{ITR} = D / \bar{I}$ | Stock rotation rate | times/year |
| 10 | **Order Interval ($T_o$)** | $T_o = \text{working days/year} / N^*$ | Days between orders | days |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notation Convention for KTU Exams:** Use $D$ for annual demand, $C_o$ for ordering cost per order, $C_h$ for holding cost per unit per year, $C_u$ or $P$ for unit purchase price. The KTU board will mark you wrong if you confuse $C_o$ with $C_h$ — never mix them up.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** EOQ is used to decide how many brake-pad sets to order from a supplier for an assembly line running at 200 cars/day.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** ROP and Safety Stock calculations are critical because a stock-out of an essential drug can be life-threatening.
- **Electronics Manufacturing:** PCBs and ICs are ordered in batches using EOQ, with the *joint replenishment problem* (JRI)* used when multiple items are ordered from the same supplier.
- **Aerospace:** High-value, low-volume items use **periodic review systems** rather than continuous review EOQ.

### 2.5 Inventory Classification Systems (Board-Favorite)

#### 2.5.1 ABC Analysis (Always Better Control) — Pareto Principle

| Class | % of Items | % of Annual Value | Control Level |
|-------|-----------|-------------------|---------------|
| **A** | ~10–20% | ~70–80% | **Tight control**, frequent review, accurate records |
| **B** | ~20–30% | ~15–25% | **Moderate control** |
| **C** | ~50–70% | ~5–10% | **Loose control**, simple systems, bulk ordering |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Jargon:** ABC is also called the **"80-20 rule"** or **Pareto's Principle**. Remember: A-items are the *vital few*, C-items are the *trivial many*.

#### 2.5.2 Other Classification Methods (Quick Reference)

- **FSN (Fast, Slow, Non-moving):** Based on issue/consumption rate.
- **VED (Vital, Essential, Desirable):** Based on criticality of item for production.
- **SDE (Scarce, Difficult, Easy):** Based on procurement difficulty.
- **HML (High, Medium, Low):** Based on unit price of items.
- **GOLF (Government, Ordinary, Local, Foreign):** Based on source of supply.

### 2.6 Inventory Control Systems

**A. Continuous Review (Q-system):** Inventory is checked continuously; when level hits ROP, a fixed quantity $Q^*$ is ordered. ⇒ **EOQ/ROP model**.

**B. Periodic Review (P-system):** Inventory is checked at fixed intervals (e.g., weekly); a variable quantity is ordered to bring stock up to a target level.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write "EOQ is for periodic review and ROP is for continuous review." This is **WRONG**. EOQ gives the *order quantity* used in continuous review; ROP gives the *trigger level*. The system using both (EOQ + ROP) is the **continuous review system**.

### 2.7 Linkage with ERP (Context for Module 2)

Modern **ERP systems** (SAP, Oracle, Microsoft Dynamics) integrate inventory modules with:
- **Materials Management (MM)** — Procurement, vendor management.
- **Production Planning (PP)** — MRP run, BOM explosion.
- **Sales & Distribution (SD)** — Demand forecasting.
- **Finance/Controlling (FI/CO)** — Inventory valuation (FIFO, LIFO, Weighted Average).

Within ERP, the inventory control module executes EOQ, ROP, safety stock, and ABC logic automatically as **standard reports** or **background jobs**.
