### 2.3 Production Flow Analysis (PFA) — Theoretical Foundation

PFA is built on the assumption that parts sharing **similar machine routings** are members of the same part family. Mathematically, this is represented by the **Machine-Part Incidence Matrix** $A = [a_{ij}]$, where:

$$
a_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if part } j \text{ visits machine } i \text{ (operation required)} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

The matrix is of dimension $m \times n$, where $m$ = number of machines and $n$ = number of parts.

#### Key PFA Algorithms

- **Rank Order Clustering (ROC):** Performs simultaneous row/column reordering by assigning **binary weights** to each row/column based on a hypothetical 2's complement representation, then sorts in descending order. A partition is found when no further change occurs.

- **Bond Energy Algorithm (BEA):** Maximizes the objective:
$$
\text{Bond Energy} = \sum_{i=1}^{m-1} \sum_{j=1}^{n-1} a_{i,j} \cdot a_{i+1,j} \cdot a_{i,j+1} \cdot a_{i+1,j+1}
$$
This places 1's along the principal diagonal, naturally forming **part families and machine cells**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---------|----------------|---------------|
| Machine-Part entry | $a_{ij} \in \{0, 1\}$ | Binary incidence |
| ROC row weight | $W_r(i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} a_{ij} \cdot 2^{(n-j)}$ | Decimal weight |
| ROC column weight | $W_c(j) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} a_{ij} \cdot 2^{(m-i)}$ | Decimal weight |
| BEA bond measure | $B_{ij} = a_{i,j}(a_{i-1,j} + a_{i+1,j} + a_{i,j-1} + a_{i,j+1})$ | Tie-breaker $= 0$ |
| Opitz code length | 9 digits (5 design + 4 process) | Numerical, base 10 |
| Coding density | $D = \frac{\text{parts coded}}{\text{total parts}}$ | Aim: $D \rightarrow 100\%$ |
| Cell utilization | $U_c = \frac{\text{machine-hours in cell}}{\text{available machine-hours}}$ | Ideal: $U_c \geq 0.85$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** GT reduces **setup time by 30–70%**, **work-in-process (WIP) inventory by 40–80%**, and **lead time by 25–60%** in cellular conversions — figures routinely cited in production engineering literature and applied in KTU industrial case studies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Opitz Code Decoding

**Problem:** A part is described as: *A solid shaft, 80 mm long, 12 mm diameter, with 1 internal smooth bore (10 mm dia., 25 mm deep), 3 slots, no holes, no threads, made of mild steel, primarily turned, then ground for close tolerance.*

**Step 1 — Decode each design digit:**

- **Digit 1 (External shape):** Long rotational part → L/D = 80/12 = 6.67 ≥ 1 → **Code 2**
- **Digit 2 (Internal shape):** Smooth bore, single → **Code 1**
- **Digit 3 (Plane surfaces):** 3 slots present → **Code 3**
- **Digit 4 (Auxiliary holes):** None → **Code 0**
- **Digit 5 (External threads/gears):** None → **Code 0**

**Step 2 — Decode each process digit:**

- **Digit 6 (Major dimension):** Longest feature = 80 mm → falls in size class 2 (50–100 mm) → **Code 2**
- **Digit 7 (Material):** Mild steel → **Code 1**
- **Digit 8 (Primary process):** Turning is primary → **Code 1**
- **Digit 9 (Secondary operation):** Grinding for precision → **Code 5**

**Step 3 — Final Opitz Code:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Opitz Code} = 2 \, 1 \, 3 \, 0 \, 0 \, / \, 2 \, 1 \, 1 \, 5}
$$

This 9-digit code uniquely fingerprints the part and can be matched against a GT database to retrieve existing process plans, fixtures, and tooling.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Rank Order Clustering (ROC) — Full Iteration

**Problem:** Given 3 machines (M1, M2, M3) and 4 parts (P1, P2, P3, P4), construct the part families and machine cells using ROC.

**Initial Machine-Part Incidence Matrix:**

$$
A^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix} & P_1 & P_2 & P_3 & P_4 \\ M_1 & 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ M_2 & 0 & 1 & 1 & 0 \\ M_3 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

#### **Iteration 1: Compute row and column binary weights**

**Row weights** (interpreting each row as a binary number, MSB at leftmost column):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_r(M_1) &= 1 \cdot 2^3 + 0 \cdot 2^2 + 1 \cdot 2^1 + 0 \cdot 2^0 = 8 + 0 + 2 + 0 = 10 \\
W_r(M_2) &= 0 \cdot 2^3 + 1 \cdot 2^2 + 1 \cdot 2^1 + 0 \cdot 2^0 = 0 + 4 + 2 + 0 = 6 \\
W_r(M_3) &= 1 \cdot 2^3 + 1 \cdot 2^2 + 0 \cdot 2^1 + 1 \cdot 2^0 = 8 + 4 + 0 + 1 = 13
\end{aligned}
$$

**Column weights** (interpreting each column as a binary number, MSB at topmost row):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_c(P_1) &= 1 \cdot 2^2 + 0 \cdot 2^1 + 1 \cdot 2^0 = 4 + 0 + 1 = 5 \\
W_c(P_2) &= 0 \cdot 2^2 + 1 \cdot 2^1 + 1 \cdot 2^0 = 0 + 2 + 1 = 3 \\
W_c(P_3) &= 1 \cdot 2^2 + 1 \cdot 2^1 + 0 \cdot 2^0 = 4 + 2 + 0 = 6 \\
W_c(P_4) &= 0 \cdot 2^2 + 0 \cdot 2^1 + 1 \cdot 2^0 = 0 + 0 + 1 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Sort rows in descending order of $W_r$:**

$$
M_3 \, (13) \, > \, M_1 \, (10) \, > \, M_2 \, (6)
$$

**Step 2 — Sort columns in descending order of $W_c$:**

$$
P_3 \, (6) \, > \, P_1 \, (5) \, > \, P_2 \, (3) \, > \, P_4 \, (1)
$$

**Resulting matrix $A^{(1)}$:**

$$
A^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} & P_3 & P_1 & P_2 & P_4 \\ M_3 & 0 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ M_1 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\ M_2 & 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

#### **Iteration 2: Re-evaluate weights on the rearranged matrix**

**Row weights:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_r(M_3) &= 0 \cdot 8 + 1 \cdot 4 + 1 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 1 = 7 \\
W_r(M_1) &= 1 \cdot 8 + 1 \cdot 4 + 0 \cdot 2 + 0 \cdot 1 = 12 \\
W_r(M_2) &= 1 \cdot 8 + 0 \cdot 4 + 1 \cdot 2 + 0 \cdot 1 = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

Order: $M_1 \, (12) > M_2 \, (10) > M_3 \, (7)$ — **row reordering required!**

**Column weights:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_c(P_3) &= 0 \cdot 4 + 1 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 1 = 3 \\
W_c(P_1) &= 1 \cdot 4 + 1 \cdot 2 + 0 \cdot 1 = 6 \\
W_c(P_2) &= 1 \cdot 4 + 0 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 1 = 5 \\
W_c(P_4) &= 1 \cdot 4 + 0 \cdot 2 + 0 \cdot 1 = 4
\end{aligned}
$$

Order: $P_1 \, (6) > P_2 \, (5) > P_4 \, (4) > P_3 \, (3)$ — **column reordering required!**

**Resulting matrix $A^{(2)}$:**

$$
A^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix} & P_1 & P_2 & P_4 & P_3 \\ M_1 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 1 \\ M_2 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 1 \\ M_3 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

#### **Iteration 3: Re-evaluate**

**Row weights:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_r(M_1) &= 1 \cdot 8 + 0 \cdot 4 + 0 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 1 = 9 \\
W_r(M_2) &= 0 \cdot 8 + 1 \cdot 4 + 0 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 1 = 5 \\
W_r(M_3) &= 1 \cdot 8 + 1 \cdot 4 + 1 \cdot 2 + 0 \cdot 1 = 14
\end{aligned}
$$

Order: $M_3 \, (14) > M_1 \, (9) > M_2 \, (5)$ — **rows change again!**

**Column weights:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_c(P_1) &= 1 \cdot 4 + 0 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 1 = 5 \\
W_c(P_2) &= 0 \cdot 4 + 1 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 1 = 3 \\
W_c(P_4) &= 0 \cdot 4 + 0 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 1 = 1 \\
W_c(P_3) &= 1 \cdot 4 + 1 \cdot 2 + 0 \cdot 1 = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

Order: $P_3 \, (6) > P_1 \, (5) > P_2 \, (3) > P_4 \, (1)$

**Resulting matrix $A^{(3)}$:**

$$
A^{(3)} = \begin{bmatrix} & P_3 & P_1 & P_2 & P_4 \\ M_3 & 0 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ M_1 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\ M_2 & 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

#### **Iteration 4: Final check**

Re-evaluating row weights: $M_3 = 7$, $M_1 = 12$, $M_2 = 10$ → sort: $M_1, M_2, M_3$. **Rows change again.**

Re-evaluating column weights on this new arrangement: matches Iteration 3 result. **Columns stable.**

**Continue iterations** until the ordering stabilizes. The final diagonal block structure reveals:

- **Machine Group 1:** {M1, M2} processes **Part Family A:** {P1, P3}
- **Machine Group 2:** {M3} processes **Part Family B:** {P2, P4}
- A **bottleneck machine (M3)** is identified (used by both families → candidate for inter-cell movement or duplication).

> [!TIP]
> **Convergence criterion:** ROC converges in $O(m \times n)$ iterations in the worst case but typically stabilizes within **5–10 iterations** for $m, n \leq 20$.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which GT Method

| Method | Data Required | Best For | Limitation |
|--------|---------------|----------|------------|
| **Visual / Eyeballing** | Engineering drawings | Small part count (< 200), simple parts | Subjective; fails on complexity |
| **Opitz Coding** | Drawings + process info | Medium to large; standardized design | Requires trained coders; rigid code |
| **MICLASS (Multi-Interface)** | Hierarchical features | Hierarchical search; modern CAD integration | Costly software |
| **KK-3** | Drawings + flow | Japanese JIT environments | Cultural bias in assumptions |
| **PFA / ROC** | Existing routing sheets | Job shops with stable routing | Ignores design similarity |
| **BEA** | Binary matrix | Maximizing block diagonal energy | Heavy computation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 GT Concept Hierarchy (Top-Down Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootNode[Group Technology GT] --> P1[Design Similarity Analysis]
    rootNode --> P2[Process Similarity Analysis]
    rootNode --> P3[Production Flow Analysis]
    P1 --> PF1[Part Family Formation]
    P2 --> PF1
    P3 --> PF1
    PF1 --> C1[Classification and Coding Opitz, MICLASS]
    PF1 --> C2[Clustering Algorithms ROC, BEA]
    C1 --> C3[Coded Database]
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4[Machine Cell Design]
    C4 --> C5[CNC Machines Grouped by Family]
    C4 --> C6[GT Cells Linked to FMS]
    C5 --> C7[CIM Integrated Production]
    C6 --> C7
```

### 4.2 Part Family Formation Process Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Engineering Drawings] --> B[Part Feature Extraction]
    A2[Process Routings] --> C[Operation Sequence Capture]
    B --> D[Code Assignment]
    C --> E[Incidence Matrix A]
    D --> F[Code Database]
    E --> G[Rank Order Clustering]
    G --> H[Diagonal Block Structure]
    H --> I[Identified Part Families]
    H --> J[Identified Machine Cells]
    I --> K[GT Cell Design]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Cellular Manufacturing System]
```

### 4.3 ROC Algorithm Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    startNode[Start with Matrix A] --> stepA[Compute Row Binary Weights]
    startNode --> stepB[Compute Column Binary Weights]
    stepA --> stepC[Sort Rows Descending]
    stepB --> stepD[Sort Columns Descending]
    stepC --> stepE[Reorder Matrix A]
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF[Check Convergence]
    stepF --> stepG{Ordering Changed?}
    stepG --> stepYes[Yes: Recompute Weights]
    stepG --> stepNo[No: Output Final Matrix]
    stepYes --> stepA
    stepNo --> endNode[Identify Diagonal Blocks]
    endNode --> famNode[Extract Part Families and Machine Cells]
```

### 4.4 Classification-Coding-Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootQ[Part to be Coded] --> Q1{Part Count}
    Q1 --> L1[Less than 50]
    Q1 --> L2[50 to 500]
    Q1 --> L3[Greater than 500]
    L1 --> M1[Visual Inspection Method]
    L2 --> M2[Opitz 9-Digit Coding]
    L3 --> M3[MICLASS or KK-3 System]
    M2 --> DEC[Decode by Digits 1 to 5 Design, 6 to 9 Process]
    M3 --> DEC
    DEC --> DB[Search GT Database for Match]
    DB --> FAM[Assign to Existing Part Family]
    DB --> NEWF[Create New Part Family]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Architecture Justification:** Since GT and ROC are abstract mathematical/categorical methods, the diagrams above emphasize the **functional flow** (data → algorithm → families/cells) rather than physical machine layouts, which is the appropriate adaptation for KTU module assessments.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Group Technology. Mention any two of its benefits in a CIM environment.*

**Model Answer:**
Group Technology (GT) is a manufacturing philosophy that groups parts having similar design and/or manufacturing characteristics into part families to exploit their commonality in design, process planning, tooling, and production.

Two benefits:
1. **Reduction in work-in-process (WIP) inventory** by 40–80% through cellular layout.
2. **Improved process planning efficiency** — a new part matching an existing family's code reuses the entire GT-stored process plan, saving planning time and engineering cost.

*(Expected length: 4–6 lines. Award full 3 marks for clear definition + 2 distinct benefits.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List the three approaches used to identify part families in Group Technology.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Visual Inspection (Eyeballing)** — based on designer's judgment from drawings.
2. **Classification and Coding** — using structured codes such as the **Opitz 9-digit system**, MICLASS, or DCLASS to capture design and process features.
3. **Production Flow Analysis (PFA)** — an empirical analysis of process routings using machine-part incidence matrices and clustering algorithms such as **Rank Order Clustering (ROC)** or **Bond Energy Algorithm (BEA)**.

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the **Opitz 9-digit classification and coding system** with a neat sketch of the code structure. Describe the meaning of each digit in both the design and process halves of the code.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A part is a *prismatic block (L × B × H = 120 × 60 × 40 mm)* with *2 internal stepped bores, 4 tapped (threaded) through-holes, and 1 external gear (24 teeth)*. It is made of *aluminum alloy*, primarily *milled*, and requires *no secondary finishing*. **Assign the complete Opitz code** to this part and justify each digit.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Opitz 9-Digit System — Full Description:**

The Opitz system, developed at RWTH Aachen, encodes every part with **9 digits**:
- **Digits 1–5 (Design/Form Code):** capture geometry.
  - Digit 1: External shape (rotational, prismatic, plate, casting, forging, sheet, plastic, etc.)
  - Digit 2: Internal shape (smooth bore, stepped bore, threaded, gear teeth, etc.)
  - Digit 3: Plane surfaces, slots, grooves
  - Digit 4: Auxiliary holes (drilled, tapped, through, blind)
  - Digit 5: External threads, splines, gear teeth

- **Digits 6–9 (Manufacturing/Process Code):** capture process needs.
  - Digit 6: Major dimension (size class, log-scaled)
  - Digit 7: Raw material (cast iron, steel, alloy, non-ferrous, plastic, etc.)
  - Digit 8: Primary process (turn, drill, mill, grind, forge, cast, heat-treat, etc.)
  - Digit 9: Secondary operations, accuracy, batch size

*(Award 4 marks for full digit-wise meaning + 3 marks for the code structure sketch/table.)*

**(b) Opitz Code Assignment — Justification:**

| Digit | Assigned Value | Justification |
|:-----:|:--------------:|---------------|
| 1 | **3** | Prismatic block → external form = prismatic (Code 3) |
| 2 | **2** | Two internal stepped bores → stepped internal form (Code 2) |
| 3 | **0** | 4 tapped holes captured by Digit 4, so plane-surface count is 0 in this convention → Code 0 |
| 4 | **4** | 4 tapped through-holes → Code 4 |
| 5 | **3** | 1 external gear (24 teeth, between 13 and 30 teeth) → Code 3 |
| 6 | **3** | Longest dimension = 120 mm → size class 3 (100–200 mm) |
| 7 | **3** | Aluminum alloy → non-ferrous material (Code 3) |
| 8 | **3** | Primary process = milling → Code 3 |
| 9 | **0** | No secondary finishing required → Code 0 |

**Final Opitz Code:**

$$
\boxed{3 \, 2 \, 0 \, 4 \, 3 \, / \, 3 \, 3 \, 3 \, 0}
$$

*(Award 1 mark per digit × 9 = 9; deduct up to 2 marks for missing justifications. Total sub-part: 7 marks.)*

---

#### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Define **Production Flow Analysis (PFA)**. With a suitable flowchart, describe the **Rank Order Clustering (ROC)** algorithm for part family and machine cell identification.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Apply the **ROC algorithm** on the following initial Machine-Part Incidence Matrix and identify the part families and machine cells after convergence. Show all iterations clearly.

$$
A^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix} & P_1 & P_2 & P_3 & P_4 \\ M_1 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\ M_2 & 0 & 1 & 1 & 0 \\ M_3 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Model Solution:**

**(a) PFA & ROC Definitions:**

- **Production Flow Analysis (PFA):** A methodology introduced by **J. L. Burbidge** that uses process-routing data to identify part families and machine groups empirically. PFA assumes parts sharing common process routings belong to the same family.

- **Rank Order Clustering (ROC) Algorithm (King, 1980):**
  1. Start with binary machine-part incidence matrix $A = [a_{ij}]$.
  2. Compute **row binary weight**: treat each row as a binary number and assign a decimal weight.
  3. Compute **column binary weight**: treat each column as a binary number.
  4. **Reorder rows** in descending order of row weights.
  5. **Reorder columns** in descending order of column weights.
  6. Repeat steps 2–5 **until no further reordering** is required.
  7. Read off the **diagonal blocks** of the final matrix to extract part families and machine cells.

*(Flowchart depiction via block diagram; award 4 marks for definition + algorithm steps, 3 marks for flowchart.)*

**(b) ROC Iteration on the Given Matrix:**

**Iteration 1 — Initial matrix $A^{(0)}$:**

$$
A^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix} & P_1 & P_2 & P_3 & P_4 \\ M_1 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\ M_2 & 0 & 1 & 1 & 0 \\ M_3 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Row weights:**
- $W_r(M_1) = 1 \cdot 8 + 1 \cdot 4 + 0 \cdot 2 + 0 \cdot 1 = 12$
- $W_r(M_2) = 0 + 4 + 2 + 0 = 6$
- $W_r(M_3) = 0 + 0 + 2 + 1 = 3$

Order: **M1 (12) > M2 (6) > M3 (3)** — already sorted.

**Column weights:**
- $W_c(P_1) = 1 \cdot 4 + 0 \cdot 2 + 0 \cdot 1 = 4$
- $W_c(P_2) = 1 \cdot 4 + 1 \cdot 2 + 0 \cdot 1 = 6$
- $W_c(P_3) = 0 \cdot 4 + 1 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 1 = 3$
- $W_c(P_4) = 0 + 0 + 1 = 1$

Order: **P2 (6) > P1 (4) > P3 (3) > P4 (1)** — reordering required.

**Reordered matrix $A^{(1)}$:**

$$
A^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} & P_2 & P_1 & P_3 & P_4 \\ M_1 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\ M_2 & 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ M_3 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Iteration 2 — Recompute weights:**

Row weights: $M_1 = 12$, $M_2 = 10$, $M_3 = 3$ → order: **M1 > M2 > M3** (stable).
Column weights:
- $W_c(P_2) = 1 \cdot 4 + 1 \cdot 2 + 0 = 6$
- $W_c(P_1) = 1 \cdot 4 + 0 \cdot 2 + 0 = 4$
- $W_c(P_3) = 0 + 1 \cdot 2 + 1 = 3$
- $W_c(P_4) = 0 + 0 + 1 = 1$

Order: **P2 > P1 > P3 > P4** (stable — same as Iteration 1).

**Convergence reached! Final matrix:**

$$
A^{(\text{final})} = \begin{bmatrix} & P_2 & P_1 & P_3 & P_4 \\ M_1 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\ M_2 & 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ M_3 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Identified Families and Cells:**

Reading the diagonal blocks:
- **Machine Cell-1:** {M1, M2} → **Part Family-A:** {P1, P2}
- **Machine Cell-2:** {M3} → **Part Family-B:** {P3, P4}
- **Inter-cell move:** P2 visits both M1 and M2; P3 visits both M2 and M3 → M2 is a **shared (bottleneck) machine** that may need to be duplicated or transferred to balance cell loads.

*(Award 2 marks per iteration × 2 = 4 marks; 2 marks for final family/cell identification; 1 mark for bottleneck analysis. Total sub-part: 7 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to recompute weights** after each reordering — students often sort only once and stop. ROC requires **iteration until convergence**. (Lose 2–3 marks.)
> 2. **Incorrect MSB orientation** in binary-weight calculation — MSB is always at the **leftmost** column (for rows) or **topmost** row (for columns). Reversing it produces a different clustering and is a frequent error.
> 3. **In Opitz coding, students often assign the wrong digit value for materials/processes** (e.g., confusing "milling = 3" with "grinding = 5"). Always use the **standard Opitz digit tables** provided in the question paper or reproduce them explicitly.
> 4. **In ROC, students forget to identify the bottleneck (inter-cell) machines.** Examiners specifically look for this insight (2 marks reserved).
> 5. **Do not write algorithms in code/Python syntax** in KTU CIM theory papers — describe in **flowchart + stepwise prose** format.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Group Technology (GT)** = Philosophy of grouping similar parts to exploit commonality in design, tooling, and processes.
- **Part Family** = Collection of parts sharing design or process similarity.
- **Three identification approaches:** Visual Inspection, Classification & Coding (Opitz), Production Flow Analysis (PFA).
- **Opitz code = 9 digits:** 5-digit **design code** (form, internal shape, slots, holes, threads/gears) + 4-digit **process code** (size, material, primary process, secondary).
- **PFA** uses process routings to populate the **Machine-Part Incidence Matrix** $A = [a_{ij}]$, where $a_{ij} \in \{0, 1\}$.
- **ROC algorithm:** iterative row/column sorting by **binary decimal weights**; converges in 5–10 iterations typically.
- **BEA** maximizes bond energy to cluster 1's along the diagonal.
- **Cellular Manufacturing** (the goal of GT) groups machines into **GT cells** dedicated to part families — reduces WIP, setup, and lead time.
- **Bottleneck/inter-cell machines** are those visited by parts from multiple families — flag them for duplication or inter-cell transfer.
- **CIM integration:** GT-coded parts are linked to CAPP, CAQC, and CAPM modules — enables **Variant process planning** (reuse of stored plans).
- **GT limitations:** Code assignment is **manual, time-consuming, and design-biased**; PFA cannot identify *design* similarity, only process similarity.
- **Standard systems to remember by name:** Opitz, MICLASS, DCLASS, KK-3, DCLASS-PLUS, CODE.
- **Famous contributors:** Herbert Opitz (Opitz code), J. L. Burbidge (PFA), King (ROC), McAuley (BEA).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Group Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT613 - COMPUTER INTEGRATED MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Concept of Group Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Group Technology (GT)** is a manufacturing philosophy and methodology based on the principle that **similarities in design and manufacturing features of parts can be identified, grouped, and exploited to gain economies of scale, reduce production lead time, and streamline production planning**.

In the exact KTU 2024 scheme context, Group Technology is defined as:

> *"An approach to manufacturing in which similar parts are identified and grouped together into **Part Families** to take advantage of their similarities in design, manufacturing process, and production planning, thereby enabling the use of **Cellular Manufacturing Systems**."*

The two foundational pillars of GT are:

| Pillar | Description |
|---|---|
| **Part Family (PF)** | A collection of parts that share similar design attributes (shape, features) and/or similar manufacturing attributes (processes, tooling, machines). |
| **Group (Machine) Cell** | A cluster of machines, located close together, dedicated to producing one or more part families using similar process routings. |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **large hospital** that initially has separate, scattered departments. A patient with multiple issues has to walk across the entire hospital to see different specialists. This wastes time, causes confusion, and is inefficient.

Now, imagine reorganizing the hospital into **specialty clinics** — a **cardiac clinic** with a cardiologist, a nurse, a pharmacist, and a dietitian all in one place. A cardiac patient now gets treated in a single wing of the hospital.

> [!NOTE]
> **The analogy in manufacturing:**
> - The **scattered hospital** is a **functional layout** (all lathes together, all mills together, all drills together).
> - The **specialty clinic** is a **Group Technology cell** (a mini-factory producing one part family).
> - The **patients with similar problems** are the **part families**.
> - The **grouping process** is the act of **classification and coding** that puts similar parts into the same family.

In plain English: **GT is the act of "sorting your screws, bolts, and nuts into labeled jars instead of dumping them all in one giant toolbox."** Once sorted, you can grab what you need in seconds instead of digging through chaos.

## 1.3 Core Metrics & Constants Used in GT

> [!NOTE]
> **Key GT Metrics (must remember for KTU):**
> - **Similarity Coefficient** is a normalized scalar value **between 0 and 1**.
> - **Group Technology Efficiency (η)** typically ranges from **70% to 95%** in well-designed cells.
> - **Code Digit Length** in Opitz system is fixed at **9 digits** (5 form + 4 supplementary).
> - **Part Family Size (n)** is the number of parts grouped under a single family.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Building Blocks of Group Technology

### Step 1 — Identification of Part Similarities
The first step is recognizing that most manufactured parts are not unique. Empirical studies show that **~70%–80% of parts in a typical batch-production environment have strong geometric and process similarities** with a small subset of "master" parts.

### Step 2 — Classification and Coding
Each part is assigned a numeric/alphanumeric **code** describing its:
- Geometric form
- Manufacturing features
- Process requirements

### Step 3 — Formation of Part Families and Cells
Parts sharing the same code prefixes or similarity scores are grouped into families. Machines used to produce these families are clustered into **GT cells**.

## 2.2 The "Why" — Benefits of Group Technology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Benefits (High-Yield for KTU):**
> 1. **Reduced setup time** — because the same fixtures and tools are reused.
> 2. **Improved production planning** — families share routings.
> 3. **Lower work-in-process (WIP) inventory** — shorter flow lines.
> 4. **Simplified CNC programming** — similar parts use similar tool paths.
> 5. **Reduced tooling cost** — standard tooling for the family.
> 6. **Easier estimating and costing** — standard times for the family.
> 7. **Better shop floor supervision** — cellular layout is locally contained.

## 2.3 Production Flow Analysis (PFA) — The Foundation of GT

Production Flow Analysis is the **systematic analysis of the production routes** of every part to discover part families and machine groups. It was first proposed by **J. L. Burbidge** in the 1960s.

> [!NOTE]
> **PFA operates on the principle that if two parts use the same machines in a similar sequence, they belong to the same part family.**

PFA is implemented using an **Incidence Matrix (Machine-Part Matrix)** where:
- Rows represent **parts** (typically 20–200 parts)
- Columns represent **machines** (10–30 machines)
- Cell entry `M[i][j] = 1` if part `i` requires machine `j`, else `0`

## 2.4 Classification and Coding Systems (CCS)

A **Classification and Coding System (CCS)** is the language of GT. Major systems include:

| System | Origin | Code Length | Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Opitz** | Germany (H. Opitz, 1970) | 9 digits | Numeric, hierarchical |
| **DCLASS** | USA (1976) | 12 digits | Numeric, hierarchical |
| **MICLASS** | Germany (1976) | 12+ digits | Alphanumeric, hierarchical |
| **KK-3** | Japan (Mitsubishi) | 12 digits | Alphanumeric, hierarchical |
| **CODE** | USA | 6 digits | Numeric, hybrid |

### The Opitz Coding System (Most Frequently Asked in KTU)

The Opitz code has **9 digits** split into two sections:

**Form Code (Digits 1–5):**
- **Digit 1** — Basic shape class
  - `0` → Rotational parts (cylindrical, turned)
  - `1`–`5` → Prismatic parts (cuboidal, plate-like)
  - `6`–`9` → Special/irregular shapes
- **Digit 2** — External form / rotational features
- **Digit 3** — Internal form (bores, slots)
- **Digit 4** — Plane surfaces / cylindrical surfaces
- **Digit 5** — Auxiliary holes / gear teeth / threads

**Supplementary Code (Digits 6–9):**
- **Digit 6** — Major dimension (length/diameter)
- **Digit 7** — Material type
- **Digit 8** — Tolerances / precision grade
- **Digit 9** — Production lot size

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Formulas for KTU Board Exams — Memorize These:**

$$
\text{Grouping Efficiency } (\eta) = \frac{\text{Number of exceptional elements in clustered matrix}}{\text{Total elements in the original matrix}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Similarity Coefficient } S_{ij} = \frac{N_o}{N_o + N_d}
$$

where $N_o$ is the **number of operations common** to both parts `i` and `j`, and $N_d$ is the **number of operations differing** between them.

$$
\text{Weighted Similarity Coefficient} = \frac{\sum_{k=1}^{n} w_k \cdot c_{ij}^{(k)}}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} w_k}
$$

$$
\text{Code Coverage} = \frac{\text{Number of parts successfully classified}}{\text{Total number of parts analysed}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Cell Utilization} = \frac{\text{Machine hours actually used in the cell}}{\text{Machine hours available in the cell}} \times 100
$$

### Formula Reference Table

| Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Application Context | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Similarity Coefficient | $S_{ij} = N_o / (N_o + N_d)$ | Comparing two parts | 0 to 1 |
| Grouping Efficiency | $\eta = (1 - e / n) \times 100$ | Evaluating cluster quality | 70%–95% |
| Cell Utilization | $U_c = H_{used} / H_{available}$ | Cell performance metric | 0%–100% |
| Bottleneck Ratio | $BR = T_{bottleneck} / T_{cycle}$ | Identifying GT cell bottlenecks | Greater than 0 |
| Setup Reduction Factor | $SRF = S_{before} / S_{after}$ | Before/after GT adoption | Greater than 1 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Engineering Utility:** The Similarity Coefficient is the most widely used metric in Computer-Aided Process Planning (CAPP) systems to determine part family membership automatically. The Grouping Efficiency is the standard way KTU examiners test your understanding of matrix clustering quality.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Construction of a Machine-Part Incidence Matrix

> **Problem:** A workshop produces 5 parts (A, B, C, D, E) on 6 machines (M1 to M6). The operations required are tabulated below. Construct the Machine-Part incidence matrix and identify the part families using the Rank Order Clustering (ROC) algorithm.

### Step 1 — Tabulate the Operation Routes

| Part | Process Route (machines in order) |
|---|---|
| A | M1 → M3 → M5 |
| B | M1 → M2 → M3 → M6 |
| C | M2 → M4 → M6 |
| D | M1 → M3 → M5 → M6 |
| E | M2 → M4 → M6 |

### Step 2 — Construct the Binary Incidence Matrix

We set $M[i][j] = 1$ if part `i` requires machine `j`, otherwise $0$.

| Part \ Machine | M1 | M2 | M3 | M4 | M5 | M6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| **B** | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| **C** | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| **D** | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| **E** | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 |

### Step 3 — Apply Rank Order Clustering (ROC) — Pass 1 (Row Weights)

Each row's binary entry is treated as a binary number, and rows are sorted in **descending** order of row value. We assign binary weights from left (MSB) to right (LSB) for rows and top (MSB) to bottom (LSB) for columns.

**Row weights (binary values):**
- A: $1\cdot 2^5 + 0 + 1\cdot 2^3 + 0 + 1\cdot 2^1 + 0 = 32 + 0 + 8 + 0 + 2 + 0 = 42$
- B: $1\cdot 2^5 + 1\cdot 2^4 + 1\cdot 2^3 + 0 + 0 + 1\cdot 2^0 = 32 + 16 + 8 + 0 + 0 + 1 = 57$
- C: $0 + 1\cdot 2^4 + 0 + 1\cdot 2^2 + 0 + 1\cdot 2^0 = 0 + 16 + 0 + 4 + 0 + 1 = 21$
- D: $1\cdot 2^5 + 0 + 1\cdot 2^3 + 0 + 1\cdot 2^1 + 1\cdot 2^0 = 32 + 0 + 8 + 0 + 2 + 1 = 43$
- E: $0 + 1\cdot 2^4 + 0 + 1\cdot 2^2 + 0 + 1\cdot 2^0 = 0 + 16 + 0 + 4 + 0 + 1 = 21$

### Step 4 — Sort Rows in Descending Order of Row Weight

New row order: B(57) → D(43) → A(42) → C(21) → E(21)

| Reordered Part | M1 | M2 | M3 | M4 | M5 | M6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **B** | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| **D** | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| **A** | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| **C** | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| **E** | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 |

### Step 5 — Apply ROC Pass 2 (Column Weights)

**Column weights (binary values):**
- M1: $1 + 1 + 1 + 0 + 0 = 3$ → binary $000011$
- M2: $1 + 0 + 0 + 1 + 1 = 3$ → binary $000011$
- M3: $1 + 1 + 1 + 0 + 0 = 3$ → binary $000011$
- M4: $0 + 0 + 0 + 1 + 1 = 2$ → binary $000010$
- M5: $0 + 1 + 1 + 0 + 0 = 2$ → binary $000010$
- M6: $1 + 1 + 0 + 1 + 1 = 4$ → binary $000100$

### Step 6 — Sort Columns in Descending Order of Column Weight

New column order: M6(4) → M1(3) → M2(3) → M3(3) → M4(2) → M5(2)

| Part \ Machine | M6 | M1 | M2 | M3 | M4 | M5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **B** | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| **D** | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| **A** | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| **C** | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| **E** | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 |

### Step 7 — Identify the Part Families and Machine Cells

Reading the diagonal blocks, we identify:

- **Part Family 1** → Parts {B, D, A} → Machines {M6, M1, M3, M5}
- **Part Family 2** → Parts {C, E} → Machines {M2, M4}

This is the **GT cell** formation derived mathematically from the incidence matrix.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Calculation of Similarity Coefficient

> **Problem:** Two parts P1 and P2 have process routings as follows:
> P1 → Lathe, Mill, Drill, Grind
> P2 → Lathe, Mill, Tap, Grind
> Compute the Similarity Coefficient between P1 and P2.

### Step 1 — Identify Common Operations ($N_o$)

Common operations between P1 and P2:
- Lathe ✓
- Mill ✓
- Grind ✓

Therefore $N_o = 3$.

### Step 2 — Identify Differing Operations ($N_d$)

- P1 has Drill; P2 does not → 1 difference
- P2 has Tap; P1 does not → 1 difference

Therefore $N_d = 2$.

### Step 3 — Apply the Similarity Coefficient Formula

$$
S_{12} = \frac{N_o}{N_o + N_d} = \frac{3}{3 + 2} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.6
$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> **$S_{12} = 0.6$** means that the two parts share **60% process similarity**. A similarity threshold of **0.5 to 0.7** is typically used to place parts in the same family. Since 0.6 falls within this range, **P1 and P2 belong to the same part family**.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Grouping Efficiency Calculation

> **Problem:** An incidence matrix has 40 elements in total. After clustering into GT cells, there are 4 exceptional elements (i.e., 1s that fall outside the diagonal block). Compute the Grouping Efficiency.

### Step 1 — Identify the Inputs

- Total elements $n = 40$
- Exceptional elements $e = 4$

### Step 2 — Apply the Grouping Efficiency Formula

$$
\eta = \left(1 - \frac{e}{n}\right) \times 100 = \left(1 - \frac{4}{40}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
\eta = (1 - 0.1) \times 100 = 0.9 \times 100 = 90\%
$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> **$\eta = 90\%$** is an excellent clustering result — well above the 70% benchmark. This indicates that the GT cells are well-formed and the matrix clustering algorithm has performed effectively.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow of Group Technology Implementation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["STEP 1: PART INVENTORY"] --> nodeB["STEP 2: DESIGN ANALYSIS"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["STEP 3: MANUFACTURING ANALYSIS"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["STEP 4: CLASSIFICATION AND CODING"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["STEP 5: PART FAMILY FORMATION"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["STEP 6: MACHINE CELL DESIGN"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["STEP 7: CELLULAR LAYOUT"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["STEP 8: PERFORMANCE MONITORING"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["OUTPUT: CELLULAR MANUFACTURING SYSTEM"]

    nodeB --> nodeB1["Identify shape features"]
    nodeB --> nodeB2["Identify tolerances"]
    nodeB --> nodeB3["Identify dimensions"]

    nodeC --> nodeC1["Identify processes"]
    nodeC --> nodeC2["Identify tooling"]
    nodeC --> nodeC3["Identify machine sequence"]

    nodeD --> nodeD1["Apply Opitz Code"]
    nodeD --> nodeD2["Apply MICLASS Code"]
    nodeD --> nodeD3["Apply DCLASS Code"]

    nodeE --> nodeE1["Group by similar codes"]
    nodeE --> nodeE2["Validate by similarity coefficient"]

    nodeF --> nodeF1["Cluster machines by PFA"]
    nodeF --> nodeF2["Compute grouping efficiency"]

    style nodeA fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style nodeI fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    style nodeD fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    style nodeE fill:#ea580c,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 The Group Technology Process Flow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawA["RAW DESIGN DATA"] --> c1["CLASSIFICATION"]
    rawB["RAW PROCESS DATA"] --> c1
    c1 --> c2["CODING SYSTEM"]
    c2 --> c3["PART FAMILY DATABASE"]
    c3 --> c4["PFA INCIDENCE MATRIX"]
    c4 --> c5["CLUSTERING ALGORITHM"]
    c5 --> c6["PART FAMILIES"]
    c5 --> c7["MACHINE GROUPS"]
    c6 --> c8["GT CELL DESIGN"]
    c7 --> c8
    c8 --> c9["CELLULAR LAYOUT"]
    c9 --> c10["CIM INTEGRATION"]

    style rawA fill:#0369a1,color:#ffffff
    style rawB fill:#0369a1,color:#ffffff
    style c2 fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style c4 fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style c5 fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style c8 fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    style c9 fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    style c10 fill:#581c87,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 The Opitz Code Structure (Hierarchical Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    codeRoot["OPITZ 9-DIGIT CODE"]

    codeRoot --> formBlock["FORM CODE: DIGITS 1-5"]
    codeRoot --> suppBlock["SUPPLEMENTARY CODE: DIGITS 6-9"]

    formBlock --> d1["DIGIT 1: BASIC SHAPE CLASS"]
    formBlock --> d2["DIGIT 2: EXTERNAL FORM"]
    formBlock --> d3["DIGIT 3: INTERNAL FORM"]
    formBlock --> d4["DIGIT 4: PLANE SURFACES"]
    formBlock --> d5["DIGIT 5: AUXILIARY HOLES"]

    suppBlock --> d6["DIGIT 6: MAJOR DIMENSION"]
    suppBlock --> d7["DIGIT 7: MATERIAL"]
    suppBlock --> d8["DIGIT 8: TOLERANCE"]
    suppBlock --> d9["DIGIT 9: LOT SIZE"]

    d1 --> d1a["0: ROTATIONAL"]
    d1 --> d1b["1-5: PRISMATIC"]
    d1 --> d1c["6-9: SPECIAL"]

    style codeRoot fill:#1e40af,color:#ffffff
    style formBlock fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    style suppBlock fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    style d1a fill:#fbbf24,color:#000000
    style d1b fill:#fbbf24,color:#000000
    style d1c fill:#fbbf24,color:#000000
```

## 4.4 Functional Layout vs. Cellular Layout Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph functionalLayout["FUNCTIONAL LAYOUT - BEFORE GT"]
        fA["ALL LATHES"] --> fB["ALL MILLS"] --> fC["ALL DRILLS"] --> fD["ALL GRINDERS"]
    end

    subgraph cellularLayout["CELLULAR LAYOUT - AFTER GT"]
        cA["CELL 1: PART FAMILY 1"]
        cB["CELL 2: PART FAMILY 2"]
        cC["CELL 3: PART FAMILY 3"]
    end

    functionalLayout -->|GT IMPLEMENTATION| cellularLayout

    style functionalLayout fill:#fee2e2,color:#000000
    style cellularLayout fill:#dcfce7,color:#000000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1

**Q: Define Group Technology. List any four benefits of implementing Group Technology in a manufacturing environment.**

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Group Technology (GT) is a manufacturing philosophy in which parts with similar design and/or manufacturing features are identified, classified, and grouped into **part families** so that they can be processed efficiently in **GT cells**.

**Four Benefits:**
1. **Reduced setup time** — Standard fixtures and tooling are reused for all parts in a family.
2. **Lower work-in-process (WIP) inventory** — Cellular layout shortens the flow line.
3. **Simplified process planning** — Family routings replace individual routings.
4. **Reduced tooling cost** — Standard tool sets suffice for a family.

---

### Question A2

**Q: What is a Part Family? Distinguish between a Part Family and a Machine Cell.**

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Dec 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Part Family:** A collection of parts that share similar geometric features (shape, size) and/or similar manufacturing process requirements (machines, tooling, sequence).

**Machine Cell:** A cluster of physically adjacent machines arranged to produce one or more part families with minimal material handling.

| Aspect | Part Family | Machine Cell |
|---|---|---|
| What it is | A group of similar parts | A group of machines |
| Focus | Design/manufacturing attributes | Physical resources |
| Identified by | Similarity Coefficient, Coding | PFA, Clustering |
| Physical form | Abstract (database entry) | Concrete (shop floor layout) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

> `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the **Opitz Classification and Coding System** in detail. With a neat block diagram, describe the significance of each of the 9 digits.

**(b)** Two parts P1 and P2 are processed as follows:
- P1 → Lathe, Mill, Drill, Grind, Inspect
- P2 → Lathe, Mill, Tap, Inspect

Calculate the **Similarity Coefficient** and comment on whether they can be grouped into the same part family.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

The **Opitz coding system** is a 9-digit hierarchical numeric code developed by **H. Opitz** in 1970. It is divided into two blocks:

**Block A — Form Code (Digits 1 to 5):**
- **Digit 1** — Classifies the basic shape:
  - `0` → Rotational (turned) parts
  - `1` to `5` → Prismatic parts of varying length-to-width ratios
  - `6` to `9` → Special irregular shapes
- **Digit 2** — External rotational form features (steps, threads, gear teeth).
- **Digit 3** — Internal rotational form features (bores, internal threads).
- **Digit 4** — Planar or cylindrical machining surfaces (slots, flat faces).
- **Digit 5** — Auxiliary holes, bores, or gear-tooth features.

**Block B — Supplementary Code (Digits 6 to 9):**
- **Digit 6** — Major dimension classification (range-binned).
- **Digit 7** — Material type (ferrous, non-ferrous, plastic, etc.).
- **Digit 8** — Tolerance and precision grade.
- **Digit 9** — Production lot size bracket.

**Significance:** The Opitz code allows retrieval of parts with similar design and process characteristics from a database. The first 5 digits cover geometry; the next 4 digits cover production context. KTU examiners expect a **block diagram** similar to the one shown in **Section 4.3** above.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Identify Common Operations ($N_o$):**
Operations present in both P1 and P2:
- Lathe ✓
- Mill ✓
- Inspect ✓

$$
N_o = 3
$$

**Step 2 — Identify Differing Operations ($N_d$):**
- Drill (P1 only) → 1 difference
- Grind (P1 only) → 1 difference
- Tap (P2 only) → 1 difference

$$
N_d = 3
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Similarity Coefficient Formula:**

$$
S_{12} = \frac{N_o}{N_o + N_d} = \frac{3}{3 + 3} = \frac{3}{6} = 0.5
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**
Since $S_{12} = 0.5$, the parts share **50% process similarity**. This is exactly at the **lower threshold** of the typical 0.5–0.7 grouping range, meaning the parts are **borderline candidates** for the same family. With additional weighted analysis on production volume, the GT engineer may group them together, but caution is advised.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

> `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**Q: (a)** Define **Production Flow Analysis (PFA)**. Explain the methodology of PFA with reference to the **Machine-Part Incidence Matrix** and **Rank Order Clustering (ROC)** algorithm.

**(b)** A small machine shop processes 4 parts on 5 machines. The operation routes are given below. Construct the Machine-Part incidence matrix, apply **one pass of ROC**, and identify the part families.

| Part | Process Route |
|---|---|
| P1 | M1 → M3 → M4 |
| P2 | M1 → M2 → M5 |
| P3 | M2 → M3 → M5 |
| P4 | M1 → M4 → M5 |

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Definition of PFA:** Production Flow Analysis is a methodology developed by **J. L. Burbidge** in the 1960s that analyzes the process routes of all parts produced in a factory to identify natural part families and corresponding machine groups.

**Methodology:**

1. **Data Collection** — Tabulate the complete process route of every part.
2. **Incidence Matrix Construction** — Build a binary matrix $M[i][j]$ where rows are parts, columns are machines, and entries are 0 or 1.
3. **Clustering** — Rearrange rows and columns to bring 1s into diagonal blocks.
4. **Family Identification** — Diagonal blocks of 1s define the part family and its machine cell.

**Rank Order Clustering (ROC) Algorithm:**
- Treat each row as a binary number with the **leftmost column as MSB**.
- Sort rows in **descending order** of their row-weight value.
- Recompute column weights and sort columns in descending order.
- Repeat until no further rearrangement occurs (convergence).

The final diagonal-blocked matrix reveals the part families.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Build the Incidence Matrix:**

| Part \ Machine | M1 | M2 | M3 | M4 | M5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **P1** | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| **P2** | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| **P3** | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| **P4** | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 |

**Step 2 — Compute Row Weights (binary, MSB = M1, LSB = M5):**

$$
W(P1) = 1\cdot 16 + 0 + 1\cdot 4 + 1\cdot 2 + 0 = 22
$$

$$
W(P2) = 1\cdot 16 + 1\cdot 8 + 0 + 0 + 1\cdot 1 = 25
$$

$$
W(P3) = 0 + 1\cdot 8 + 1\cdot 4 + 0 + 1\cdot 1 = 13
$$

$$
W(P4) = 1\cdot 16 + 0 + 0 + 1\cdot 2 + 1\cdot 1 = 19
$$

**Step 3 — Sort Rows in Descending Order of Row Weight:**

Order: P2(25) → P1(22) → P4(19) → P3(13)

| Reordered Part | M1 | M2 | M3 | M4 | M5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **P2** | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| **P1** | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| **P4** | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| **P3** | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 |

**Step 4 — Compute Column Weights (MSB = P2, LSB = P3):**

$$
W(M1) = 1\cdot 8 + 1\cdot 4 + 1\cdot 2 + 0 = 14
$$

$$
W(M2) = 1\cdot 8 + 0 + 0 + 1\cdot 1 = 9
$$

$$
W(M3) = 0 + 1\cdot 4 + 0 + 1\cdot 1 = 5
$$

$$
W(M4) = 0 + 1\cdot 2 + 1\cdot 1 + 0 = 3
$$

$$
W(M5) = 1\cdot 1 + 0 + 1\cdot 1 + 1\cdot 1 \text{ (using lower bit) ... recompute as 1,0,1,1} = 1011_2 = 11
$$

**Step 5 — Sort Columns in Descending Order of Column Weight:**

Order: M1(14) → M5(11) → M2(9) → M3(5) → M4(3)

| Part \ Machine | M1 | M5 | M2 | M3 | M4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **P2** | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| **P1** | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| **P4** | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| **P3** | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 |

**Step 6 — Identify Part Families from Diagonal Blocks:**

- **Part Family 1** → {P2, P1} → Machines {M1, M5, M2, M3, M4}
- **Part Family 2** → {P4, P3} → Machines {M1, M5, M2, M3, M4}

> **Note:** With only one ROC pass, the diagonal is not yet perfectly blocked; KTU examiners accept a single pass with explanation of the family identification logic.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse the similarity coefficient formula** with the grouping efficiency formula. The Similarity Coefficient uses $N_o / (N_o + N_d)$ while Grouping Efficiency uses $(1 - e/n) \times 100$.
> 2. **Always show binary-weight calculations** explicitly. Skipping this step costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Order of MSB/LSB matters in ROC.** Reversing MSB/LSB direction leads to a different sorted order and is a common mistake.
> 4. **Part families ≠ Machine cells** — make this distinction explicit in your answer.
> 5. **The Opitz code is hierarchical** — do not mix the supplementary digits with the form digits.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Concept of Group Technology:**
> - **GT** = philosophy of grouping similar parts into **part families** and clustering **machines** into **cells**.
> - **Part Family** is identified by the **Similarity Coefficient** $S_{ij} = N_o / (N_o + N_d)$.
> - **Machine Cell** is identified by **Production Flow Analysis (PFA)** and the **Machine-Part Incidence Matrix**.
> - **Opitz code** has **9 digits**: 5 form + 4 supplementary; Digit 1 = basic shape class.
> - **PFA** was developed by **J. L. Burbidge**; the **ROC algorithm** sorts rows/columns by binary weight.
> - **Grouping Efficiency** $\eta = (1 - e/n) \times 100$; good cells achieve **70%–95%**.
> - **Benefits** of GT: reduced setup, lower WIP, simplified planning, lower tooling cost, easier estimating.
> - **GT** is a **prerequisite** for **Cellular Manufacturing** and **FMS (Flexible Manufacturing Systems)** in a CIM environment.
> - **Functional layout** groups machines by type; **Cellular layout** groups machines by part family — GT enables the transition.
> - **Threshold** for family membership: Similarity Coefficient $\geq 0.5$ to 0.7.
> - **GT integrates with CAPP, CAD, and PPC** modules in a CIM architecture (relevant for OERUT613).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification and Coding of Part Families: Production Flow Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT613 - COMPUTER INTEGRATED MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Classification and Coding of Part Families: Production Flow Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Classification and Coding of Parts — Formal Definition

In Group Technology (GT), **Classification and Coding** is the systematic procedure of assigning symbolic alphanumeric identifiers (codes) to manufactured parts based on their design attributes (geometry, dimensions, tolerances) and/or manufacturing attributes (processes, operations, tooling, machines). The code acts as a compressed, retrievable database key that reveals everything about a part's design and manufacture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight**
> *Classification* = the act of sorting parts into families.
> *Coding* = the assignment of symbols (digits/letters) that describe a part's attributes.
> A **Part Family** is a collection of parts that share similar design features *and/or* similar manufacturing operations.

### Types of Coding Systems (KTU Syllabus Focus)

| Coding System | Basis | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Opitz System** | Design + Manufacturing | 9-digit mono-code, 3-digit additional code |
| **DCLASS (Brisch)** | Design | 12-digit code, decimal-based |
| **MICLASS** | Design + Manufacturing | 12-digit hierarchical (Netherlands origin) |
| **KK-3 (Suhov)** | Manufacturing | 3-digit code based on machine sequences |
| **PFA (Burbridge)** | Production routes | No formal code; route-based clustering |

## 1.2 Production Flow Analysis (PFA) — Formal Definition

**Production Flow Analysis (PFA)** is an analytical methodology developed by **J.L. Burbridge (1963)** that examines the routing of all parts through a factory and groups them into **Part Families** whose members require similar sequences of operations and machines. The result is the formation of **Machine Cells** (also called *Group Technology Cells*), which are physical groupings of machines dedicated to producing one or more part families.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core PFA Premise (Burbridge's Hypothesis):**
> *If parts require similar processing sequences, they should be classified together into a Part Family. Conversely, if machines process a similar set of parts, they should be grouped together into a Machine Cell.*

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Think of a Huge Restaurant Menu:** 🍽️
Imagine a giant restaurant that serves thousands of dishes. Without organization, cooks would waste time searching for recipes, ingredients, and equipment. So, the chef:
1. **Classifies** dishes into "Families" (Italian, Chinese, Desserts, Beverages).
2. **Codes** each dish with a symbol (e.g., `IT-01 = Margherita Pizza`).
3. **Groups** all pizza-making equipment (dough roller, oven, pizza cutter) into a single "Pizza Station" (Machine Cell).
4. Now, whenever order `IT-01` arrives, the pizza station handles it end-to-end — *no shuttling between far-apart stations*.

PFA does exactly this in a factory — it reorganizes a chaotic shop floor into **logical cells** that mirror part families, drastically reducing material handling, setup time, and work-in-process (WIP).

## 1.4 The Group Technology Linkage

Classification/Coding and PFA are **complementary** tools under GT:

- **Classification & Coding** → Identity side (tells us *what* a part is)
- **PFA** → Operational side (tells us *how* it is made and *where* it should flow)

Together, they form the foundation of **Cellular Manufacturing**, which is the practical realization of GT on the shop floor.

> [!TIP]
> **Remember:** PFA does not require a formal coding system. It uses **route sheets and production data** directly. This is a frequent KTU exam point — do not confuse PFA with Opitz/MICLASS.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Opitz Classification System (Most Tested in KTU)

Developed by **H. Opitz (1970, Aachen, Germany)**, this is the most widely cited mono-code system. It is a **hierarchical hybrid code** combining both design and manufacturing data.

### Structure of the Opitz Code (9 Digits + 3 Digits)

**Main Code (9 digits) — Form (F) and Function (P):**

| Digit Position | Category | Sub-digit Meaning | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | Form Class (external shape) | 0=rotational, 1=prismatic, … 9=misc | Design |
| **2** | Form Code (external shape detail) | L, N, R, V, etc. (shape features) | Design |
| **3** | Form Code (rotational/transversal features) | Holes, grooves, threads, etc. | Design |
| **4** | Form Code (plane surfaces, gear teeth) | Slots, flanges, gear | Design |
| **5** | Auxiliary form code | Functional elements, threads, fits | Design |
| **6, 7, 8** | **Primary Process (P1)** | Sequence of processes (10, 20, 30, …, 90) | Manufacturing |
| **9** | **Major Dimension** | Size code (1 to 9) | Design |

**Supplementary Code (3 digits) — for production data:**
- **Digit 10** → Work material (type of metal/plastic)
- **Digit 11** → Condition of stock (bar, casting, forging)
- **Digit 12** → Production lot size / accuracy

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction (KTU Favorite):**
> Digits **1–5** = **Form Code (Design)**
> Digits **6–9** = **Primary Process (Manufacturing)**
> Digits **10–12** = **Supplementary (Production)**
> The process code (6,7,8) is **ordered sequentially** based on the actual production route.

## 2.2 PFA Methodology — The Three-Stage Burbridge Procedure

Burbridge's PFA proceeds in three progressive stages, each adding complexity and precision:

### **Stage 1: Analysis of Production Flow (Single-Pass Clustering)**
- Construct a **Part-Machine Incidence Matrix (P-M Matrix)** where rows = parts, columns = machines.
- Entry `M[i][j] = 1` if part `i` visits machine `j`; `0` otherwise.
- Rearrange rows and columns to cluster all `1`s near the diagonal.
- The dense diagonal blocks define **initial part families and machine cells**.

### **Stage 2: Analysis of Production Flow (Two-Stage Analysis)**
- Begins where Stage 1 leaves off.
- Allows **exceptional parts** (those that don't fit cleanly) to be assigned to multiple cells or routed to a **remainder cell** (for "stragglers").
- Reduces the number of inter-cell moves.

### **Stage 3: Full Production Flow Analysis**
- Considers **operation sequences**, not just machine visits.
- Builds a **From-To Matrix** or **Operation Process Chart** for each part.
- Uses **Cluster Analysis** techniques (Rank Order Clustering, Single Linkage, Bond Energy Algorithm) to identify optimal cell formation.
- Provides quantitative measures of cell performance (grouping efficiency).

## 2.3 Tools and Techniques Used in PFA

| Technique | Inventor | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Rank Order Clustering (ROC)** | King & Nakornchai (1982) | Binary matrix row/column reordering to expose diagonal blocks |
| **Bond Energy Algorithm (BEA)** | McCormick, Schweitzer, White (1972) | Permutes matrix to maximize weighted adjacent 1s |
| **Single Linkage Cluster Analysis** | Sneath (1957) | Agglomerative hierarchical clustering of part-machine incidence |
| **Similarity Coefficient** | Various | Measures pairwise part-to-part / machine-to-machine likeness |

## 2.4 PFA — Key Output: The Machine Cell

A **Machine Cell** is a physical cluster of machines arranged to process a specific part family. The transition from a process-based layout to a cell-based layout yields:

- Reduced material handling (typically 30–70%)
- Lower WIP inventory
- Shorter throughput time (lead time)
- Improved operator skill specialization
- Easier production scheduling and control

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Grouping Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{m \cdot n - \sum\limits_{i} n_i \cdot \sum\limits_{j} m_j + m \cdot n_{ex}}{m \cdot n}$ | Kumar & Panneerselvam measure of cell performance |
| **Part-Machine Incidence** | $A = [a_{ij}]_{m \times n}$ | Binary matrix (1 = part visits machine, 0 = otherwise) |
| **Rank Order Score (ROC)** | $R_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} a_{ij} \cdot 2^{(j-1)}$ | Binary-decimal ranking weight for sorting |
| **Jaccard Similarity** | $S_{AB} = \dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | Part-to-part similarity coefficient |
| **Machine Utilization (Cell)** | $U_j = \dfrac{T_{prod}}{T_{avail}}$ | Ratio of productive time to available time |
| **Number of Inter-Cell Moves** | $M_{inter} = \sum (\text{moves between cells})$ | Should be minimized for efficient cells |
| **Burbridge Production Range** | $n_{pr} = \text{Number of part families}$ | Output of Stage 1 PFA |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU derivations, **never use `|` for absolute value inside a markdown table** — write `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent table corruption.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

PFA and GT-based coding systems are foundational to:
- **FMS (Flexible Manufacturing Systems)** — machines grouped by cell logic
- **CAPP (Computer-Aided Process Planning)** — variant planning uses GT codes to retrieve standard process plans
- **Lean Manufacturing & Just-in-Time** — cellular layout is the physical embodiment of flow
- **CNC Setup Reduction** — parts in the same family share fixtures and tools
- **CAD/CAM Integration** — GT codes enable standard part retrieval and design reuse

> [!TIP]
> KTU often asks: *"How does PFA differ from a formal coding system?"* — **Answer:** PFA analyses *actual production routes* and does not require prior part codes. It is *data-driven*, while Opitz is *code-driven*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: PFA Part-Machine Incidence Matrix (Stage 1)

Consider a small factory with **5 parts (P1–P5)** and **6 machines (M1–M6)**. The routing data is given in the table below. We will perform **Rank Order Clustering (ROC)** to identify the cell.

### Given Part Routing

| Part | Process Route (Sequence of Machines) |
|---|---|
| **P1** | M1 → M3 → M5 |
| **P2** | M1 → M2 → M4 |
| **P3** | M2 → M4 → M6 |
| **P4** | M3 → M5 → M6 |
| **P5** | M1 → M2 → M5 |

### Step 1: Construct the Initial Part-Machine Incidence Matrix

We set $a_{ij} = 1$ if part $i$ uses machine $j$, else $0$.

$$
A^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix}
 & M1 & M2 & M3 & M4 & M5 & M6 \\
P1 & 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
P2 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\
P3 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 1 \\
P4 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 1 & 1 \\
P5 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2: Rank Order Clustering (ROC) — Step-by-Step

**ROC Procedure:**
- For each **row**, interpret the binary entries as a binary number and convert to decimal.
- Sort rows in **descending order of row decimal value**.
- Repeat for columns on the new matrix.
- Iterate until stable.

**Row Decimal Values (Treating 1 = LSB at column 1):**

$$
R(P1) = 1(2^0) + 0(2^1) + 1(2^2) + 0(2^3) + 1(2^4) + 0(2^5) = 1 + 4 + 16 = 21
$$

$$
R(P2) = 1 + 2 + 0 + 8 + 0 + 0 = 11
$$

$$
R(P3) = 0 + 2 + 0 + 8 + 0 + 32 = 42
$$

$$
R(P4) = 0 + 0 + 4 + 0 + 16 + 32 = 52
$$

$$
R(P5) = 1 + 2 + 0 + 0 + 16 + 0 = 19
$$

**Sort rows in descending order:** P4 (52) > P3 (42) > P1 (21) > P5 (19) > P2 (11)

$$
A^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix}
 & M1 & M2 & M3 & M4 & M5 & M6 \\
P4 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 1 & 1 \\
P3 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 1 \\
P1 & 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
P5 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
P2 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Column Decimal Values:**

$$
C(M1) = 0+0+1+1+1 = 3, \quad C(M2)=0+1+0+1+1=3, \quad C(M3)=1+0+1+0+0=2
$$

$$
C(M4)=0+1+0+0+1=2, \quad C(M5)=1+0+1+1+0=3, \quad C(M6)=1+1+0+0+0=2
$$

**Interpretation:** Sorting columns by descending value gives: M1, M2, M5, M3, M4, M6 (all ties broken by reading right-to-left)

$$
A^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix}
 & M1 & M2 & M5 & M3 & M4 & M6 \\
P4 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 1 \\
P3 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 1 \\
P1 & 1 & 0 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\
P5 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
P2 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Recalculate Row Values:**

$$
R(P4) = 0+0+4+8+0+32=44, \quad R(P3)=0+2+0+0+16+32=50
$$

$$
R(P1) = 1+0+4+8+0+0=13, \quad R(P5)=1+2+4+0+0+0=7
$$

$$
R(P2) = 1+2+0+0+16+0=19
$$

**Sort Rows Descending:** P3 (50) > P4 (44) > P2 (19) > P1 (13) > P5 (7)

$$
A^{(3)} = \begin{bmatrix}
 & M1 & M2 & M5 & M3 & M4 & M6 \\
P3 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 1 \\
P4 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 1 \\
P2 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
P1 & 1 & 0 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\
P5 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Recalculate Columns:**

$$
C(M1)=3, \ C(M2)=3, \ C(M5)=3, \ C(M3)=2, \ C(M4)=2, \ C(M6)=2
$$

No change in column order (sort M1, M2, M5, M3, M4, M6 — stable).

**Recalculate Rows on $A^{(3)}$:**

$$
R(P3) = 0+2+0+0+16+32 = 50, \quad R(P4)=0+0+4+8+0+32=44
$$

$$
R(P2)=1+2+0+0+16+0=19, \quad R(P1)=1+0+4+8+0+0=13
$$

$$
R(P5)=1+2+4+0+0+0=7
$$

**Row order is stable: P3, P4, P2, P1, P5.** Algorithm converged.

### Step 3: Identify the Cells from the Diagonal Blocks

Reading the final matrix $A^{(3)}$:

$$
A^{(final)} = \begin{bmatrix}
 & M1 & M2 & M5 & M3 & M4 & M6 \\
P3 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 1 \\
P4 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 1 \\
P2 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
P1 & 1 & 0 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\
P5 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

- **Cell 1 (Top-Left block)**: Parts {P3, P4} use Machines {M2, M5, M3, M4, M6} — high diagonal density
- **Cell 2 (Bottom-Left block)**: Parts {P2, P1, P5} use Machines {M1, M2, M5}

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** Two part families emerge: **Family A = {P3, P4}** and **Family B = {P1, P2, P5}**, with overlapping machines (M2, M5) being shared, requiring careful cell design.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Grouping Efficiency Calculation

**Given (from the final matrix above):**
- Total parts: $m = 5$, Total machines: $n = 6$
- Let the two cells be defined by the blocks.

**Kumar & Panneerselvam Grouping Efficiency Formula:**

$$
\eta = \frac{m \cdot n - \sum_i n_i \left( \sum_j m_j - 1 \right)}{m \cdot n - \text{Max}(\sum_i n_i, \sum_j m_j)}
$$

For our case (with appropriate block extraction, assume Cell 1 = (2 parts, 3 machines), Cell 2 = (3 parts, 2 machines), with 2 shared machines and 0 exceptional parts):

$$
\eta = \frac{5 \cdot 6 - [3(2-1) + 2(3-1)]}{5 \cdot 6 - \text{Max}(3,2)} = \frac{30 - [3 + 4]}{30 - 3} = \frac{23}{27} \approx 0.8519
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta \approx 85.19\%}
$$

This means the PFA solution groups ~85% of operations within cells — a strong result indicating good cell formation.

> [!TIP]
> KTU problems typically expect students to show the **full ROC iteration** (rows + columns) and clearly state the **part families and machine cells**. Marks are awarded for showing each iteration explicitly.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Decoding an Opitz Code

**Problem:** A part has Opitz code `1 2 3 4 5 - 10 30 50 - A B C`. Interpret it.

**Decoding:**

| Code Digit | Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | `1` | Form class = Prismatic part (block-type) |
| **2** | `2` | External shape = Cylindrical/stepped block |
| **3** | `3` | Plane surfaces with holes/threads |
| **4** | `4` | Auxiliary features (gear teeth or slots) |
| **5** | `5` | Functional elements: threads + close tolerances |
| **6** | `10` | Primary Process 1: Turning (lathe) |
| **7** | `30` | Primary Process 2: Drilling |
| **8** | `50` | Primary Process 3: Milling |
| **9** | (size) | Major dimension category |
| **10** | `A` | Material = Steel (carbon) |
| **11** | `B` | Stock = Bar / Hot-rolled |
| **12** | `C` | Lot size = Medium batch |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process order is sequential:** 10 → 30 → 50 means: **Turn → Drill → Mill**. The order of digits 6, 7, 8 reflects the actual production route sequence. KTU often tests this interpretation logic.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — PFA vs. Formal Coding

| Parameter | PFA | Opitz / MICLASS / DCLASS |
|---|---|---|
| Data source | Production route sheets | Design drawings + process data |
| Code requirement | None required | Mandatory pre-coding |
| Stage of GT | Implementation stage | Database creation stage |
| Best suited for | Existing products with routing data | New product design, variant CAPP |
| Output | Machine cells + Part families | Standard codes for retrieval |
| Strength | Captures real shop-floor practice | Enables design reuse, CAPP |
| Limitation | Ignores design similarity | Requires accurate process data |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End PFA Process Flow (Mermaid)

The diagram below shows the complete Burbridge PFA methodology from data collection to cell implementation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Collect Production Data"] --> B["Gather Route Sheets for All Parts"]
    B --> C["Identify All Machines in Shop Floor"]
    C --> D["Build Part Machine Incidence Matrix"]
    D --> E["Apply Matrix Reordering Algorithm"]
    E --> F["Stage 1: Single Pass Clustering"]
    F --> G{Is the Diagonal Clean?}
    G -- No --> H["Stage 2: Two Stage Analysis"]
    H --> I["Allow Exceptional Parts Remainder Cell"]
    I --> J{Is Sequence Order Preserved?}
    J -- No --> K["Stage 3: Full Production Flow Analysis"]
    K --> L["Build From To Operation Matrix"]
    L --> M["Apply Rank Order Clustering or Bond Energy"]
    M --> N["Identify Part Families and Machine Cells"]
    J -- Yes --> N
    G -- Yes --> N
    N --> O["Calculate Grouping Efficiency"]
    O --> P["Design Physical Cell Layout"]
    P --> Q["END: Implement Cellular Manufacturing"]
    Q --> R["Validate with Pilot Run"]
    R --> S["Continuous Improvement Loop"] --> A
```

```mermaid
    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    A1 --> B3
    A2 --> C1
    A2 --> C2
    A3 --> C3
    B1 --> D1
    B2 --> D1
    B3 --> D1
    C1 --> D2
    C2 --> D2
    C3 --> D2
    D1 --> D3
    D1 --> D4

    D2 --> E3

```

## 4.3 Opitz Code Structure — Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Digit 1: Form Class 0 to 9"] --> B["Digit 2: External Shape Code"]
    B --> C["Digit 3: Internal Features Code"]
    C --> D["Digit 4: Plane Surface Code"]
```
