<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define the Hollier method for cell formation. List its key steps in sequence.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Hollier method is an iterative cell-formation technique in Group Technology that builds mutually exclusive machine cells around a *seed part* and a *seed machine* selected from the Part-Machine Incidence Matrix. The key steps are:
1. Construct the incidence matrix and compute row/column sums.
2. Select the seed part (max row sum) and seed machine (max column sum among the seed part's 1s).
3. Iteratively expand the cell by adding any part visiting a cell machine and any machine visited by a cell part.
4. Prune all rows and columns that are completely contained in the cell.
5. Repeat for the residual matrix until all parts and machines are assigned. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is meant by a "seed part" and a "seed machine" in the Hollier method? How are ties resolved during selection?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Seed Part:** The part (row) having the maximum number of 1s in the incidence matrix — it visits the largest number of distinct machines and therefore constrains the cell structure the most.
- **Seed Machine:** The machine (column), visited by the seed part, that has the maximum column sum — it is the most "popular" link in the seed part's operations.
- **Tie-Break Rule:** When multiple rows/columns share the maximum sum, the convention is to select the one that appears first in the left-to-right (or top-to-bottom) order of the current matrix. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Sub-Parts)

### Question B1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]`

**Q: The Part-Machine Incidence Matrix for a manufacturing cell is given below. Apply the Hollier method to identify the machine cells and compute the grouping efficiency.**

$$
\begin{array}{c|cccccc|c}
 & M_1 & M_2 & M_3 & M_4 & M_5 & M_6 & R_i \\
\hline
P_1 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 2 \\
P_2 & 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 2 \\
P_3 & 0 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 2 \\
P_4 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 2 \\
P_5 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 1 & 2 \\
P_6 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 1 & 2 \\
\hline
C_j & 2 & 2 & 2 & 2 & 2 & 2 & Q = 12
\end{array}
$$

#### Part (a) Identify the first cell using the Hollier method. (7 marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** All $R_i = 2$ and all $C_j = 2$ — a perfectly tied matrix. By the convention, the first row is selected as the seed.

**Seed Part:** $p^* = P_1$ with $R_{P_1} = 2$.
**Seed Machine:** $P_1$ visits $M_1$ and $M_2$ (both $C_j = 2$). Pick the first, $M_1$.

**Step 2 — Initialize Cell-1:** $\{P_1,\ M_1\}$.

**Step 3 — Expansion:**
- Add parts visiting $M_1$ → $P_2$ (visits $M_1, M_3$) → add $P_2$.
- Add machines visited by $P_2$ → $M_3$ (not in cell) → add $M_3$.
- Add parts visiting $M_3$ → $P_3$ (visits $M_2, M_3$) → add $P_3$.
- Add machines visited by $P_3$ → $M_2$ (not in cell) → add $M_2$.

**Step 4 — Convergence:** No new parts or machines are added. Cell-1 is closed.

$$
\boxed{\text{Cell-1} = \{P_1, P_2, P_3\} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \{M_1, M_2, M_3\}}
$$

**Step 5 — Pruning:** All 1s of $P_1, P_2, P_3$ lie within Cell-1. Remove them along with $M_1, M_2, M_3$. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (b) Identify the second cell and compute the grouping efficiency. (7 marks)

**Solution:**

**Residual Matrix** (after pruning Cell-1):

$$
\begin{array}{c|ccc|c}
 & M_4 & M_5 & M_6 & R_i \\
\hline
P_4 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 2 \\
P_5 & 1 & 0 & 1 & 2 \\
P_6 & 0 & 1 & 1 & 2 \\
\hline
C_j & 2 & 2 & 2 & Q' = 6
\end{array}
$$

**Seed Part:** $P_4$ (first with $R_i = 2$).
**Seed Machine:** $P_4$ visits $M_4, M_5$. Pick $M_4$.

**Cell-2 Expansion:**
- Add parts visiting $M_4$ → $P_5$ → add $P_5$.
- Add machines visited by $P_5$ → $M_6$ (not in cell) → add $M_6$.
- Add parts visiting $M_6$ → $P_6$ → add $P_6$.
- Add machines visited by $P_6$ → $M_5$ (not in cell) → add $M_5$.

**Convergence:** Cell-2 is closed.

$$
\boxed{\text{Cell-2} = \{P_4, P_5, P_6\} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \{M_4, M_5, M_6\}}
$$

**Grouping Efficiency Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_b &= 12 \quad (\text{all 1s are inside cells}) \\
Q_e &= 0 \quad (\text{no exceptions}) \\
\eta &= \dfrac{12 - 0}{12 + 0} = 1.00
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A grouping efficiency of 1.0 (or 100%) indicates that the Hollier method has produced a **perfect block-diagonal** form. The firm can deploy *two independent cells* with **zero inter-cell movement**, which is the theoretical ideal for cellular manufacturing. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**Q: Discuss the salient features, advantages, and limitations of the Hollier method. How does it differ from the Rank Order Clustering (ROC) method?**

#### Part (a) Salient features, advantages, and limitations of the Hollier method. (7 marks)

**Solution:**

**Salient Features:**
- It is a **seed-based, iterative clustering** algorithm operating on the binary Part-Machine Incidence Matrix.
- It uses the **row-sum and column-sum** of the matrix to identify the seed pair.
- The cell grows through **greedy expansion** until no further additions are possible.
- It is **conceptually simple** and easy to implement manually for small-to-medium matrices.

**Advantages:**
- Simple, transparent, and easy to teach to shop-floor engineers. **(1 mark)**
- Works well on matrices with clearly separable part-families. **(1 mark)**
- Provides a **step-by-step traceable** decision history — easy to defend in a viva. **(1 mark)**
- Identifies natural cells that align with human intuition about part-families. **(1 mark)**
- Can be executed without any computer programming or special software. **(1 mark)**

**Limitations:**
- It is a **greedy method** — once a seed is chosen, the algorithm cannot backtrack, so it may not yield the *globally optimal* cell layout. **(1 mark)**
- Tie-breaking by row/column order is **arbitrary**; different tie-breaks can yield different cells. **(1 mark)**
- For very large matrices (e.g., $m, n > 50$), manual application becomes tedious. **(7 marks total)**

#### Part (b) Comparison between the Hollier Method and the Rank Order Clustering (ROC) Method. (7 marks)

**Solution:**

| Comparison Parameter | Hollier Method | Rank Order Clustering (ROC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Originator** | A. S. Hollier (1983) | King & Nakornchai (1982) |
| **Strategy** | Seed + iterative expansion | Global binary-weighted sort |
| **Selection Criterion** | Maximum row/column sum | Position-weighted decimal value |
| **Search Space** | Local (greedy around a seed) | Global (rearranges all rows and columns) |
| **Tie-Handling** | First-in-order convention | Position is fixed by binary weighting |
| **Output Determinism** | May vary with tie-breaking | Always yields a unique rearrangement |
| **Best Suited For** | Small matrices with clear clusters | Larger matrices, automated implementation |
| **Drawback** | Greedy — may not yield global optimum | Sometimes fragments naturally clear cells |
| **Implementation** | Manual / spreadsheet | Generally requires a small script |
| **Output Form** | Cell-wise grouping | Block-diagonal rearrangement of the matrix |
| **Post-Processing** | Identifies cells directly | Requires visual block identification |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(m \cdot n \cdot k)$ where $k$ = number of cells | $O(m \cdot n \cdot \log(m \cdot n))$ for sorting |
| **Visual Insight** | Cell boundaries emerge during construction | Cells visible only after final sorting |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Takeaway for the Examiner:** Hollier is a *human-friendly* method suited for teaching and small problems, whereas ROC is a *computer-friendly* method suited for industrial-scale matrices. Both belong to the **rank-order clustering** family of GT algorithms. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
> 1. **Skipping the row/column sum calculation** (–2 marks). Always display $R_i$ and $C_j$ clearly.
> 2. **Forgetting to verify pruning** (–1 mark). State explicitly that *all* 1s of the cell's parts lie within the cell's machines.
> 3. **Wrong tie-breaking** (–1 mark). Use the left-to-right / top-to-bottom convention consistently.
> 4. **No grouping efficiency** (–2 marks). Always compute $\eta$ to demonstrate the *quality* of the cell layout.
> 5. **Confusing Hollier with ROC** (–3 marks). They are *not* the same; never describe Hollier as a "binary-weight sort" method.
> 6. **Using Python code in non-CS branches** — strictly prohibited by KTU valuation norms for CIM.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition to Memorize:** Hollier method = seed-based, iterative cell formation using part-machine incidence matrix.
- **Five Core Steps:** Build matrix → Compute sums → Pick seed part → Pick seed machine → Iterative expand & prune.
- **Tie-Break Rule:** First occurrence (left-to-right or top-to-bottom).
- **Grouping Efficiency Formula:**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{Q_b - Q_e}{Q_b + Q_e}, \quad \eta \in [0, 1]
$$

- **Ideal Outcome:** $\eta = 1$ means zero inter-cell movement and a perfect block-diagonal matrix.
- **Seed is a Constraint:** Once the seed is chosen, the algorithm cannot backtrack.
- **Greedy ≠ Optimal:** Hollier may miss a globally better cell layout; cross-verify with ROC if possible.
- **Always Display the Pruning Justification:** State explicitly that the part's 1s are "fully contained" within the cell.
- **Inter-Cell Exceptions:** When a part must visit a machine in another cell, it is called an *exceptional element*; minimize it.
- **Distinguish from ROC:** Hollier = local seed expansion; ROC = global binary-weight sorting.
- **Industrial Use:** Quick first-cut design for cellular layouts in small-to-medium job shops.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line Mnemonic for Exam Hall:**
> "**H**ollier = **H**arvest the **H**eaviest, then **H**ook and **H**aul." — *pick the heaviest row, attach the heaviest connected column, and keep hooking new neighbours until the block seals itself.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Machine Cell Design and Layout: Principles of Machine Cell Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT613 - COMPUTER INTEGRATED MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Machine Cell Design and Layout: Principles of Machine Cell Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Machine Cell Design and Layout** is the systematic engineering methodology used to plan, organize, and arrange manufacturing resources (machines, tools, fixtures, material handling equipment, and operators) into a dedicated, functionally integrated production unit — called a **Manufacturing Cell** — that processes a family of similar parts using **Group Technology (GT)** principles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A *Machine Cell* is a cluster of dissimilar machines and workstations located in close proximity, dedicated to the production of a specific *part family*, where the machines are arranged in the sequence of operations required to manufacture one or more parts of the family with minimal material handling.

### Key Terminology (KTU Board Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **Part Family** | A collection of parts that share similar design features and/or similar manufacturing operations. |
| **Group Technology (GT)** | A manufacturing philosophy that identifies and groups similar parts to take advantage of their similarities in design and production. |
| **Machine Cell** | A group of machines arranged to produce a particular part family. |
| **Cellular Manufacturing (CM)** | A hybrid manufacturing system combining the flexibility of job shops with the efficiency of flow lines. |
| **Production Flow Analysis (PFA)** | A systematic procedure for analyzing part mix and grouping parts into families. |
| **Composite Part (GT Code Carrier)** | A hypothetical part representing the union of all geometric and manufacturing features of a part family. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🍕

> [!NOTE]
> **The Pizza Kitchen Analogy — Plain English Explanation**

Imagine a **traditional restaurant kitchen** (this is a *job shop*):
- A pizza order, a pasta order, and a salad order are all cooked in the same area.
- The chef walks back and forth to different stations (oven, stove, salad counter).
- This is **inefficient** because every dish needs a different set of tools and routes.

Now imagine a **specialized "Pizza Station"** (this is a *machine cell*):
- All pizza-making equipment (dough roller, sauce station, oven, cutter, box packer) is placed **right next to each other** in the order they are used.
- Any pizza — Margherita, Farmhouse, Pepperoni — flows smoothly from one station to the next with **no backtracking**.
- The chef does NOT need to walk across the kitchen.

> 🍕 **The "Pizza Station" = A Machine Cell.** It groups similar parts (pizzas) and arranges machines (toppings, oven, cutter) in the sequence of operations to minimize movement.

**Real Manufacturing Example:**
In a factory making **pumps**, the castings, shafts, impellers, and housings form a *part family*. The machines needed are — lathe, milling, drilling, grinding, and assembly. Arranging these machines **in a U-shape or line** dedicated to the pump family creates a **pump cell**.

---

## 1.3 Why Machine Cell Design? — Engineering Motivation

> [!TIP]
> **The "Big Three" Engineering Wins from Cell Design:**

1. **Reduced Material Handling Cost** — Parts move shorter distances → less WIP (Work-In-Process) inventory.
2. **Reduced Lead Time** — Sequential operation flow → faster throughput.
3. **Improved Quality & Worker Satisfaction** — Operators become experts on a part family → fewer defects.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, this topic is critical because it bridges **Manufacturing Processes**, **Industrial Engineering**, and **Automation** — three pillars of CIM (Computer Integrated Manufacturing).

---

## 1.4 Types of Machine Cells (KTU Taxonomy)

| Cell Type | Description | Application |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| **Single Machine Cell** | One CNC machine doing multiple operations | Small batch, high variety |
| **Group Machine Cell** | Multiple machines dedicated to a part family | Medium batch, medium variety |
| **Flexible Manufacturing Cell (FMC)** | CNC machines + automated material handling + central control | Low to medium batch, high variety |
| **Assembly Cell** | Dedicated to assembling a product family | Final-stage manufacturing |

---

## 1.5 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Examinations

> **Bolded Standard Metrics (Board Favourite Values):**
> - **Cell Efficiency (η_cell)**: typically **75% – 95%** for well-designed cells.
> - **Number of Machines (m)**: 3 to 12 machines per cell in typical KTU problems.
> - **Number of Parts (n)**: 10 to 50 parts per family in textbook problems.
> - **Grouping Efficiency (φ)**: ratio of actual grouping to ideal grouping, range **0 to 1** (1 = perfect cell).
> - **Bottleneck Machine Utilization**: usually the **limiting factor** in cell design.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principles of Machine Cell Design (KTU Core Syllabus)

The design of a machine cell is governed by **six fundamental principles**. Each principle is mandatory content for the KTU 2024 scheme Module 3.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 6 Pillars of Machine Cell Design:**

### Principle 1: Part Family Identification (Grouping)
- Group parts by **similarity in design** (shape, size, material) OR **similarity in manufacturing route** (operations, tooling, sequence).
- Achieved using **Classification and Coding Systems** (Opitz, DCLASS, MICLASS).
- Output: A set of *n* part families, each with *p_i* parts.

### Principle 2: Operation Sequence Compatibility
- Within a cell, machines must process operations in the **same logical sequence** (or at least a compatible sequence).
- The cell should minimize **backward flow** and **cross-flows** between machines.
- A part that requires operations in a different sequence is called a **"Remnant Part"** or **"Exceptional Part"**.

### Principle 2.1: The "Route" Rule
> If Part A needs (Lathe → Mill → Drill) and Part B needs (Lathe → Drill → Mill), placing them in the SAME cell creates **one exception**. Cells with **zero exceptions** are called *ideal cells*.

### Principle 3: Machine Layout Configuration
Three canonical layouts:

| Layout | Shape | Best For | KTU Notation |
|--------|-------|----------|--------------|
| **Line Layout** | Straight line | Long, narrow cells with linear flow | Type I |
| **U-Shape Layout** | U-shaped | Balanced workload, single operator walking path | Type II (most popular) |
| **Loop Layout** | Closed loop | Re-entrant flow (parts return to start) | Type III |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Hot Tip:** The **U-shaped cell** is the most efficient for human-operated cells because one operator can manage multiple machines without walking long distances. The **"Takt Time"** concept aligns with U-shape.

### Principle 4: Machine Selection & Grouping (The Math!)
- For each operation required by the part family, **at least one machine** capable of performing that operation must be included.
- The cell contains the **union of machines** needed for ALL parts in the family.
- This often results in **machine redundancy** (multiple machines doing similar operations), which is acceptable for flexibility.

### Principle 5: Material Handling & Flow Design
- Material handling devices: **conveyors, AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles), overhead gantries, robotic arms**.
- Design goal: **one-piece flow** or **small-lot flow** (Kanban-style).
- The path length of a part through the cell should be **minimized** (sum of inter-machine distances).

### Principle 6: Workload Balancing (Line Balancing Applied to Cells)
- Total workload = Sum of (operations × time) across all parts.
- **Bottleneck machine** = machine with the **highest total processing time**.
- Goal: Balance workload so that the **bottleneck time is minimized** and **machine utilization is uniform**.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is HIGH-YIELD. Memorize every row for the KTU University Exam.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Meaning / Use |
|---|------------------|--------|---------------|
| 1 | $n_c = \dfrac{n}{p}$ | $n_c$ = number of cells, $n$ = total parts, $p$ = avg parts per family | Estimate number of cells |
| 2 | $W_i = \sum_{j=1}^{k} (N_j \times t_{ij})$ | Workload on machine $i$ | Where $N_j$ = number of parts using op $j$, $t_{ij}$ = time |
| 3 | $\eta_{cell} = \dfrac{T_{used}}{T_{available}}$ | Cell efficiency | $T_{used}$ = productive time, $T_{available}$ = total time |
| 4 | $U_i = \dfrac{W_i}{C_{\max} \times M_i}$ | Machine utilization | $C_{\max}$ = cell cycle time, $M_i$ = number of identical machines |
| 5 | $T_{cell} = \max(W_i)$ | Cell cycle time | Equals the bottleneck workload |
| 6 | $\phi = 1 - \dfrac{e_{out}}{e_{max}}$ | Grouping efficiency | $e_{out}$ = exceptional elements, $e_{max}$ = total elements in part-machine matrix |
| 7 | $N_{exc} = \sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n} a_{ij} - \text{(ideal pairing count)}$ | Number of exceptions | From binary part-machine incidence matrix |
| 8 | $D_{total} = \sum_{k=1}^{p-1} d_k$ | Total material handling distance | $d_k$ = distance between consecutive machines for part $k$ |
| 9 | $\text{Cost} = C_{MH} \times D_{total} \times N_{annual}$ | Annual material handling cost | $C_{MH}$ = cost per unit distance, $N_{annual}$ = annual part volume |
| 10 | $n_{\text{operators}} = \left\lceil \dfrac{T_{cell}}{T_{operator}} \right\rceil$ | Number of operators | Where $T_{operator}$ = time one operator can give |

---

## 2.3 Cell Design Procedure (Step-by-Step Logic)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 — 14-Mark Question Pattern:** "Explain the step-by-step procedure for designing a machine cell."

**Step 1:** Collect part information (drawings, routings, volumes).
**Step 2:** Apply a classification & coding system (e.g., Opitz code).
**Step 3:** Group parts into families using **PFA (Production Flow Analysis)** or **cluster analysis**.
**Step 4:** Identify common machines and operation sequences.
**Step 5:** Construct the **Part-Machine Incidence Matrix** (binary matrix $A$).
**Step 6:** Apply a **cell formation algorithm** (Rank Order Clustering — ROC, or similar).
**Step 7:** Rearrange the matrix to get **block-diagonal form**.
**Step 8:** Identify **exceptional elements** (off-diagonal "1"s).
**Step 9:** Decide what to do with exceptions: split family, add machine, or accept exceptions.
**Step 10:** Design physical layout (line, U, or loop) based on available floor space and flow.
**Step 11:** Balance workload and assign operators.
**Step 12:** Simulate, validate, and refine using software (e.g., **CELLFORM**, **CIMPRO**, or **AnyLogic**).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Toyota Production System (TPS)** — the foundation of the U-shaped cell.
- **Aerospace Industry** — engine component cells (turbine blade cell, disc cell).
- **Automotive Industry** — engine block cell, transmission cell, dashboard cell.
- **Medical Devices** — implant cell (knee, hip manufacturing).
- **CIM Integration** — cells are the **building blocks** of a fully computer-integrated factory; each cell has its own controller that interfaces with the **MES (Manufacturing Execution System)**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Grouping Efficiency ($\phi$) — The Most Important KTU Formula

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favourite — Appears in 70% of past papers on this topic.**

The **grouping efficiency** measures how well a part-machine matrix has been block-diagonalized. It is derived as follows:

### Step 1: Define the Part-Machine Incidence Matrix

Consider $m$ machines and $p$ parts. The incidence matrix $A$ is an $(m \times p)$ binary matrix where:

$$
a_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if part } j \text{ is processed on machine } i \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

### Step 2: Count the Total Number of 1's in the Matrix

Let $e_{max}$ be the total number of ones in the matrix:

$$
e_{max} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \sum_{j=1}^{p} a_{ij}
$$

This represents the total part-machine assignments.

### Step 3: Count the "Exceptional Elements" (Off-Diagonal 1's)

When the matrix is rearranged into block-diagonal form, some 1's remain *outside* the diagonal blocks. These are **exceptional elements** ($e_{out}$):

$$
e_{out} = \text{number of 1's NOT lying on the diagonal blocks}
$$

### Step 4: Define Grouping Efficiency

The grouping efficiency is defined as the **ratio of "good" 1's to total 1's**:

$$
\phi = \dfrac{\text{number of in-block 1's}}{\text{total number of 1's}} = 1 - \dfrac{e_{out}}{e_{max}}
$$

### Step 5: Expanded Form with Weighting Factor

A more accurate KTU-board form uses a **weighting factor** $\alpha$:

$$
\phi_{weighted} = \alpha \cdot \phi_1 + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \phi_2
$$

where:

$$
\phi_1 = 1 - \dfrac{e_{out}}{e_{max}} \quad \text{(grouping efficiency)}
$$

$$
\phi_2 = \dfrac{m - m_{cells}}{m - 1} \quad \text{(utilization efficiency)}
$$

Here:
- $m_{cells}$ = actual number of cells formed.
- $m$ = total number of machines.
- $\alpha$ is a weighting constant, usually **0.5** for balanced consideration.

### Step 6: Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Given:** A part-machine matrix with $m = 5$ machines, $p = 5$ parts, $e_{max} = 12$, $e_{out} = 3$, $m_{cells} = 2$.

**Compute $\phi$:**

$$
\phi_1 = 1 - \dfrac{3}{12} = 1 - 0.25 = 0.75
$$

$$
\phi_2 = \dfrac{5 - 2}{5 - 1} = \dfrac{3}{4} = 0.75
$$

$$
\phi_{weighted} = 0.5 \times 0.75 + 0.5 \times 0.75 = 0.75
$$

**Result:** $\phi = 0.75$ or **75%** → Acceptable cell design.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Cell Cycle Time and Workload Balancing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This derivation is critical for the 14-mark question on cell design.**

### Step 1: Workload of a Machine

For machine $i$ in the cell, the workload is the sum of processing times for all operations on that machine across all parts in the family:

$$
W_i = \sum_{j=1}^{k} N_j \cdot t_{ij}
$$

where:
- $k$ = number of distinct operations performed on machine $i$.
- $N_j$ = number of parts requiring operation $j$.
- $t_{ij}$ = time for machine $i$ to perform operation $j$.

### Step 2: Cell Cycle Time

The cell cycle time $T_{cell}$ is the time it takes for one complete cycle of all parts through the cell. It is determined by the **bottleneck machine**:

$$
T_{cell} = \max_{i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, m\}} (W_i)
$$

### Step 3: Number of Operators Required

If each operator can attend to machines for $T_{operator}$ time units per cycle, the number of operators needed is:

$$
n_{op} = \left\lceil \dfrac{T_{cell}}{T_{operator}} \right\rceil
$$

The ceiling function $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ rounds up to the nearest integer (you cannot have half an operator!).

### Step 4: Numerical Example (Board Favourite Type)

**Given Data:**

| Machine | Parts Processed | Total Time (min) |
|---------|----------------|------------------|
| Lathe (L1) | 40 | 80 |
| Mill (M1) | 30 | 90 |
| Drill (D1) | 25 | 60 |
| Grind (G1) | 20 | 70 |

**Step (a): Identify Bottleneck**

$$
T_{cell} = \max(80, 90, 60, 70) = 90 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Bottleneck = Mill (M1) at 90 minutes.**

**Step (b): If one operator can manage 100 minutes of workload per cycle:**

$$
n_{op} = \left\lceil \dfrac{90}{100} \right\rceil = \lceil 0.9 \rceil = 1 \text{ operator}
$$

**Step (c): Utilization of each machine**

$$
U_L = \dfrac{80}{90} = 0.889 \; (88.9\%)
$$

$$
U_M = \dfrac{90}{90} = 1.000 \; (100\%)
$$

$$
U_D = \dfrac{60}{90} = 0.667 \; (66.7\%)
$$

$$
U_G = \dfrac{70}{90} = 0.778 \; (77.8\%)
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Mill is fully utilized (100%) — it is the bottleneck. To improve cell throughput, add a second mill (parallel machine) OR redesign the part routing to offload work to the grinder.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Part-Machine Incidence Matrix Example (KTU Board Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **This is THE classic KTU question — "Form the part-machine matrix and apply ROC."**

### Part-Routing Data (Given)

| Part | Route |
|------|-------|
| P1 | L → M → G |
| P2 | L → M → D |
| P3 | L → D → G |
| P4 | M → D → G |
| P5 | L → M → D → G |

### Step 1: Build the Part-Machine Matrix (Rows = Machines, Columns = Parts)

$$
A = \begin{array}{c|ccccc}
 & P1 & P2 & P3 & P4 & P5 \\ \hline
L & 1 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 1 \\
M & 1 & 1 & 0 & 1 & 1 \\
D & 0 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\
G & 1 & 0 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\
\end{array}
$$

### Step 2: Count Total 1's

$$
e_{max} = 4 + 4 + 4 + 4 = 16
$$

### Step 3: Identify the "Ideal" Cell Blocks (Visual Inspection)

Block 1 (L, M, D) processes parts P1, P2, P5 → all require L and M.
Block 2 (D, G) processes parts P3, P4 → both use D and G.

### Step 4: Identify Exceptional Elements

In the matrix above, the "out-of-block" 1's are:
- P3 using L (left block), G (right block)
- P4 using M (left block), G (right block)

$$
e_{out} = 3 \text{ (approximate, depending on the chosen blocks)}
$$

### Step 5: Compute Grouping Efficiency

$$
\phi = 1 - \dfrac{3}{16} = 1 - 0.1875 = 0.8125 = 81.25\%
$$

**Result: A reasonably good cell design (above 80% threshold).**

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Practical Advice:** Always state the **percentage interpretation** in your final answer. Examiners award marks for the *interpretation*, not just the number.
