# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define a Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS). List any four types of flexibility provided by FMS.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):**
> A Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS) is a highly automated, computer-integrated group of machines and material handling equipment, controlled by a hierarchical computer system, capable of producing a variety of part types in small to medium batch sizes with minimal manual intervention.
>
> **Four Types of Flexibility (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
> 1. **Machine Flexibility** — ability to produce a range of part types without major setup.
> 2. **Product Flexibility** — ability to introduce new products quickly.
> 3. **Routing Flexibility** — ability to use alternative machines in different sequences.
> 4. **Volume Flexibility** — ability to operate profitably at varied production volumes.

---

### **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between FMC and FMS. (Any 3 points)**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Table Format Recommended):**

| Parameter | Flexible Manufacturing Cell (FMC) | Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS) |
|-----------|-----------------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| Number of machines | 1–3 machines | 4–10 or more machines |
| Complexity | Simple, single workstation group | Complex, multi-station networked |
| Control | Single cell controller | Central cell + area controllers |
| Capital cost | Moderate | Very high |
| Application | Single part family | Multiple part families |
| Throughput | Low–medium | Medium–high |

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question B.A** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 4 Choice Q1]`

**Answer the following:**
- **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the various **subsystems of a Flexible Manufacturing System** with neat block diagrams.
- **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the different **types of layouts** used in FMS and state their advantages.

---

### **Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
An FMS consists of three core subsystems (workstation, material handling, computer control) plus an optional tool management subsystem. These subsystems communicate in real-time to enable flexible production.

**Subsystem 1 — Workstation Subsystem (2 marks):**
This subsystem consists of CNC machine tools (machining centers, turning centers) and inspection stations (CMMs). Each workstation has:
- An Automatic Tool Changer (ATC) with 24–120 tool capacity.
- A pallet changer for loading/unloading during machining.
- A local CNC controller (Level 1) executing NC programs.
- Coolant and chip management systems.

**Subsystem 2 — Material Handling Subsystem (2 marks):**
The MHS transports workpieces, tooling, and operators' instructions between workstations. Common MHS elements include:
- Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs) — for long distances.
- Conveyors (roller, belt) — for fixed paths.
- Industrial robots — for loading/unloading within cells.
- Tow-line systems — for heavy pallets over 500 m.

The MHS uses standardized **pallets and fixtures** so parts can be transferred between any machine without re-clamping.

**Subsystem 3 — Computer Control Subsystem (1.5 marks):**
This is the 5-level hierarchical software/hardware network:
- Level 4: ERP system (business planning).
- Level 3: Shop floor server (production scheduling).
- Level 2: Cell controller (FMS dispatching, routing).
- Level 1: CNC controllers (machine-level execution).
- Level 0: Sensors and actuators.

**Optional — Tool Management Subsystem (0.5 marks):**
Central tool magazine, tool presetter, automatic tool changers, and tool life monitoring software.

**Block Diagram (0.5 marks)** — See Diagram 1 in Section 4 above.

---

### **Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks**

**Introduction (0.5 mark):**
FMS layout defines the physical arrangement of machines and material handling paths. The choice of layout affects routing flexibility, throughput, and capital cost.

**Layout 1 — Linear / In-line (1.5 marks):**
- Machines arranged sequentially along a conveyor.
- **Advantage:** Simple control, low software cost, easy to program.
- **Disadvantage:** No routing flexibility; if one machine fails, line stops.
- **Best for:** Sequential operations on similar parts.

**Layout 2 — Loop (1.5 marks):**
- Machines arranged in a closed loop; AGVs circulate pallets.
- **Advantage:** Routing flexibility, multiple path options, fault tolerant.
- **Disadvantage:** Requires tow-line or circular AGV track.
- **Best for:** Medium-sized FMS (4–8 machines).

**Layout 3 — L-shape / U-shape (1 mark):**
- Machines arranged in an L or U around a central load/unload station.
- **Advantage:** Compact, allows shared tool magazine, easy operator access.
- **Disadvantage:** Limited expansion.
- **Best for:** Cells (FMC) and small FMS.

**Layout 4 — Open-field / Cellular (1.5 marks):**
- Central staging area with machines arranged around it; AGVs move freely in 2D.
- **Advantage:** Maximum routing flexibility, easy to add machines.
- **Disadvantage:** Complex routing software; AGV traffic management is critical.
- **Best for:** Large FMS (10+ machines) with high variety.

**Layout 5 — Hierarchical (1 mark):**
- Cells within cells; each cell is itself an FMC or mini-FMS.
- **Advantage:** Scalable, modular, fault isolation.
- **Disadvantage:** Very complex control hierarchy.
- **Best for:** Plant-wide FMS networks.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
Selection depends on part variety, batch size, capital, and required flexibility.

---

### **Question B.B** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 4 Choice Q2]` **(ALTERNATIVE)**

**Answer the following:**
- **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **eight types of flexibility** in FMS with suitable examples.
- **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **various phases of FMS planning and design** methodology in detail.

---

### **Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks**

**(Each flexibility: 0.75 mark — Definition 0.5 + Example 0.25)**

1. **Machine Flexibility** — The capacity of a single machine to produce a variety of part types without requiring major setup changes. *Example:* A CNC turning center producing shafts, pins, and bushings by simply changing the program and tooling.

2. **Product Flexibility** — The ability to introduce and produce new parts in the system without major reconfiguration. *Example:* Adding a new aerospace bracket to an existing FMS by writing a new NC program and adding required tools.

3. **Process Flexibility** — The ability of a system to produce a given part using multiple alternative processes or alternative machines. *Example:* A part can be drilled on either Machine M2 or M4.

4. **Routing Flexibility** — The ability to vary the sequence of operations on a part across multiple machines. *Example:* Part can go M1→M3→M2 or M1→M2→M3.

5. **Volume Flexibility** — The ability to maintain profitability across a range of production volumes. *Example:* Same FMS producing 500 or 5000 units profitably.

6. **Expansion Flexibility** — The ability to easily add new workstations, machines, or capabilities. *Example:* Modular FMS that accepts an additional CNC machine without redesigning control software.

7. **Operation Flexibility** — The ability to perform the same operation on different machines within the FMS. *Example:* A milling operation can be done on either a VMC or HMC.

8. **Production Flexibility** — The ability to produce a mixed batch of parts simultaneously without disrupting ongoing production. *Example:* Producing 10 different parts in random order on the same FMS in a single shift.

**Hierarchical Note (0.5 mark):**
Machine ⊂ Process ⊂ Product ⊂ Production flexibility (nested hierarchy).

---

### **Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks**

**Introduction (0.5 mark):**
FMS planning is a 6-phase systematic methodology that converts a manufacturing need into an operational FMS.

**Phase 1 — Part Family Formation (1.5 marks):**
- Group parts with similar design features or manufacturing operations.
- Uses **Classification & Coding** systems: **Opitz, MICLASS, DCLASS, KK-3**.
- Output: families of similar parts (e.g., all shafts, all flanges).

**Phase 2 — Process Planning (1.5 marks):**
- Determine operations and routing for each part family.
- Use **Group Technology (GT)** to identify common processes.
- Output: standard route sheets for each family.

**Phase 3 — Workstation Selection (1 mark):**
- Match required operations to available machines.
- Build a **Machine Capability Matrix**.
- Output: list of machines assigned to each operation.

**Phase 4 — Material Handling Selection (1 mark):**
- Analyze part flow distances, weights, and batch sizes.
- Choose between AGVs, conveyors, robots.
- Output: MHS specification and pallet design.

**Phase 5 — Layout Configuration (1 mark):**
- Decide physical layout: linear, loop, L-shape, open-field, hierarchical.
- Optimize using flow analysis and distance minimization.
- Output: floor plan with machine positions.

**Phase 6 — Control System Design (1 mark):**
- Specify 5-level computer hierarchy hardware and software.
- Decide dispatching rules (CR, FCFS, EDD).
- Output: control software specification and network diagram.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
A well-executed 6-phase methodology ensures the FMS meets production targets, ROI, and flexibility goals.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Don't confuse FMC with FMS.** A cell with 1–3 machines is FMC. Four or more machines = FMS. Marks lost frequently in 1-mark definition questions.
> 2. **Don't list only 4–5 flexibilities.** KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists **8 flexibilities**. If you write 5, you lose 3 marks in a 7-mark question.
> 3. **AGV vs Conveyor vs Robot — know the distinction.** Conveyors = fixed path; AGV = free movement; Robot = within-cell tending only.
> 4. **"Layout" and "configuration" are NOT synonyms.** Layout = physical arrangement. Configuration = machine grouping logic. Examiners deduct for interchange.
> 5. **In CR-based numerical, always show the formula before plugging values.** Examiners reward 0.5 marks for the formula statement.
> 6. **Don't write "FMS reduces inventory" without quantifying.** Use Little's Law ($L = \lambda W$) to justify numerically.

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist — Pin This in Your Notebook**

- ✅ **Definition:** FMS = Computer-controlled integration of machines + MHS + control software producing a variety of parts in mid-batch sizes.
- ✅ **8 Flexibilities:** Machine, Product, Process, Routing, Volume, Expansion, Operation, Production (memorize in this order).
- ✅ **Hierarchy (Nested):** Machine ⊂ Process ⊂ Product ⊂ Production.
- ✅ **3 Subsystems (+ 1 optional):** Workstation, Material Handling, Computer Control, Tool Management.
- ✅ **FMC vs FMS:** 1–3 machines = FMC; ≥4 = FMS.
- ✅ **5-Level Control Hierarchy:** L4 ERP → L3 Shop Floor → L2 Cell → L1 CNC → L0 Sensors.
- ✅ **6-Phase Planning:** Part Family → Process Plan → Workstation Selection → MHS Selection → Layout → Control Design.
- ✅ **Key Dispatching Rule:** Critical Ratio $CR = (D-t)/T_{rem}$ — lowest CR wins.
- ✅ **Little's Law:** $L = \lambda \cdot W$ for WIP sizing.
- ✅ **Utilization Target:** $U = 0.75$–$0.85$ is the healthy range.
- ✅ **Layouts:** Linear, Loop, L-shape/U-shape, Open-field, Hierarchical.
- ✅ **AGV Navigation:** Wire, Inertial, Optical, Laser, Vision (in increasing precision).
- ✅ **Tool Magazine Capacity:** Standard sizes 24, 40, 80, 120.
- ✅ **Classification & Coding Systems:** Opitz, MICLASS, DCLASS (used in Phase 1).
- ✅ **KTU Definition to Memorize Verbatim:** *"An FMS is a manufacturing system in which a computer controls the flow of work between work-stations..."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types and Components of FMS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OERUT613 - COMPUTER INTEGRATED MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Types and Components of FMS # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS):** A highly automated group technology (GT) cell, consisting of a group of numerically controlled (NC) machine workstations connected together by an automated material handling system and controlled by a distributed computer system, capable of processing simultaneously a variety of part types within a part family at intermediate production volumes. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OERUT613)** syllabus, an FMS is a manufacturing system that combines the **high productivity of a transfer line** with the **flexibility of a job shop** to produce a variety of parts in low-to-medium batch sizes. > [!NOTE] > **Group Technology (GT)** is the grouping of similar parts into part families to reduce the variety of processing requirements and simplify production planning. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **hospital operation theater complex**: - The **operating rooms** = Workstations (CNC machines). - The **patient transport system** (corridors, lifts, automated trolleys) = Material Handling System (AGVs/conveyors). - The **central control desk with nurses and monitors** = Computer Control System. - The **sterile instrument trays and tool carts** = Tooling Management. - **Supporting services** (sterilization, cleaning, air-conditioning) = Cleaning, chip removal, and coolant systems. Just as the theater complex can perform **many different types of surgeries** (flexibility) at **high efficiency** (productivity), an FMS can manufacture **a variety of parts** with **minimal manual intervention** and **high throughput**. ## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics of FMS > [!IMPORTANT] > Standard FMS design metrics every KTU student must memorize: > - **Flexibility** = Ability to produce diverse part types. > - **Productivity** = Output per unit time (parts/hour). > - **System Utilization** = Effective working time / Total available time. > - **Lead Time** = Time from order release to part delivery. > - **Work-in-Process (WIP)** = Number of parts in the system at any time. > - **Machine Utilization** = (Cutting time + Setup time) / Available time. ## 1.4 Scope of FMS in KTU Module 4 This module covers two critical dimensions: 1. **Components of an FMS** — the building blocks (hardware + software). 2. **Types of FMS** — based on scale, flexibility, and layout. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Major Components of an FMS A complete FMS is composed of **five major sub-systems** working in coordination: ### 2.1.1 Workstations (Machines) The **primary processing elements** that perform the actual value-addition operations on parts. **Why needed:** They execute the operations (milling, turning, drilling, grinding, etc.) required to transform raw blanks into finished parts. **KTU Classification of Workstations:** | Type of Workstation | Primary Function | Typical KTU Use | |---|---|---| | **CNC Machining Centers** | Multi-axis milling, drilling, tapping | Most common FMS workstation | | **CNC Turning Centers** | Turning, boring, threading | Rotational part families | | **Inspection Stations** | In-process and post-process measurement using CMMs | Quality assurance | | **Robotic Workstations** | Welding, assembly, deburring, loading | Assembly-type FMS | | **Sheet Metal Processing** | Laser cutting, punching, bending | Sheet metal FMS | | **Additive Manufacturing** | 3D printing of metal/polymer parts | Hybrid FMS | > [!NOTE] > In a typical **KTU question**, workstations are often considered to be CNC machining centers with pallet changers, automatic tool changers (ATC), and automatic work changers (AWC) to maximize unattended operation. ### 2.1.2 Automated Material Handling & Storage System (AMHS) The **"circulatory system"** of the FMS — it transports parts, tooling, and raw stock between storage, workstations, and inspection points without manual intervention. **Key Sub-components:** 1. **Automated Storage & Retrieval System (AS/RS)** — for raw stock, WIP, and finished parts. 2. **Conveyors** — roller conveyors, belt conveyors, chain conveyors. 3. **Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs)** — battery-powered driverless carts following embedded wire, magnetic tape, or laser guidance. 4. **Robotic Loading/Unloading** — industrial robots handling parts at each workstation. 5. **Pallets & Fixtures** — standardized carriers holding the workpiece. > [!IMPORTANT] > A **pallet** in FMS is a standardized work-holding platform fitted with an **encoder/identification chip** that is read by every workstation. The pallet enables **random routing** of parts through the system. ### 2.1.3 Computer Control System (CCS) The **"brain and nervous system"** — coordinates every operation in real time. **Hierarchical Architecture (KTU 2024 Standard):** | Level | Computer | Function | |---|---|---| | **Level 1 — Shop Floor** | CNC Controller (e.g., FANUC, Siemens) | Direct machine control | | **Level 2 — Cell Controller** | Cell Computer (FMS host) | Coordinates workstations, AGVs, tools | | **Level 3 — Area Controller** | Plant Server | Routes orders, manages part programs | | **Level 4 — Host/ERP** | Mainframe/Cloud | Production planning, scheduling | **Software Modules of CCS:** - **Distributed Numerical Control (DNC)** — distributes NC part programs to multiple CNC machines. - **Manufacturing Execution System (MES)** — executes the production plan in real time. - **Tool Management Software** — tracks tool life, location, regrind schedules. - **Production Scheduling & Dispatching** — assigns parts to machines dynamically. ### 2.1.4 Tooling Management System Manages the **entire tool life cycle** within the FMS to reduce downtime. **Components:** - **Central Tool Magazines (CTM)** — bulk storage holding hundreds of tools. - **Tool Delivery System** — exchanges tools between CTM and machine tool magazines. - **Tool Pre-setter** — measures tool geometry offline, reducing setup time. - **Tool Identification** — RFID or barcode-based tool tracking. - **Automatic Tool Changer (ATC)** — robotic arm on the CNC machine. ### 2.1.5 Supporting Services The **"housekeeping"** components that ensure uninterrupted operation: - **Coolant Supply System** — central coolant tank and pumps. - **Chip Removal System** — augers, conveyors, or wash-down systems to remove metal chips. - **Cleaning Station** — washes parts before inspection or next operation. - **Environmental Control** — temperature, humidity, and lighting control. - **Safety Systems** — light curtains, interlocks, and emergency stops. ## 2.2 Types of FMS (KTU 2024 Classification) ### 2.2.1 Classification by Scale (Most Common in KTU Exams) | Type | Abbrev. | Number of Workstations | Typical Production Volume | KTU Weightage | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Flexible Manufacturing Cell** | FMC | **2 to 3** machines + robot | Low–Medium | High (3-marks) | | **Flexible Manufacturing System** | FMS | **4 or more** machines | Medium | Very High (14-marks) | | **Flexible Manufacturing Line** | FML | Multiple machines in **fixed sequence** | High | Medium | | **Flexible Transfer Line** | FTL | Linked transfer line with NC stations | High | Low | ### 2.2.2 Classification by Level of Flexibility > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU frequently asks: **"Differentiate between Sequential FMS and Random-Order FMS."** | Aspect | Sequential FMS | Random-Order FMS | |---|---|---| | **Part Mix** | Parts enter in a fixed sequence | Parts enter in any sequence | | **Routing** | Fixed path through machines | Variable path; dynamic routing | | **Control** | Simple | Complex computer control | | **Buffering** | Small or no buffers | Large central buffer (load/unload station) | | **Tooling** | Pre-set for one job type | Must accommodate all part types | | **Use Case** | Low-variety, high-volume families | High-variety, medium-volume families | ### 2.2.3 Other Common FMS Classifications 1. **Dedicated FMS** — designed to produce a single part family over its entire life. 2. **Engineered FMS** — designed for a specific initial part family but with provision for future expansion. 3. **Modular FMS** — built from standardized modules that can be reconfigured. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > All formulas below are **exam-ready** for KTU 2024 scheme derivations. | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $n = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{p} \left( \dfrac{Q_i}{T_i} \right)}{H \cdot U \cdot E}$ | Number of machines in FMS | Machines | | 2 | $T_c = \dfrac{L \cdot \eta}{v \cdot f}$ | Cutting time per operation | minutes | | 3 | $U = \dfrac{T_c + T_s + T_h}{T_a}$ | Machine utilization (decimal) | fraction | | 4 | $R = \lambda \cdot T_h$ | Number of parts in system (Little's Law) | parts | | 5 | $\eta_{sys} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \eta_i$ | System efficiency from station efficiencies | fraction | | 6 | $N_{tool} = \sum_{j=1}^{k} N_{j}$ | Total tool slots needed | slots | | 7 | $T_{lead} = T_{queue} + T_{process} + T_{transit} + T_{wait}$ | Total lead time | hours | **Notation used in KTU valuation key:** - $n$ = number of machines, $Q_i$ = annual demand of part $i$, $T_i$ = time per part $i$ (min), $H$ = annual hours, $U$ = utilization, $E$ = efficiency. - $L$ = length of cut, $\eta$ = number of passes, $v$ = feed, $f$ = feed rate. - $T_s$ = setup time, $T_h$ = handling time, $T_a$ = available time. - $\lambda$ = arrival rate, $R$ = WIP. - $N_j$ = tools required for operation $j$, $k$ = total operations. ### 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Formulas In the **automotive industry**, FMS formulas are used to determine: - How many machining cells are needed to produce **2 lakh cylinder blocks annually** (Formula 1). - Whether the **tool magazine capacity** is sufficient for a 5-operation part (Formula 6). - The **WIP limit** for a just-in-time pull system (Formula 4 — Little's Law). - **Bottleneck station identification** through system efficiency (Formula 5). --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Number of Workstations in an FMS > [!NOTE] > This derivation is a **KTU 2024 high-yield problem** — students lose marks by skipping the units. ### Problem Statement A part family consists of 3 part types. The annual demands and processing times per part are given below. The FMS operates for **2 shifts × 8 hours × 250 days = 4000 hours/year**. Assume **85% utilization** and **90% efficiency**. Find the number of machines required. | Part | Annual Demand $Q_i$ | Time/Part $T_i$ (min) | |---|---|---| | A | 5,000 | 12 | | B | 3,000 | 20 | | C | 2,000 | 18 | ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — State the formula.** $$n = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{p} \left( \dfrac{Q_i}{T_i} \right)}{H \cdot U \cdot E}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the workload contribution of each part type.** For part A: $$\dfrac{Q_A}{T_A} = \dfrac{5{,}000}{12} = 416.667 \;\text{parts/hour equivalent}$$ For part B: $$\dfrac{Q_B}{T_B} = \dfrac{3{,}000}{20} = 150.000 \;\text{parts/hour equivalent}$$ For part C: $$\dfrac{Q_C}{T_C} = \dfrac{2{,}000}{18} = 111.111 \;\text{parts/hour equivalent}$$ **Step 3 — Sum the workload contributions.** $$\sum_{i=1}^{3} \dfrac{Q_i}{T_i} = 416.667 + 150.000 + 111.111 = 677.778 \;\text{parts/hour}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the available productive hours per machine.** $$H \cdot U \cdot E = 4{,}000 \times 0.85 \times 0.90 = 3{,}060 \;\text{productive hours/year}$$ **Step 5 — Calculate the number of machines required.** $$n = \dfrac{677.778}{3{,}060} = 0.2215 \;\text{machines}$$ **Step 6 — Round up to the next whole number.** Since a fraction of a machine is not physical: $$n = 1 \;\text{machine (minimum requirement)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** In FMS design, the number is always **rounded UP** to the next whole integer, because a partial machine cannot be installed. Also note the **units must be consistent** — convert all times to hours. ### 3.2 Derivation 2: System Efficiency for a 4-Machine FMS > [!NOTE] > A typical 14-mark KTU question uses this derivation with **inspection station efficiency** added. ### Problem An FMS has 4 workstations. Their individual efficiencies are: 0.95, 0.90, 0.88, and 0.92. If the system works as a series, find the overall system efficiency. ### Solution **Step 1 — Identify the formula.** For series-arranged stations: $$\eta_{sys} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \eta_i$$ **Step 2 — Substitute each value.** $$\eta_{sys} = 0.95 \times 0.90 \times 0.88 \times 0.92$$ **Step 3 — Multiply progressively.** $$0.95 \times 0.90 = 0.8550$$ $$0.8550 \times 0.88 = 0.7524$$ $$0.7524 \times 0.92 = 0.6922$$ **Step 4 — Express the result.** $$\eta_{sys} = 0.6922 = 69.22\%$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical interpretation:** The system loses about **30.78%** of its theoretical capacity due to series-arranged station inefficiencies. This is why **parallel redundancy** and **buffer stations** are added in real FMS. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — FMS Component Classification | FMS Component | Hardware Item | Software Equivalent | Failure Consequence | Maintenance Priority | |---|---|---|---|---| | Workstations | CNC machine, robot | NC Part Program | Production stoppage | High | | AMHS | AGV, conveyor, AS/RS | Routing algorithm | Workpiece delay | High | | CCS | Cell computer, DNC | MES, scheduler | System chaos | **Critical** | | Tooling | CTM, ATC, pre-setter | Tool life DB | Tool breakage | Medium | | Support | Coolant, chip conveyor | Sensor monitors | Quality degradation | Low | ## 3.4 Design Trade-off Analysis > [!IMPORTANT] > A frequently asked 7-mark KTU sub-question: **"Discuss the trade-offs in FMS design."** | Design Choice | Higher Productivity | Higher Flexibility | |---|---|---| | **Number of Machines** | More machines | Fewer machines (lower cost) | | **Tool Magazine Size** | Larger magazine | Smaller (cheaper) | | **Buffer Size** | Larger buffers (no starvation) | Smaller (less WIP) | | **Routing Logic** | Fixed sequence | Random order | | **Control Architecture** | Centralized | Distributed | | **Layout Type** | In-line (Type I) | Cluster (Type III) | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 FMS Component Block Diagram (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TB node1["WORKSTATIONS
CNC Machines and Robots"] node2["MATERIAL HANDLING
AGV Conveyor and AS RS"] node3["COMPUTER CONTROL SYSTEM
Cell Controller and DNC and MES"] node4["TOOLING MANAGEMENT
CTM and ATC and Pre Setter"] node5["SUPPORTING SERVICES
Coolant and Chip and Cleaning"] node6["RAW MATERIALS IN"] node7["FINISHED PARTS OUT"] node6 --> node2 node2 --> node1 node1 --> node2 node2 --> node7 node3 -.controls.-> node1 node3 -.controls.-> node2 node3 -.controls.-> node4 node4 -.feeds.-> node1 node5 -.serves.-> node1 node5 -.serves.-> node2 node5 -.serves.-> node4 ``` ## 4.2 FMS Types Classification Tree ```mermaid flowchart TB A["FLEXIBLE MANUFACTURING SYSTEM"] A --> B1["BY SCALE"] A --> B2["BY FLEXIBILITY"] A --> B3["BY PURPOSE"] B1 --> C1["FMC 2 to 3 machines"] B1 --> C2["FMS 4 plus machines"] B1 --> C3["FML fixed sequence line"] B1 --> C4["FTL transfer line variant"] B2 --> D1["SEQUENTIAL FMS fixed routing"] B2 --> D2["RANDOM ORDER FMS variable routing"] B3 --> E1["DEDICATED FMS single family"] B3 --> E2["ENGINEERED FMS future expansion"] B3 --> E3["MODULAR FMS reconfigurable"] ``` ## 4.3 FMS Hierarchical Computer Control Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB L4["LEVEL 4 HOST COMPUTER ERP and Planning"] L3["LEVEL 3 AREA CONTROLLER Scheduling and Routing"] L2["LEVEL 2 CELL CONTROLLER FMS Coordinator"] L1["LEVEL 1 SHOP FLOOR CNC and Robot Controllers"] L0["LEVEL 0 ACTUATORS and SENSORS and AGV"] L4 --> L3 L3 --> L2 L2 --> L1 L1 --> L0 ``` ## 4.4 Functional Architecture — FMS Material Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A1["LOAD STATION Raw Blank In"] A2["AS RS Buffer Storage"] A3["MACHINE 1 Milling"] A4["MACHINE 2 Turning"] A5["MACHINE 3 Grinding"] A6["INSPECTION CMM"] A7["UNLOAD STATION Finished Part Out"] A1 --> A2 A2 --> A3 A3 --> A4 A4 --> A5 A5 --> A6 A6 --> A2 A6 --> A7 AGV["AGV NETWORK"] -.transports.-> A2 AGV -.transports.-> A3 AGV -.transports.-> A4 AGV -.transports.-> A5 AGV -.transports.-> A6 ``` ## 4.5 Sequential vs Random-Order FMS — Routing Logic ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph S1["SEQUENTIAL FMS Fixed Path"] direction LR s1["Machine 1"] --> s2["Machine 2"] --> s3["Machine 3"] --> s4["Inspection"] end subgraph S2["RANDOM ORDER FMS Dynamic Path"] direction TB r1["Load"] r2["Machine 1"] r3["Machine 2"] r4["Machine 3"] r5["Buffer Pool"] r6["Inspection"] r7["Unload"] r1 --> r5 r5 --> r2 r5 --> r3 r5 --> r4 r2 --> r5 r3 --> r5 r4 --> r5 r5 --> r6 r6 --> r7 end ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Q: Differentiate between a Flexible Manufacturing Cell (FMC) and a Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS).** **Model Answer:** | Feature | FMC | FMS | |---|---|---| | **Number of Machines** | 2 to 3 workstations | 4 or more workstations | | **Material Handling** | Robot or simple conveyor | AGVs, conveyors, AS/RS | | **Scale** | Small, localized | Large, integrated system | | **Control** | Single cell controller | Hierarchical multi-level control | | **Investment** | Lower | Higher | | **Use** | Single part family, low volume | Multiple part families, medium volume | ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Q: List any four major components of a Flexible Manufacturing System and state their function in one line each.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Workstations** — perform the actual machining and processing operations on parts. 2. **Automated Material Handling System (AMHS)** — transports parts and tools between stations without manual intervention. 3. **Computer Control System (CCS)** — coordinates, schedules, and monitors all activities of the FMS. 4. **Tooling Management System** — supplies the correct tools to machines and tracks tool usage and life. ## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) ### **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]** > **Q: (a)** Explain in detail the major components of a Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS) with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the computer control system with reference to the hierarchical architecture. **(7 marks)** > > **Q: (b)** Differentiate clearly between **Sequential FMS** and **Random-Order FMS**. Mention the typical production scenarios where each is preferred. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution #### Part (a) — Components of FMS (7 marks) The five major components of an FMS are: **1. Workstations (2 marks):** The work-performing stations of the FMS. They include CNC machining centers, turning centers, robotic cells, inspection stations, and assembly stations. Each workstation has **Automatic Tool Changer (ATC)**, **Automatic Pallet Changer (APC)**, and is interfaced with the cell controller. Workstations are the elements where actual value addition occurs. **2. Material Handling & Storage System (1.5 marks):** The connecting tissue of the FMS. It includes Automated Storage & Retrieval Systems (AS/RS), conveyors, Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs), and robotic loaders. The system is responsible for moving raw blanks, WIP, and finished parts between stations and storage with no manual help. **3. Computer Control System (1.5 marks):** The brain of the FMS. It operates in a **hierarchical architecture**: - **Level 0** — Sensors/actuators. - **Level 1** — CNC controllers. - **Level 2** — Cell controller (coordinates the FMS). - **Level 3** — Area controller (routing, scheduling). - **Level 4** — Host (planning, ERP). **4. Tooling Management System (1 mark):** Includes Central Tool Magazine (CTM), tool delivery system, tool pre-setter, and tool identification (RFID). Reduces tool-related downtime. **5. Supporting Services (1 mark):** Coolant supply, chip removal, cleaning stations, environmental control, and safety systems. #### Part (b) — Sequential vs Random-Order FMS (7 marks) | Parameter | Sequential FMS (3.5 marks) | Random-Order FMS (3.5 marks) | |---|---|---| | **Routing** | Fixed; parts follow a pre-determined path | Variable; computer decides dynamic path | | **Part Mix** | Limited variety; fixed order | Wide variety; mixed order | | **Buffering** | Minimal or no central buffer | Large central buffer at load/unload | | **Tooling** | Pre-set for the part family | Must hold tools for all variants | | **Control Software** | Simple schedule-driven | Complex real-time dispatcher | | **Throughput** | High (if sequence is steady) | Slightly lower due to routing decisions | | **Investment** | Lower | Higher | | **Application** | Dedicated part family with stable demand | Job-shop-like environment with variable demand | **Sequential FMS — preferred scenario:** A factory producing 5 variants of a gearbox housing in a fixed weekly mix, e.g., automotive OEM tier-1 supplier. **Random-Order FMS — preferred scenario:** A machine shop receiving daily orders for 20+ different bracket types, e.g., aerospace job shop. ## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice ### **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]** > **Q: (a)** With the help of neat sketches, describe the **four types of FMS layouts** classified by machine arrangement. Compare their material flow efficiency. **(7 marks)** > > **Q: (b)** An FMS has to produce 3 part types. The data is as follows: > > | Part | Annual Demand | Time per part (min) | > |---|---|---| > | P1 | 8000 | 15 | > | P2 | 4000 | 25 | > | P3 | 3000 | 20 | > > The FMS operates **2400 hours/year**, with **80% utilization** and **90% efficiency**. Calculate the number of machines required. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution #### Part (a) — Four FMS Layout Types (7 marks) **Layout Type I — In-line with Pallet Carrier Loop:** Machines arranged in a straight line; a closed-loop pallet conveyor passes each machine once. Best for long parts and high-volume production. **Layout Type II — In-line with Overhead Conveyor:** Machines in a line; an overhead gantry or trolley carries parts above. Useful when floor space is limited. **Layout Type III — Cluster (Cell) Layout:** Machines arranged in a closed rectangular loop connected by conveyors/AGVs. Most flexible, supports random routing. **Layout Type IV — Rectangular (Open) Layout:** Machines arranged in a rectangular grid pattern with AGVs free to move between any pair. Most flexible but requires sophisticated guidance. **Material flow efficiency comparison:** | Layout | Flow Efficiency | Flexibility | Best For | |---|---|---|---| | I — Loop In-line | High | Low | Long parts, fixed sequence | | II — Overhead | Medium | Low | Small floor area | | III — Cluster | Medium-High | High | Random-order FMS | | IV — Rectangular | Medium | Very High | Large job shops | #### Part (b) — Number of Machines Calculation (7 marks) **Step 1 — State formula:** $$n = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{p} \left( \dfrac{Q_i}{T_i} \right)}{H \cdot U \cdot E}$$ **Step 2 — Compute each part's workload:** $$\dfrac{Q_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{8{,}000}{15} = 533.33$$ $$\dfrac{Q_2}{T_2} = \dfrac{4{,}000}{25} = 160.00$$ $$\dfrac{Q_3}{T_3} = \dfrac{3{,}000}{20} = 150.00$$ **Step 3 — Sum the workloads:** $$\sum = 533.33 + 160.00 + 150.00 = 843.33$$ **Step 4 — Effective available time per machine:** $$H \cdot U \cdot E = 2{,}400 \times 0.80 \times 0.90 = 1{,}728 \;\text{hours/year}$$ **Step 5 — Final calculation:** $$n = \dfrac{843.33}{1{,}728} = 0.488 \;\text{machines}$$ **Step 6 — Round up:** $$\boxed{n = 1 \;\text{machine}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Always convert units** — minutes to hours before division. Mixing units is the #1 reason for losing marks in FMS numerical problems. > 2. **Always round UP**, never round down or to the nearest. Partial machines are not installable. > 3. **Show the formula first** before substitution — the KTU valuation key allots 1 mark for the correct formula statement alone. > 4. **Do not forget** the utilization and efficiency factors — leaving them out loses 2 marks. > 5. In layout-type questions, **labelling the diagram** earns 2 marks even if the description is incomplete. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4, Types and Components of FMS** - **FMS = CNC machines + AMHS + Computer Control + Tooling + Support services** — remember all five. - **FMC = 2–3 machines**; **FMS = 4+ machines**; **FML = dedicated sequence line**; **FTL = transfer-line variant**. - **Sequential FMS = fixed routing**, low flexibility, low cost. **Random-order FMS = dynamic routing**, high flexibility, high cost. - **Hierarchical control** has 5 levels: Level 0 (sensors) → Level 1 (CNC) → Level 2 (cell) → Level 3 (area) → Level 4 (host). - **Pallets have identification chips** for random routing — they are not just carriers. - **Number of machines formula** uses sum of (Demand / Time per part) divided by (Hours × Utilization × Efficiency) — always round UP. - **System efficiency = product of individual efficiencies** in series configuration. - **AGVs follow** embedded wire, magnetic tape, or laser guidance — three types KTU expects. - **Tool management includes** Central Tool Magazine (CTM), Automatic Tool Changer (ATC), and tool pre-setter. - **AS/RS = Automated Storage & Retrieval System** — used for raw stock, WIP, and finished goods. - **DNC (Distributed Numerical Control)** is the software for distributing NC part programs to multiple machines. - **Four FMS layout types** — In-line loop (I), Overhead (II), Cluster (III), Rectangular (IV). - **Group Technology (GT)** is the foundation of FMS — parts grouped into families based on similar processing. - **Flexibility types** in FMS include **Machine flexibility, Process flexibility, Product flexibility, Routing flexibility, Volume flexibility, Expansion flexibility**. - **Work-in-Process (WIP)** is governed by **Little's Law:** $R = \lambda \cdot T_h$. ---

topics.title: Applications and Benefits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT613 - COMPUTER INTEGRATED MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applications and Benefits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Applications and Benefits of CIM Enablers (Group Technology, Cellular Manufacturing & FMS):**
> In the context of Computer Integrated Manufacturing, *Applications* refer to the specific industrial, manufacturing, and engineering scenarios in which CIM enablers — namely **Group Technology (GT)**, **Classification & Coding Systems**, **Production Flow Analysis (PFA)**, **Cellular Manufacturing (CM)**, and **Flexible Manufacturing Systems (FMS)** — are deployed to solve part-family grouping, routing standardization, process planning, and shop-floor automation problems. *Benefits* refer to the quantified (and qualitative) gains in productivity, lead-time reduction, inventory turnover, work-in-process (WIP) reduction, tooling cost savings, quality improvement, and managerial decision support achieved through the structured implementation of these enablers.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Library" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: A Library vs. A Bookstore**
> Imagine two ways of organizing a million books:
> - **Traditional Functional Layout (Process Layout)** = A massive bookstore where mystery novels are in Aisle 1, biographies in Aisle 2, science in Aisle 3. To read a *single author's* works you must walk across the entire store. → **High travel, long lead time, lost books.**
> - **Group Technology / Cellular Layout** = A *smart library* that groups all books by the *same author* or *same subject family* into a single shelf (a **cell**). To find everything by Author "X," you walk to **one** cell. → **Low travel, short lead time, easy retrieval.**
>
> CIM does the same thing in a factory: it groups similar parts into **part families** and similar machines into **cells**, dramatically cutting movement, waiting, and information loss. The *Applications* describe **where** this is done (aerospace, automotive, electronics, job shops), and the *Benefits* describe **how much** is saved (typically **20–60% reduction in lead time** and **30–50% reduction in WIP** in published case studies).

### 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics Highlighted

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Benchmark Constants (Published Industrial Averages):**
> - Lead-time reduction: **30%–60%**
> - Work-In-Process (WIP) reduction: **40%–80%**
> - Setup time reduction: **50%–75%**
> - Throughput increase: **20%–50%**
> - Inventory carrying cost reduction: **20%–40%**
> - Labour productivity gain: **15%–30%**

### 1.4 KTU 2024 Course Outcome Mapping

| CO ID | Course Outcome | Bloom's Level |
|:-----:|:---------------|:-------------:|
| **CO4** | Apply Group Technology, classification & coding, and cellular manufacturing concepts to design part-family grouping and cell formation solutions. | Apply (L3) |
| **CO5** | Evaluate the applications, benefits, and limitations of CIM enablers in real industrial contexts. | Evaluate (L5) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Applications (Where are CIM Enablers Deployed?)

CIM enablers from Module 4 are not isolated academic exercises — they are deployed in well-defined industrial scenarios. The applications can be classified along **three orthogonal axes**:

### Axis A — By Manufacturing Volume (Production Type)

| Production Type | Volume Range | GT/CM/FMS Applicability | KTU-Standard Example |
|:---------------|:------------:|:-----------------------|:---------------------|
| **Job Shop** | 1–100 units | ★★★★★ (Highest) | Tool-room, defence spares |
| **Batch Production** | 100–10,000 units | ★★★★★ (Core domain) | Pumps, valves, fasteners |
| **Mass Production** | >10,000 units | ★★★ (Moderate) | Automotive engine blocks |
| **Continuous Process** | 24×7 flow | ★ (Low — FMS only) | Petrochemical, steel rolling |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** *Batch and Job-Shop production account for approximately **75%–80%** of all manufactured items in a typical industrialized nation.* This is precisely why GT and CM were invented — to bring the efficiency of mass production *into* the batch environment.

### Axis B — By Industry Sector

- **Automotive:** FMS for engine block machining, GT for axle/shaft part families.
- **Aerospace:** GT coding (e.g., **Opitz**, **DCLASS**) for managing 1M+ unique part numbers with high similarity.
- **Electronics:** SMT line cellular layout; FMS for PCB drilling.
- **Medical Devices:** Cellular manufacturing for orthopaedic implant families.
- **Heavy Engineering:** GT grouping of large castings and forgings.
- **Defense:** FMS for low-volume, high-variety spares with guaranteed traceability.

### Axis C — By Functional Use Inside CIM

| Functional Use | Enabling Technology | Application |
|:---------------|:-------------------|:------------|
| Part-family identification | Classification & Coding (Opitz, DCLASS, MICLASS) | Retrieval, standardization, CAPP |
| Cell formation | Rank-Order Clustering (ROC), ROC2, K-means | Shop-floor layout redesign |
| Routing standardization | Production Flow Analysis (PFA) | Bottleneck identification |
| Process planning | Variant CAPP, Generative CAPP | NC program reuse |
| Real-time control | FMS DNC/CNC network | Tool management, AGV routing |

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Benefits (What is Gained?)

### Category 1 — Operational Benefits (Quantifiable on the shop floor)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **These are the figures KTU examiners expect you to quote verbatim in 14-mark answers.**

1. **Reduced Material Handling** — Parts travel shorter distances inside cells.
2. **Reduced Setup Time** — Similar parts share fixtures and tooling.
3. **Reduced Work-In-Process (WIP) Inventory** — Queue lengths shrink.
4. **Shorter Manufacturing Lead Time (MLT)** — From days to hours.
5. **Improved Machine Utilization** — From ~30% to ~70–80%.
6. **Reduced Floor Space** — Typically **30%–50%** less.
7. **Lower Tooling Cost** — Standard tools across the family.

### Category 2 — Managerial / Strategic Benefits

8. **Simplified Production Planning & Control** — Fewer part numbers to track.
9. **Improved Cost Estimation & Quotation** — Standard routings → standard costs.
10. **Better Design for Manufacture (DFM)** — Designers reuse existing part families.
11. **Easier Workforce Training** — Operators specialize in one cell, not many departments.
12. **Enhanced Quality Control** — Dedicated inspection within the cell; closed-loop SPC.

### Category 3 — Information-System Benefits (CIM-Specific)

13. **Direct CAD/CAPP/CAM Linkage** — Coding system bridges design and process planning.
14. **MRP-II / ERP Integration** — GT codes become the *common key* across all modules.
15. **Computer-Aided Process Planning Reuse** — Variant CAPP uses GT codes to retrieve existing plans.
16. **Improved Database Search & Retrieval** — Engineers find similar past designs in seconds.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Math Rule:** The table uses `\vert` instead of the literal pipe symbol `|` to prevent markdown-table corruption. In your answer scripts, use plain `|x|`.

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Units | Typical Industrial Value |
|:-:|:-----------------|:-----------|:------|:------------------------|
| 1 | **Manufacturing Lead Time (MLT)** | $MLT = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Q_i + W_i + T_i + M_i)$ where $Q$=queue, $W$=wait, $T$=transport, $M$=machining | hours / days | 60%–80% reduction post-CM |
| 2 | **Group Technology Efficiency (η)** | $\eta_{GT} = \dfrac{N_{clustered}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | % | 70%–90% in well-formed cells |
| 3 | **Cell Formation Index (CFI)** | $CFI = \dfrac{a}{a+b}$ where $a$=intra-cell moves, $b$=inter-cell moves | dimensionless | 0.7–1.0 (ideal = 1) |
| 4 | **Work-In-Process (Little's Law)** | $WIP = TH \times CT$ where $TH$=throughput, $CT$=cycle time | units | 40%–80% reduction |
| 5 | **Machine Utilization (U)** | $U = \dfrac{T_{productive}}{T_{available}} \times 100\%$ | % | rises from 30% → 70% |
| 6 | **Reduction in Setup Time** | $\Delta S = S_{before} - S_{after}$ | minutes | 50%–75% |
| 7 | **Cost Savings per Part Family** | $\Delta C = (C_{process-layout} - C_{cell-layout}) \times V$ | ₹ / year | depends on volume $V$ |
| 8 | **PFA Clustering Coefficient** | $\phi = \dfrac{\text{Intra-cluster similarity}}{\text{Inter-cluster similarity}}$ | dimensionless | > 1 indicates good grouping |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DFM (Design for Manufacture):** GT codes act as a *lingua franca* between design and production.
- **Agile Manufacturing:** A part-family database is the foundation for rapid new-part introduction.
- **Mass Customization:** FMS + GT enables *batch-of-one* production at near-mass-production cost.
- **Digital Twin Synchronization:** GT codes link the physical factory to its digital twin in Industry 4.0.
- **Sustainability:** Reduced transport, energy, and waste contribute to *green manufacturing* KPIs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Manufacturing Lead Time (MLT) Reduction in a Cell

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this derivation matters:** *Lead-time reduction* is the **single most quoted benefit** in KTU answer scripts. A 14-mark question often begins with a process layout and asks the student to compute MLT before/after cell conversion. Below is the exhaustive mathematical treatment.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Generic definition of MLT in a process layout**

In a process (functional) layout, a part visits $n$ different departments. The lead time is the sum of all waiting, queue, transport, and processing times.

$$MLT_{process} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Q_i + W_i + T_i + M_i)$$

where each term is explained below.

$$Q_i = \text{queue time before operation } i \quad\quad W_i = \text{waiting/idle time at } i$$

$$T_i = \text{transport time from } i \text{ to } i{+}1 \quad\quad M_i = \text{machining time at } i$$

**Step 2 — Recognize the dominant term**

In a process layout with $n=5$ operations on a typical batch of 50 parts:

$$\sum Q_i \gg \sum M_i$$

Empirically, $Q_i + W_i \approx 80\text{–}95\%$ of $MLT_{process}$, while $M_i \approx 5\text{–}20\%$.

**Step 3 — Effect of cellular conversion**

When parts are regrouped into a cell, the queue and waiting terms collapse because:
- All machines in a cell are *colocated*.
- Similar parts share tooling (no setup change between consecutive parts).
- A single operator (or small team) handles the entire route.

Thus:

$$MLT_{cell} \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} M_i + \epsilon_i$$

where $\epsilon_i$ is a small residual transport within the cell.

**Step 4 — Compute the percentage reduction**

$$\% \Delta MLT = \dfrac{MLT_{process} - MLT_{cell}}{MLT_{process}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example (KTU Board Standard)**

Given: $n=5$, per operation: $Q_i = 4\,\text{hr}$, $W_i = 2\,\text{hr}$, $T_i = 0.5\,\text{hr}$, $M_i = 0.5\,\text{hr}$.

$$MLT_{process} = 5 \times (4 + 2 + 0.5 + 0.5) = 5 \times 7 = 35\,\text{hours}$$

$$MLT_{cell} \approx 5 \times (0.5 + 0.1) = 5 \times 0.6 = 3\,\text{hours}$$

$$\% \Delta MLT = \dfrac{35 - 3}{35} \times 100\% \approx 91.4\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A reduction of 60%–90% is realistic and is the benchmark figure used in textbook case studies (e.g., the classic *John Deere* and *Yamaha* CIM case studies).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Work-In-Process Reduction via Little's Law

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Little's Law statement**

$$L = \lambda W$$

In manufacturing notation: $WIP = TH \times CT$, where $TH$ = throughput (parts/day), $CT$ = cycle time (days).

**Step 2 — For a process layout**

$$WIP_{process} = TH \times CT_{process}$$

**Step 3 — For a cellular layout**

$$WIP_{cell} = TH \times CT_{cell}$$

Since $CT$ is proportional to $MLT$ (cycle time = lead time per part in steady state):

$$CT_{cell} = k \cdot MLT_{cell}, \quad CT_{process} = k \cdot MLT_{process}$$

where $k$ is a constant of proportionality. Throughput $TH$ is assumed constant (same demand).

**Step 4 — WIP ratio**

$$\dfrac{WIP_{cell}}{WIP_{process}} = \dfrac{CT_{cell}}{CT_{process}} = \dfrac{MLT_{cell}}{MLT_{process}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Plug-in**

Using the previous example: $MLT_{process}=35\,\text{hr}$, $MLT_{cell}=3\,\text{hr}$, throughput $TH = 10$ parts/day.

$$WIP_{process} = 10 \times \dfrac{35}{24} \approx 14.6 \text{ parts}$$

$$WIP_{cell} = 10 \times \dfrac{3}{24} \approx 1.25 \text{ parts}$$

$$\% \Delta WIP = \dfrac{14.6 - 1.25}{14.6} \times 100\% \approx 91.4\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorist's Note:** Little's Law is exact under steady-state conditions. In transient production (very common in job shops), the *instantaneous* WIP can be even higher, making the *peak* WIP reduction even more dramatic.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cell Formation Index (CFI)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define intra-cell and inter-cell moves**

For a part $p$ with operation sequence $\{O_1, O_2, \dots, O_n\}$ mapped to cells $C(O_i)$:

$$\text{Intra-cell moves for } p = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \mathbb{1}\{C(O_i) = C(O_{i+1})\}$$

$$\text{Inter-cell moves for } p = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \mathbb{1}\{C(O_i) \ne C(O_{i+1})\}$$

**Step 2 — Sum over all parts $P$**

$$a = \sum_{p \in P} \text{Intra-cell moves for } p$$

$$b = \sum_{p \in P} \text{Inter-cell moves for } p$$

**Step 3 — Define CFI**

$$CFI = \dfrac{a}{a + b}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**

- $CFI = 1$ → **Perfect cell** (all moves intra-cell).
- $CFI = 0$ → **Worst case** (all moves inter-cell, i.e., a process layout in disguise).
- $CFI \ge 0.7$ → Industry-accepted threshold for a "good" cell.

**Step 5 — Numerical Example**

Suppose 10 parts, total moves = 100, of which $a = 80$ are intra-cell.

$$CFI = \dfrac{80}{80 + 20} = 0.80 \;\; \checkmark \; \text{Good cell}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Process Layout vs. Cellular Layout vs. FMS

| Performance Parameter | Process Layout (Functional) | Cellular Layout (GT-based) | Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS) |
|:----------------------|:---------------------------:|:--------------------------:|:----------------------------------:|
| **Lead Time** | High (baseline) | ↓ 60%–90% | ↓ 70%–95% |
| **WIP Inventory** | High | ↓ 40%–80% | ↓ 50%–90% |
| **Setup Time** | Long | ↓ 50%–75% | ↓ 60%–85% |
| **Machine Utilization** | 25%–35% | 60%–75% | 75%–85% |
| **Labour Productivity** | Baseline | ↑ 15%–30% | ↑ 25%–50% |
| **Floor Space** | Baseline (100%) | ↓ 30%–50% | ↓ 40%–60% |
| **Flexibility (mix)** | Very High | Medium-High | Very High |
| **Volume Range** | Low–Medium | Medium–High | Medium–High, multi-product |
| **Capital Cost** | Low | Medium | Very High |
| **Suitability for CIM** | Poor | Good | Excellent |
| **Information System Need** | Low | Medium (DB for codes) | High (real-time DNC/MES) |
| **Typical Industries** | Job shops, tool rooms | Pumps, valves, fasteners | Aerospace, automotive, defence |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked *“Compare process layout and cellular layout”*, present a **table** like the one above. It scores 3–4 marks on its own and is faster to write than prose.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each module contributes 2–3 Part-A questions of 3 marks each. Answers should be 4–6 sentences, with one or two numerical values where relevant.

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`

**Q: List any six applications of Group Technology and Cellular Manufacturing in modern industries.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Part-family identification** for design reuse in CAD databases.
2. **Process planning automation** via Variant CAPP driven by GT codes.
3. **Cell formation** to reorganize shop floor from process layout to cellular layout.
4. **Cost estimation and quotation** using standard routings.
5. **Tooling standardization** across part families reducing setup time.
6. **MRP/ERP integration** where GT codes serve as the *common key* linking design, planning, and shop-floor data.
7. **FMS configuration** where machines and tools are selected based on part-family analysis.

*(3 marks: 1 mark per 2 valid applications)*

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sample Paper]`

**Q: State the typical percentage reduction in (a) manufacturing lead time and (b) work-in-process inventory achieved by converting a process layout into a cellular layout.**

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Manufacturing lead time** is typically reduced by **60%–90%** after cellular conversion, because queue and waiting times (which dominate process-layout lead time) collapse inside a cell.

(b) **Work-in-process (WIP) inventory** is typically reduced by **40%–80%**, derivable from Little's Law ($WIP = TH \times CT$); since $CT$ falls in proportion to lead time, $WIP$ falls likewise.

*(3 marks: 1.5 marks per part with one quantitative figure each.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Rule (Module Internal Choice):** *“Each module shall have an internal choice in the ESE Part B. The student shall answer either Option A or Option B.”* Both options below are tuned to the **same cognitive level and mark distribution** for fair examiner moderation.

---

### Question A — Option 1 `[14 Marks, Module-4 Internal Choice, KTU 2024 Pattern]`

**Q: (a) Explain the major applications of Group Technology in a CIM environment. Discuss with examples how GT acts as a bridge between CAD, CAPP, and CAM. (7 marks)**
**(b) A batch production unit has 5 operations. In a process layout, the average queue time is 4 hr, waiting time 2 hr, transport time 0.5 hr, and machining time 0.5 hr per operation. After converting to a cellular layout, the queue and waiting time are eliminated, and transport is reduced to 0.1 hr per operation. Calculate the percentage reduction in manufacturing lead time and the consequent reduction in WIP (assume throughput = 10 parts/day). (7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Answer

**Applications of GT in CIM (4 marks):**

1. **Part-Family Identification** — Opitz/DCLASS codes cluster similar parts.
2. **Standard Process Routing** — Identical codes ⇒ identical routes.
3. **Variant CAPP** — GT code triggers retrieval of standard plan.
4. **DFM/Design Reuse** — Designers search by code to find similar past designs.
5. **Cost Estimation** — Standard cost = standard time × standard rate.
6. **FMS Configuration** — Tool magazine size, AGV routes derived from family analysis.

**GT as the CAD–CAPP–CAM Bridge (3 marks):**

- **CAD → CAPP:** The GT code generated during design is *automatically* read by the CAPP module to retrieve a standard process plan.
- **CAPP → CAM:** The plan is downloaded to CAM, which generates NC code with the correct tools, fixtures, and feeds for the part family.
- **CIM-Level Integration:** The same code propagates into MRP-II, ERP, CAQC, and shop-floor control, ensuring data consistency from design to dispatch.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Step 1 — Lead time in process layout**

$$MLT_{process} = 5 \times (4 + 2 + 0.5 + 0.5) = 5 \times 7 = 35\,\text{hr}$$

**Step 2 — Lead time in cellular layout**

Queue and waiting → **0**. Transport reduced to 0.1 hr. Machining unchanged at 0.5 hr.

$$MLT_{cell} = 5 \times (0.5 + 0.1) = 5 \times 0.6 = 3\,\text{hr}$$

**Step 3 — Percentage reduction**

$$\% \Delta MLT = \dfrac{35 - 3}{35} \times 100\% \approx 91.43\%$$

**Step 4 — WIP via Little's Law**

$$WIP_{process} = 10 \times \dfrac{35}{24} = 14.58 \text{ parts}$$

$$WIP_{cell} = 10 \times \dfrac{3}{24} = 1.25 \text{ parts}$$

$$\% \Delta WIP = \dfrac{14.58 - 1.25}{14.58} \times 100\% \approx 91.43\%$$

**Final Answer:** Lead time and WIP both reduce by **≈ 91.4%**. (7 marks: 2 marks formula, 3 marks numerical computation, 2 marks final conclusion with units.)

---

### Question B — Option 2 `[14 Marks, Module-4 Internal Choice, KTU 2024 Pattern]`

**Q: (a) Discuss the qualitative and quantitative benefits of cellular manufacturing and FMS in modern industries. Quote typical industrial values. (7 marks)**
**(b) A company has identified 10 part families using GT. The total number of inter-cell moves is 25 and the total number of intra-cell moves is 75. Calculate the Cell Formation Index (CFI) and comment on the quality of the cell formation. If the company is considering FMS conversion, list the four key benefits that would justify the additional capital investment. (7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Answer

**Quantitative Benefits (4 marks):**

| Benefit | Typical Value |
|:--------|:--------------|
| Lead time reduction | 60%–90% |
| WIP reduction | 40%–80% |
| Setup time reduction | 50%–75% |
| Machine utilization | rises from 30% → 70% |
| Floor-space reduction | 30%–50% |
| Labour productivity | +15%–30% |
| Throughput | +20%–50% |

**Qualitative Benefits (3 marks):**

1. **Simpler production planning** — fewer part numbers per cell.
2. **Better operator morale** — single-team ownership of a complete part family.
3. **Improved quality** — closed-loop inspection within the cell.
4. **Easier workforce training** — operators learn one cell, not many departments.
5. **Better information flow** — GT codes link CAD, CAPP, MRP, ERP.
6. **Sustainability** — reduced energy, transport, and waste.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Step 1 — Compute CFI**

$$CFI = \dfrac{a}{a + b} = \dfrac{75}{75 + 25} = 0.75$$

**Step 2 — Comment on quality**

Since $CFI = 0.75 \ge 0.70$, the cell formation is **GOOD** by industry standards. However, 25 inter-cell moves per planning horizon indicate **residual inter-cell dependency** that should be minimized via *re-clustering* or *bottleneck machines shared between cells.*

**Step 3 — Four Key FMS Benefits Justifying Investment (4 × 1 = 4 marks):**

1. **Higher machine utilization** (75%–85% vs. 30% in job shop).
2. **24×7 unmanned production** (lights-out manufacturing).
3. **Mass customization capability** — batch-of-one at mass-production cost.
4. **Reduced work-in-process and lead time** — typical reduction 70%–95%.
5. *(Bonus)* Better tool management and integrated quality control.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** A 7-mark problem that ends with a bare number like *"91.4"* (no `%` sign) loses **0.5 mark**. Always write `"91.4%"` or `"≈ 91.4 percent"`.
> 2. **Skipping the formula:** Even if the numerical answer is correct, omitting the formula statement (e.g., $MLT = \sum(Q+W+T+M)$) costs **1 mark**. KTU valuation strictly follows a *step-marking* pattern: 1 mark for formula, 2–3 marks for substitution, 1 mark for final answer.
> 3. **Confusing WIP with finished-goods inventory:** They are *different*. WIP is in-process; finished goods are stored at the end. Most students write *"inventory"* generically and lose **1 mark** on clarity.
> 4. **Quoting benefits without numbers:** A 14-mark question on applications and benefits **must** include at least 4–5 numerical benchmark values (e.g., 60%–90% lead time). A purely descriptive answer is capped at ~9 marks.
> 5. **Skipping the GT-CAD-CAPP-CAM linkage diagram:** In a 7-mark sub-question, a small block diagram earns the *extra* illustration mark. Draw it even if rough.
> 6. **Spelling "Opitz" as "Opitz**e**" or "Optiz":** This is the most common spelling error and examiners *do* notice.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section. Read it the night before the exam. It contains the *high-frequency* points KTU examiners love to test.**

- ✅ **Definition:** Applications = *where* CIM enablers are used; Benefits = *what is gained* (qualitative + quantitative).
- ✅ **Top 5 industries for GT/CM/FMS:** Automotive, Aerospace, Electronics, Medical Devices, Defence.
- ✅ **Production type where GT shines:** **Batch production (100–10,000 units).**
- ✅ **CIM hierarchy of integration:** CAD → CAPP → CAM → CAQC → FMS, with **GT codes as the binding layer**.
- ✅ **Lead time reduction in a cell:** typically **60%–90%**.
- ✅ **WIP reduction in a cell:** typically **40%–80%** (Little's Law).
- ✅ **Setup time reduction in a cell:** typically **50%–75%**.
- ✅ **Machine utilization rise:** from **~30% (process layout)** to **~70% (cell)**, up to **~85% (FMS)**.
- ✅ **Floor-space reduction:** **30%–50%** in a cell, **40%–60%** in FMS.
- ✅ **Cell Formation Index (CFI):** $CFI = a / (a + b)$; threshold for a "good" cell is **CFI ≥ 0.70**.
- ✅ **Little's Law:** $WIP = TH \times CT$.
- ✅ **MLT formula:** $MLT = \sum (Q_i + W_i + T_i + M_i)$.
- ✅ **PFA → Cell formation → Cellular layout** is the standard GT pipeline.
- ✅ **Most-quoted GT code systems:** **Opitz**, **DCLASS (Doolittle)**, **MICLASS** — remember all three.
- ✅ **FMS four key benefits:** high utilization, lights-out production, mass customization, lead-time/WIP reduction.
- ✅ **Green-manufacturing angle:** reduced transport + reduced energy = sustainability benefit (often a 1-mark differentiator in 2024 papers).
- ✅ **Examiner's favourite trick question:** *"Does GT apply to mass production?"* — Answer: *limited applicability, but useful for **part standardization** and **DFM** even in mass production.*
- ✅ **KTU 2024 specific:** Internal choice in Part B is *module-internal*, not question-internal — read the question paper carefully.

> **END OF NOTES — MODULE 4: APPLICATIONS AND BENEFITS**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme | Course: OERUT613 Computer Integrated Manufacturing*
