topics.title: Planning and Control of FMS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT613 - COMPUTER INTEGRATED MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Planning and Control of FMS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS) Planning and Control** refers to the integrated set of information-based decision-making procedures used to manage, regulate, and optimize the flow of materials, tools, pallets, and information through an automated cellular manufacturing environment, in order to meet pre-defined production targets under varying part-mix and volume conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Working Definition:** Planning and Control in FMS is the systematic process that bridges *Manufacturing Engineering Planning* (off-line, strategic) with *Shop Floor Execution* (on-line, real-time), enabling a computer-integrated production cell to produce a family of parts within the available machine, tool, and AGV/robot capacity.

From a control-engineering perspective, FMS planning and control operates at **three hierarchical decision levels**:

| Level | Time Horizon | Decision Type | Functional Owner |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Long Term** | Months – 1 Year | System Design, Part Family Selection | Corporate / Industrial Engineering |
| **Medium Term** | Weeks – Month | Loading, Detailed Scheduling, Tool Plans | Production Planning Dept. |
| **Short Term** | Hours – Day | Dispatching, Real-time Monitoring, Exception Handling | Cell Computer / FMS Controller |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Smart Airport Control Tower"

Imagine a busy international airport that has to land **15 different aircraft types** (A320, B777, A380, regional jets) at only **4 runways**, with limited **fuel trucks**, **boarding bridges**, and **ground crew**. The airport does NOT use the same runway for every plane; instead, a **Central Control Tower** does the following:

1. **Long Term (Yearly):** Decides which airline routes (part families) will be served, and what new runways to build.
2. **Medium Term (Daily):** Assigns each flight to a specific runway, time-slot, and gate; plans the crew and fuel truck usage.
3. **Short Term (Real-time):** The moment a plane is delayed, the tower re-routes it to a free runway and dynamically reassigns ground equipment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mapping the Analogy to FMS:**
> - **Aircraft = Parts** (different part types in the FMS part family)
> - **Runways = CNC Machines** (flexible workstations, each with its own tooling capacity)
> - **Fuel Trucks = Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs)**
> - **Boarding Bridges = Tool Magazines / Pallets**
> - **Control Tower = Central Cell Computer running FMS Planning & Control Software**

Just as the tower prevents runway collisions and idle time, the **FMS control system** prevents machine starvation (idle), machine overloading (bottleneck), and tool conflicts (no required tool in the magazine).

## 1.3 Key Quantitative Metrics (KTU Board Favourites)

The following standard metrics are **universally tested** in KTU 2024 scheme examinations on this module:

- **Production Ratio ($R_i$)** – the fraction of total production volume contributed by part $i$.
- **Machine Loading Factor ($L_k$)** – fraction of available time machine $k$ is engaged.
- **Weighted Scoring Index for Part Type Selection** – used during FMS design.
- **Tool Life Ratio ($T_{rem}$)** – remaining useful life of a tool in the magazine.
- **Makespan ($C_{max}$)** – total time to complete the production batch.
- **Tardiness / Lateness** – deviation from the due date.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – OERUT613 Module 4):** Questions are frequently asked on *Part Type Selection Criteria*, *Production Ratio analysis*, *Machine Loading* and the *Computer Control Hierarchy of FMS*. Memorize the hierarchy diagram — it appears almost every exam cycle.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of FMS Planning and Control

The discipline is built on **four interlocking pillars**. Each pillar addresses a specific question that the FMS controller must answer continuously.

### Pillar 1 – Part Type Selection & Production Ratio Analysis
**Question answered:** *"Which parts should be made in the FMS, and in what proportion?"*

- **Part Type Selection** is an *off-line (long-term)* decision made during FMS design or re-configuration.
- A set of candidate parts is evaluated against multiple criteria using a **weighted scoring method**.
- **Production Ratio** $R_i$ defines the relative demand of part $i$ in a planning period (usually a week or month).

### Pillar 2 – Machine Loading
**Question answered:** *"Which operations of which part should be assigned to which machine?"*

- Given the routing of each part (sequence of operations) and the production ratios, the planner determines how much work each CNC machine will receive.
- Goal: balance the load so that no machine becomes a bottleneck.

### Pillar 3 – Scheduling (Medium-Term)
**Question answered:** *"In what exact time-window should each operation be performed?"*

- Generates a time-bound sequence of operations feeding into the cell.
- Includes **tool scheduling** and **AGV dispatch scheduling**.

### Pillar 4 – Real-Time Dispatching & Shop-Floor Control
**Question answered:** *"Right now, which job should the machine accept, and which tool should the AGV bring?"*

- Executes the schedule; reacts to disruptions (tool break, machine down, AGV stuck).
- Uses **priority dispatch rules** (e.g., SPT, EDD, CR).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table compiles every equation required to solve numerical problems from this module.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Production Ratio** | $R_i = \dfrac{D_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} D_j}$ | $D_i$ = demand of part $i$ (units/period), $n$ = total part types | $\sum R_i = 1$ |
| 2 | **Workload on Machine $k$ (MTH)** | $L_k = \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i \cdot \sum_{j \in O_i} t_{ijk}$ | $t_{ijk}$ = standard time (min) of operation $j$ of part $i$ on machine $k$ | Measured in **min/period** |
| 3 | **Machine Loading Factor** | $LF_k = \dfrac{L_k}{A_k}$ | $A_k$ = available time of machine $k$ (min/period) | If $LF_k > 1$, **overloaded** |
| 4 | **Part Selection Score** | $S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{m} w_j \cdot r_{ij}$ | $w_j$ = weight of criterion $j$, $r_{ij}$ = rating (0–10) of part $i$ on criterion $j$ | $\sum w_j = 1$ |
| 5 | **Tool Life Fraction Used** | $u_T = \dfrac{t_{used}}{T_{life}}$ | $t_{used}$ = cutting time so far, $T_{life}$ = nominal tool life | If $u_T \ge 1$ → **replace** |
| 6 | **Makespan (parallel machines)** | $C_{max} = \max_k (C_k)$ | $C_k$ = completion time of machine $k$ | Lower is better |
| 7 | **Lateness** | $L_i = C_i - d_i$ | $C_i$ = completion time, $d_i$ = due date | $L_i < 0$ ⇒ early |
| 8 | **Tardiness** | $T_i = \max(0, L_i)$ | – | Non-negative |
| 9 | **Tool Magazine Utilization** | $TMU = \dfrac{N_{in\text{-}use}}{N_{slots}}$ | $N_{in\text{-}use}$ = active tools, $N_{slots}$ = capacity | Target 0.6 – 0.85 |
| 10 | **FMS Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Value-Added Time}}{\text{Throughput Time}}$ | – | Aim for > 0.30 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** When transcribing absolute-value expressions inside a markdown table, **always** use the LaTeX commands $\vert$ or $\mid$ in place of the vertical pipe $\vert$, because the pipe is the markdown table-cell separator. Using a raw pipe will **break the table rendering** — a common reason students lose marks when they paste solutions into answer sheets.

## 2.3 Why FMS Planning and Control is Engineering-Critical

In conventional transfer lines, planning is **static** — once the line is built, the sequence of operations is locked. In an FMS:

- The **part mix changes daily** (batch-of-one, mass-customization era).
- The **routing is flexible** — a part can visit machines in different orders.
- **Tooling is finite** — only 60–120 tools fit in a typical FMS magazine.
- The **cost of an idle minute** on a $500,000 CNC machining centre is very high.

Hence, an **optimized real-time planning & control system** is the difference between an FMS that delivers the promised *flexibility* and one that is merely an expensive set of machines. Real-world deployments in automotive (e.g., engine block families) and aerospace (turbine disc families) have shown that good planning software improves machine utilization by **20–35 %** and reduces work-in-progress (WIP) by **40–60 %**.

## 2.4 Operational Logic Flow (Pillar-by-Pillar)

1. **Inputs** → Part master file, routing sheets, tooling catalog, demand forecast, due dates.
2. **Long-Term** → Compute $R_i$ for each part, run weighted scoring $S_i$ to shortlist the part family.
3. **Medium-Term** → For the chosen part family, generate a master production schedule; perform machine loading to get $LF_k$ for each machine.
4. **Tool Planning** → Pre-allocate tools to magazines, compute $u_T$ for every tool at every planning horizon.
5. **Scheduling** → Solve a permutation flow-shop / job-shop problem to minimize $C_{max}$.
6. **Dispatching** → Apply a priority rule (SPT, EDD, SLACK, CR) at each machine queue.
7. **Feedback** → Update the database with actual times, tool wear, machine breakdowns.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Production Ratio from a Given Demand Vector

**Problem Setup (typical KTU 3-mark / 7-mark format):**
> "An FMS produces **4 part types** A, B, C, D. The weekly demands are: $D_A = 200$, $D_B = 150$, $D_C = 100$, $D_D = 50$. Compute the **production ratio** for each part type."

**Step 1 — Identify the total demand over the planning horizon.**

The total demand $D_{tot}$ is the sum of individual demands:

$$
D_{tot} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} D_j
$$

Substituting the four given values:

$$
D_{tot} = 200 + 150 + 100 + 50 = 500 \text{ units/week}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the definition of Production Ratio.**

By definition, the production ratio of part $i$ is the share of that part in the total demand:

$$
R_i = \frac{D_i}{D_{tot}}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate $R_i$ for each part type one by one.**

$$
R_A = \frac{200}{500} = 0.40
$$

$$
R_B = \frac{150}{500} = 0.30
$$

$$
R_C = \frac{100}{500} = 0.20
$$

$$
R_D = \frac{50}{500} = 0.10
$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check (the Σ=1 invariant).**

$$
\sum_{i=A}^{D} R_i = 0.40 + 0.30 + 0.20 + 0.10 = 1.00
$$

The invariant holds, so the calculation is consistent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation in the FMS context:** When this FMS is loaded, for every **10 parts** produced, **4 will be type A, 3 will be type B, 2 will be type C, and 1 will be type D**. This ratio feeds directly into the **machine loading** equation in Derivation 2.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Machine Loading and Loading Factor

**Problem Setup:**
> "A 2-machine FMS produces the 4 parts (A, B, C, D) from Derivation 1. The standard machining times (in minutes) are tabulated below. The available time per shift on each machine is **480 min**. Compute the workload and loading factor for each machine."

**Routing Table (Operation times in min):**

| Part | Op 10 (M1) | Op 20 (M2) | Op 30 (M1) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| A | 12 | 8 | 15 |
| B | 10 | 14 | – |
| C | 18 | 6 | 12 |
| D | 8 | 10 | – |

**Step 1 — Recall the workload formula.**

The expected workload on machine $k$ is the production-ratio-weighted sum of all operation times performed on that machine:

$$
L_k = \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i \cdot \left( \sum_{j \in O_i \cap M_k} t_{ijk} \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the inner sum (routing time on the machine) for each part.**

- Part A on M1: $12 + 15 = 27$ min, on M2: $8$ min.
- Part B on M1: $10$ min, on M2: $14$ min.
- Part C on M1: $18 + 12 = 30$ min, on M2: $6$ min.
- Part D on M1: $8$ min, on M2: $10$ min.

**Step 3 — Substitute into the workload formula for M1.**

$$
L_{M1} = (0.40)(27) + (0.30)(10) + (0.20)(30) + (0.10)(8)
$$

$$
L_{M1} = 10.80 + 3.00 + 6.00 + 0.80 = 20.60 \text{ min per part-equivalent}
$$

> If we wish to scale to a full week's batch of 500 parts:

$$
L_{M1}^{week} = 20.60 \times 500 = 10{,}300 \text{ min/week on M1}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute for M2.**

$$
L_{M2} = (0.40)(8) + (0.30)(14) + (0.20)(6) + (0.10)(10)
$$

$$
L_{M2} = 3.20 + 4.20 + 1.20 + 1.00 = 9.60 \text{ min per part-equivalent}
$$

Scaling up:

$$
L_{M2}^{week} = 9.60 \times 500 = 4{,}800 \text{ min/week on M2}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the loading factor $LF_k$.**

$$
LF_k = \frac{L_k^{week}}{A_k}
$$

If the cell runs **two shifts** per day, 5 days a week, the available time per week is:

$$
A_k = 2 \text{ shifts/day} \times 5 \text{ days} \times 480 \text{ min/shift} = 4{,}800 \text{ min/week}
$$

For M1:

$$
LF_{M1} = \frac{10{,}300}{4{,}800} = 2.146
$$

For M2:

$$
LF_{M2} = \frac{4{,}800}{4{,}800} = 1.000
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering Decision.**

> [!WARNING]
> $LF_{M1} = 2.15 > 1.00$. **Machine 1 is severely overloaded** — it would need more than 2 shifts of weekly time. The planner must either (a) add a second M1-equivalent machine, (b) re-route some operations to M2, or (c) outsource a portion of the batch. M2 is exactly fully loaded — a healthy target.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Weighted Scoring for Part Type Selection

**Problem Setup (favourite KTU 7-mark style):**
> "Three candidate parts (P1, P2, P3) are being considered for a new FMS. The selection criteria and weights are: *Annual Volume* (0.30), *Similarity to Existing Family* (0.25), *Process Compatibility* (0.25), *Profit Margin* (0.20). Ratings (0–10) are given in the table. Compute the weighted score and recommend the part."

| Criterion (Weight) | P1 | P2 | P3 |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Annual Volume (0.30) | 8 | 6 | 9 |
| Similarity (0.25) | 9 | 5 | 7 |
| Process Compat. (0.25) | 7 | 8 | 6 |
| Profit Margin (0.20) | 6 | 9 | 7 |

**Step 1 — Apply the scoring formula.**

$$
S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{m} w_j \cdot r_{ij}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $S_{P1}$.**

$$
S_{P1} = (0.30)(8) + (0.25)(9) + (0.25)(7) + (0.20)(6)
$$

$$
S_{P1} = 2.40 + 2.25 + 1.75 + 1.20 = 7.60
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $S_{P2}$.**

$$
S_{P2} = (0.30)(6) + (0.25)(5) + (0.25)(8) + (0.20)(9)
$$

$$
S_{P2} = 1.80 + 1.25 + 2.00 + 1.80 = 6.85
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $S_{P3}$.**

$$
S_{P3} = (0.30)(9) + (0.25)(7) + (0.25)(6) + (0.20)(7)
$$

$$
S_{P3} = 2.70 + 1.75 + 1.50 + 1.40 = 7.35
$$

**Step 5 — Rank and Recommend.**

$$
S_{P1} = 7.60 > S_{P3} = 7.35 > S_{P2} = 6.85
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** **Part P1** should be selected for the FMS part family. If the FMS has slots for two parts, **P1 and P3** are chosen.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Dispatching Rules

| Priority Rule | Symbol | Selection Logic | Best Used When | Weakness |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **First-Come-First-Served** | FCFS | Earliest arrival first | Stable load | Ignores due date |
| **Shortest Processing Time** | SPT | Smallest $t_{p}$ first | Minimize WIP & flow time | Starves long jobs |
| **Earliest Due Date** | EDD | Smallest $d_i$ first | Tight due dates | Ignores processing time |
| **Critical Ratio** | CR | Minimize $\frac{d_i - t_{now}}{t_{rem}}$ | Mixed shop | Complex to compute |
| **Slack per Remaining Operation** | S/OPN | Minimize slack / #ops | FMS with many operations | Needs accurate data |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The FMS Planning & Control Hierarchy (Block-Level Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the full five-tier FMS planning-and-control pyramid that KTU examiners expect you to redraw in every module-4 answer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    t1["Level 1 Corporate Planning 12 to 24 months horizon"]
    t2["Level 2 FMS Design and Part Family Selection 6 to 12 months horizon"]
    t3["Level 3 Production Planning and Master Scheduling 1 to 12 weeks horizon"]
    t4["Level 4 Detailed Scheduling and Tool Planning 1 to 7 days horizon"]
    t5["Level 5 Real Time Shop Floor Control and Dispatching Minutes to hours horizon"]

    t1 --> t2
    t2 --> t3
    t3 --> t4
    t4 --> t5
    t5 -.Feedback Loop.-> t3
    t5 -.Exception Reports.-> t2
```

**How to read this diagram (for the exam):**
- **Arrows pointing downward** carry *plans* (instructions).
- **Dashed arrows pointing upward** carry *feedback* (actuals, deviations).
- Each level has progressively shorter time-horizons but progressively higher data-rate.

---

## 4.2 The Computer Control Hierarchy of an FMS

This is the *hardware-and-software* counterpart of the planning hierarchy. KTU 2024 examiners love to ask students to list the layers of this pyramid.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    c0["Enterprise Resource Planning ERP System"]
    c1["FMS Host Computer Scheduling and Database Management"]
    c2["Cell Controller Real Time Coordinator"]
    c3["Workstation Controllers CNC Machine M1 M2 M3"]
    c4["Sensor and Actuator Layer Tool Sensors AGV Encoders Robots"]

    c0 -- "Daily Production Plan" --> c1
    c1 -- "Weekly Schedule and Tool Plan" --> c2
    c2 -- "Job Dispatch Commands" --> c3
    c3 -- "Status Reports every 2 to 5 sec" --> c2
    c2 -- "Acknowledgment and Asks" --> c1
    c3 -- "Low Level Motion Control" --> c4
    c4 -- "Sensor Data Tool Wear Position" --> c3
```

**Key Mapping to Real Equipment:**
- **ERP Layer** → SAP / Oracle Manufacturing module running in corporate IT.
- **FMS Host** → Sun/HP server running CIM software.
- **Cell Controller** → PLC-class industrial PC (e.g., Siemens S7-1500).
- **Workstation Controller** → FANUC / Siemens / Mitsubishi CNC controller.
- **Sensor/Actuator Layer** → Limit switches, tool-breakage probes, RFID pallet readers, AGV IR sensors.

---

## 4.3 Functional Flow — How a Job Moves Through Planning & Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a1["Receive Customer Order"]
    a2["Verify Part in FMS Part Master File"]
    a3["Generate Routing and Tool List"]
    a4["Run Machine Loading to Assign Work"]
    a5["Generate Detailed Schedule"]
    a6["Pre Allocate Tools to Magazines"]
    a7["Dispatch First Operation via AGV"]
    a8["Machine Processes and Reports Status"]
    a9["Schedule Next Operation or Exit FMS"]

    a1 --> a2
    a2 --> a3
    a3 --> a4
    a4 --> a5
    a5 --> a6
    a6 --> a7
    a7 --> a8
    a8 --> a9
    a9 -.If More Operations.-> a5
    a9 -.If Complete.-> END1((Job Finished and Shipped))
```

---

## 4.4 Real-Time Decision Loop for a Single Machine Queue

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    b1["Start of Decision Cycle"]
    b2["Read Machine Status Idle Busy Down"]
    b3{"Is Machine Idle?"}
    b4["Continue Current Operation"]
    b5["Read Job Queue from Scheduler"]
    b6["Apply Priority Rule SPT EDD CR"]
    b7["Verify Required Tool is in Magazine"]
    b8{"Tool Available?"}
    b9["Dispatch Selected Job"]
    b10["Trigger Tool Change if Needed"]
    b11["Wait for Tool from Central Magazine or Replan"]
    b12["Send Status Update to Cell Controller"]

    b1 --> b2
    b2 --> b3
    b3 -- "No" --> b4
    b3 -- "Yes" --> b5
    b5 --> b6
    b6 --> b7
    b7 --> b8
    b8 -- "Yes" --> b9
    b8 -- "No" --> b11
    b9 --> b10
    b10 --> b12
    b11 --> b12
    b12 --> b1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam tip:** If the question is "Explain the real-time control loop of an FMS," reproduce this exact decision cycle in your answer script. Examiners give full marks for showing the *sense → decide → act* pattern and the *tool-availability contingency*.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are mapped to the **KTU 2024 Scheme OERUT613 – Module 4** learning outcomes. Marks are distributed as per the official ESE pattern: **Part A (3 marks each)** and **Part B (14 marks each, with internal choice)**.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"List the three hierarchical levels of FMS planning and state one decision taken at each level."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Level | Time Horizon | One Typical Decision |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Long-Term Planning** | Months – Year | Selection of part family to be produced in the FMS |
| **Medium-Term Planning** | Weeks – Month | Machine loading and master production scheduling |
| **Short-Term Control** | Hours – Day | Real-time dispatching of jobs at each machine queue |

> **Mark split (3):** 1 mark for the three-level naming, 1 mark for the time horizon of each, 1 mark for one correct decision per level.

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Define Production Ratio. An FMS produces 3 part types with monthly demands 600, 300 and 100 units. Compute the production ratio of each part."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Production Ratio $R_i$ of a part is the ratio of its demand $D_i$ to the total demand of all part types in the planning horizon:

$$
R_i = \frac{D_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} D_j}
$$

**Computation (2 marks):**

$$
D_{tot} = 600 + 300 + 100 = 1000
$$

$$
R_1 = \frac{600}{1000} = 0.60, \quad R_2 = \frac{300}{1000} = 0.30, \quad R_3 = \frac{100}{1000} = 0.10
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

### Question B — Choice A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **four functions of FMS Planning and Control** with a neat block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Three parts P1, P2, P3 are evaluated for inclusion in an FMS. The weights of the criteria — *Annual Volume, Process Similarity, Tooling Compatibility, Profit* — are **0.25, 0.30, 0.20, 0.25** respectively. The ratings (out of 10) are given below. Compute the weighted score of each part and recommend the part to be produced. **(7 marks)**

| Criterion (Weight) | P1 | P2 | P3 |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Annual Volume (0.25) | 7 | 9 | 5 |
| Process Similarity (0.30) | 8 | 6 | 7 |
| Tooling Compatibility (0.20) | 6 | 8 | 9 |
| Profit (0.25) | 9 | 5 | 6 |

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

The four functions of FMS Planning and Control are:

1. **Part Type Selection & Production Planning (1.5 marks):**
   - Identifies the part family the FMS will produce.
   - Uses **weighted scoring** and **production ratio** analysis to choose parts that match the FMS's process capability and tooling.

2. **Machine Loading (1.5 marks):**
   - Distributes operations of the chosen parts across the available CNC machines.
   - Goal: balanced workload such that $LF_k \approx 1$ for all $k$.
   - Uses the formula $L_k = \sum_i R_i \cdot \sum_{j} t_{ijk}$.

3. **Scheduling (2 marks):**
   - Determines the *time order* in which operations will be performed.
   - Includes **tool scheduling** (which tool is mounted on which machine and when) and **AGV scheduling** (when each pallet moves).
   - Output is a Gantt-chart-like dispatch list for the cell.

4. **Real-Time Shop-Floor Control / Dispatching (2 marks):**
   - At every decision epoch, the cell controller decides **which job to load next** on an idle machine.
   - Uses a priority rule (SPT, EDD, CR) and verifies **tool availability** before dispatch.
   - Maintains feedback: tool-life tracking, machine-status monitoring, exception reporting.

A neat block diagram (1 mark):

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    f1["Part Type Selection"]
    f2["Machine Loading"]
    f3["Scheduling and Tool Planning"]
    f4["Real Time Dispatching and Control"]

    f1 --> f2 --> f3 --> f4
    f4 -.Feedback.-> f1
```

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the weighted-score formula:

$$
S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{4} w_j \cdot r_{ij}
$$

**Step 2 (4 marks):** Compute scores part-by-part.

For P1:

$$
S_{P1} = (0.25)(7) + (0.30)(8) + (0.20)(6) + (0.25)(9)
$$

$$
S_{P1} = 1.75 + 2.40 + 1.20 + 2.25 = 7.60
$$

For P2:

$$
S_{P2} = (0.25)(9) + (0.30)(6) + (0.20)(8) + (0.25)(5)
$$

$$
S_{P2} = 2.25 + 1.80 + 1.60 + 1.25 = 6.90
$$

For P3:

$$
S_{P3} = (0.25)(5) + (0.30)(7) + (0.20)(9) + (0.25)(6)
$$

$$
S_{P3} = 1.25 + 2.10 + 1.80 + 1.50 = 6.65
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Rank the parts.

$$
S_{P1} = 7.60 > S_{P2} = 6.90 > S_{P3} = 6.65
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** **Recommendation:** **Part P1** is selected for the FMS. If a second slot exists, **P2** is the next best.

---

### Question B — Choice B (14 Marks — Alternative)
**[KTU University Exam – Supplementary June 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the **computer control hierarchy of an FMS** with a block diagram. State the function of **each level**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An FMS has **2 machines (M1, M2)** producing 2 part types (X, Y) with production ratios $R_X = 0.6$ and $R_Y = 0.4$. The standard operation times (min/part) are:

| Part | Op 10 (M1) | Op 20 (M2) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| X | 20 | 30 |
| Y | 40 | 10 |

Available time per machine = **2400 min/week**. Compute the workload and loading factor of each machine and comment. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

The FMS computer-control hierarchy is a **5-layer pyramid** (1 mark for the diagram; remaining 6 marks for function description).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    g1["Level 1 Enterprise Host ERP Server"]
    g2["Level 2 FMS Host Computer Scheduling DB"]
    g3["Level 3 Cell Controller Real Time"]
    g4["Level 4 Workstation Controllers CNC"]
    g5["Level 5 Sensors and Actuators"]

    g1 --> g2 --> g3 --> g4 --> g5
    g5 -.Sensory Feedback.-> g4
    g4 -.Status.-> g3
    g3 -.Reports.-> g2
```

**Functions of each level (6 × 1 mark each):**

| Level | Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| **1 – Enterprise Host** | Long-term business planning, demand forecast, order acceptance |
| **2 – FMS Host** | Master scheduling, tool planning, AGV route planning, database management |
| **3 – Cell Controller** | Real-time coordination, dispatching, conflict resolution, performance monitoring |
| **4 – Workstation Controller** | Direct numerical control of each CNC; tool-change sequencing, status reporting |
| **5 – Sensors/Actuators** | Tool-breakage detection, position feedback, pallet identification, AGV navigation |

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Apply the workload formula.

$$
L_{M1} = R_X \cdot t_{X,M1} + R_Y \cdot t_{Y,M1}
$$

$$
L_{M2} = R_X \cdot t_{X,M2} + R_Y \cdot t_{Y,M2}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Substitute.

$$
L_{M1} = (0.6)(20) + (0.4)(40) = 12.0 + 16.0 = 28.0 \text{ min/part-eq.}
$$

$$
L_{M2} = (0.6)(30) + (0.4)(10) = 18.0 + 4.0 = 22.0 \text{ min/part-eq.}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Scale to weekly workload (assuming 1000 part-equivalents/week).

$$
L_{M1}^{week} = 28.0 \times 1000 = 28{,}000 \text{ min/week}
$$

$$
L_{M2}^{week} = 22.0 \times 1000 = 22{,}000 \text{ min/week}
$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Compute loading factors.

$$
LF_{M1} = \frac{28{,}000}{2400} = 11.67
$$

$$
LF_{M2} = \frac{22{,}000}{2400} = 9.17
$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** **Comment / Engineering interpretation.**

> [!WARNING]
> Both loading factors are **far above 1.0**, meaning the available time is grossly insufficient for the planned production. To make the system viable: (i) **increase capacity** by adding parallel machines, (ii) **reduce the production volume**, or (iii) **out-source** a fraction of the work. The current configuration cannot be recommended.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students lose marks in this module — KTU 2024 valuation pattern observed:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Σ=1 check on production ratios.** A single mismatch = **−1 mark** even if individual $R_i$ are right.
> 2. **Computing $L_k$ without scaling to the available time** — students often give minutes/part and stop, forgetting to multiply by the batch size.
> 3. **Confusing $LF_k > 1$ (overloaded) with $LF_k = 0$ (idle)**. State explicitly: $LF_k > 1$ means the machine **cannot** complete the assigned work in the available time.
> 4. **Writing a block diagram without arrows or labels.** KTU examiners expect at least one labeled arrow showing data/control flow direction.
> 5. **In weighted-scoring questions, students add the weights but forget to verify $\sum w_j = 1$**. If the given weights don't sum to 1, you must **normalize** them first and *state* that you have done so.
> 6. **Mixing up "tardiness" and "lateness".** Lateness can be negative (early delivery); tardiness is the non-negative part.
> 7. **Skipping the "Comment" step.** A numerical answer without interpretation usually attracts partial — not full — marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **FMS Planning and Control** = the decision-making system that converts customer orders into machine-level actions inside an FMS.
- [ ] **Three planning levels:** Long-term (part family), Medium-term (loading + scheduling), Short-term (real-time dispatch).
- [ ] **Five control levels:** ERP → FMS Host → Cell Controller → Workstation Controller → Sensors/Actuators.
- [ ] **Production Ratio** $R_i = D_i / \sum D_j$; the **Σ-invariance** is your best sanity check.
- [ ] **Workload** $L_k = \sum_i R_i \cdot \sum_j t_{ijk}$ (units: min/part-equivalent, scale to period).
- [ ] **Loading Factor** $LF_k = L_k^{period} / A_k$; $LF_k > 1$ ⇒ **overloaded**, $LF_k \ll 1$ ⇒ **under-utilized**.
- [ ] **Weighted-Score Selection** uses formula $S_i = \sum_j w_j r_{ij}$; **always normalize the weights** if they do not sum to 1.
- [ ] **Priority Dispatch Rules** to memorize: FCFS, SPT, EDD, CR, S/OPN — know the *selection logic* of each.
- [ ] **Tool management** is part of planning: monitor $u_T = t_{used}/T_{life}$, keep $TMU$ between **0.6 and 0.85** for healthy operation.
- [ ] **Real-time control loop** is *sense → decide → act*; always mention the **tool-availability contingency** path.
- [ ] **Feedback** flows upward in the hierarchy: machine status → cell controller → FMS host → ERP.
- [ ] In numerical answers, **state the units** in every line and **comment on the engineering meaning** of the final number.
- [ ] The **"comment step"** in machine-loading problems is where most of the difference between 7 and 14 marks is made.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Planning and Control of FMS**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automated Guided Vehicle Systems (AGVS): Overview of AGVS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT613 - COMPUTER INTEGRATED MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Automated Guided Vehicle Systems (AGVS): Overview of AGVS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Automated Guided Vehicle System (AGVS)** is a computer-controlled, driverless material handling system that uses a fleet of self-propelled vehicles to transport raw materials, work-in-progress (WIP), and finished goods between designated stations along predefined paths in a manufacturing or warehouse environment, with centralized traffic management, dispatching, and route optimization handled by a supervisory control system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim standard):**  
> An AGVS is an *integrated network of self-propelled, computer-controlled vehicles*, operating on fixed or flexible guide paths, that performs *horizontal transport* of materials in a flexible manufacturing system (FMS) or automated warehouse, with *no human onboard operator*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Synonyms frequently used in KTU exam answers:**  
> AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle), Driverless Vehicle System (DVS), Self-Guided Vehicle (SGV), Automated Transport System (ATS).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **hospital corridor where small motorized carts** silently glide along a **painted line on the floor**, carrying meals, medicines, and laundry between the kitchen, pharmacy, and patient rooms. The carts never bump into each other because a **central control room** (like an air-traffic controller) tells each cart *when* to move, *which lane* to take, and *when to stop* to let another cart pass. They recharge themselves at night when the hospital is quiet.

> **An AGVS is exactly that — but inside a factory.**  
> The "painted line" can be a **magnetic tape, buried wire, reflective tape, laser targets, or even QR codes on the floor**. The "central control room" is the **AGV host computer** that dispatches vehicles in real time.

### 1.3 Core Operational Metrics (Industry-Standard Values)

| Parameter | Typical Industrial Value |
|---|---|
| Operating Speed | **0.5 m/s to 1.5 m/s** (loaded), up to **2 m/s** (unloaded) |
| Payload Capacity | **50 kg to 50,000 kg** |
| Vehicle Battery Voltage | **24 V / 48 V / 72 V DC** |
| Battery Recharge Time | **6 to 12 hours** (opportunity fast charge: **15 min**) |
| Path Width | **1.0 m to 2.5 m** minimum |
| Fleet Size (typical plant) | **3 to 50 vehicles** |
| Positioning Accuracy | **± 10 mm** (with laser/odometry) |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU numerical problems, the **default design speed** assumed by examiners is **v = 1.0 m/s** unless otherwise stated. The **default acceleration** is **a = 0.3 m/s²**.

### 1.4 Position of AGVS in the CIM Hierarchy

$$
\text{CIM} \rightarrow \text{FMS} \rightarrow \text{AS/RS} \; \& \; \text{AGVS} \rightarrow \text{Workstations}
$$

AGVS acts as the **horizontal transport backbone** that physically links workstations, storage systems (AS/RS), and assembly lines in a Computer Integrated Manufacturing (CIM) environment.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Anatomy of an AGVS

Every AGVS consists of **four tightly coupled subsystems** — this is the KTU 4-point model and a guaranteed exam short-answer:

1. **The Vehicle (AGV)** — the physical mobile platform with drive, steering, payload handling (roller, fork, lift, conveyor, tugger), battery, and onboard controller.
2. **The Guidance System** — the *physical* or *logical* path that the vehicle follows (wire, tape, laser map, beacons).
3. **The Communication & Control System** — the wireless link + central host computer that dispatches vehicles and resolves conflicts.
4. **The Interface Subsystem** — pickup/drop-off stations, battery charging docks, traffic intersections, buffer zones.

### 2.2 Vehicle Guidance Technologies (Most-Examined Topic)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU syllabus explicitly tests the **comparison of guidance methods**. Master the *technology*, *accuracy*, *flexibility*, and *cost* of each.

| Guidance Type | Working Principle | Accuracy | Flexibility | Relative Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Wire-Guided** (Inductive) | Vehicle senses **electromagnetic field** from a buried wire carrying **low-frequency AC** (typically **5 kHz to 30 kHz**, 100–500 mA) | **± 25 mm** | Very Low — path is permanent | **Lowest** |
| **Magnetic Tape** | Adhesive magnetic strip on floor sensed by Hall-effect sensors | **± 12 mm** | Low — tape must be re-laid | Low |
| **Optical / Reflective Tape** | Vehicle's optical scanner follows **reflective paint or tape** | **± 6 mm** | Low | Low |
| **Laser Guidance (LGV)** | Onboard laser scanner triangulates position using **retro-reflective targets** (2–4 mounted on walls/posts) | **± 5 mm** | **High** — paths are software-defined | **High** |
| **Inertial / Gyroscopic** | Onboard gyroscope + odometer updates position; uses **magnet markers in floor** for correction | **± 25 mm** | High | Medium |
| **Vision-Based (V-Guide)** | Onboard camera reads **floor-mounted QR/barcodes** (every 2–5 m) for absolute correction | **± 10 mm** | Very High | Medium |
| **GPS / RTK** (outdoor) | Differential GPS with **Real-Time Kinematic** correction | **± 30 mm** | Highest | High (outdoor only) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Most KTU-asked fact:** *Laser-guided vehicles (LGVs) are the modern industry standard because they require NO floor modifications — paths exist only as software routes.*

### 2.3 Vehicle Types (by Function)

- **Unit Load AGV** — carries a single pallet or tote (fork-lift style).
- **Tugger AGV** — pulls a train of trailers (used in milk-run / parts-to-line delivery).
- **Fork AGV** — onboard fork mechanism (replaces forklifts, ideal for warehouse aisles).
- **Assembly AGV** — large platform carrying entire chassis in automotive assembly (line-side automation).
- **Light-Duty AGV** — small tabletop units for cleanrooms / electronics (often called *AMR* cousins).

### 2.4 Traffic Management Strategies

1. **Zone Control** — track is divided into blocks; only **one vehicle per block** at a time (block = single signal logic).
2. **Forward-Sensing Collision Avoidance** — vehicle-mounted **LiDAR / ultrasonic / bumper sensors** detect obstacles; emergency stop triggered.
3. **Centralized Dispatching** — host computer uses **shortest path algorithm** and **priority rules** (FIFO, nearest vehicle, earliest deadline).
4. **On-the-Fly Re-routing** — used by *autonomous mobile robots (AMRs)* only; AGVs use fixed paths.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Quick-Revision)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5 formulas below cover 95% of KTU numerical problems on AGVS.**

| Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Average Travel Time** | $T_t = \dfrac{D}{v} + t_{pickup} + t_{dropoff}$ | $D$ = one-way distance (m), $v$ = speed (m/s), $t_{pickup}, t_{dropoff}$ in seconds | Assumes straight, no-traffic path |
| **Vehicle Cycle Time** | $T_c = T_{load} + T_t + T_{unload} + T_{return}$ | $T_{load}$ = loading time, $T_{return}$ = return travel time | One complete delivery round |
| **Number of Vehicles Required** | $N = \left\lceil \dfrac{R \cdot T_c}{3600 \cdot U} \right\rceil$ | $R$ = delivery rate (deliveries/hr), $U$ = utilization factor (0.7–0.85) | **Ceiling** function mandatory |
| **System Throughput** | $\eta = \dfrac{N \cdot 3600 \cdot U}{T_c}$ | $\eta$ in deliveries/hour | $N$ must be integer |
| **Steady-State Utilization** | $U = \dfrac{R \cdot T_c}{N \cdot 3600}$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) | If $U \geq 1$ → need more vehicles |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units cheat code for KTU:** Always convert travel time to **seconds** before plugging into the $\eta$ formula (hence the $3600$ factor for hour-to-second).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Toyota, BMW, Tesla** — automotive final assembly lines use **assembly AGVs** to move the body at the *pace of the slowest station* (takt-time synchronized).
- **Amazon Kiva robots** — modern descendants of AGVS; use **floor QR codes + vision** (now Amazon Robotics, ~750,000 units deployed).
- **Hospitals** — meal and linen delivery, reducing labor cost by ~40%.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Number of AGVs Required (Closed-Form)

**Problem Statement (typical KTU 14-mark setup):**  
A factory requires **60 deliveries/hour** between the store and the assembly line. Each delivery cycle involves **D = 120 m** one-way travel at **v = 1.0 m/s**, with **t_load = 30 s** and **t_unload = 25 s**. The return trip is identical. Maximum allowable fleet utilization is **U = 0.80**. Find the *minimum number of AGVs* required.

---

**Step 1 — Compute the one-way travel time.**

$$
T_t = \frac{D}{v} = \frac{120 \text{ m}}{1.0 \text{ m/s}} = 120 \text{ s}
$$

*Logic:* Travel time equals distance divided by average speed; since the AGV is loaded in one direction and empty in the other, both trips take equal time under the no-traffic assumption.

---

**Step 2 — Compute the return travel time.**

$$
T_{return} = T_t = 120 \text{ s}
$$

*Logic:* For a symmetric milk-run, return distance equals forward distance, so travel times are equal.

---

**Step 3 — Compute the total cycle time per delivery.**

$$
T_c = T_{load} + T_t + T_{unload} + T_{return}
$$

$$
T_c = 30 + 120 + 25 + 120 = 295 \text{ s}
$$

*Logic:* A complete cycle = load at source + travel out + unload at destination + travel back to source. This is the *time one vehicle is occupied* per delivery.

---

**Step 4 — Write the utilization-based vehicle count formula.**

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{R \cdot T_c}{3600 \cdot U} \right\rceil
$$

*Logic derivation from first principles:*  
Each vehicle can complete $\dfrac{3600 \cdot U}{T_c}$ deliveries per hour (with $U$ fraction being useful work). The system must satisfy rate $R$, so:

$$
N = \frac{\text{required rate}}{\text{rate per vehicle}} = \frac{R}{\left( \frac{3600 \cdot U}{T_c} \right)} = \frac{R \cdot T_c}{3600 \cdot U}
$$

---

**Step 5 — Substitute the numbers.**

$$
N = \frac{60 \cdot 295}{3600 \cdot 0.80} = \frac{17700}{2880} = 6.1458
$$

---

**Step 6 — Apply the ceiling function (cannot deploy 0.145 of a vehicle).**

$$
N = \lceil 6.1458 \rceil = 7 \text{ vehicles}
$$

---

**Step 7 — Verify utilization with N = 7.**

$$
U_{actual} = \frac{R \cdot T_c}{N \cdot 3600} = \frac{60 \cdot 295}{7 \cdot 3600} = \frac{17700}{25200} = 0.7024 \; (70.24\%)
$$

*Logic check:* Utilization dropped from the 80% design ceiling to 70.24% — well within the **safe operating band of 60–80%** (if $U > 0.85$, vehicles become saturated and queues form).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** $N_{min} = 7$ AGVs, with a steady-state utilization of **70.24%**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum System Throughput Given a Fixed Fleet

**Given:** Same parameters as above, but **N = 10** vehicles available.  
**Find:** Maximum sustainable throughput $\eta_{max}$.

$$
\eta_{max} = \frac{N \cdot 3600 \cdot U_{max}}{T_c}
$$

$$
\eta_{max} = \frac{10 \cdot 3600 \cdot 0.80}{295} = \frac{28800}{295} = 97.63 \text{ deliveries/hour}
$$

*Engineering interpretation:* With 10 vehicles, the plant can absorb almost a **38% increase** in demand (from 60 to 97.6 deliveries/hr) before saturation — important for **capacity planning** during product-mix changes.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Guidance Technology

| Decision Criterion | Best Guidance Choice | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Lowest initial cost, permanent layout** | Wire-guided (inductive) | Wire is cheap, but floor must be cut open |
| **Frequent layout changes** | Laser-guided (LGV) | Paths redefined in software — no floor work |
| **Outdoor, large yard (containers)** | GPS / RTK | Only practical option at >50 m scale |
| **Cleanroom / electronics** | Magnetic tape or vision | No wire cutting, no dust generation |
| **High precision ± 5 mm assembly** | Laser-guided with micro-targets | Sub-cm accuracy unmatched by tape/wire |
| **Hospitals / mixed human traffic** | Vision-based AMR (QR + obstacle AI) | Maximum safety + flexibility |

---

### 3.4 Edge Cases & Pitfalls (Numerical)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU valuation errors (Examiner's Pitfall Callout):**
> 1. Forgetting the **ceiling function** — writing $N = 6.14$ instead of $7$. (Penalty: 1 mark)
> 2. Mixing **minutes** and **seconds** in $T_c$. (Penalty: 2 marks — full method mark lost)
> 3. Forgetting to include **both $T_t$ and $T_{return}$** in the cycle time. (Penalty: 2 marks)
> 4. Not specifying the **assumption of no traffic congestion** in the answer. (Penalty: 1 mark for incomplete engineering justification)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 AGVS Block Architecture (Top-Down Functional View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HostCtrl["AGV HOST COMPUTER\nDispatch + Traffic Mgmt"]

    GuidSys["GUIDANCE SYSTEM\nWire  Laser  Tape  Vision"]

```

---

### 4.2 Decision Flow — Vehicle Dispatch Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[NEW TRANSPORT REQUEST ARRIVES] --> B{ANY IDLE VEHICLE AVAILABLE?}
    B -- YES --> C[ASSIGN NEAREST IDLE VEHICLE]
    B -- NO --> D{ANY VEHICLE NEAR COMPLETION}
    D -- YES --> E[QUEUE REQUEST ASSIGN ON RELEASE]
    D -- NO --> F{CRITICAL PRIORITY REQUEST}
    F -- YES --> G[PREEMPT LOWEST PRIORITY VEHICLE]
    F -- NO --> H[ADD TO QUEUE WAIT FOR AVAILABILITY]
    C --> I[UPDATE CENTRAL MAP]
    E --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[DISPATCH COMMAND WIRELESSLY]
```

### 4.3 Guidance Technology Comparison Tree

### 4.4 AGV Vehicle Internal Subsystem Map

```mermaid
    GuidSens["GUIDANCE SENSOR ARRAY"] --> OnboardCPU["ONBOARD MICROCONTROLLER"]
    Odom["WHEEL ODOMETER"] --> OnboardCPU

    Bumper["EMERGENCY BUMPER"] --> OnboardCPU
    
    OnboardCPU --> Wireless["WIRELESS MODEM"]

    OnboardCPU --> PayloadMech["PAYLOAD MECHANISM\nLift, Roller, Conveyor, Fork"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model Paper)]**  
*Define an Automated Guided Vehicle System (AGVS). List any four advantages of AGVS over conventional conveyor-based material handling.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Automated Guided Vehicle System (AGVS)** is a computer-controlled material handling system that uses *driverless, self-propelled vehicles* to transport materials between predefined stations along fixed or virtual guide paths, with dispatching and traffic management handled by a central host computer.

**Four advantages** (1/2 mark each, totalling 2 marks):

1. **Flexibility** — routes can be changed by *software redefinition* (especially with laser guidance) without altering physical infrastructure.
2. **Scalability** — fleet size can be increased one vehicle at a time as demand grows.
3. **Labor-free 24/7 operation** — no driver cost, no shift restrictions.
4. **Space efficiency** — narrow aisles (as low as 1 m) compared to conveyors; usable in modular layouts.
5. *Bonus (any one of):* Better safety (sensors, e-stops), reduced product damage, real-time tracking via host computer.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]**  
*Compare wire-guided and laser-guided AGV systems on the basis of accuracy, flexibility, installation cost, and path-change difficulty.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — tabular form expected):**

| Parameter | Wire-Guided AGV | Laser-Guided AGV (LGV) |
|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | **± 25 mm** (moderate) | **± 5 mm** (high) |
| **Flexibility** | Very low (fixed wire) | Very high (software-defined paths) |
| **Installation Cost** | **Low** (wire + trench) | **High** (laser unit + targets) |
| **Path-Change Difficulty** | Difficult — must cut floor, re-lay wire | Trivial — change in host software |

> **Examiner's note:** A *clean 2-column table* with 4 rows scores the full 3 marks. Writing only a paragraph costs you 1 mark for poor presentation.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — AGVS Sizing & Numerical Design**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 (Predicted Pattern)]**  
A flexible manufacturing system uses AGVs to transport workpieces from a central storage to **three machining cells**. Each delivery cycle covers **D = 150 m** (one way) at an average speed of **v = 1.2 m/s**. The loading and unloading times are **t_load = 25 s** and **t_unload = 20 s** respectively. The plant needs a delivery rate of **R = 45 deliveries/hour**.

**(a)** Determine the *minimum number of AGVs* required, assuming maximum allowable utilization of **U_max = 0.80**. (7 marks)

**(b)** If only **6 AGVs** are available, what is the *maximum sustainable delivery rate* and the *new steady-state utilization*? Comment on the operational impact. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Minimum AGV count**

**Step 1:** Compute one-way travel time.

$$
T_t = \frac{D}{v} = \frac{150}{1.2} = 125 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute full cycle time per delivery (assuming symmetric return).

$$
T_c = T_{load} + T_t + T_{unload} + T_{return} = 25 + 125 + 20 + 125 = 295 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the vehicle-count formula.

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{R \cdot T_c}{3600 \cdot U_{max}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{45 \cdot 295}{3600 \cdot 0.80} \right\rceil
$$

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{13275}{2880} \right\rceil = \lceil 4.609 \rceil = 5 \text{ AGVs}
$$

*(1 mark for correct formula, 2 marks for substitution, 1 mark for ceiling application, 3 marks for answer + interpretation)*

**Part (b) — New throughput with 6 AGVs**

**Step 1:** Maximum sustainable rate.

$$
\eta_{max} = \frac{N \cdot 3600 \cdot U_{max}}{T_c} = \frac{6 \cdot 3600 \cdot 0.80}{295} = \frac{17280}{295} = 58.58 \text{ deliveries/hour}
$$

**Step 2:** Actual rate to meet demand of 45 deliveries/hr.

$$
U_{actual} = \frac{R \cdot T_c}{N \cdot 3600} = \frac{45 \cdot 295}{6 \cdot 3600} = \frac{13275}{21600} = 0.6146
$$

$$
U_{actual} = 61.46\%
$$

**Step 3 — Operational impact comment (1 mark):**  
With 6 vehicles and demand of 45 deliveries/hr, utilization drops to a **healthy 61.46%**, well below the 80% design ceiling. This provides:
- **Buffer capacity** of $58.58 - 45 = 13.58$ deliveries/hr (~30% headroom) for demand surges.
- Reduced **vehicle queuing**, less traffic congestion, and **no need for additional procurement** until demand crosses ~58 deliveries/hr.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**  
> 1. **Forgetting the ceiling** in part (a) — writing $N = 4.6$ will be marked wrong (lose 1 mark).  
> 2. **Unit mismatch** in $T_c$ — using minutes instead of seconds silently corrupts every subsequent calculation. Always state the unit.  
> 3. **Missing the operational comment** in part (b) — KTU awards 1 mark for the *engineering interpretation*, not just the number. Always end with a 1-line impact statement.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Conceptual / Comparative (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]**  
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **four major subsystems** of an AGVS. Describe the **working principle of laser-guided AGVs** with the role of *retro-reflective targets*. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare **AGVS with conventional conveyor systems** under the heads: *flexibility, installation cost, space requirement, maintenance, and suitability for FMS*. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution (Key Points):**

**Part (a) — Four subsystems (3.5 marks) + LGV working (3.5 marks):**

*Four subsystems* (½ mark each + ½ for neat diagram = 3 marks total):
1. **Vehicle subsystem** — chassis, drive, steering, battery, payload mechanism.
2. **Guidance subsystem** — wire / tape / laser / vision.
3. **Communication & control subsystem** — wireless + host computer + dispatcher.
4. **Interface subsystem** — pickup/drop-off stations, charging docks, traffic intersections.

*Laser-guided working principle* (3.5 marks):
- A **laser scanner** mounted on top of the AGV emits a **rotating fan-beam** (typically 360°, ~25 Hz).
- The beam strikes **rear-reflective targets** (cylindrical prismatic stickers) mounted on walls/pillars at known map coordinates.
- The scanner measures the **angle** to each target and the **time-of-flight distance**.
- An **onboard processor** triangulates the AGV's **(x, y) position** in real time (typically **±5 mm** accuracy).
- The current position is **compared with the pre-loaded digital map** in host software, and **steering corrections** are issued every **20–50 ms**.

**Part (b) — Comparison Table (7 marks = 7 cells, 1 mark each):**

| Parameter | AGVS | Conventional Conveyor |
|---|---|---|
| **Flexibility** | Very high — software-redefined routes | Very low — fixed mechanical path |
| **Installation Cost** | Medium-high (vehicles + control) | High for long runs, but per-meter cheaper |
| **Space Requirement** | Narrow aisles, 3-D paths possible | Fixed footprint, must follow plant layout |
| **Maintenance** | Low (fewer moving parts) | High (belts, rollers, bearings, drives) |
| **FMS Suitability** | **Excellent** — synchronizes with variable routes | Poor — designed for fixed mass-production |
| **Energy Efficiency** | Battery, opportunity charging | Continuous motor draw |
| **Fault Tolerance** | One AGV failure = reroute; low impact | Belt break = line stops; catastrophic |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Drawing the AGVS block diagram *without labels* loses 1 mark. KTU examiners explicitly state "neat labelled diagram" in the question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — AGVS Overview (Module 4)**

- **Definition:** AGVS = computer-controlled, driverless material handling system using self-propelled vehicles on fixed/virtual paths.
- **Four Subsystems:** Vehicle | Guidance | Communication & Control | Interface.
- **Six Guidance Technologies:** Wire | Magnetic Tape | Optical | Laser (LGV) | Inertial | Vision/GPS.
- **Laser guidance** is the modern industry benchmark (no floor work, ±5 mm accuracy).
- **AGVS vs AMR:** AGVS = fixed/virtual paths; AMR = free-navigation with SLAM. KTU 2024 syllabus still focuses on **AGVS**.
- **Core formula** $N = \lceil (R \cdot T_c) / (3600 \cdot U) \rceil$ — memorize the ceiling.
- **Cycle time** $T_c$ **must** include both forward and return travel + load + unload.
- **Always state units** — KTU examiners deduct heavily for silent unit errors.
- **Healthy utilization band** = **60% to 80%**. If $U > 0.85$ → add vehicles; if $U < 0.50$ → fleet is over-provisioned.
- **Default design speed** in KTU problems = **1.0 m/s** unless stated otherwise.
- **Three KTU-favourite 14-mark patterns:** (1) Vehicle sizing numerical, (2) Guidance technology comparison, (3) AGVS vs Conveyor comparison.
- **Two KTU-favourite 3-mark patterns:** (1) Define AGVS + 4 advantages, (2) Compare any two guidance types in a table.
- **Real-world example** to cite: *Amazon Kiva* (now Amazon Robotics), *Toyota assembly AGVs*, *BMW paint-shop tugger AGVs*.
- **Safety first** — always mention **LiDAR + emergency bumper + E-stop** in any diagram for full marks.

> **End of Module 4 — AGVS Overview Notes** | *Good luck with the KTU ESE!*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT613 - COMPUTER INTEGRATED MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applications and Benefits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Applications and Benefits of CIM Enablers (Group Technology, Cellular Manufacturing & FMS):**
> In the context of Computer Integrated Manufacturing, *Applications* refer to the specific industrial, manufacturing, and engineering scenarios in which CIM enablers — namely **Group Technology (GT)**, **Classification & Coding Systems**, **Production Flow Analysis (PFA)**, **Cellular Manufacturing (CM)**, and **Flexible Manufacturing Systems (FMS)** — are deployed to solve part-family grouping, routing standardization, process planning, and shop-floor automation problems. *Benefits* refer to the quantified (and qualitative) gains in productivity, lead-time reduction, inventory turnover, work-in-process (WIP) reduction, tooling cost savings, quality improvement, and managerial decision support achieved through the structured implementation of these enablers.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Library" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: A Library vs. A Bookstore**
> Imagine two ways of organizing a million books:
> - **Traditional Functional Layout (Process Layout)** = A massive bookstore where mystery novels are in Aisle 1, biographies in Aisle 2, science in Aisle 3. To read a *single author's* works you must walk across the entire store. → **High travel, long lead time, lost books.**
> - **Group Technology / Cellular Layout** = A *smart library* that groups all books by the *same author* or *same subject family* into a single shelf (a **cell**). To find everything by Author "X," you walk to **one** cell. → **Low travel, short lead time, easy retrieval.**
>
> CIM does the same thing in a factory: it groups similar parts into **part families** and similar machines into **cells**, dramatically cutting movement, waiting, and information loss. The *Applications* describe **where** this is done (aerospace, automotive, electronics, job shops), and the *Benefits* describe **how much** is saved (typically **20–60% reduction in lead time** and **30–50% reduction in WIP** in published case studies).

### 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics Highlighted

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Benchmark Constants (Published Industrial Averages):**
> - Lead-time reduction: **30%–60%**
> - Work-In-Process (WIP) reduction: **40%–80%**
> - Setup time reduction: **50%–75%**
> - Throughput increase: **20%–50%**
> - Inventory carrying cost reduction: **20%–40%**
> - Labour productivity gain: **15%–30%**

### 1.4 KTU 2024 Course Outcome Mapping

| CO ID | Course Outcome | Bloom's Level |
|:-----:|:---------------|:-------------:|
| **CO4** | Apply Group Technology, classification & coding, and cellular manufacturing concepts to design part-family grouping and cell formation solutions. | Apply (L3) |
| **CO5** | Evaluate the applications, benefits, and limitations of CIM enablers in real industrial contexts. | Evaluate (L5) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Applications (Where are CIM Enablers Deployed?)

CIM enablers from Module 4 are not isolated academic exercises — they are deployed in well-defined industrial scenarios. The applications can be classified along **three orthogonal axes**:

### Axis A — By Manufacturing Volume (Production Type)

| Production Type | Volume Range | GT/CM/FMS Applicability | KTU-Standard Example |
|:---------------|:------------:|:-----------------------|:---------------------|
| **Job Shop** | 1–100 units | ★★★★★ (Highest) | Tool-room, defence spares |
| **Batch Production** | 100–10,000 units | ★★★★★ (Core domain) | Pumps, valves, fasteners |
| **Mass Production** | >10,000 units | ★★★ (Moderate) | Automotive engine blocks |
| **Continuous Process** | 24×7 flow | ★ (Low — FMS only) | Petrochemical, steel rolling |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** *Batch and Job-Shop production account for approximately **75%–80%** of all manufactured items in a typical industrialized nation.* This is precisely why GT and CM were invented — to bring the efficiency of mass production *into* the batch environment.

### Axis B — By Industry Sector

- **Automotive:** FMS for engine block machining, GT for axle/shaft part families.
- **Aerospace:** GT coding (e.g., **Opitz**, **DCLASS**) for managing 1M+ unique part numbers with high similarity.
- **Electronics:** SMT line cellular layout; FMS for PCB drilling.
- **Medical Devices:** Cellular manufacturing for orthopaedic implant families.
- **Heavy Engineering:** GT grouping of large castings and forgings.
- **Defense:** FMS for low-volume, high-variety spares with guaranteed traceability.

### Axis C — By Functional Use Inside CIM

| Functional Use | Enabling Technology | Application |
|:---------------|:-------------------|:------------|
| Part-family identification | Classification & Coding (Opitz, DCLASS, MICLASS) | Retrieval, standardization, CAPP |
| Cell formation | Rank-Order Clustering (ROC), ROC2, K-means | Shop-floor layout redesign |
| Routing standardization | Production Flow Analysis (PFA) | Bottleneck identification |
| Process planning | Variant CAPP, Generative CAPP | NC program reuse |
| Real-time control | FMS DNC/CNC network | Tool management, AGV routing |

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Benefits (What is Gained?)

### Category 1 — Operational Benefits (Quantifiable on the shop floor)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **These are the figures KTU examiners expect you to quote verbatim in 14-mark answers.**

1. **Reduced Material Handling** — Parts travel shorter distances inside cells.
2. **Reduced Setup Time** — Similar parts share fixtures and tooling.
3. **Reduced Work-In-Process (WIP) Inventory** — Queue lengths shrink.
4. **Shorter Manufacturing Lead Time (MLT)** — From days to hours.
5. **Improved Machine Utilization** — From ~30% to ~70–80%.
6. **Reduced Floor Space** — Typically **30%–50%** less.
7. **Lower Tooling Cost** — Standard tools across the family.

### Category 2 — Managerial / Strategic Benefits

8. **Simplified Production Planning & Control** — Fewer part numbers to track.
9. **Improved Cost Estimation & Quotation** — Standard routings → standard costs.
10. **Better Design for Manufacture (DFM)** — Designers reuse existing part families.
11. **Easier Workforce Training** — Operators specialize in one cell, not many departments.
12. **Enhanced Quality Control** — Dedicated inspection within the cell; closed-loop SPC.

### Category 3 — Information-System Benefits (CIM-Specific)

13. **Direct CAD/CAPP/CAM Linkage** — Coding system bridges design and process planning.
14. **MRP-II / ERP Integration** — GT codes become the *common key* across all modules.
15. **Computer-Aided Process Planning Reuse** — Variant CAPP uses GT codes to retrieve existing plans.
16. **Improved Database Search & Retrieval** — Engineers find similar past designs in seconds.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Math Rule:** The table uses `\vert` instead of the literal pipe symbol `|` to prevent markdown-table corruption. In your answer scripts, use plain `|x|`.

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Units | Typical Industrial Value |
|:-:|:-----------------|:-----------|:------|:------------------------|
| 1 | **Manufacturing Lead Time (MLT)** | $MLT = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Q_i + W_i + T_i + M_i)$ where $Q$=queue, $W$=wait, $T$=transport, $M$=machining | hours / days | 60%–80% reduction post-CM |
| 2 | **Group Technology Efficiency (η)** | $\eta_{GT} = \dfrac{N_{clustered}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | % | 70%–90% in well-formed cells |
| 3 | **Cell Formation Index (CFI)** | $CFI = \dfrac{a}{a+b}$ where $a$=intra-cell moves, $b$=inter-cell moves | dimensionless | 0.7–1.0 (ideal = 1) |
| 4 | **Work-In-Process (Little's Law)** | $WIP = TH \times CT$ where $TH$=throughput, $CT$=cycle time | units | 40%–80% reduction |
| 5 | **Machine Utilization (U)** | $U = \dfrac{T_{productive}}{T_{available}} \times 100\%$ | % | rises from 30% → 70% |
| 6 | **Reduction in Setup Time** | $\Delta S = S_{before} - S_{after}$ | minutes | 50%–75% |
| 7 | **Cost Savings per Part Family** | $\Delta C = (C_{process-layout} - C_{cell-layout}) \times V$ | ₹ / year | depends on volume $V$ |
| 8 | **PFA Clustering Coefficient** | $\phi = \dfrac{\text{Intra-cluster similarity}}{\text{Inter-cluster similarity}}$ | dimensionless | > 1 indicates good grouping |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DFM (Design for Manufacture):** GT codes act as a *lingua franca* between design and production.
- **Agile Manufacturing:** A part-family database is the foundation for rapid new-part introduction.
- **Mass Customization:** FMS + GT enables *batch-of-one* production at near-mass-production cost.
- **Digital Twin Synchronization:** GT codes link the physical factory to its digital twin in Industry 4.0.
- **Sustainability:** Reduced transport, energy, and waste contribute to *green manufacturing* KPIs.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Manufacturing Lead Time (MLT) Reduction in a Cell

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this derivation matters:** *Lead-time reduction* is the **single most quoted benefit** in KTU answer scripts. A 14-mark question often begins with a process layout and asks the student to compute MLT before/after cell conversion. Below is the exhaustive mathematical treatment.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Generic definition of MLT in a process layout**

In a process (functional) layout, a part visits $n$ different departments. The lead time is the sum of all waiting, queue, transport, and processing times.

$$MLT_{process} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Q_i + W_i + T_i + M_i)$$

where each term is explained below.

$$Q_i = \text{queue time before operation } i \quad\quad W_i = \text{waiting/idle time at } i$$

$$T_i = \text{transport time from } i \text{ to } i{+}1 \quad\quad M_i = \text{machining time at } i$$

**Step 2 — Recognize the dominant term**

In a process layout with $n=5$ operations on a typical batch of 50 parts:

$$\sum Q_i \gg \sum M_i$$

Empirically, $Q_i + W_i \approx 80\text{–}95\%$ of $MLT_{process}$, while $M_i \approx 5\text{–}20\%$.

**Step 3 — Effect of cellular conversion**

When parts are regrouped into a cell, the queue and waiting terms collapse because:
- All machines in a cell are *colocated*.
- Similar parts share tooling (no setup change between consecutive parts).
- A single operator (or small team) handles the entire route.

Thus:

$$MLT_{cell} \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} M_i + \epsilon_i$$

where $\epsilon_i$ is a small residual transport within the cell.

**Step 4 — Compute the percentage reduction**

$$\% \Delta MLT = \dfrac{MLT_{process} - MLT_{cell}}{MLT_{process}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example (KTU Board Standard)**

Given: $n=5$, per operation: $Q_i = 4\,\text{hr}$, $W_i = 2\,\text{hr}$, $T_i = 0.5\,\text{hr}$, $M_i = 0.5\,\text{hr}$.

$$MLT_{process} = 5 \times (4 + 2 + 0.5 + 0.5) = 5 \times 7 = 35\,\text{hours}$$

$$MLT_{cell} \approx 5 \times (0.5 + 0.1) = 5 \times 0.6 = 3\,\text{hours}$$

$$\% \Delta MLT = \dfrac{35 - 3}{35} \times 100\% \approx 91.4\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A reduction of 60%–90% is realistic and is the benchmark figure used in textbook case studies (e.g., the classic *John Deere* and *Yamaha* CIM case studies).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Work-In-Process Reduction via Little's Law

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Little's Law statement**

$$L = \lambda W$$

In manufacturing notation: $WIP = TH \times CT$, where $TH$ = throughput (parts/day), $CT$ = cycle time (days).

**Step 2 — For a process layout**

$$WIP_{process} = TH \times CT_{process}$$

**Step 3 — For a cellular layout**

$$WIP_{cell} = TH \times CT_{cell}$$

Since $CT$ is proportional to $MLT$ (cycle time = lead time per part in steady state):

$$CT_{cell} = k \cdot MLT_{cell}, \quad CT_{process} = k \cdot MLT_{process}$$

where $k$ is a constant of proportionality. Throughput $TH$ is assumed constant (same demand).

**Step 4 — WIP ratio**

$$\dfrac{WIP_{cell}}{WIP_{process}} = \dfrac{CT_{cell}}{CT_{process}} = \dfrac{MLT_{cell}}{MLT_{process}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Plug-in**

Using the previous example: $MLT_{process}=35\,\text{hr}$, $MLT_{cell}=3\,\text{hr}$, throughput $TH = 10$ parts/day.

$$WIP_{process} = 10 \times \dfrac{35}{24} \approx 14.6 \text{ parts}$$

$$WIP_{cell} = 10 \times \dfrac{3}{24} \approx 1.25 \text{ parts}$$

$$\% \Delta WIP = \dfrac{14.6 - 1.25}{14.6} \times 100\% \approx 91.4\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorist's Note:** Little's Law is exact under steady-state conditions. In transient production (very common in job shops), the *instantaneous* WIP can be even higher, making the *peak* WIP reduction even more dramatic.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cell Formation Index (CFI)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define intra-cell and inter-cell moves**

For a part $p$ with operation sequence $\{O_1, O_2, \dots, O_n\}$ mapped to cells $C(O_i)$:

$$\text{Intra-cell moves for } p = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \mathbb{1}\{C(O_i) = C(O_{i+1})\}$$

$$\text{Inter-cell moves for } p = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \mathbb{1}\{C(O_i) \ne C(O_{i+1})\}$$

**Step 2 — Sum over all parts $P$**

$$a = \sum_{p \in P} \text{Intra-cell moves for } p$$

$$b = \sum_{p \in P} \text{Inter-cell moves for } p$$

**Step 3 — Define CFI**

$$CFI = \dfrac{a}{a + b}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**

- $CFI = 1$ → **Perfect cell** (all moves intra-cell).
- $CFI = 0$ → **Worst case** (all moves inter-cell, i.e., a process layout in disguise).
- $CFI \ge 0.7$ → Industry-accepted threshold for a "good" cell.

**Step 5 — Numerical Example**

Suppose 10 parts, total moves = 100, of which $a = 80$ are intra-cell.

$$CFI = \dfrac{80}{80 + 20} = 0.80 \;\; \checkmark \; \text{Good cell}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Process Layout vs. Cellular Layout vs. FMS

| Performance Parameter | Process Layout (Functional) | Cellular Layout (GT-based) | Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS) |
|:----------------------|:---------------------------:|:--------------------------:|:----------------------------------:|
| **Lead Time** | High (baseline) | ↓ 60%–90% | ↓ 70%–95% |
| **WIP Inventory** | High | ↓ 40%–80% | ↓ 50%–90% |
| **Setup Time** | Long | ↓ 50%–75% | ↓ 60%–85% |
| **Machine Utilization** | 25%–35% | 60%–75% | 75%–85% |
| **Labour Productivity** | Baseline | ↑ 15%–30% | ↑ 25%–50% |
| **Floor Space** | Baseline (100%) | ↓ 30%–50% | ↓ 40%–60% |
| **Flexibility (mix)** | Very High | Medium-High | Very High |
| **Volume Range** | Low–Medium | Medium–High | Medium–High, multi-product |
| **Capital Cost** | Low | Medium | Very High |
| **Suitability for CIM** | Poor | Good | Excellent |
| **Information System Need** | Low | Medium (DB for codes) | High (real-time DNC/MES) |
| **Typical Industries** | Job shops, tool rooms | Pumps, valves, fasteners | Aerospace, automotive, defence |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked *“Compare process layout and cellular layout”*, present a **table** like the one above. It scores 3–4 marks on its own and is faster to write than prose.
