- **Vision system:** Camera → Frame grabber → Image processor → Feature extraction → Robot action.
- **Tactile sensor:** Pressure-sensitive array on gripper; provides spatial force map.
- **Nyquist criterion:** Sample at $\geq 2 \times$ max signal frequency.
- **Signal standard:** Industrial 4–20 mA current loop is noise-immune over long distances.
- **CIM integration:** Sensor data flows to PLC → Cell controller → MES → ERP (pyramid).
- **Safety sensors:** Proximity + force = foundation of **collaborative robots (cobots)**.
- **Key equation to memorize:** $V_{out} = K_e \omega$; $\theta_{LSB} = 360°/2^n$; $d = vt/2$; $\varepsilon = \Delta R/(R G)$.
- **Selection criteria:** Range, resolution, accuracy, linearity, bandwidth, robustness, output type.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always draw a **neat block diagram** before writing a sensor answer. Examiners award at least 1–2 marks for a clear diagram with labeled blocks. Also, wherever a numerical value is computed, **state the unit explicitly** — this alone can elevate a 7-mark answer to a full-score response.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robot Accuracy and Applications: Robot Accuracy and Repeatability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT613 - COMPUTER INTEGRATED MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Robot Accuracy and Applications: Robot Accuracy and Repeatability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Robot Accuracy (Pose Accuracy)

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition
> **Robot Accuracy** is the ability of a robot to position its end-effector at a *target point* in the *absolute 3D workspace* that has been programmed or commanded, measured as the deviation between the *commanded pose* and the *mean of the attained poses* over a statistically significant number of repetitions (as per **ISO 9283**).

In simple engineering terms, accuracy is *how close you get to the bullseye* when you ask the robot to go to a specific coordinate (X, Y, Z) in space.

> [!NOTE] Key Concept
> Accuracy is a **one-shot spatial fidelity metric** — it answers: "Did the robot reach where I told it to go in absolute space?"

---

## 1.2 Robot Repeatability (Pose Repeatability)

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition
> **Robot Repeatability** is the ability of the robot to return to a previously attained pose (the *target pose*) when commanded to do so from the *same location*, measured as the closeness of the attained poses (over a number of repetitions) to their *mean value*, typically expressed as a radius of a sphere containing a defined percentage (commonly 95% or 100%) of the measured points.

In plain words, repeatability is *how tightly the robot can cluster its hits* when repeatedly asked to go to the same taught point.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy: The Archery Example

Imagine a person shooting arrows at a target, blindfolded, with the target's position announced aloud.

| Shooter Behavior | Robot Equivalent | Performance Metric |
|---|---|---|
| Arrows cluster **tightly in a group**, but the cluster is **off-center** from the bullseye | Robot returns to *almost* the same spot every cycle, but that spot is shifted from the commanded coordinate | **High Repeatability, Low Accuracy** |
| Arrows scatter **all over the target**, but the **average position** is exactly the bullseye | Robot's average position equals the commanded pose, but each cycle is unpredictable | **High Accuracy, Low Repeatability** |
| Arrows cluster **tightly ON the bullseye** | Robot repeatedly hits the commanded pose with negligible deviation | **High Repeatability AND High Accuracy** |
| Arrows scatter **widely AND off-center** | Robot is unreliable and unusable in production | **Poor Performance** |

> [!TIP] Board Exam Tip
> In KTU valuation, examiners love asking the **archery analogy** in 3-mark Part A questions. Always conclude with: *"Industrial robots typically have HIGH repeatability but MODERATE accuracy — this is why we TEACH points rather than program absolute coordinates."*

---

## 1.4 Why This Distinction Matters in CIM

> [!WARNING] Common Student Misconception
> Students often conflate "accuracy" and "precision/repeatability." They are **NOT** synonyms. A robot can be very repeatable (±0.02 mm) but still inaccurate by 5 mm due to kinematic parameter errors, gear backlash, link deflection, or calibration offsets. CIM students must understand this for *off-line programming (OLP)* and *cell design*.

**Core takeaway:** Repeatability is an **intrinsic** property of the mechanism (gears, encoders, backlash). Accuracy is an **extrinsic** property influenced by **calibration, payload, temperature, and speed**.

---

## 1.5 Standard Governing These Metrics

**ISO 9283:1998 — Manipulating industrial robots — Performance criteria and related test methods** is the universally accepted standard (and the one KTU examiners expect you to cite).

> [!NOTE] ISO 9283 Coverage
> - Pose accuracy and pose repeatability (AP, RP)
> - Multi-directional pose accuracy variation
> - Distance accuracy and distance repeatability
> - Path accuracy and path repeatability (AT, RT)
> - Cornering deviations
> - Velocity characteristics
> - Static compliance
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Performance Metrics

The robot's end-effector pose in 3D Cartesian space is described by **six degrees of freedom**: 3 positional (X, Y, Z) and 3 orientational (roll, pitch, yaw) — collectively called a **pose**.

### 2.1.1 Pose Accuracy (AP)
- The robot is commanded to reach pose **P_cmd** = (x_c, y_c, z_c, φ_c, θ_c, ψ_c).
- The robot executes this **N times** (typically N = 30 in ISO 9283).
- Each execution yields an attained pose **P_i**.
- The **mean attained pose** is computed: **P̄ = (1/N) Σ P_i**.
- The error is the spatial distance between **P_cmd** and **P̄**.

### 2.1.2 Pose Repeatability (RP)
- From the N attained poses, compute their centroid **P̄**.
- Compute the radial distance of each **P_i** from **P̄**.
- **RP** is defined as **r + 3σ**, where:
  - **r** = radius of the sphere containing the **mean position**.
  - **σ** = standard deviation of the distance distribution.
- Practically, ISO 9283 defines **RP** as: **D̄ + 3·S_D**, where D is the distance of each P_i from the centroid.

### 2.1.3 Multi-Directional Pose Accuracy Variation (vAP)
- The robot is commanded to reach the **same Cartesian point from N different approach directions** (typically 5 or more).
- The accuracy is measured for each approach direction.
- **vAP** = the *range* (max - min) of the accuracy values across directions.
- This captures the effect of **joint configuration-dependent stiffness and backlash**.

### 2.1.4 Path Accuracy (AT) and Path Repeatability (RT)
- For continuous trajectories (welding, painting, gluing).
- **Path accuracy (AT)** = the maximum deviation between the *commanded path* and the *mean attained path* over the entire trajectory.
- **Path repeatability (RT)** = the band width (usually ±3σ) around the mean attained path within which **all repetitions** lie at any given point.

### 2.1.5 Distance Accuracy (AD) and Distance Repeatability (RD)
- Distance between two commanded poses, **P1** and **P2**.
- **AD** = difference between the *commanded distance* and the *mean attained distance*.
- **RD** = closeness of repeated distance measurements.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] High-Yield Table for KTU 2024 Board Exams
> Below is the **must-memorize** formula sheet. All variables are scalar distances unless otherwise specified.

| # | Parameter | Formula / Definition | Units | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Pose Accuracy (AP)** | $AP = \sqrt{(x_c - \bar{x})^2 + (y_c - \bar{y})^2 + (z_c - \bar{z})^2}$ | mm | ISO 9283 |
| 2 | **Pose Repeatability (RP)** | $RP = \bar{D} + 3 \cdot S_D$ | mm | ISO 9283 |
| 3 | **Mean Distance (D̄)** | $\bar{D} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} D_i$ | mm | Statistical |
| 4 | **Sample Standard Deviation (S_D)** | $S_D = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (D_i - \bar{D})^2}$ | mm | Statistical |
| 5 | **Distance of i-th pose from centroid** | $D_i = \sqrt{(x_i - \bar{x})^2 + (y_i - \bar{y})^2 + (z_i - \bar{z})^2}$ | mm | Euclidean norm |
| 6 | **Multi-Directional Pose Accuracy Variation (vAP)** | $vAP = AP_{\max} - AP_{\min}$ (over N approach directions) | mm | ISO 9283 |
| 7 | **Path Accuracy (AT)** | $AT = \max_t \left\vert \text{CommandedPath}(t) - \overline{\text{AttainedPath}(t)} \right\vert$ | mm | ISO 9283 |
| 8 | **Path Repeatability (RT)** | $RT(t) = \pm 3 \cdot \sigma(t)$ about the mean attained path | mm | ISO 9283 |
| 9 | **Distance Accuracy (AD)** | $AD = \left\vert d_{cmd} - \bar{d}_{att} \right\vert$ | mm | ISO 9283 |
| 10 | **Distance Repeatability (RD)** | $RD = \bar{d} + 3 \cdot S_d$ | mm | ISO 9283 |
| 11 | **Compliance Deflection** | $\delta = \dfrac{F}{K_{eff}}$, where K_eff is effective stiffness | mm | Static |
| 12 | **Bias Error (Systematic)** | $Bias = \bar{D} - 0$ (offset of centroid from commanded pose) | mm | Statistical |

> [!TIP] Exam Pattern Warning
> For KTU numerical problems, you will almost always be asked to compute **AP** and **RP** from a given data set of attained coordinates. The trick is to (a) compute centroid, (b) compute D_i for each, (c) find D̄, (d) find S_D, and (e) apply **RP = D̄ + 3S_D**.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility — Where This Matters

| Application | Critical Metric | Typical Value Needed |
|---|---|---|
| **Spot Welding (Automotive)** | Pose Repeatability (RP) | ± 0.1 mm |
| **Arc Welding (Seam Tracking)** | Path Repeatability (RT) | ± 0.5 mm |
| **PCB Drilling/Assembly** | Pose Accuracy (AP) | ± 0.02 mm |
| **Pick-and-Place (Packaging)** | Pose Repeatability (RP) | ± 0.05 mm |
| **Painting / Coating** | Path Accuracy (AT) | ± 1.0 mm |
| **Adhesive Bead Application** | Path Repeatability (RT) | ± 0.2 mm |
| **Deburring / Machining** | Multi-Directional Accuracy (vAP) | ± 0.5 mm |

> [!IMPORTANT] Design Insight
> The most important industrial lesson: **for 80% of manufacturing applications, repeatability is what matters** (because the *taught* point IS the reference). Accuracy only dominates in *off-line programmed* tasks where the robot has never been physically to the point before.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Mean Attained Pose

When the robot is commanded to move to a target pose **P_cmd = (x_c, y_c, z_c)** in Cartesian space N times, the encoders/measurement system record the **attained** Cartesian coordinates:

$$P_i = (x_i, y_i, z_i) \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, 3, \ldots, N$$

**Step 1: Compute the mean (centroid) attained pose.**

The x-coordinate of the centroid is the arithmetic mean of all x_i values:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$$

**Step 2: Repeat for y and z coordinates independently.**

$$\bar{y} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i$$

$$\bar{z} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} z_i$$

**Result:** The mean attained pose is **P̄ = (x̄, ȳ, z̄)**. This is the *best estimate* of where the robot "actually thinks" the commanded point is.

**Physical Interpretation:** P̄ represents the **systematic bias** of the robot — the location it consistently goes to, regardless of scatter.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Pose Accuracy (AP)

**Step 1:** The commanded position is (x_c, y_c, z_c). The mean attained position is (x̄, ȳ, z̄).

**Step 2:** Compute the position error vector in each axis:

$$\Delta x = x_c - \bar{x}, \quad \Delta y = y_c - \bar{y}, \quad \Delta z = z_c - \bar{z}$$

**Step 3:** The magnitude of the position error is the **Euclidean norm** of the error vector:

$$AP = \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2 + (\Delta z)^2}$$

**Substituting the definitions:**

$$AP = \sqrt{\left(x_c - \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i\right)^2 + \left(y_c - \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i\right)^2 + \left(z_c - \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} z_i\right)^2}$$

> [!NOTE] Engineering Meaning
> AP captures both **systematic errors** (kinematic parameter mismatch, link length errors, encoder zero-offsets) and **random errors** (backlash, thermal drift, vibration). A high AP means the robot's *internal model of itself* does not match its *real physical geometry*.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Pose Repeatability (RP)

**Step 1:** For each attained pose P_i, compute its radial distance from the centroid P̄:

$$D_i = \sqrt{(x_i - \bar{x})^2 + (y_i - \bar{y})^2 + (z_i - \bar{z})^2}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the mean radial distance:

$$\bar{D} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} D_i$$

**Step 3:** Compute the sample standard deviation of the D_i values:

$$S_D = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (D_i - \bar{D})^2}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the ISO 9283 definition of repeatability — the radius of a sphere centered at P̄ that **contains 99.5% of all attained points** (equivalent to **D̄ + 3·S_D** under the assumption of near-Gaussian distribution):

$$RP = \bar{D} + 3 \cdot S_D$$

> [!IMPORTANT] Statistical Justification
> The factor **3σ** corresponds to a confidence level of **99.73%** for a normal distribution. This is why industrial specifications like "±0.05 mm repeatability" are statistically robust — they represent the worst-case bound for 997 out of 1000 cycles.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

> [!TIP] This is the EXACT pattern KTU examiners use for 14-mark Part B questions.

**Problem Statement:**
A 6-axis articulated robot is commanded to reach the point **(500, 300, 200) mm** in its workspace. It performs **N = 5** repetitions, and the laser tracker records the following attained positions:

| Trial (i) | x_i (mm) | y_i (mm) | z_i (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 500.08 | 300.12 | 199.95 |
| 2 | 499.95 | 299.98 | 200.06 |
| 3 | 500.02 | 300.05 | 200.01 |
| 4 | 500.10 | 299.92 | 199.98 |
| 5 | 499.97 | 300.08 | 200.04 |

**Required:** Compute **(a) Pose Accuracy (AP)** and **(b) Pose Repeatability (RP)** as per ISO 9283.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Compute the centroid (mean) of attained positions.**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{500.08 + 499.95 + 500.02 + 500.10 + 499.97}{5} = \frac{2500.12}{5} = 500.024 \text{ mm}$$

$$\bar{y} = \frac{300.12 + 299.98 + 300.05 + 299.92 + 300.08}{5} = \frac{1500.15}{5} = 300.030 \text{ mm}$$

$$\bar{z} = \frac{199.95 + 200.06 + 200.01 + 199.98 + 200.04}{5} = \frac{1000.04}{5} = 200.008 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Compute the Pose Accuracy (AP).**

$$\Delta x = 500 - 500.024 = -0.024 \text{ mm}$$
$$\Delta y = 300 - 300.030 = -0.030 \text{ mm}$$
$$\Delta z = 200 - 200.008 = -0.008 \text{ mm}$$

$$AP = \sqrt{(-0.024)^2 + (-0.030)^2 + (-0.008)^2}$$

$$AP = \sqrt{0.000576 + 0.000900 + 0.000064}$$

$$AP = \sqrt{0.001540} = 0.0392 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Compute the radial distance D_i for each trial from the centroid (x̄, ȳ, z̄) = (500.024, 300.030, 200.008).**

**For i = 1:** (500.08, 300.12, 199.95)
$$D_1 = \sqrt{(0.056)^2 + (0.090)^2 + (-0.058)^2} = \sqrt{0.003136 + 0.008100 + 0.003364} = \sqrt{0.014600} = 0.1208 \text{ mm}$$

**For i = 2:** (499.95, 299.98, 200.06)
$$D_2 = \sqrt{(-0.074)^2 + (-0.050)^2 + (0.052)^2} = \sqrt{0.005476 + 0.002500 + 0.002704} = \sqrt{0.010680} = 0.1033 \text{ mm}$$

**For i = 3:** (500.02, 300.05, 200.01)
$$D_3 = \sqrt{(-0.004)^2 + (0.020)^2 + (0.002)^2} = \sqrt{0.000016 + 0.000400 + 0.000004} = \sqrt{0.000420} = 0.0205 \text{ mm}$$

**For i = 4:** (500.10, 299.92, 199.98)
$$D_4 = \sqrt{(0.076)^2 + (-0.110)^2 + (-0.028)^2} = \sqrt{0.005776 + 0.012100 + 0.000784} = \sqrt{0.018660} = 0.1366 \text{ mm}$$

**For i = 5:** (499.97, 300.08, 200.04)
$$D_5 = \sqrt{(-0.054)^2 + (0.050)^2 + (0.032)^2} = \sqrt{0.002916 + 0.002500 + 0.001024} = \sqrt{0.006440} = 0.0802 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Compute the mean radial distance D̄.**

$$\bar{D} = \frac{0.1208 + 0.1033 + 0.0205 + 0.1366 + 0.0802}{5} = \frac{0.4614}{5} = 0.0923 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: Compute the sample standard deviation S_D.**

Squared deviations from the mean:

| i | D_i (mm) | (D_i − D̄)² |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.1208 | (0.1208 − 0.0923)² = (0.0285)² = 0.000812 |
| 2 | 0.1033 | (0.1033 − 0.0923)² = (0.0110)² = 0.000121 |
| 3 | 0.0205 | (0.0205 − 0.0923)² = (−0.0718)² = 0.005155 |
| 4 | 0.1366 | (0.1366 − 0.0923)² = (0.0443)² = 0.001962 |
| 5 | 0.0802 | (0.0802 − 0.0923)² = (−0.0121)² = 0.000146 |

**Sum of squared deviations:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{5} (D_i - \bar{D})^2 = 0.000812 + 0.000121 + 0.005155 + 0.001962 + 0.000146 = 0.008196$$

$$S_D = \sqrt{\frac{0.008196}{5-1}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.008196}{4}} = \sqrt{0.002049} = 0.0453 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6: Compute the Pose Repeatability (RP).**

$$RP = \bar{D} + 3 \cdot S_D = 0.0923 + 3 \times 0.0453 = 0.0923 + 0.1359 = 0.2282 \text{ mm}$$

### Final Answer (Model Board Solution):

> **Pose Accuracy (AP) = 0.039 mm (approx 0.04 mm)**
> **Pose Repeatability (RP) = 0.228 mm (approx 0.23 mm)**

**Interpretation:** The robot is **more accurate** (only 0.04 mm off from the commanded point) than repeatable (scatter of 0.23 mm). In this case, the bias error is small, but the cycle-to-cycle scatter dominates — typical of a robot with **gear backlash** but **good kinematic calibration**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sources of Inaccuracy

| Source | Effect Type | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Kinematic parameter errors (link length, joint offset) | Systematic bias → **Reduces AP** | Robot calibration using laser tracker or vision |
| Gear backlash in joints | Random scatter → **Reduces RP** | Anti-backlash gears, preloaded reducers |
| Link compliance (deflection under payload) | Pose-dependent bias | Stiffness modeling, payload compensation |
| Thermal expansion of links | Slow drift → Variable bias | Temperature compensation, warm-up cycles |
| Encoder resolution / quantization | Random, smaller magnitude | Higher-resolution encoders |
| TCP (Tool Center Point) calibration error | Systematic bias at tool | Proper TCP teaching procedure |
| Joint zero-offset drift | Systematic bias | Periodic master reset / mastering |
| Payload inertia mismatch | Path deviation under acceleration | Adaptive path planning, payload identification |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram: Accuracy vs Repeatability in 2D Workspace

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the **decision logic** an engineer uses to diagnose whether a robot has an *accuracy problem* or a *repeatability problem* based on a measurement campaign.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    cmdPose[Commanded Pose Pcmd] --> execLoop[Execute Command N Times with Measurement]
    execLoop --> measData[Collect N Attained Poses P1 P2 ... PN]
    measData --> centroidCalc[Compute Centroid Pbar = Mean of Pi]
    centroidCalc --> apCalc[Compute AP = Distance Pcmd to Pbar]
    centroidCalc --> rpCalc[Compute RP = Dbar + 3 Sigma Di]
    apCalc --> apCheck{AP within tolerance}
    rpCalc --> rpCheck{RP within tolerance}
    apCheck -- YES --> accOK[Accuracy Acceptable]
    apCheck -- NO --> calibFix[Perform Kinematic Calibration]
    rpCheck -- YES --> repOK[Repeatability Acceptable]
    rpCheck -- NO --> mechFix[Inspect Backlash Gears and Compliance]
    calibFix --> retest[Retest Performance]
    mechFix --> retest
    accOK --> deploy[Deploy to Production]
    repOK --> deploy
    retest --> measData
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: ISO 9283 Test Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph testSetup[Test Setup]
        robot[Industrial Robot 6 DOF]
        tracker[Laser Tracker or Coordinate Measuring Machine]
        cube[ISO 9283 Test Cube in Workspace]
    end
    subgraph measurementPhase[Measurement Phase]
        robot -->|Commanded Poses| cube
        cube -->|Attained Poses| tracker
        tracker -->|Raw Data X Y Z| dsp[Data Acquisition System]
    end
    subgraph analysisPhase[Analysis Phase]
        dsp --> apModule[Pose Accuracy Module AP]
        dsp --> rpModule[Pose Repeatability Module RP]
        dsp --> vapModule[Multi Directional Accuracy Module vAP]
        dsp --> atModule[Path Accuracy Module AT]
        dsp --> rtModule[Path Repeatability Module RT]
    end
    subgraph reportingPhase[Reporting Phase]
        apModule --> report[Robot Performance Report ISO 9283]
        rpModule --> report
        vapModule --> report
        atModule --> report
        rtModule --> report
    end
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Robot Calibration Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Start Calibration Campaign] --> meas1[Place Robot in 50 Distinct Configurations]
    meas1 --> meas2[Measure End Effector Position with External Sensor]
    meas2 --> iden[Identify Kinematic Parameter Errors using Least Squares]
    iden --> comp[Compute Correction Parameters Delta Theta Delta Alpha Delta a]
    comp --> update[Update Robot Controller Kinematic Model]
    update --> retest[Re measure Performance Metrics]
    retest --> check{AP and RP within Spec}
    check -- NO --> meas1
    check -- YES --> finish[Robot Calibrated and Production Ready]
```

## 4.4 Application Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    apps[Industrial Robot Applications] --> spotW[Spot Welding]
    apps --> arcW[Arc Welding]
    apps --> paint[Painting and Coating]
    apps --> pick[Pick and Place]
    apps --> assy[Assembly]
    apps --> pcb[PCB Drilling]
    spotW --> reqRP[Requires High Pose Repeatability]
    arcW --> reqRT[Requires High Path Repeatability]
    paint --> reqAT[Requires High Path Accuracy]
    pick --> reqRP
    assy --> reqAP[Requires High Pose Accuracy]
    pcb --> reqAP
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Define Robot Pose Accuracy and Robot Pose Repeatability as per ISO 9283. How do they fundamentally differ from each other?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks – 60–80 words):**

**Pose Accuracy (AP)** is the deviation between the **commanded pose** and the **mean of the attained poses** when the robot is asked to reach a target point multiple times. **Pose Repeatability (RP)** is the **closeness of attained poses to one another**, measured as the radius (D̄ + 3σ) of a sphere centered at the centroid containing 99.5% of the measured points. The fundamental difference is that **AP is a one-shot spatial fidelity metric against an absolute reference**, while **RP measures cycle-to-cycle consistency** and is an *intrinsic* mechanical property. A robot can be highly repeatable but inaccurate (e.g., due to poor calibration), but cannot be highly accurate if it is not also repeatable.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**A 6-axis robot is commanded to reach a point in 3D space. List the steps required to compute the Pose Repeatability (RP) experimentally as per ISO 9283.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks – numbered list, 60–80 words):**

1. **Command** the robot to reach the target pose **N times** (typically N = 30 per ISO 9283) and record the attained Cartesian coordinates (x_i, y_i, z_i) using a precision measurement device (laser tracker, CMM).
2. **Compute the centroid** (x̄, ȳ, z̄) of the N attained poses.
3. **Calculate the radial distance** D_i from each P_i to the centroid using the Euclidean norm.
4. **Compute the mean radial distance D̄** and the **sample standard deviation S_D** of the D_i values.
5. **Apply the ISO 9283 formula** RP = D̄ + 3·S_D to obtain the pose repeatability in mm.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice (ESE Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 4 Past Paper]**

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the **archery target analogy** to differentiate between **robot accuracy** and **robot repeatability**. Discuss why industrial robots typically have high repeatability but moderate accuracy, and how this affects **off-line programming (OLP)** in a CIM cell.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A robot is commanded to reach a Cartesian point (250, 400, 150) mm. Five repetitions yielded the following attained positions. Compute (i) **Pose Accuracy (AP)**, (ii) **Mean radial distance (D̄)**, and (iii) **Pose Repeatability (RP)** as per ISO 9283.

| Trial | x (mm) | y (mm) | z (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 250.10 | 400.20 | 150.05 |
| 2 | 250.05 | 399.95 | 149.98 |
| 3 | 249.98 | 400.08 | 150.10 |
| 4 | 250.15 | 400.02 | 149.92 |
| 5 | 250.00 | 400.12 | 150.03 |

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) Model Answer:**

*Sketch description (2 marks):* Draw a circular target with four quadrants — (i) tight cluster ON bullseye, (ii) tight cluster OFF bullseye, (iii) scattered ON center, (iv) scattered OFF center. Label each with appropriate robot metric.

*Explanation (3 marks):* A tight cluster on the bullseye represents **high accuracy + high repeatability**. A tight cluster off the bullseye represents **high repeatability but low accuracy** — the robot goes to the same place every time, but that place is wrong. Scattered points around the center represent **high accuracy but low repeatability**. Scattered points off-center represent **poor performance** on both.

*Industrial reality (1 mark):* Robots have high RP (±0.02 to ±0.1 mm) but moderate AP (±1 to ±5 mm) due to kinematic parameter errors, link deflection, and thermal effects. **OLP Impact (1 mark):** Because of this, OLP-generated programs must be **fine-tuned by teaching critical points**, and **kinematic calibration** is essential for high-accuracy applications like machining or PCB drilling.

**Part (b) Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the centroid.**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{250.10 + 250.05 + 249.98 + 250.15 + 250.00}{5} = \frac{1250.28}{5} = 250.056 \text{ mm}$$

$$\bar{y} = \frac{400.20 + 399.95 + 400.08 + 400.02 + 400.12}{5} = \frac{2004.37}{5} = 400.874 \text{ ...}$$

> **Correction during solution writing:** $\bar{y} = \dfrac{400.20 + 399.95 + 400.08 + 400.02 + 400.12}{5} = \dfrac{2004.37}{5} = 400.874 \text{ mm}$

$$\bar{z} = \frac{150.05 + 149.98 + 150.10 + 149.92 + 150.03}{5} = \frac{750.08}{5} = 150.016 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Compute Pose Accuracy (AP).**

$$\Delta x = 250 - 250.056 = -0.056 \text{ mm}$$
$$\Delta y = 400 - 400.874 = -0.874 \text{ mm}$$
$$\Delta z = 150 - 150.016 = -0.016 \text{ mm}$$

$$AP = \sqrt{(-0.056)^2 + (-0.874)^2 + (-0.016)^2}$$

$$AP = \sqrt{0.003136 + 0.763876 + 0.000256}$$

$$AP = \sqrt{0.767268} = 0.876 \text{ mm} \approx 0.88 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Compute D_i for each trial.**

Centroid: (250.056, 400.874, 150.016)

| i | D_i Computation | D_i (mm) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | √((0.044)² + (-0.674)² + (0.034)²) = √(0.001936 + 0.454276 + 0.001156) | 0.677 |
| 2 | √((-0.006)² + (-0.924)² + (-0.036)²) = √(0.000036 + 0.853776 + 0.001296) | 0.925 |
| 3 | √((-0.076)² + (-0.794)² + (0.084)²) = √(0.005776 + 0.630436 + 0.007056) | 0.801 |
| 4 | √((0.094)² + (-0.854)² + (-0.096)²) = √(0.008836 + 0.729316 + 0.009216) | 0.866 |
| 5 | √((-0.056)² + (-0.754)² + (0.014)²) = √(0.003136 + 0.568516 + 0.000196) | 0.756 |

**Step 4: Compute D̄.**

$$\bar{D} = \frac{0.677 + 0.925 + 0.801 + 0.866 + 0.756}{5} = \frac{4.025}{5} = 0.805 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: Compute S_D.**

| i | D_i | (D_i − D̄)² |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.677 | (−0.128)² = 0.016384 |
| 2 | 0.925 | (0.120)² = 0.014400 |
| 3 | 0.801 | (−0.004)² = 0.000016 |
| 4 | 0.866 | (0.061)² = 0.003721 |
| 5 | 0.756 | (−0.049)² = 0.002401 |

$$\sum (D_i - \bar{D})^2 = 0.036922$$

$$S_D = \sqrt{\frac{0.036922}{4}} = \sqrt{0.009231} = 0.0961 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6: Compute RP.**

$$RP = \bar{D} + 3 \cdot S_D = 0.805 + 3 \times 0.0961 = 0.805 + 0.2883 = 1.093 \text{ mm} \approx 1.09 \text{ mm}$$

### **Final Answer for Part (b):**
- **Pose Accuracy (AP) = 0.88 mm**
- **Mean Radial Distance (D̄) = 0.805 mm**
- **Pose Repeatability (RP) = 1.09 mm**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the various **performance metrics** specified by **ISO 9283** for industrial robot testing. Describe in detail **Pose Accuracy, Pose Repeatability, Multi-directional Pose Accuracy Variation, Path Accuracy, and Path Repeatability** with their mathematical formulations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **key factors affecting robot accuracy** in an industrial environment. Explain how **kinematic calibration using a laser tracker** improves the absolute accuracy of a 6-DOF articulated robot. Illustrate your answer with a calibration flow diagram.

#### Model Answer (Condensed – Key Points):

**Part (a) Key Points (each metric ~1.4 marks):**

1. **Pose Accuracy (AP):** $AP = \sqrt{(x_c - \bar{x})^2 + (y_c - \bar{y})^2 + (z_c - \bar{z})^2}$. Measures deviation between commanded and mean attained pose.
2. **Pose Repeatability (RP):** $RP = \bar{D} + 3 S_D$. Measures cycle-to-cycle scatter, statistical radius for 99.5% confidence.
3. **Multi-Directional Pose Accuracy Variation (vAP):** $vAP = AP_{\max} - AP_{\min}$ measured over multiple approach directions. Captures joint-configuration-dependent errors.
4. **Path Accuracy (AT):** Maximum deviation of mean attained path from commanded path. Critical for arc welding, painting.
5. **Path Repeatability (RT):** Band width ±3σ of attained paths. Measures trajectory consistency.

**Part (b) Key Points:**

*Factors (4 marks):* Kinematic parameter errors (link lengths, twist angles, joint offsets), gear backlash, link compliance, thermal expansion, TCP calibration, payload variation, encoder resolution, joint zero-offset drift, base frame misalignment.

*Calibration flow (3 marks – use the diagram from Section 4.3 as reference):* (i) Measure robot end-effector in 50+ configurations using laser tracker, (ii) Identify kinematic errors via least-squares optimization, (iii) Update controller DH parameters, (iv) Re-test and verify improvement in AP from ~2-5 mm to ~0.1-0.3 mm. Result: robot is now usable for off-line programmed high-accuracy tasks like aerospace drilling or assembly.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in 14-Mark Questions
> 1. **Confusing accuracy with repeatability** in definitions — a fatal flaw that loses 2-3 marks immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting to use N−1 in sample standard deviation** (Bessel's correction) — losing 1 mark on the statistical formulation.
> 3. **Computing AP as the maximum D_i instead of the centroid distance** — AP uses the *centroid* of attained poses, not the worst trial.
> 4. **Using the 2D Euclidean formula** (√(Δx² + Δy²)) instead of the 3D formula (√(Δx² + Δy² + Δz²)) for spatial problems.
> 5. **Not citing ISO 9283** in definitions — examiners expect this standard name explicitly in the answer.
> 6. **Mixing up AP and AT** (pose vs path) — pose is a single point; path is a continuous trajectory.
> 7. **Skipping units (mm)** in the final numerical answer — minor but consistently penalized.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST**

- ✅ **Accuracy = how close to the commanded (absolute) position** (one-shot, spatial bias).
- ✅ **Repeatability = how close to its own previous attempts** (cycle-to-cycle scatter).
- ✅ **Standard to cite: ISO 9283:1998** — performance criteria for manipulating industrial robots.
- ✅ **AP formula uses centroid of attained poses**; **RP formula uses D̄ + 3S_D**.
- ✅ **The 3σ rule** = 99.73% confidence interval (Gaussian assumption).
- ✅ **Industrial robots: HIGH repeatability (±0.02 mm), MODERATE accuracy (±1-5 mm)**.
- ✅ **vAP = AP_max − AP_min** over multiple approach directions.
- ✅ **AT and RT** apply to continuous paths (welding, painting); **AP and RP** apply to discrete points.
- ✅ **Calibration** (laser tracker-based) improves accuracy dramatically but does not change repeatability.
- ✅ **Repeatability is intrinsic** (mechanism quality); **accuracy is extrinsic** (calibration, environment).
- ✅ **For pick-and-place and spot welding → repeatability matters most.**
- ✅ **For OLP-based machining and drilling → accuracy matters most.**
- ✅ **Sample standard deviation uses N−1** (Bessel's correction) in the denominator.
- ✅ **N = 30 trials** is the standard ISO 9283 sample size for full characterization.
- ✅ **TCP (Tool Center Point) calibration** is the FIRST step in any robot deployment — it sets the reference frame for the end-effector.
- ✅ **Application mapping:** Spot Welding = RP, Arc Welding = RT, Painting = AT, PCB Drilling = AP, Assembly = AP, Pick-and-Place = RP.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Industrial Applications of Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT613 - COMPUTER INTEGRATED MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Industrial Applications of Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Industrial Robot** is defined by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO 8373:2021)** as an *automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator, programmable in three or more axes, which can be either fixed in place or mobile for use in industrial automation applications.*

In the context of **Computer Integrated Manufacturing (CIM)**, *Industrial Applications of Robots* refers to the systematic deployment of these reprogrammable manipulators — integrated with sensors, controllers, and the manufacturing information system — to perform tasks traditionally executed by human operators or hard automation, with the goal of achieving higher throughput, consistency, safety, and flexibility.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Core Definition**: The three mandatory traits that distinguish an *industrial robot* from a simple pick-and-place machine or hard automation are: **(1) Reprogrammability**, **(2) Multifunctionality**, and **(3) Three or more programmable axes of motion**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine a **versatile factory worker who never sleeps, never takes a tea break, never makes a mistake, and can lift weights ten times heavier than a human**. That is essentially an industrial robot.

- **Re-programmability** is like teaching the worker a new job every morning: today welding, tomorrow painting, next week palletizing.
- **Sensors and feedback** act as the worker's *eyes and skin* — they "feel" force, "see" position, and "sense" obstacles.
- **The end-effector (gripper/tool)** is the worker's *hand* — swap it, and the same robot becomes a welder, a painter, or a screw-driver.

In a CIM cell, this "worker" is connected to a **central Manufacturing Execution System (MES)**, which tells the robot *what* to make, *when* to start, and *how many* to produce — much like a supervisor giving instructions to a human employee. The robot then executes these instructions with micron-level repeatability, **24 hours a day, 7 days a week**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters in KTU CIM**: Robots are the *physical execution arm* of the CIM pyramid. Without them, the integration between the *planning level* (ERP/MRP) and the *shop-floor level* (CNC, conveyor, assembly) remains incomplete. The robot bridges the digital plan to the physical product.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Standard Industrial Values)

| Metric | Typical Industrial Value |
|---|---|
| **Payload capacity** | 3 kg to 800 kg |
| **Reach (working envelope)** | 0.5 m to 4.0 m |
| **Repeatability** | $\pm 0.02$ mm to $\pm 0.1$ mm |
| **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** | 4 to 7 |
| **Cycle time (typical pick-place)** | 0.5 s to 5 s |
| **Operating temperature** | 0 °C to 45 °C |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Industrial Robot Applications

Industrial robot applications are broadly classified into **four functional families**. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly expects students to identify *which type of robot configuration (articulated, SCARA, Cartesian, Delta)* suits a given application.

#### 2.1.1 Material Handling & Transfer
- **Loading/Unloading** CNC machines, presses, injection-moulding machines.
- **Palletizing & Depalletizing** boxes, bags, bottles onto pallets.
- **Pick-and-Place** of components on a conveyor.
- **Why robots?** — High repeatability, no fatigue, 24×7 uptime.

#### 2.1.2 Processing Operations
- **Welding**: Spot welding (automotive body), Arc welding (MIG/MAG/TIG), Laser welding.
- **Painting & Coating**: Automotive body painting, powder coating, adhesive dispensing.
- **Machining**: Deburring, drilling, grinding, polishing, water-jet cutting.
- **Heat treatment, sealing, gluing**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip**: When asked to justify a robot for painting, always mention **Hazardous Environment + Human Safety** as the prime driver, not just speed.

#### 2.1.3 Assembly Operations
- **Peg-in-hole insertion**, screw driving, snap-fit assembly.
- **PCB component placement**, soldering, wire harnessing.
- **Gluing, riveting, clinching**.

#### 2.1.4 Inspection & Quality Control
- **Dimensional metrology** using vision systems.
- **Surface defect detection**, leak testing, functional testing.
- Robots carry the inspection probe; vision/CNC backs the data processing.

### 2.2 Robot Selection Decision Tree (Why & How)

A KTU 2024 student must master the *reasoning* behind robot selection, not just memorize robot types.

1. **Identify the dominant process** — Material handling? Welding? Assembly?
2. **Quantify the payload** — Mass of workpiece + mass of end-effector + safety factor (typically ×1.5 to ×2.0).
3. **Map the working envelope** — Compute the maximum radial reach required; select a robot whose *envelope just contains* the work volume (over-spec wastes money).
4. **Determine DOF needed**:
   - 4 DOF → pick-place, palletizing.
   - 5–6 DOF → welding, painting, complex assembly.
   - 7 DOF → confined-space machining, collaborative work.
5. **Specify repeatability**: Welding $\pm 0.1$ mm, Assembly $\pm 0.02$ mm, Painting $\pm 1$ mm.
6. **Check environmental constraints**: temperature, dust, humidity, explosive atmosphere (ATEX rating).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following equations are **guaranteed to appear** in either Part A (3-mark derivations) or Part B (14-mark economic-justification problems). Memorize the *meaning* of every variable, not just the symbol.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical LaTeX Note**: In the table below, the absolute value / division bar uses `\vert` to prevent breaking the markdown table.

| # | Formula | Description / Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{PB} \;=\; \dfrac{I_{0}}{S_{a}}$ | **Payback Period** (years) = Initial Investment / Annual Savings. Used to decide *how fast* the robot pays for itself. |
| 2 | $\text{ROI} \;=\; \dfrac{S_{a} - C_{a}}{I_{0}} \times 100$ | **Return on Investment** (%). Compares net annual savings to initial capital outlay. |
| 3 | $\eta_{u} \;=\; \dfrac{t_{\text{operational}}}{t_{\text{available}}} \times 100$ | **Robot Utilization Efficiency** (%). KTU benchmark $\geq 80\%$. |
| 4 | $R_{p} \;=\; \dfrac{60 \times \eta_{u}}{T_{c} \times 100}$ | **Production Rate** (parts/min) where $T_c$ is cycle time in minutes. |
| 5 | $C_{\text{unit}} \;=\; \dfrac{C_{a} + I_{0}/N}{R_{p} \times t_{\text{shift}}}$ | **Cost per unit produced** ($N$ = planning horizon in years). |
| 6 | $P_{\text{dyn}} \;=\; m \cdot (g + a) \cdot F_{s}$ | **Dynamic payload** (N) where $m$ = mass, $a$ = acceleration, $F_s$ = safety factor (1.5–2.0). |
| 7 | $t_{\text{cycle}} \;=\; t_{\text{move}} + t_{\text{process}} + t_{\text{wait}}$ | **Cycle time decomposition** — sum of motion + processing + wait/idle. |
| 8 | $\text{NPV} \;=\; \sum_{t=1}^{N} \dfrac{S_{a,t}}{(1+r)^{t}} - I_{0}$ | **Net Present Value** with discount rate $r$ (used in advanced economic justification). |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are the *lingua franca* of manufacturing engineering. A factory automation engineer uses $T_{PB}$ to convince a CFO to fund a robot project; the design engineer uses $P_{\text{dyn}}$ to size the robot's wrist motor; the production manager uses $\eta_u$ to schedule the line. In KTU examinations, a candidate who presents the *formula → variable definition → substitution → final answer* flow scores full marks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Payback Period & ROI from First Principles

**Problem (KTU Style):** A CIM cell investment of ₹ 24,00,000 in a 6-axis articulated robot is expected to save ₹ 6,00,000 per year in labour + scrap + downtime costs. Calculate (a) the payback period, and (b) the ROI over a 5-year horizon.

**Step 1 — Identify the variables and write the governing equation.**

The **Payback Period** is the time taken for cumulative savings to equal the initial investment. Mathematically, if $S_a$ is the *constant annual savings* and $I_0$ is the *one-time investment*:

$$
T_{PB} \;=\; \frac{I_{0}}{S_{a}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**

Here, $I_0 = \text{₹ } 24{,}00{,}000$ and $S_a = \text{₹ } 6{,}00{,}000$ per year. Substituting:

$$
T_{PB} \;=\; \frac{24{,}00{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000} \text{ years}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the ratio.**

$$
T_{PB} \;=\; 4 \text{ years}
$$

This means the robot pays for itself in exactly 4 years. After the 4th year, every rupee saved is pure profit to the company.

**Step 4 — Derive the ROI equation from fundamentals.**

ROI is defined as the *ratio of net savings over the planning horizon to the initial investment, expressed as a percentage*. For an $N$-year horizon:

$$
\text{ROI} \;=\; \frac{\text{Net Savings over } N \text{ years}}{I_{0}} \times 100
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the net savings for $N=5$ years.**

Total savings over 5 years:

$$
S_{\text{total}} \;=\; S_{a} \times N \;=\; 6{,}00{,}000 \times 5
$$

$$
S_{\text{total}} \;=\; 30{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute into the ROI formula.**

$$
\text{ROI} \;=\; \frac{30{,}00{,}000 - 24{,}00{,}000}{24{,}00{,}000} \times 100
$$

**Step 7 — Final arithmetic.**

$$
\text{ROI} \;=\; \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{24{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \;=\; 25\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** A 25% return over 5 years translates to an average of **5% per year** — acceptable for manufacturing, where the typical hurdle rate is 8–12%. If the discount rate $r$ is high, a more rigorous NPV analysis (Equation 8) should be used.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Dynamic Payload Sizing for a Welding Robot

**Problem:** A 6-axis robot must hold a welding torch of mass 8 kg and a workpiece of mass 12 kg. The combined mass accelerates at $a = 2 \;\text{m/s}^2$ during motion. The safety factor is $F_s = 1.5$. Compute the *required dynamic payload rating* of the robot.

**Step 1 — Write the dynamic payload equation.**

$$
P_{\text{dyn}} \;=\; m_{\text{total}} \cdot (g + a) \cdot F_{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the total mass (torch + workpiece).**

$$
m_{\text{total}} \;=\; m_{\text{torch}} + m_{\text{workpiece}} \;=\; 8 + 12
$$

$$
m_{\text{total}} \;=\; 20 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the gravitational and acceleration terms.**

Using $g = 9.81 \;\text{m/s}^2$ and $a = 2 \;\text{m/s}^2$:

$$
P_{\text{dyn}} \;=\; 20 \cdot (9.81 + 2) \cdot 1.5
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the bracket.**

$$
9.81 + 2 \;=\; 11.81 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply the three terms.**

$$
P_{\text{dyn}} \;=\; 20 \times 11.81 \times 1.5
$$

$$
P_{\text{dyn}} \;=\; 354.3 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to kg-equivalent for robot selection.**

$$
m_{\text{rated}} \;=\; \frac{P_{\text{dyn}}}{g} \;=\; \frac{354.3}{9.81} \;\approx\; 36.1 \text{ kg}
$$

**Conclusion:** The engineer should select a robot with a *rated payload of at least 40 kg* (next standard size above 36.1 kg).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Application → Robot Type Mapping

This matrix is **directly aligned** with KTU 2024 syllabus outcomes CO4 (analyze robot selection for industrial tasks). Memorize this mapping.

| Application | Typical Robot Type | Key Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Spot welding (car body) | **6-axis articulated** | High DOF needed to reach complex 3D seams. |
| Arc welding (MIG/TIG) | **6-axis articulated** | Continuous-path control, dexterity. |
| Painting | **6-axis articulated (explosion-proof)** | Reach into cavities; hazardous environment. |
| Palletizing | **4-axis articulated (heavy payload)** | Simple up/down + rotation; high payload. |
| PCB pick-and-place | **SCARA** | Fast planar motion, vertical insertion. |
| High-speed packaging | **Delta (spider) robot** | Sub-millisecond pick-place from conveyor. |
| CNC machine tending | **6-axis articulated** | Reach into machine tool enclosure. |
| Adhesive dispensing | **Cartesian / gantry** | Linear path, large work envelope. |
| Inspection / metrology | **6-axis + vision** | Precise probe positioning. |
| Foundry / forging | **Heavy-duty articulated (foundry grade)** | Heat-resistant, IP67 sealing. |
| Collaborative assembly | **7-DOF cobot** | Safe human-robot interaction, force sensing. |
| Polishing / deburring | **6-axis + force/torque sensor** | Compliance control for surface finish. |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define an industrial robot as per ISO 8373. List the three mandatory characteristics.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An industrial robot, as per **ISO 8373:2021**, is defined as an *automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator, programmable in three or more axes, which can be either fixed in place or mobile for use in industrial automation applications.* The three mandatory characteristics that distinguish an industrial robot from other automation equipment are:
1. **Reprogrammability** — task definition can be altered via software without physical reconstruction.
2. **Multifunctionality** — the same robot can perform multiple tasks by changing the end-effector.
3. **Three or more programmable axes** — provides the dexterity needed for spatial manipulation.

**(3 marks: 1 mark for ISO definition, 1 mark for reprogrammability + multifunctionality, 1 mark for three-or-more axes).**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4 Expected]**
*State any four industrial applications of robots and justify robot use in one of them.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Four industrial applications of robots:
1. **Material handling & palletizing** — high payload, repetitive motion.
2. **Spot and arc welding** in automotive body shops.
3. **Painting and coating** in hazardous vapour environments.
4. **Assembly operations** such as screw driving and PCB placement.
5. **Machine tending** for CNC/press loading.

**Justification (pick one, e.g., painting):**
In automobile painting, the work environment contains volatile organic compounds and isocyanates toxic to humans. A **6-axis articulated, explosion-proof robot** offers a sealed, intrinsically safe actuation system, consistent spray pattern (reducing paint wastage by up to 30%), and continuous operation without fatigue — all justifying the investment.

**(3 marks: 1 mark for listing four apps, 2 marks for the justified explanation).**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to include safety factor** in dynamic-payload derivation. Most students lose **2 marks** here.
> 2. **Stating only the payback formula without variable definitions** — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark per missing variable.
> 3. **Confusing SCARA with Cartesian** in PCB applications — these are *different* robot types.
> 4. **Omitting the rate of return / NPV discussion** in economic-justification problems reduces a 14-mark answer to 10.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify the major industrial applications of robots. For each category, state the typical robot configuration and one real-world example.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A manufacturing firm is evaluating the installation of a robotic MIG-welding cell. The total investment is **₹ 18,00,000**. The cell is expected to save **₹ 4,50,000 per year** in labour, **₹ 50,000 per year** in scrap reduction, and eliminate **₹ 1,00,000 per year** in rework. The annual operating cost of the cell is **₹ 2,00,000**. Compute (i) the payback period and (ii) the ROI over a 6-year horizon. Comment on the investment decision.

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) [7 marks] Classification of Industrial Applications of Robots:**

| # | Category | Typical Robot | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Material Handling & Transfer** | 4–6 axis articulated, heavy payload | Palletizing cement bags at a plant |
| 2 | **Welding** (spot, arc, MIG, TIG, laser) | 6-axis articulated | Car body assembly line (e.g., Maruti Suzuki plant) |
| 3 | **Painting & Coating** | 6-axis, explosion-proof, hollow-wrist | Automotive primer + basecoat booth |
| 4 | **Machining Support** (deburring, grinding, polishing) | 6-axis + force sensor | Turbine blade deburring in aerospace |
| 5 | **Assembly** (peg-in-hole, screw, PCB) | SCARA, 6-axis, delta | Mobile phone PCB assembly |
| 6 | **Machine Tending** | 6-axis articulated | Loading billets into a CNC turning centre |
| 7 | **Inspection & Metrology** | 6-axis + vision + laser scanner | CMM-traced dimensional inspection of castings |
| 8 | **Adhesive / Sealant Dispensing** | Cartesian gantry | Windshield bonding in automotive |

*(1 mark for correctly naming each category; 1 mark for the correct robot type; 1 mark for example. Total 3 categories × 2 marks + flexibility for additional points.)*

**(b) [7 marks] Economic Justification Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Compute Gross Annual Savings.**

$$
S_{\text{gross}} \;=\; 4{,}50{,}000 + 50{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
S_{\text{gross}} \;=\; 6{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Net Annual Savings (after operating cost).**

$$
S_{a} \;=\; S_{\text{gross}} - C_{a} \;=\; 6{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
S_{a} \;=\; 4{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Payback Period.**

$$
T_{PB} \;=\; \frac{I_{0}}{S_{a}} \;=\; \frac{18{,}00{,}000}{4{,}00{,}000}
$$

$$
T_{PB} \;=\; 4.5 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Total Savings over 6 years.**

$$
S_{6} \;=\; 4{,}00{,}000 \times 6 \;=\; 24{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Net Profit over 6 years.**

$$
\text{Net Profit} \;=\; S_{6} - I_{0} \;=\; 24{,}00{,}000 - 18{,}00{,}000 \;=\; 6{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 6 — Compute ROI.**

$$
\text{ROI} \;=\; \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{18{,}00{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{ROI} \;=\; 33.33\%
$$

This corresponds to an average annual return of approximately **5.56%** over 6 years.

**Step 7 — Comment on Investment Decision.**

If the firm's hurdle rate is **10% per annum** (typical for Indian manufacturing SMEs), the simple ROI is *insufficient* to justify investment on a pure-financial basis. However, secondary benefits such as **improved weld quality, reduced worker exposure to UV/fumes, and 24×7 production** may tip the decision in favour. A more rigorous NPV analysis with discount rate $r = 10\%$ is recommended:

$$
\text{NPV} \;=\; \sum_{t=1}^{6} \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{(1.10)^{t}} - 18{,}00{,}000
$$

Computing the geometric series:

$$
\text{NPV} \;=\; 4{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{1 - (1.10)^{-6}}{0.10} - 18{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{NPV} \;=\; 4{,}00{,}000 \times 4.355 - 18{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{NPV} \;=\; 17{,}42{,}000 - 18{,}00{,}000 \;\approx\; -58{,}000
$$

The NPV is **slightly negative**, so the project is marginal. The firm should renegotiate the robot cost or look for additional savings (e.g., energy reduction) before proceeding.

*(Mark distribution: Step 1–2: 2 marks; Step 3: 2 marks; Step 4–6: 2 marks; Step 7 (comment + NPV): 1 mark.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of a robotic **arc-welding** cell. List any **five** process parameters that the robot controller must monitor in real time.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A pick-and-place SCARA robot handles a 1.5 kg component. The horizontal reach is 600 mm and the cycle consists of: *move to pick = 0.4 s*, *grip close = 0.1 s*, *move to place = 0.4 s*, *release = 0.1 s*. The cell operates two shifts of 8 hours each, 250 days per year. The robot availability is **95%**. Compute the **annual production rate (parts/year)**.

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) [7 marks] Arc-Welding Robotic Cell:**

A **robotic arc-welding cell** consists of:
- **6-axis articulated robot** with a hollow-wrist design for cable routing.
- **Welding power source** (MIG/MAG/TIG inverter).
- **Wire feeder**, **gas cylinder** (Argon/CO₂ mix), and **torch** mounted on the robot flange.
- **Rotating table / positioner** to orient the workpiece for optimal torch angle (typically push-angle of 10–15°).
- **Seam-tracking sensor** (through-the-arc or laser) for adaptive weld-path correction.
- **Safety enclosure** with light curtains and fume extraction.

**Working principle:** The robot follows a *continuous-path* trajectory programmed via *teach-pendant* or *offline programming (OLP)*. The controller synchronizes the **travel speed, wire feed rate, arc voltage, and current** to maintain a stable weld pool.

**Five real-time monitored parameters:**

1. **Arc current (A)** — controls penetration depth.
2. **Arc voltage (V)** — controls bead width.
3. **Wire feed speed (m/min)** — must match travel speed.
4. **Travel speed (mm/s)** — affects heat input.
5. **Torch-to-work distance (mm)** — stick-out; affects arc stability.
6. *(Optional 6th)* Shielding gas flow rate (L/min).

*(Block diagram: 3 marks; working description: 2 marks; five parameters: 2 marks.)*

**(b) [7 marks] Production Rate Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Compute the total cycle time.**

$$
T_{c} \;=\; t_{\text{move-pick}} + t_{\text{grip}} + t_{\text{move-place}} + t_{\text{release}}
$$

$$
T_{c} \;=\; 0.4 + 0.1 + 0.4 + 0.1
$$

$$
T_{c} \;=\; 1.0 \text{ seconds per part}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute parts per minute (theoretical).**

$$
R_{p,\text{theoretical}} \;=\; \frac{60}{T_{c}} \;=\; \frac{60}{1.0} \;=\; 60 \text{ parts/min}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute available operating time per day.**

$$
t_{\text{day}} \;=\; 2 \text{ shifts} \times 8 \text{ h} \;=\; 16 \text{ h/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to minutes and apply availability factor.**

$$
t_{\text{effective}} \;=\; 16 \times 60 \times 0.95 \;=\; 912 \text{ min/day}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute parts per day.**

$$
P_{\text{day}} \;=\; R_{p,\text{theoretical}} \times t_{\text{effective}} \;=\; 60 \times 912
$$

$$
P_{\text{day}} \;=\; 54{,}720 \text{ parts/day}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute annual production.**

$$
P_{\text{year}} \;=\; P_{\text{day}} \times 250 \;=\; 54{,}720 \times 250
$$

$$
P_{\text{year}} \;=\; 1{,}36{,}80{,}000 \text{ parts/year}
$$

$$
P_{\text{year}} \;\approx\; 1.37 \text{ crore parts/year}
$$

*(Mark distribution: Step 1: 1 mark; Step 2: 1 mark; Step 3–4: 2 marks; Step 5: 1 mark; Step 6: 1 mark; Unit & interpretation: 1 mark.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision — Memorize This Block:**

- **ISO 8373:2021** definition must be quoted *verbatim* for full credit.
- An industrial robot is **reprogrammable + multifunctional + ≥ 3 axes** (all three required).
- **Application taxonomy** = Material handling, Processing, Assembly, Inspection.
- **SCARA** = PCB, fast planar; **Delta** = high-speed packaging; **Cartesian** = dispensing; **6-axis articulated** = welding/painting; **Cobot** = collaborative assembly.
- **Payback Period** $T_{PB} = I_0 / S_a$ — always state units (years).
- **ROI** = (Net Savings / Investment) × 100 — net, not gross.
- **Dynamic payload** must include the **safety factor (1.5–2.0)** and the **acceleration term**.
- **Cycle time** = move time + process time + wait time — not just motion alone.
- **Utilization** ≥ 80% is a KTU benchmark for a healthy CIM cell.
- **Hazardous applications** (painting, foundry, welding) → **explosion-proof, IP67+ sealed robots**.
- **Economic justification** requires both *simple payback* AND *NPV/ROI* discussion for full marks.
- **CIM hierarchy**: ERP → MES → SCADA → PLC/CNC → Robot/Sensor (Level 0).
- Always convert **grams → kg, mm → m** before substitution in $P_{\text{dyn}}$ to avoid mark loss.
- For **14-mark problems**, present *Formula → Variable Definition → Substitution → Result → Interpretation*. Missing any one step costs 1 mark.
- KTU 2024 frequently tests the **decision matrix** (Figure 2) — memorize the application-to-robot-type mapping.
- **Don't confuse payload (kg) with reach (m)** — payload is a force rating; reach is a geometric envelope.
